Hence, it was customary for the men who spliced the mainbrace to be rewarded with an extra ration of rum. Over time, the order to "splice the mainbrace" came to mean that the whole crew would receive an extra ration of rum, issued after victory or to celebrate a special occasion such as a royal birth.

name: Major Bailey #1
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE (only to bruise) mint with gin in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into glass half filled with crushed ice
CHURN (stir) drink with ice using a barspoon
Top the glass to the brim with more crushed ice and churn again
Serve with straws.
review: As Victor says of this gin based Julep, "This is a hell of a drink."
history: Adapted from a recipe in the 1947 Trader Vic's Bartender Guide by Victor Bergeron.

name: Major Bailey #2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SLING GLASS.
BLEND all ingredients with 7oz scoop of crushed ice.
Serve with straws.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: Made well this is a long, fruity, brilliant frozen Daquiri.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Victor Bergeron's 1972 Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide, Revised.

name: Autumn Punch
ingredients: Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SLING GLASS.
Cut passion fruit in half and scoop out flesh into shaker.
ADD vodka, passion fruit syrup, pear and lemon juice.
SHAKE with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: Garnish with physalis (cape gooseberry) on rim.
review: Autumnal in colour with a wonderful meld of complementary flavours.
history: Created in 2001 by Max Warner at Baltic Bar, London, England.

name: Mandarintini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: This bittersweet palate cleanser looks like pink grapefruit juice.
history: Adapted from a cocktail discovered in 2005 at Aqua Spirit in Hong Kong.

name: Manhattan Island
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: A twist on the classic Harvard, or brandy-based Sweet Manhattan.
history: Manhattan cocktail history

name: Margarita (frozen)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
DRY BLEND (without ice) first 4 ingredients (all but Xanthan gum) on a slow setting and then gradually add Xanthan while continuing to blend.
Add 7oz scoop of crushed ice and BLEND some more.
POUR into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime slice on rim.
review: Citrus freshness with tequila's agave notes served frozen.
history: Margarita cocktail history

name: Maria Theresa Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Originally sweetened with sugar syrup, this is better smoothed with honey.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Victor J. Bergeron and published in his 1972 Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide Revised.

name: Marny Cocktail
instructions: STIR (or shake) all ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Originally shaken but perhaps better stirred. Simple but no less tasty for it.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Martini Special
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A 'Sweet' Martini [it's dry and aromatic] aromatised with orange flower water, absinthe and angostura bitters.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Mary Pickford Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: When made correctly, this pale-pink cocktail has a perfect balance between the fruit flavours and the spirit of the rum.
history: Created in the 1920s (during Prohibition) by Fred Kaufman at the Hotel Nacional de Cuba, for the silent movie star and wife of Douglas Fairbanks. Mary was in Cuba filming a movie with her husband and his best friend, Charlie Chaplin. This is recounted on page 40 of Basil Woon's 1928 book When It's Cocktail Time in Cuba, "The Mary Pickford, invented during a visit to Havana of the screen favourite by Fred Kaufman, is two-thirds pineapple-juice and one-third Bacardi, with a dash of grenadine. Both cocktails [The Presidenté is also mentioned] are sweetish and should be well shaken. The pineapple juice must be fresh-squeezed." Thus, it would appear that the dash of maraschino liqueur, now viewed as an essential ingredient, is a later addition to the Mary Pickford.

name: Aviation Cocktail (D's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist (& optional sugar rim).
review: Citrusy, floral gin with a lightly sour finish.
history: An adaptation of Hugo R. Ensslin's Aviation Cocktail from his 1916 Recipes for Mixed Drinks by yours truly.

name: Medium Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A classic Martini served 'perfect' (with dry and rosso vermouth) and very wet.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Menehune Juice
instructions: Select a DOUBLE OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: Slightly sweet and strong. According to Vic, "One sip and you may see a Menehune."
history: Adapted from a recipe in Victor Bergeron's 1947-72 Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide.

name: Mexican Martini (Añejo Margarita)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered olives.
review: Basically, a top-shelf Margarita garnished with jalapeño stuffed olives.

name: Mexican Mule
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COPPER MUG OR COLLINS GLASS.
POUR first 3 ingredients into mug/glass and briefly stir.
Two-thirds fill glass with cubed ice.
TOP with ginger beer and briefly stir.
Fill glass with a crown of crushed ice.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: As the name suggests, this is a tequila-based version of the Moscow Mule - simple as that. And very tasty.

name: Mexican Tea (Hot)
ingredients: Top up with Hot breakfast tea
instructions: Place bar spoon in warmed glass
POUR all ingredients into glass and stir.
review: Tiffin will never be the same again.

name: Mexico City
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A hint of flavoursome cranberry gives this tasty margarita a light pink hue.
history: Adapted from a cocktail discovered in 2002 at the Merc Bar in New York City, USA.

name: Mexicano (Hot)
ingredients: Top up with Hot filter coffee
instructions: POUR tequila and liqueur into warmed glass and top with coffee
FLOAT cream over drink.
review: A spicy, flavour-packed hot coffee. Lightly whip or simply shake cream in container before pouring over the bowl of a spoon. It also helps if the cream is gently warmed.

name: Livorno Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
DRY SHAKE all ingredients (without ice) to emulsify.
SHAKE again with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist expressed and discarded. Adorn with a Luxardo Maraschino Cherry dropped into cocktail..
review: Delicate anise and peppermint with citrus freshness. Old Tom gin fortifies and adds piney juniper while amplifying pleasing liquorice notes. A cleansing aperitivo.
history: Created in 2006 by yours truly and originally served on-the-rocks, but revisited and reformulated in 2023 as straight-up serve.

name: Million Dollar Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review:
It's fluffy and the recipe may look sweet, but this classic is dry and herbal with layers of fruit.

The recipe card depicted above was picked up at the Long Bar at Raffles Hotel by a fellow Discerning Drinker in 2001 (Many thanks, Guy.)
history:
Some say the Million Dollar Cocktail was created as early as 1894 by Louis Eppinger (the bartender credited for creating the Bamboo Cocktail) at The Grand Hotel, Yokohama, Japan. Others maintain it was created around 1910 by Ngiam Tong Boon at The Long Bar, Raffles Hotel, Singapore.  While, in his 1939 book The Gentleman's Companion volume II, Charles H. Baker, Jr. credits his first recipe for a Million Dollar Cocktail to Shideaki Saito, "Saito, No. 1 Bar Boy at The Imperial Hotel, Tokyo, Japan, 1926, 1931 & 1932", as his second recipe to "Nomura the No. 1 Bar Boy at Tor Hotel, Kobe, dating from 1926, to 1931 & 1932."

David Wondrich, the noted cocktail historian, discounts Singapore, Yokohama and Tokyo in favour of the Million Dollar Cocktail originating at the Shanghai Club in China. Founded as The Correspondent's Club in 1861, this was the principal men's club for British residents of Shanghai. Wondrich says, [courtesy of spiritsandcocktails.community] "The earliest and sanest recipe I've found for the drink dates back to 1922, when J.A. Wolfson, a Manila lawyer, included it in a little cocktails section he contributed to Elsie Gaches's Good Cooking and Health in the Tropics, published in Manila that year."

The Million Dollar Cocktail notably also features in Jean Lupoiu's 1928 book 370 Recettes de Cocktails, and Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book, while the 1933 Fourth Edition of O'Dell's Book of Cocktails and Fancy Drinks in English and Japanese has two recipes.

name: Million Dollar Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Garnish with a lime wedge.
review: Although a relatively dry Margarita, this particular millionaire benefits from the rich notes of a cognac-based orange liqueur.
history: Adapted from a cocktail discovered in 2006 at Maison 140 Hotel in Los Angeles, California, where I paid a mere $41.14 plus tip for one.

name: Milly Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE basil (just to bruise) in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim &/or float basil leaf boat.
review: Gin and pineapple with a pleasing hint of basil.
history: Created in 2003 by Shelim Islam at the GE Club in London at the request of Kate Moss, the famous model. This cocktail was subsequently served (as the "Tanqueray Belle") for her 30th birthday party which Shelim describes as "an unforgettable evening, to say the least!"

name: Milano
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: A vodka-based Negroni.

name: Mint Cocktail
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
(There's no need to muddle mint if a good shake and ice is used.)
review: A great grassy, minty digestif with a good balance between acidity and sweetness.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Mint Daiquiri
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE (just to bruise) mint in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
(Even with a fine strainer, if your finished cocktail doesn't end up with tiny fragments of mint on the surface, you've not shaken hard enough.)
review: Reminiscent of a short, concentrated Mojito, this Daiquiri works well with either a charcoal filtered 'carta blanca' style light rum or a lightly aged 'gold' rum. The former produces a brighter and fresher Daiquiri, while the latter arguably has more character. In a quest for the best of both worlds, I've used equal parts of both rums.
history: Created in 2006 by yours truly.

name: Mint Limeade
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE (just to bruise) mint in base of shaker
Add next three ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with lemonade, lightly stir and serve with straws.
review: Superbly refreshing, mint and lime served long.
history: Created in 2006 by yours truly (Simon D), London, England.

name: Missionary's Downfall
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE mint (just to bruise) with rum in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review:
Recipes for this venerable Tiki classic vary enormously and are often confused with the Aku Aku which this drink morphed into at the hands of its creator, Donn Beach. The original recipe calls for peach brandy (distilled from fermented peaches) rather than peach liqueur (based on neutral spirit), but due to its clarity and clean flavour, I prefer peach schnapps as a substitute.

Many recipes blend fresh mint and pineapple, and while blending the mint gives the drink a vibrant green colour, it does resemble stagnant pond water and looks like something you'd only drink for the promise of perceived health-giving properties rather than for pleasure. Hence, my version is shaken, but be sure to use generous amounts of mint to impart colour and flavour.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in the late 1930s by Donn Beach at his Don The Beachcomber restaurant in Hollywood, California, USA.

name: Mona Lisa
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring tonic water.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice wheel.
review: Chartreuse fans will appreciate this drink, which is also an approachable way for novices to acquire a taste for the green stuff. It's also a great brunch cocktail.

name: Morning Glory Fizz
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE first 6 ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.

STRAIN and slowly pour 2/3rds of the contents of the shaker from a higher than normal height into a chilled empty glass (no ice in glass) while simultaneously pouring soda with your other hand. The cocktail and soda should form a single stream of liquid falling into the glass.

As when pouring a Guinness, to achieve a perfect head on a fizz, the cocktail should be left to settle for at least a minute before topping off with what remains in the shaker. Ideally, during the settling period, the cocktail should be placed in a glass froster/fridge/freezer.
Slowly POUR the final contents of the shaker into the glass. The head should rise like a soufflé.
garnish: Garnish with lime slice wheel.
review: This classic, sour and aromatic cocktail is traditionally considered a morning after a pick-me-up / rejuvenative 'hair-of-the-dog'.
history:
This restorative fizz features in two 1884 books, George Winter's How to Mix Drinks and O.H Byron's  The Modern Bartenders' Guide

Byron says of this drink, "To be drank immediately, or the effect will be lost. It is a morning beverage, a tonic and a nerve quieter."

name: Mulata Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A classic Daiquiri with aged rum and a hint of chocolate.
history:
Thought to have been created by Constantino (Constante) Ribalaigua Vert at Havana's Floridita bar, but in the Cuban book, Bartender's Sixth Sense, the Mulata is said to have been created in the 1940s by one Jose Maria Vazquez.

This cocktail was originally made with Bacardi Elixir, a liqueur made by infusing plums in rum, but since the 1970s, crème de cacao liqueur has been used as a substitute.

Production of Bacardi Elixir ended when Bacardi was forced to leave Cuba, but a small batch of around 60 bottles was made to celebrate Bacardi Legacy Competition in April 2013.

See: Daiquiri cocktail history

name: Hotel Nacional Special
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple (or lime) wedge on rim.
review: A fruity but sophisticated riff on the classic Daiquiri with fresh pineapple and apricot liqueur. It would appear the Hotel Nacional was originally made with a charcoal filtered light white rum but in his influential 1939 travel log, Charles H. Baker says, "We indicate Gold Label Bacardi for the simple reason that Carta Blanca is so delicate in flavour it barely comes through any rich drink. " This cocktail does indeed suit a more characterful rum, to the extent that I like to add a splash of grassy agricole rum. If you have it, use it. And the minty pineapple notes of Daiquiri Bitters brilliantly suit this cocktail.
history:
The flagship cocktail of Havana's famous Hotel Nacional de Cuba was created sometime during or after the hotel's opening in 1930. The hotel's elegant opulence was a magnet to the style set and celebrities, including Ava Gardner and Nat King Cole, as well as mobsters such as "Lucky" Luciano. 

Such a hotel opening off the US coast during the height of Prohibition attracted some of America's greatest bartenders, including Will (or Wil) P. Taylor, Eddie Woelke and Fred Kaufman. All three of these have been credited with creating the Hotel Nacional Special, and perhaps they all contributed. However, Charles H. Baker's 1939 The Gentleman's Companion, credits Wil P. Taylor, describing the cocktail as "one of the three finest Bacardi drinks known to science."

The "National" is the first and very much the lead entry in the "Cuban Concoctions" amendment of Albert Stevens Crockett's 1935 Old Waldorf Astoria Book. This and the "other Havana recipes" that follow were contributed by "Will P. Taylor, manager of the Hotel National in Havana."

name: Naranja Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of ¼ orange wheel slice on rim.
review: An Orange Daiquiri with orange juice notes amplified by cognac orange liqueur.
history: Pronounced 'Na-Rahn-ha' (or thereabouts), Naranja is Spanish for 'orange', both the fruit and the colour.

name: Nautilus
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: Margarita-style and lengthened with cranberry juice, this is surprisingly tasty. The recipe above is pleasingly simple, but you may find the following better fits your glass: 50ml tequila, 50ml cranberry juice, 25 lime juice, and 12.5 sugar syrup.
history: Created by Victor Bergeron and featured in his 1972 Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide.

name: Honey Rum Grog
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR honey with other ingredients in base of shaker until the honey is dissolved.
SHAKE with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Not to be confused with the famous Tiki cocktail, Navy Grog, this Navy rum and honey sour is very enjoyable.
history: This Grog traces its origin back to the British Navy and Admiral Edward "Old Grog" Vernon's 1740 Order to Captains No.349 instructing that the daily ration of high-proof Navy rum was diluted with water and that where possible lime juice and sugar was made available.

name: Nevada Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A pungent Daiquiri with the intense flavour of Navy Rum.

name: Ace Of Clubs Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with cocoa powder.
review: A Daiquiri with a hint of chocolate.
history: Created in the 1930s at a Bermudian nightclub of the same name.

name: Nice Pear
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pear wedge on rim.
review: Despite the "no missus" Frankie Howerd-like, English seaside postcard humour name, this is a very tasteful cocktail – pear and cognac is a delicious flavour combination.
history: Created in 2002 by yours truly.

name: Noble Europe
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: A delicious cocktail that harnesses the rich, sweet flavours of Tokaji.
history: Created in 2002 by Dan Spink at Brown's, St Martin's Lane, London, England.

name: Noon
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
garnish: EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and float as garnish.
review: A gin-laced herbal, orange sour.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Stanley M. Jones' 1977 Jones' Complete Barguide.

name: No. 10 Lemonade
instructions: MUDDLE blueberries in base of shaker
Add next three ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda.
review: Basically a long blueberry Daiquiri.
history: Adapted from a drink discovered in 2006 at Double Seven, New York City, USA.

name: Oh Gosh!
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A subtley orange twist on a classic Daiquiri.
history: Created by Tony Conigliaro in 2001 at Isola, London, England. A customer requested a Daiquiri with a difference - when this was served he took one sip and exclaimed "Oh Gosh!".

name: Bacardi Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: This classic salmon-pinky cocktail perfectly combines and balances the light rum with the assertive sourness of lime juice and rich sweetness of pomegranate syrup.
history:
The Bacardi Cocktail originated in Cuba in 1917 and quickly grew in popularity with the growth of the cocktail culture in the USA after Prohibition, so much so that it became known simply as 'the Bacardi'.

There are two schools of thought over the original ingredients, some believe it was simply a Daiquiri but made using Bacardi rum and that the now ubiquitous grenadine version originated in New York sometime after Prohibition. Others hold that the grenadine was there at inception, after all the earliest known Daiquiri recipe was made with Bacardi rum so grenadine must be the distinguishing factor. However, the number of vintage cocktail books listing a Bacardi Cocktail without grenadine would seem to back up the inclusion of grenadine being a later addition.

Like the Daiquiri, The Bacardi Cocktail was sometimes served frozen and talking about the iced version, Jack Doyle, former barman at Sloppy Joe's in Key West, then Bacardi Imports Inc. barman in New York, explained, "The secret to the iced version was to shake the flaked iced until it looked like sherbet." This technique became known as frappé.

As the Bacardi Cocktail rapidly grew in popularity a small number of establishments neglected to use Bacardi rum as the base ingredient. In 1936, this led the Bacardi Company to take the Barbizon-Plaza Hotel and the Wivel Restaurant in New York's West Fifty-Fourth Street to court to ensure that when a customer asked for "Bacardi" by name they were given Bacardi Rum, so protecting the company's trademark.

Bacardi's case at New York's Supreme Court involved the premise that theirs was a unique rum and Bacardi family members travelled from Cuba to New York to appear as witnesses. Even Enrique Schueg, the third President of the Bacardi Company, took to the witness stand. When asked by Justice Walsh, "Well, how is this Bacardi Rum of yours made?" he replied, "Oh! That is my secret." Despite this secrecy, after deliberation, Justice John L. Walsh eventually affirmed that "Bacardi Rum is unique and uncopyable" and issued a ruling that a Bacardi Cocktail must legally be made with rum manufactured by the Compania Ron Bacardi.

name: Olympic
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: The perfect balance of cognac and orange.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Opal
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Fresh, fragrant flavours of zesty orange and gin with a subtle floral note.
history: Thought to have originated in the 1920s, the Opal notably appears in in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Bahama Mama
ingredients: 7.5 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill a TIKI MUG OR COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge, Luxardo Maraschino Cherry and mint sprigs bouquet.
review: The holy trinity of rum, coconut and pineapple served in a well-balanced, not overly sweet Tiki-style cocktail.
history:
The bartender most associated with this cocktail is Oswald "Slade" Greenslade, who was born in the Bahamas and worked at the Pink Elephant nightclub. This is where he claims to have created this cocktail in 1963 for three women at his bar who wanted something fruity they'd never had before.

Some question Slade's claims, saying the cocktail appeared on menus in the 1940s-50s, although I've not yet seen evidence of such a menu. However, in September 2024, Slade sent us his original recipe and the story of his Bahama Mama.

Oswald Greenslade's Bahama Mama recipe
1¼ oz Dark Bacardi 
1¼ oz Coconut Rum
1 oz Grendene
2 oz Pineapple juice
2 oz Orange juice
4 oz Cracked ice
Shake well and pour into a 12 oz cocktail glass.
Garnish with pineapple, orange slice and cherry.

name: Orange Brûlée
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR first 3 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
FLOAT thin layer of cream evenly over cocktail.
garnish: Dust with cocoa powder or freshly grated nutmeg.
review: A great-looking creamy topped after-dinner cocktail.
history: Created in 2005 by Xavier Laigle at Bar le Forum in Paris, France.

name: Orange Custard Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A smooth creamy orangey dessert cocktail that is surprisingly boozy and far from overly sweet. However, be warned, it is thick and rich - just as a dessert cocktail should be.
history: Created in 2002 by yours truly after rediscovering advocaat on a trip to Amsterdam. This cocktail was originally an "Orange Custard Martini" - every new cocktail was a Martini in those days. Firstly, for reasons of decency, I switched Martini for Cocktail in the name, then, In January 2019, I reduced the amount of advocaat a tad, swapped the now relatively obscure Tuaca liqueur for brandy, and added the vanilla bitters. I think for the better all around.

name: Orange Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Exactly as it says on the tin: a classic Daiquiri with a fruity splash of freshly squeezed orange juice.

name: Orange Daiquiri No.2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Créole shrubb is an unusual liqueur made by infusing orange peel in casks of mature Martinique rum.
history: Conceived in 1998 by yours truly after visiting the London Dockland-based company which was then importing Créole shrubb. I took a bottle to London's Met Bar where I asked Ben Reed to mix me my first Orange Daiquiri.

name: Orange Mojito
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE mint (just to bruise) in base of glass
Add other ingredients and half fill glass with crushed ice
CHURN (stir) with bar spoon
Fill with more crushed ice and churn some more
TOP with soda, stir and serve with straws.
review: Mint and orange combine to make a wonderfully fresh drink.
history:
Created in 2001 by Jamie MacDonald while working in Sydney, Australia.

For more recipes, its origins and history, see the Mojito Cocktail.

name: Bahamas Daiquiri
ingredients: 45 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Punchy rum with the established accompaniments of pineapple and lime, with a splash of coffee liqueur.
history: Adapted from the Bahamas Martini created in 2002 by Yannick Miseriaux at the Fifth Floor Bar, Harvey Nichols, London, England.

name: Pago Pago Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: A Pineapple Daiquiri with two liqueurs giving a herbal chocolate influence.
history: Adapted from Jeff Berry's Beachbum Berry Remixed, and, according to Jeff, originally from a 1940 book called The How and When by Hyman Gale and Gerald F. Marco (although it doesn't appear under this name in our 1945 Third Revised Edition of the book).

name: Painkiller
instructions: Select and pre-chill a TIKI MUG OR COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of dust with freshly grated nutmeg. Garnish with pineapple wedge.
review: The broadside firepower of navy rum balances sweet pineapple and coconut in what is an all too delicious Tiki-style libation.
history:
From the Soggy Dollar bar on the island of Jost Van Dyke in the British Virgin Islands. The bar's name is logical, as most of the clientele are sailors and there is no dock. Hence they have to swim ashore, often paying for drinks with wet dollars.

A variation on the classic Pina Colada. See our main Piña Colada page for the story behind the classic and for links to numerous other variations.

name: Paloma
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with grapefruit soda and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with grapefruit wedge.
review: Reminiscent of a long fruity Margarita with refreshing effervescence.
history: Paloma is Spanish for 'dove'. Its origins are unknown although it is reputed to date back to the 1950s. Some attribute its creation to the legendary Don Javier Delgado Corona, the former owner/bartender of La Capilla (The Chapel) in Tequila, Mexico, who created the Batanga.

name: Palermo
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge on rim.
review: This cocktail beautifully combines vanilla rum with tart wine and the richness of pineapple juice. Best made with a lightly aged (1-3 year old) light rum infused with a vanilla pod rather than a commercially produced vanilla-flavoured rum.
history: Adapted from a cocktail discovered in 2001 at Hotel du Vin, Bristol, England.

name: Park Avenue
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review:
Very fruity and well-balanced rather than dry or sweet.

The recipe previously on this page was in line with that in the 1977 Jones' Complete Barguide:
50ml (1 2/3oz) Dry gin
20ml (2/3oz) Pineapple juice
10ml (2/3oz) Orange Curaçao
10ml (1/3oz) Rosso (sweet) vermouth
But thanks to a comment by Clarence Castillo (see below), in February 2023 I adopted Ted Haigh's 2009 Park Avenue. Then, a month later, thanks to further encouragement from Clarence, I dropped the gin back a tad and added Daiquiri (roasted pineapple & mint bitters).
history: A degree of mystery surrounds this vintage cocktail, partly due to its first known appearance in a recipe book, Dexter Mason's 1930 The Art of Living, being quite a different recipe to that recognised as a Park Avenue today.

name: Park Lane
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Gin, apricot and orange combine in this easy-drinking frothy topped cocktail.

name: Passion Fruit Caipirinha
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
CUT lime in half (from pole to pole) and then slice one of the halves into eight 3-4mm thick half-moon shaped segments and drop into the base of a robust rocks glass.
Add 3 bar spoons caster sugar (or ½oz / 15ml sugar syrup 2:1) and MUDDLE.
Chop passion fruit in half and scoop flesh into the rocks glass and MUDDLE some more.
When sure fruit juice is fully extracted and the sugar dissolved, TRANSFER the muddled fruit and sugar into serving glass (preferably a 30cl / 10oz hi-ball glass).
POUR cachaça (stored in the freezer) into the rocks glass (used to muddle) and swirl to rinse glass before pouring cachaça into serving glass.
STIR to ensure all ingredients are well mixed.
FILL glass with small cubes or cracked ice. (If using large ice cubes, crack (rather than crush) four to five large ice cubes using an ICE TAPPER and drop into serving glass.)
Briefly STIR before topping with more ice.
garnish: Serve with a stirrer (but no straws).
review: A very tasty fruity Caipirinha.
history: For information on the history and other variations of the Caipirinha, please see our Caipirinha cocktail page.

name: Bajito
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
MUDDLE mint and basil in base of glass (just to bruise).
ADD rum, sugar and lime juice.
Half fill glass with crushed ice and CHURN (stir) with bar spoon.
Add more crushed ice and CHURN some more.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: Basically a Mojito with basil as well as mint.
history: Discovered in 2004 at Excelsior Bar, Boston, USA.

name: Passion Fruit Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SCOOP out the seeds and flesh from one of the passion fruit halves into your shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Float the remaining passion fruit half for garnish.
review: Subtle passion fruit flavour and tartness influence rather than dominate this Margarita, allowing the tequila and other ingredients to also shine. Fruity yet sophisticated.

name: Passion Fruit Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
Cut passion fruit in half and scoop out flesh into shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Float the remaining passion fruit half for garnish.
review: Simple but tasty, harnessing the flavour of passion fruit.

name: Pear and Cardamom Sidecar
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE cardamom in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pear wedge on rim.
review: A wonderful meld of aromatic ingredients. The combination of delicate cardamom spice and rich pear gives this cocktail an autumnal/winter feel.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2002 by Jason Scott at Oloroso in Edinburgh, Scotland.
See: Sidecar history and  variations

name: Pear Drop
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pear wedge on rim &/or serve with pear drop hard candy.
review: Like the eponymous candy, this after-dinner/late-night cocktail has a full-on tangy pear flavour.
history: Created in 2002 by yours truly.

name: Perfect Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Made with equal parts gin, rosso vermouth and dry vermouth the result is almost sherry-like and suits the aperitivo moment. However, if you want that spirituous Martini punch then gin-forward, as per above, is for you.
history: The Perfect Martini sits between a Dry Martini (with gin and dry vermouth) and a Sweet Martini (with gin and rosso vermouth) so is logically called a Medium Martini in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Periodista Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge skewered with maraschino cherry.
review: An orange and apricot Daiquiri. Like most Daiquiris, the Periodista benefits from a little additional dilution, hence the chilled water in my recipe.
history: Periodista (Spanish for 'journalist') originated in Havana, Cuba, where it is said to have been one of Ernest Hemingway's tipples at El Floridita. In keeping with the Cuban origin, the earliest known recipe appears in the 1948 El Arte de Cantinera, a bartenders' manual from the Club de Cantineros de la Republica de Cuba in Havana.

name: Pina Colada Cocktail (Puerto Rican style)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a PINEAPPLE SHELL (FROZEN) GLASS.
BLEND all ingredients with 7oz scoop of crushed ice.
garnish: Garnish with parasol speared pineapple wedge and maraschino cherry.
review: A wonderful creamy, fruity concoction that's not half as sticky as the world would have you believe. Too much ice will detract from the creaminess and kill the cocktail.
history: Piña Colada

name: Pineapple and Cardamom Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE cardamom in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge.
review: One of the tastiest Daiquiris we've tried.
history: Adapted from Henry Besant's excellent Pineapple & Cardamom Martini.

name: Pineapple and Cardamom 'Martini'
instructions: MUDDLE cardamom in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into a chilled glass.
review: Pineapple and cardamon are a spectacular pairing of fruit and spice in this vodka laced 'Martini'.
history:
Created in 2002 by Henry Besant at The Lonsdale, London, England.

Our Martini cocktail page details its evolution and other common variants.

name: Pineapple and Sage Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge.
review: Herbal tequila and sweet pineapple in harmony.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2005 at Green & Red Bar London, England.

name: Pineapple Daiquiri (on-the-rocks)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered pineapple wedge (or dehydrated slice) and maraschino cherry.
review: Rum and pineapple are just meant to go together. A simple but pleasingly fruity cocktail with ripe pineapple and sugar richness balanced by cleansing dry white rum and lime citrus acidity.

name: Pineapple Daiquiri (Frozen)
instructions: BLEND all ingredients with a scoop of crushed ice and serve with a straw.
review: A tad fluffy but very tasty.

name: Pineapple Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: As advertised, a Margarita with pineapple fruitiness. If you have it, a splash of pineapple syrup helps balance while amplifying the pineapple flavour.

name: Pineapple Mojito
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE mint (just to bruise) in glass.
Add other ingredients.
Two-thirds fill glass with crushed ice and CHURN with barspoon.
Fill glass with more crushed ice and briefly CHURN.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: A fruity, vanilla-ed twist on the classic Mojito.
history:
Discovered in 2003 at Apartment 195, London, England.

Mojito Cocktail hostory.

name: Pini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Use a great pisco and you'll have a wonderfully complex cocktail.
history: A cocktail which originated in London during the early 2000s.

name: Pink Gin
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review:
This is a cocktail that likes dilution, hence the shot of water in my recipe. And, a spoon full (or ½ spoon) of sugar certainly helps the medicine go down where the Pink Gin is concerned. However, the more sugar you add, the less dilution you'll need. Hit the perfect balance (hopefully my recipe) and this is a great cocktail, but it's an equilibrium that's down to personal taste so tricky to attain.

I've opted for more characterful old tom gin, but London dry gin also works well, especially if you double the sugar to 5ml (1 bar spoon).
history:
The Pink Gin cocktail is so named due to aromatic bitters giving the main ingredient, gin, a pink tinge. 

Originally, gin at room temperature was simply poured into a glass that had been primed with a few dashes of bitters and swirled to coat the inside. No ice was used. Ice simply wasn't available.

The creation of this cocktail is said to have started with the discovery of Angostura Bitters by Henry Workshop, an English ship's surgeon serving on H.M.S. Hercules. In 1826, his ship was patrolling the Caribbean on watch for slave trader vessels when he came across the bitters which had been created two years earlier by Johann Gottlieb Benjamin Siegert (1796-1870), a German doctor who had settled in Venezuela where he was Surgeon-General of a military hospital in the town of Angostura. Siegert had created the now famous bitters to treat stomach disorders and indigestion of his fellow settlers, not to make cocktails.

Now the city of Ciudad Bolivar (renamed in 1846 in honour of Simon Bolivar), the town of Angostura sits on the mighty Orinoco River, connecting it with the Caribbean basin and it is via this river, and its trade, that Siegert's bitters found their way around the islands of the Caribbean with sailors being likely customers for any potion believed to treat upset stomachs and sea sickness. 

The British Admiralty already prescribed the use of chinchona bark (quinia) to ward against tropical diseases and, as a medical man, Workshop was interested in Angostura as he had heard the preparation included chinchona and many other herbal remedies. He procured a couple of bottles of the bitters while on shore leave in George Town, a port in the Cayman Islands. Back onboard ship, he and the ship's captain, Jack Bristow, mixed a small amount of the medicinal bitters with their gin ration and found the pink drink produced agreeable. They served their new creation to the other officers on the ship. Word spread and 'Pink Gin' as it became known, became popular with officers of the Royal Navy. By the mid-19th century, the drink became widely served throughout England.

name: Pink Gin and Tonic
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR gin and bitters into ice-filled glass.
TOP with tonic water and briefly stir.
garnish: Lime slice wheel.
review: Basically a G&T with an extra pop of flavour, this has a broader appeal than the original Pink Gin.
history:
When I sampled my first Pink Gin, made with generous dashes of bitters I found this classic tough to appreciate, so in 2002, I added another medicinal ingredient - tonic water, also containing quinine from cinchona bark. Or, looking at this another way, I simply added dashes of bitters to a G&T.

I'm sure I wasn't the first to add Angostura Bitters to a G&T but I've not found an earlier reference to a "Pink Gin & Tonic". Others soon cottoned on and the gin graze, soda boom, and ready-to-drink market have combined to produce a plethora of pre-mixed Pink G&Ts as well as tonic waters specifically designed for such a serve. Hence, in 2021, I swapped out classic 'Indian' tonic water in this recipe for an aromatic tonic while also reducing the bitters dose accordingly.

name: Pink Grapefruit Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Delivers exactly what the name promises.

name: Pink Lady
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: A generous measure of gin and high-proof applejack provide spirituous spice which, along with lemon juice sourness, ensures that this cocktail is neither sweet nor weak. Indeed, this is a very tasty sour. So tasty, that to spare this Lady's blushes, our friend Ted "Dr Cocktail" Haigh would prefer you refer to it as "The Secret Cocktail".
history: The first known reference to the Pink Lady is in Jacques Straub's 1913 Manual of Mixed Drinks.

name: Pink Palace
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review:
This drink's sweet-sour balance and degree of pink hue is relative to how generous you are with the grenadine.

(When made with homemade fresh pomegranate syrup in place of grenadine, and you hit the perfect balance, this drink is delicious but sadly lacks the eponymous pink hue with the result being more golden-amber.)
history: The signature drink at the Polo Lounge, Beverly Hills Hotel, Los Angeles, USA. The hotel, which is lovingly termed the Pink Palace inspired The Eagles' Hotel California and graces the album cover.

name: Funky Pirate Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Spirituous enough for a pirate. Why the name? Well, one of the rums has some 'funk', both rums have nautical connections, the lime protects against scurvy, the cinnamon schnapps liqueur has pieces of gold and grenadine is blood red. Well, seemed appropriate to me!
history: Created in 2004 by yours truly.

name: Pisco Punch (Micheli's recipe)
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: MUDDLE orange and pineapple in base of shaker
ADD pisco and pineapple marinade
SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with no more than two shots of soda water.
review: This Pisco Punch is very fruity and slightly on the sweet side but is so tasty that it justifies its legendary reputation.
history:
Alfredo Micheli (who went by the nickname Mike) was employed at the Bank Exchange and spied on Duncan Nichol to learn how to make this legendary drink. After he believed he'd learnt the secret he left to start serving at a newly opened competitor to the Bank Exchange, Paoli's on Montgomery Street.

Our Pisco Punch cocktail page gives a more detailed history of this drink and explains the different recipes.

name: Pisco Sour (blended)
instructions: BLEND lime and sugar to dissolve sugar
Add pisco, egg white and 6oz scoop crushed ice and BLEND again
Serve with paper straws.
review: This blended version of a Pisco Sour is the authentic Peruvian and indeed Chilean serve.
history: See: Pisco Sour history.

name: Planter's Punch
ingredients: 45 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish along with mint sprigs bouquet.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange slice wheel and mint sprigs.
review: A tangy punch that harnesses the strength and rich flavours of the rum.
history:
Invented in the late 19th century by the founder of Myers's rum, Fred L. Myers. The recipe on the back of each bottle of his rum is known as the "Old Plantation formula" and uses the classic rum punch proportions: 1 sour (lime), 2 sweet (sugar), 3 strong (rum), and 4 weak (water).

Rather than this, or the American formula:  1 sour, 2 sweet, 3 weak, and 4 strong, I've followed David A. Embury's recommendation (in his 1948 The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks) of 1 sweet, 2 sour, 3 strong and 4 weak.

name: Playmate Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist and 2 drops of bitter on foamy head (spread with a cocktail stick) .
review: Cognac-laced apricot and orange. Mellow and easy drinking.

name: Pomegranate Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Earthy fruity tart pomegranate and delicately vanilla oaked tequila combine harmoniously in this Margarita.
history: Created in 2006 by yours truly.

name: Pompanski Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Zesty with the sharp freshness of grapefruit and a hint of orange rounded with dry vermouth.

name: Pooh'tini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR honey with vodka in base of shaker to dissolve honey.
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Grassy honey with spicy, slightly tannic, camomile finish.
history: Adapted from a drink discovered in 1999 at Lot 61, New York City.

name: Banana Bliss
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with dried banana chip.
review: Banana liqueur and brandy go shockingly well together, here toned with sherry and bitters.
history: Adapted from a recipe in W. J. Tarling's 1937 Café Royal Cocktail Book which is credits E. Angerosa with this cocktail's creation.

name: Pussyfoot
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: One of the better tasting non-alcoholic cocktails.
history: Created in 1920 by Robert Vermeire at the Embassy Club, London, England. This non-alcoholic is named after 'Pussyfoot' (William E.) Johnson, who was an ardent supporter of Prohibition.

name: Banana so Smoothie
instructions: BLEND ingredients with 6oz scoop of crushed ice
Pour into glass and serve immediately with straws.
review: Serve with breakfast cereal and you'll be set up for the day. The high fresh banana content means this drink will quickly turn brown if left. This can be countered by adding fresh lemon juice and balancing with more honey but this detracts from the fresh banana flavour.
history: Created in 2005 by Lisa Ball, London, England.

name: Razzzzzzberry Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of raspberries .
review: Raspberry and vanilla with characteristic dry cranberry fruit.

name: Red Lion (D's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: The sophisticated drinker's answer to a gin and juice with dry gin and orange liqueur made all the more fruity with orange juice, lemon juice and pomegranate syrup.
history:
Adapted from Arthur Tarling's 1933 competition-winning Red Lion recipe by yours truly in August 2016.
See: Red Lion cocktail history

name: Red Marauder
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered raspberries.
review: Brandy-laced fruity raspberry and but not the sweetie you might expect - indeed, it's surprisingly dry thanks to a splash of lime juice.
history: Originally created for Martell while sponsors of England's Grand National Hunt horse race, this is named after the horse that won in 2001.

name: Red Rum 'Martini'
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE redcurrants in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with redcurrants draped over rim.
review: A beautifully fruity, adult balance of bittersweet flavours.
history:
Created by Jason Scott in 2002 at Oloroso in Edinburgh, Scotland.

This cocktail, which is red and contains rum, is named after Red Rum, the only horse in history to win England's Grand National three times (on his other two attempts, he came second). He became a British hero, appeared on the BBC Sports Personality of the Year show and paraded right up to his death at age 30 in 1995.

name: Reggae Rum Punch
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge skewered with maraschino cherry and mint sprigs bouquet.
review: Jamaicans have a sweet tooth and love their rum. This drink combines sweetness, potent rum, and generous fruit.
history: The most popular punch in Jamaica, where it is sold under different names with slightly varying ingredients. It always contains orange, pineapple and most importantly, overproof rum.

name: Ritz Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail, then use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of coin-sized orange zest twist (flamed).
review: Citrus freshness with a brandy backbone lightened and dried with a splash of brut fizz.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Dale DeGroff in New York City and first published in his 2002 book The Craft Of The Cocktail, where he says, "This is my tribute to the Ritz cocktails of Paris and Madrid."

name: Barbara West
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Your choice of amontillado sherry may dramatically affect the balance of this gin and sherry sour. Choose well and you're in for a treat.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Stanley M. Jones' 1977 Jones' Complete Barguide. This classic could date from the 1930s.

name: Rocky Mountain Rootbeer
instructions: POUR all ingredients into glass and them fill glass with ice.
review: Does indeed taste reminiscent of alcoholic root beer.

name: Rosarita Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of salt rim (optional) and lime wedge on rim.
review: This peachy coloured Margarita is well-balanced and flavoursome with zesty orange liqueur sitting well with cranberry.
history: Created in 1999 by Robert Plotkin and Raymon Flores of BarMedia, USA.

name: Rose (by Johnny Milta)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: Salmon-pink and wonderfully aromatic with dry vermouth and kirsch with a blast pf fruity redcurrant syrup.
history:
Harry McElhone's 1922 Harry's ABC of mixing cocktails credits this cocktail to "Johny, Chatham Hotel Bar, Paris, 1919" and is referring to a bartender called Johnny Milta. 

This recipe is adapted from David A. Embury's 1948 The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks.

name: Rose No.3
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of redcurrants, edible rose petal or Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: Delicate, aromatic berry and orchard fruit - not too sweet.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Roselyn Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: A 2:1 Wet Martini tamed by a dash of pomegranate/grenadine syrup.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Rosita
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: Bittersweet, tequila-based, and Negroni-like. Classically garnished with a lemon zest twist but better with grapefruit.
history:
The origin of this drink remains unknown, but its popularity (like many things in the drinks industry) can be attributed to Gary 'gaz' Regan, even though for some years he was in denial.

The Rosita was introduced to gaz by a fellow cocktail geek back in 2005; who in turn had discovered the recipe in an article written by Terry Sullivan, a fellow drinks writer. Gaz questioned Sullivan as to where he found the recipe, but he could not remember so gaz let the matter drop until 2007 when he made the cocktail as an aperitif for a few friends round for dinner. Gaz's guests asked about the origins of the drink and this led gaz to contact Sullivan again.

This time Sullivan remembered and told gaz that he had found it in The Bartender's Bible. Now many of you will already be smiling as The Bible, published in 1991, was gaz Regan's first book.

Gaz imbibed and wrote about thousands of cocktails over the years, so it is understandable that he overlooked his own book. After an embarrassed oh-yeah moment, he started rummaging around in the back of the closet of his guest room, where an oversized envelope still held his working notes for the book. So it transpired that gaz purloined the recipe from a 1988 edition of Mr. Boston Official Bartender's Guide, but he had added a typical gaz touch, namely a dash of bitters, and predictably a bit more tequila.

The recipe has since been traced back to at 1974 edition of the Mr. Boston Official Bartender's Guide.

name: Royal Smile
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: This balanced sweet and sour cocktail could put a smile on anyone's face. Unless that is, "one is not amused!" Calvados notes shine through.
history: Adapted from a recipe in David A. Embury's 1948 seminal book, The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks.

name: Rude Cosmopolitan
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Don't let the pink appearance of this Cosmopolitan (made with tequila in place of vodka) fool you into thinking it's a fluffy cocktail. It's both serious and superb.
history: Cosmopolitan cocktail history and development

name: Rum Punch-Up
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Exactly what the name promises - a Rum Punch served straight-up, Daiquiri style.
history: Adapted from a cocktail discovered in 2006 at Albannach in London, England.

name: Rum Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill a DOUBLE OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Smooth and sour - well balanced.

name: Russian Spring Punch
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SLING GLASS.
SHAKE first 6 ingredients with ice.
POUR sparkling wine into shaker.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice wheel and berries.
review: Well-balanced, complex and refreshing.
history:
Created in the 1980s by Dick Bradsell, London, England. In the Dec-Jan 1998 edition of CLASS magazine (page 6), Dick wrote about his drink, "Many springs ago, I conceived the Russian Spring Punch which is basically a spiked Kir Royal over ice. This conforms to the punch formula of 1 sweet, 2 sour, 3 strong, 4 weak except the most of the 'weak' bit (normally soda or juice) is replaced with champagne, turning the recipe into a sledgehammer of a cocktail. To make, fill a Collins glass with ice. Add 50ml best vodka, 25ml lemon juice, two teaspoons of cassis and two teaspoons of sugar syrup. Top with champagne and stir. Garnish with a lemon slice and some berries."

In August 2015, Dick told us the full story: "I created this drink for friends of ours, Rebecca du Pont de Bie and Peter Kent, in the 80s. I think I was still working at Zanzibar at the time, but it might very well have been Fred's: it was before my daughter Beatrix was born in 1988.

Peter was a gay guy, who designed shops and married Hamish Bowles, who is editor-at-large for Vogue. They shared an extremely nice flat in the Knightsbridge end of Kensington, and they wanted to have a cocktail party but they didn't have enough money.

So I suggested that, as they didn't have quite enough cash for prestigious amounts of drink, they requested that everyone brought a bottle of bubbly. So if you brought rubbish, you got the drinks rubbish, and if you brought quality, you drank quality.

I stocked the bar with glasses, and in each glass was a shot of vodka, half a shot of lemon juice, two barspoons of sugar, one barspoon of cassis and ice. People got to drink their whole bottle of champagne and however many shots of vodka they felt they could manage.

It was lethal, that thing. We had people falling down the stairs and dancing around in wild abandon. I called it the Russian Spring Punch because it has vodka in it, which is Russian, and it's a Collins, or a Fizz, which is a spring drink."

name: Saketini No. 2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with cucumber slice floated on surface.
review: Dry gin and sake with a cognac orange liqueur, adding luscious richness to this Dry Martini riff.
history: Recipe by yours truly.

name: Saketini No. 3
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Salmon-coloured, light and fragrant with plum wine and sake to the fore.
history: Adapted from a recipe discovered in 2007 at Nobu Berkeley in London, England.

name: Sakini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of green apple peel.
review: Sake adds an almost wine-like delicacy to this approachable riff on the classic Dry Martini.
history: The "Sake Martini" emerged in the 1980s. This recipe by yours truly in November 2016.

name: Sangaree (Sangria)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a GOBLET GLASS.
STIR first 6 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda.
garnish: Garnish with strawberry slices, orange and lemon wheels and mint sprigs bouquet.
review: Summer in a glass. Heavy wine tannins are sweetened, lightened and infused with fruit.
history: This version of Spanish Sangria was popular in 19th-century America. The only real difference is that while Sangria is usually made in batches, Sangria is single-serve.

name: Sangrita
instructions: SHAKE ingredients with ice and strain into shot glass
Serve with a shot of tequila
The drinker can either down the tequila and chase it with sangrita or sip the two drinks alternately.
review: In Mexico the quality of the homemade Sangrita can make or break a bar. This recipe is spicy and slightly sweet and perfect for chasing tequila.
history: The name means 'little blood' in Spanish and the drink is served with tequila in every bar in Mexico.

name: Saturn 'Martini'
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE grapes in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and STIR to dissolve honey.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered grapes.
review: Delicate, beautifully balanced and subtly flavoured.
history: Created in 2001 by Tony Conigliaro at Isola, Knightsbridge, London, England.

name: Seventh Heaven No.2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of mint sprigs bouquet pegged to rim.
review: Grapefruit citrus bitterness balances rich floral maraschino liqueur in this delicately balanced gin-based cocktail.
history:
Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book:
"SEVENTH HEAVEN COCKTAIL. (No.2)
1 Tablespoonful Grapefruit Juice.
¼ Maraschino
¾ Dry Gin.
Stir well and place a sprig of green Mint on top."

name: Sicilian Negroni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Blood orange juice replaces sweet vermouth in this fruity Negroni.
history:
Discovered in 2006 at The Last Supper Club, San Francisco, USA.
Negroni cocktail history

name: Sidecar (D's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist (a lemon zest twist is more classic). There have been periods when it has been fashionable to coat the rim of the glass in which this cocktail is served with sugar. The earliest written reference to this is in 1934. Thankfully, sugar rims are now out of vogue, and, as Embury writes in his book, 'A twist of lemon may be used if desired and the peel dropped into the glass. Otherwise no decoration.'.
review: For added complexity, I've replaced the spoon of sugar syrup most find necessary to balance a Sidecar with a splash Pineau des Charentes blanc.
history: Created by yours truly and much revisited and amended between July 2021 and December 2025.

name: Silver Bullet
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: If you like Aviations then this caraway-influenced sour may appeal. It's powerfully flavoured, so also consider serving over ice to allow dilution to tame the flavours.
history: Thought to have been created in the 1920s.

name: Sloe Gin Fizz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass. (No ice in the glass.)
TOP with sparkling wine (or soda if you're cheap). Some like to add egg white to enhance foam. (The drink photographed here is without egg white and we prefer without.)
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice on rim.
review: With the right brand of sloe gin and champagne, this citrusy berry cocktail is just on the dry/tart side of balanced (add more sugar or choose your sloe gin according to taste). Obviously, when made with soda rather than sparkling wine this is a quite different but still tasty cocktail. There is no need to add egg white, and its addition detracts from this cocktail.
history: "Its haunting pink color and fruity opulence are topped with a handsome collar of natural foam which, if the sloe gin be of first quality, is gladsome aplenty." We have David Wondrich to thank for uncovering this quote about the Sloe Gin Fizz by Lawton Mackall in the August 1941 issue of Esquire.

name: Slow Screw
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Reminiscent of a fruity screwdriver with a botanical gin character. When made with a good slow gin and freshly squeezed orange juice this is a great brunch cocktail.
history: Origins and evolution of the "Slow Screw" family of cocktails.

name: Snow White Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Sip through a thick white foamy head to enjoy the layers of rum-laced, innocently fruity delights below.
history: Adapted from a recipe in David A. Embury's 1948 book The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks.

name: Snyder Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A 2:1 Dry Martini but with a splash of orange curaçao mellowing the dryness and adding more subtle complexity than orange bitters.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Sodden Grape
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE grapes in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with grapes.
review: A 'sod' is a piece of turf. Here 'sodden' refers to the Bison grass, the flavour of which combines well with the grapes and icewine.
history: Created in 2004 by yours truly, London, England.

name: Appletini (Sour Apple Martini)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry &/or apple wedge or slice.
review: This "Martini" is unashamedly fun and brash with lurid green apple liqueur at its 'core'.
history:
Unlike the origins of so many cocktails, the date when this altern'atini was created and by whom is remembered by those involved and has been widely reported. It was the Fourth of July 1996 when Loren "Lola" Dunsworth, owner of Lola's in West Hollywood was encouraged to include a Ketel One signature Martini on her restaurant's cocktail list by a representative from Ketel One Vodka. Boasting one of L.A.'s first extensive Martini menus, Lola's, which sat at 945 N. Fairfax Avenue in West Hollywood, opened in July 1996 and closed 8th August 2013.

Seeing a bottle of DeKuyper Pucker Sour Apple Liqueur on the back bar, Lola asked bartender Adam Karston to make a cocktail with this and Ketel One. He mixed equal parts Ketel One and Pucker liqueur with a splash of house-made sweet-and-sour mix. The result, "Adam's Apple Martini" was an instant hit and under its simplified name, the "Appletini", quickly became hugely popular across North America in the Noughties earning it contemporary classic status and a place in our Cocktail Hall of Fame.

At Lola's, they garnished their Appletinis with slices of Granny Smith apple marinated in iced lemon juice mixed with water to keep fresh. In other bars, the attractive colour contrast and ease of simply dropping a maraschino cherry into the drink led to this becoming the more usual garnish. In recent years, floating a dehydrated apple slice has become popular.

name: Adonis
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
THROW (or STIR) all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with skewered olive.
review:
Some describe the Adonis as "The sweet version of the Bamboo" but that hardly does this brilliant vintage aperitivo/digestivo, that's off-dry and aromatic justice.

I made the Adonis for Dan to photograph in a vintage Boadas Martini glass as the Boadas Bar's rendition of this classic is the best I've encountered. And thanks to their generosity, I have one of their last 20 original glasses and the knowledge that it's a 'secret' splash of Italian red bitter liqueur that deepens the flavour profile of their Adonis rather than the orange bitters favoured by others.

I really wanted to find a Spanish vermouth that worked best in Adonis or indeed a very expressive Torino sweet vermouth but I found these too domineering and/or overly sweet so I settled on a more standard rosso vermouth. I've also experimented with various styles of sherry and now lean towards a blend of palo cortado and amontillado.
history:
The first known mention of the Adonis in print is in the "Pearls from Billy, the bartender" column in New York's The Sun newspaper on 27th March 1887. The column describes three customers, "three young bloods, with bell-crowned beavers of matchless sheen and silver-headed canes of ultra fashionable immensity who walk into the bar where Billy was bartending and order "Three Adonis cocktails, please." Billy proceeds the confidently make and serve them three cocktails which they drink, pay "fifteen cents each" for and leave. Then it becomes clear that Billy has no idea what an Adonis is. ""What on earth is an Adonis cocktail?" asked the reporter who had watched the incident. "Blessed if I know," said Billy, with an air of perfect frankness; "but I wouldn't be much of a bartender if I let any customer bluff me off with any new-fangled name. I gave them what I always have known as a turf club cocktail, and it suited. The first principle of good bartending is to know how to make everything on earth in the drink line, no matter whether you have ever heard of it before or not.""

This irreverent column doesn't reveal what comprised an Adonis but it does tell us that it was a "new-fangled" cocktail at the time so dating its origin prior to 1897.

It's another 16 years before the Adonis appears in a recipe book, Jacques Straub's 1913 Straub's Manual of Mixed Drinks establishing the recipe as two-thirds Italian (sweet) vermouth to one-third sherry with 2 dashes orange bitters.

name: 75 Cocktail (MacElhone's 1926 recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist (discarded) and star anise.
review: Like the gun this cocktail is named after, this small cocktail packs a hell of a punch, and to ensure it does, MacElhone calls for a couple of dashes of absinthe in place of the lemon juice used in earlier Soixante-Quinze (Seventy-Five) recipes. The result is a dry, spirituous drink.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry MacElhone's's 1926 Harry ABC of Mixing Cocktails.

name: Sgroppino / Sorbetto
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
DRY BLEND (without ice) all ingredients on slow setting.
POUR into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with lemon zest grated over cocktail or garnish with lemon slice on rim.
review: Smooth and all too easy to quaff. A great dessert.
history: Pronounced 'scroe-pee-noe', this hybrid cocktail and dessert is often served after meals in Venice. The name comes from a vernacular word meaning 'untie', a reference to the belief that it relaxes your stomach after a hearty meal.

name: Sidekick
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Rich ripe pear (actually, there's no pear in this cocktail) and orange with a stabilising and cleansing hint of sour lime.
history: Adapted from a drink discovered in 2003 at Temple Bar in New York City, USA.

name: Silver Gin Fizz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FIZZ OR HIGHBALL (8OZ TO 10OZ) GLASS.
SHAKE first 5 ingredients (all but soda water) with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass and place in a freezer to allow the foam head to form a layer.
After a minute or so, slowly POUR soda from a higher-than-normal height to raise the foaming head above the rim.
garnish: Garnish with quarter lemon wheel on rim.
review:
For those who prefer their Gin Fizz mellowed and fluffed by the addition of egg white.

It's possible to make a fizz with a visually appealing head raised far above the glass rim, but the extra soda poured to achieve this thick head creates an overly diluted cocktail that's only accessible after gulping through all that foam.
history: A mid-19th century classic.

name: Slow Comfortable Screw Against The Wall
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
STIR first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
DRIZZLE sloe gin around surface so it bleeds into cocktail.
FLOAT Galliano.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Galliano adds the wall (as in Harvey Wallbanger) and some herbal peppermint to this Slow Comfortable Screw. Yes, the name's corny but made with fresh orange, this drink is a good brunch or summertime option.
history: Origins and evolution of the "Slow Screw" family of cocktails.

name: Snowfall Cocktail
instructions: Take 1/4 a vanilla pod and cut along its length
MUDDLE this in the base of a shaker
