Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: An alcoholic version of a vanilla milkshake with tiny black specs from the vanilla.
history: Originally called a Snowfall Martini and discovered in 2002 at Lot 61, New York City, USA.

name: Soyer Au Champagne
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
PLACE scoop of ice cream in base of chilled glass.
ADD next 3 ingredients and STIR to dissolve and incorporate most of the ice cream.
TOP with sparkling wine (which should foam).
garnish: Garnish with orange slice/wedge on rim skewered with maraschino cherry.
review: Ice cream is integrated, rather than dominating this classic dessert-style cocktail. It's a treat, but it's not that sweet.
history:
Named after and likely created by London's first celebrity chef Alexis Benoit Soyer (1810–1858). During the Victorian era, this flamboyant French chef wowed royalty and aristocracy, moving from their kitchens to take up residency at London's Reform Club. Soyer left the Reform (where his signature Lamb Cutlets Reform dish is still served) to establish his Gastronomic Symposium at the Great Exhibition, including an American bar which Dr. Anistatia Miller and Jared Brown say offered "40 different concoctions including his own Soyer au Champagne..."

Soyer published several influential books, including his 1846 The Gastronomic Regenerator, which includes a number of recipes using champagne but sadly not a Soyer au Champagne. However, a "Sôyer au Champagne" cocktail does appear in the earliest French cocktail book, Emile Lefeuvre's 1889 Méthode pour composer soi-même Les Boissons Américaines, but this bears little resemblance to the cocktail we recognise today.

name: Sparkling Perry
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: Garnish with pear wedge on rim.
review: Reminiscent of perry (pear cider).
history: Created in December 2002 by yours truly.

name: Stairs Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pear wedge on rim.
review: In London's cockney rhyming slang 'apples and pears' means stairs. Thus, this tasty cocktail is appropriately named.
history: Created in 2000 by Ian Baldwin at the GE Club, London, England

name: Stanley Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Salmon pink and reminiscent of a Daiquiri with a splash of gin and grenadine.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Stinger
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: Classically the Stinger is shaken and served straight-up in a chilled coupe. However, I think it makes for a more refreshing peppermint and brandy digestif when served over crushed ice.
history:
In the 1956 American musical comedy film High Society, Bing Crosby explains to Grace Kelly how the Stinger gained its name. "It's a Stinger. It removes the sting."

The Stinger appears in Jacques Straub's 1913 Straub's Manual of Mixed Drinks but owes its popularity during the 1920's to Reginald "Reggie" Vanderbilt, an American millionaire and father of fashion designer Gloria Vanderbilt.

In his 2015 Updated & Revised Imbibe David Wondrich recounts a 1923 newspaper gossip page story crediting the invention of The Stinger to Reggie, a keen cocktail maker who "was observed in all its pomp and glory in the bar of [his] home, and he himself was the high priest, the host, the mixer." The bar in his Fifth Avenue mansion was apparently modelled after the one in the William the Conqueror tavern in Normandy. The article claims "the 'Stinger' was his own invention, a short drink with a long reach, a subtle blending of ardent nectars, a boon to friendship, a dispeller of care."

Reggie's enthusiasm for cocktails, particularly the Stinger helped contribute to his death from liver failure on 4th September 1925.

Similar cocktails, under various names, date back to 1890 when Reggie was only ten. That said, the name Stinger, after the boxing term for a jab to the head, only emerged in 1913 and although a bartender named James B. Regan at New York's Knickerbocker Hotel is implicated, I like to believe that Reggie didn't just adopt the Stinger as his own but also christened it.

name: Strawberry Daiquiri (frozen)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
DRY BLEND (no ice) first 4 ingredients to breakdown strawberries.
Reduce speed of blender and ADD Xanthum gum.
Add two-thirds full 7oz scoop of crushed ice and BLEND on slow-medium speed.
POUR into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with strawberry on rim.
review: Alcoholic Strawberry Mivvi for grown-ups. What's not to like?
history: Unknown but the earliest published recipe is said to be in home economist, Mabel Stegner's 1952 book Electric Blender Recipes.

name: Sumo In A Sidecar
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Hints of sake but retains the Sidecar style.
history: See: Sidecar history.

name: Swizzles Cocktail
instructions: POUR all ingredients into chilled glass and two-thirds fill with crushed ice
SWIZZLE with a swizzle stick (or churn with a barspoon)
Add more crushed ice to fill and SWIZZLE some more
Serve with straws.
review: Somewhat reminiscent of a frozen gimlet – the combination of gin and lime shines in this freshening swizzle.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Tequila Smash
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: Simple, not too sweet - a great way to appreciate quality tequila.
history: Adapted from the classic Brandy Smash.

name: Tequila Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of coin-sized lime zest twist.
review: A Margarita without orange liqueur (e.g. a Tommy's Margarita) is not a "margarita" at all but a tequila sour. The triple sec orange liqueur is one of the things that define the margarita (which sits within the Daisy cocktail family). The Tequila Sour may lack the ring of a Margarita, but it is a very tasty cocktail nonetheless.

name: Tequila Sunrise
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR grenadine and cassis into ice-filled glass.
SHAKE other ingredients with ice.
STRAIN (pour slowly) into glass to sit over red base layer (to create a sunrise effect).
garnish: Garnish with lime wheel.
review: This 1970s zesty citrus version of the Tequila Sunrise looks the part and comes with a sweet treat at the bottom of the glass.
history:
A variation on the original 1930s Tequila Sunrise, created in 1970 by Bobby "Robert" Lozoff, when a 22 year-old rookie bartender working at the Trident in Sausalito, California, USA.

The Trident was popular with rock 'n' roll stars and one Monday night in June 1972, when the Trident was normally closed, it was the venue for an intimate Rolling Stones American Tour kick-off party. While Mick Jagger was at the bar waiting for Lozoff to make the Margarita he'd just ordered, Lozoff asked if he had ever tried a Tequila Sunrise. Jagger replied, "no." So, Lozoff made one of his signature drinks. Jagger loved the taste and that it was simple enough for the band to make while on tour. In chapter nine of Keith Richard's 2010 book Life, he says "The '72 tour was known by other names-the Cocaine and Tequila Sunrise tour." The drink's notoriety led its being printed on the back label of Jose Cuervo bottles. Then, in April 1973, the Eagles released their song Tequila Sunrise.

name: Ti Punch
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR the rum and sugar into glass.
Then SQUEEZE the lime disc between finger and thumb before dropping into the cocktail. (This expresses the oil from the skin and little of the juice into the Ti'Punch.)
STIR and consider adding ice - either one or two large cubes depending on glass size, or preferably, a small chunk of block ice.
garnish: There's no additional garnish to the lime disc using in the making of this cocktail but some like to serve with a small spoon to enable the drinker to tease out more juice from the lime disc &/or stir ice.
review: As the name suggests, Martinique's national cocktail is deliciously punchy – traditionally made with 50% alc./vol. rhum agricole. And, even if you choose to add ice, then dilution is negligible. However, the all-important sugar cane juice (not sugar syrup made from processed sugar) adds flavour and mellows the rum, so yes, it's punchy but it's also very pleasing to drink.
history:
Named "Ti", Creole for the French word 'petit', this is literally a small rum punch, and unlike most rum punches, it is not lengthened with water or juice – and often without ice, despite the warm climate from where it originates.

It is the national cocktail of the French islands of Martinique and Guadeloupe, where "Ti" is pronounced "Tee". It is also popular in Réunion, Maurice, Haiti, French Guiana and other French-speaking Caribbean islands. On these islands, it's often drunk straight without adding ice and chased by a large glass of chilled water (called a 'crase' in Martinique).

The earliest known written mention appears as a "yon Ti Punch" in an 1890 book, Two Years in the West Indies by Lafcadio Hearn, who was a travel writer and recounted his time in Martinique.

name: Tiki Max
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE first 9 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
FLOAT Navy rum.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge and mint sprigs bouquet.
review: This drink breaks the golden rule - simple is beautiful. However, it's tasty and packs a punch.
history:
Created in 2008 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

The inspiration for this drink came not from the similarly named discount clothing retailer but the venerable Tiki cocktail itself. Tiki drinks, otherwise known as 'exotics' originated in 1934 when Ernest Raymond Beaumont Gantt, a Louisiana native who had made some money bootlegging rum during Prohibition, opened a bar called Don's Beachcomber in Hollywood and began serving rum based, fruity cocktails. Soon after, Victor 'Trader Vic' Bergeron transformed his restaurant Hinky Dinks into a similar faux Polynesian style. These founding fathers spawned many Tiki imitators and despite a dip in popularity from the 1970s, happily the noughties saw a revival in the fortunes of Tiki.

name: Tipperary No.2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE mint (just to bruise) in stirring glass.
Add other ingredients and STIR with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf floated on the cocktail or pegged/stuck with spot of honey to the rim.
review: Gin-laced and Martini-like with subtle mint and orange.
history:
This drink first appears (alongside Tipperary No.1) in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book where he stipulates to "Shake well" However, in his later, The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks, David Embury uses little less vermouth and instructs to "stir with cracked ice".

Shaking seems to amplify the bitter notes in the mint so I prefer to follow Embury's stirring method while leaning more towards Craddock's more generous vermouth proportions.

name: Tiramisu Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with cocoa powder.
review: Chef meets bartender in the rich dessert cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2001 by Adam Ennis at Isola bar in London, England.

name: Tom and Jerry (serves 6)
ingredients: 15 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-heat a TODDY GLASS.
Pre-warm six 20cl (6.75oz) toddy glasses or Tom and Jerry mugs with hot water.
Using a whisk, BEAT the yolks in a mixing bowl 'until they are thin as water.' Add sugar, Jamaican rum, spices and STIR thoroughly with whisk.
In a separate bowl, use a whisk to BEAT the egg whites 'to a stiff froth.'
Use a tablespoon to FOLD the frothy egg whites into the spiced and beaten yolk mixture. This produces the gloopy base mixture known as 'Tom and Jerry batter'.

To make individual drinks, to each of the warmed glasses or mugs, POUR cognac and rum, and add two table spoons of Tom and Jerry batter. STIR each glass/mug thoroughly with a barspoon while topping with boiling water.
garnish: Each should end up with frothy head ready to receive the garnish of grated nutmeg.
review:
A warming and filling winter's meal in itself. This recipe will fill six small toddy glasses or Tom & Jerry mugs (each 20cl capacity to brim), and given the faff involved you might as well double or treble the recipe and make for a crowd, or enjoy several each.

Once a wintertime staple served in taverns and saloons across America, the Tom & Jerry cocktail/punch was relegated to obscurity until recent years when cocktail resurgence and the drink's connections to Jerry Thomas assured its resurrection - particularly as a drink served over the Christmas period.

One of the reasons for its decline could be the perceived faff involved in its production - especially the 'Tom & Jerry batter' base. However, it's easy to make and uses common household ingredients.
history:
Due to his own claims of being this drink's inventor, the Tom & Jerry was for many years credited to Jerry Thomas at the Planter's House Hotel in St Louis. However, the origins of the Tom & Jerry cocktail predate the flamboyant 'Professor'.

Although there are earlier written references, the cocktail appeared in Jerry Thomas' first 1862 edition of How To Mix Drinks.

name: Tom Collins
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda and briefly stir.
garnish: Prepare garnish of skewered orange slice wheel and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry (sail).
review: A medium-dry Gin Collins based on old tom gin. There is much confusion between Tom Collins and John Collins; arguably, the two names are interchangeable [see history below]. However, I follow the convention that a Tom Collins is based on old tom gin while a John Collins is made with London dry gin (although a John Collins was originally made with genever. Today, that's a Dutch or Genever Collins).
history: In his 1948 The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks, David A. Embury says "The original Collinses were always made with gin but, strangely enough, never with London dry gin - the very liquor that is practically always used in making a Collins today. The Tom Collins was made with Old Tom gin and the John Collins with Holland gin [jenever]." He goes on to explain the confusion/difference between a Tom and John Collins:

name: Treacle No.1
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR first half of the rum, sugar syrup and bitters into glass.
Add two ice cubes and STIR.
Add rest of rum, another couple of ice cubes and STIR again.
Fill glass with ice and STIR again.
FLOAT apple juice.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Molasses flavours feature in this Rum Old Fashioned.
history: This twist on the Old-Fashioned was created by Dick Bradsell in 1990s London. Rather than use a stirring glass, Dick made this cocktail directly in the glass the cocktail's served in, adding ingredients and cubes of ice one-after-another, stirring between editions. The apple juice Dick used is the cheap clear stuff rather than the 'posh' pressed cloudy apple juice. In this cocktail, cheap is best.

name: Tropic
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
review: A light, satisfying summertime cooler.
history: Based on a recipe believed to date back to the 1950s.

name: Tropical Caipirinha
instructions: <strong>1.</strong> Cut lime in half (from pole to pole) and then slice one of the halves into eight 3-4mm thick half-moon shaped segments and drop into the base of a robust rocks glass
<strong>2.</strong> Add 3 bar spoons caster sugar (or ½oz/15ml sugar syrup 2:1) and MUDDLE
<strong>3.</strong> When sure fruit juice is fully extracted and the sugar dissolved, transfer the muddled fruit and sugar into serving glass (preferably a 30cl / 10oz hi-ball glass)

<strong>4</strong> POUR cachaça (stored in the freezer), coconut liqueur and pineapple juice (also chilled) into the shaker base or glass used to muddle and swirl to rinse before pouring into serving glass
STIR to ensure all ingredients are well mixed
<strong>5.</strong> Use an <a href="/bar-equipment-and-barware/250/ice-tapper">Ice Tapper</a> to crack (rather than crush) four to five large ice cubes and drop into serving glass
STIR before topping with more ice and serve with a stirrer (but no straws).
review: In drink circles, tropical usually spells sweet. This drink has a tropical flavour but a definite adult sourness.
history:
Created in 2003 by yours truly.
Caipirinha cocktail history and variations

name: Turf Club
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Simple but very tasty, equal parts old tom gin and sweet vermouth with aromatic bitters. Winter's original 1884 recipe calls for "Peruvian bitters" - I found 3 dashes of Peruvian Cortesano Amargo Bitters Aromatic work well if you have them; alternatively, use a dash less of aromatic bitters (e.g. Angostura).
history:
A sibling, or at least cousin, of the Martinez and the Martini, the Turf Club is or could be, the first cocktail to combine gin and vermouth. It makes its first written appearance in George Winter's 1884 How To Mix Drinks–Bar-Keeper's Handbook as the "Turf Club" with later books most often merely titling the drink "Turf".

The name Turf Club refers to the gentlemen's clubs of the late 1800s-early 1900s, which operated as a combined restaurant, bar, meeting place, and gambling den for the gentlemen of the day. Captains of industry and the aristocracy have always liked horse racing, and where folk gather to watch racing, there is bound to be betting. Many a 'turf accountant' (bookmaker) frequented such clubs, and many a Turf Cocktail was consumed.

One of the most famous of these gentlemen's clubs, The Turf Club, stood at the corner of Madison Avenue and 26th Street in New York City. The building, Jerome Mansion, had pedigree, being the former home of Lady Randolph Churchill, the American mother of Winston Churchill - a man I suspect would have appreciated a well-made Turf Club cocktail.

name: Turkish Coffee Cocktail
instructions: MUDDLE cardamom pods in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Turkish and Arabic coffee is made with lightly roasted coffee beans that are very finely ground and boiled in water, usually with a little sugar, in a special pot called cezve in Turkey or ibrik elsewhere. It is sometimes flavoured with some cardamom and this vodka-based coffee cocktail harnesses the aromatic eucalyptus and citrus notes of this spice.
history: Created in 2003 by yours truly.

name: Tuxedo Cocktail Original
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered lemon zest twist and maraschino cherry.
review: I've tried this recipe with 2 to 3 dashes of Angostura and also orange bitters, but I prefer it with 2 dashes of both aromatic (Angostura) and orange bitters, so honouring Johnson, MacElhone, Mahoney and Grohusko. The result is a very aromatic semi-Dry Martini.
history:
Based on the earliest Tuxedo recipe I'm aware of in Harry Johnson's Bartenders Manual (1900 and later editions). The same recipe, bar the odd dash of bitters, also appears in Jack's Bar Manual by Jacob Abraham Grohusko (1910), Hoffman House Bartender's Guide by Charles Mahoney (1912) and Barflies & Cocktails by Harry MacElhone (1927).

All four of the revered bartenders stipulate equal parts old tom (sweet) gin and French (dry) vermouth with one dash each of maraschino and absinthe, but Grohusko's recipe adds 3 dashes Angostura Bitters; while MacElhone, Mahoney & Johnson call for orange bitters in place of the Angostura.
Also see: Tuxedo cocktail history

name: Twinkle
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE first 2 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: It's hard to believe this floral, dry, light golden tipple is so loaded with alcohol. Go easy twinkle toes!
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2002 by Tony Conigliaro at The Lonsdale bar in London, England. Originally made using prosecco and elderflower cordial but now mostly made with elderflower liqueur and often champagne.

name: USB Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Vanilla notes in the cognac sit beautifully with the pineapple juice while the acidity of the wine balances rich fruity sweetness.
history:
A brandy-driven riff on the Palermo cocktail created in 2003 by yours truly in London, England. 

Named, not after the Universal Serial Bus used to connect PCs and peripheral devices, but after the grape varieties Ugni and Sauvignon Blanc. Ugni Blanc is the most common grape variety in Cognac, while Sauvignon Blanc wine seems to suit this drink, (although it also works well with Chardonnay and other grape varieties).

name: Vacation Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice and strain back into the shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
POUR the liqueur into the centre of the cocktail. It should sink to create a blue base and leave a blue spot in the centre of the cocktail's frothy head.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: A great-looking holiday (Tiki-style) cocktail with vanilla, coconut and pineapple.
history: My 2003 adaptation of the then signature cocktail at the MercBar on Mercer Steet in Manhattan, New York City.

name: Vanilla and Grapefruit Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: Reminiscent of a Hemingway Special, this citrusy, vanilla-laced Daiquiri has a tangy bittersweet finish.
history: Created in 2003 by yours truly in London, England.

name: Vanilla Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A classic Natural Daiquiri with a hint of vanilla.

name: Vanilla Margarita
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and fine strain into ice-filled chilled glass.
review: A classic Margarita with a hint of vanilla.
history:
I first drank Vanilla Margaritas in 1998 at London's Café Pacifico and I drank a lot of them. I also drank VDQs (Vanilla Daiquiris) at Teatro on Shaftesbury Avenue - often on the same night. The former (Café Pacifico is an early evening haunt) was made with tequila infused with a vanilla pod while the latter (Teatro was most definitely a place to finish a night) was made with vanilla-infused Havana 3yo rum. And way before that, I drank McDonald's Vanilla shakes and ate vanilla ice-cream. Who doesn't like vanilla?

I used to, and sometimes still do, make my Vanilla Margaritas just the same way as my regular Margaritas, only based on vanilla-infused tequila (Café Pacifico-style) but thanks to a comment by Vincent Dufour on this very page, (on 5th December 2020) I developed this recipe (that's even better when also made with vanilla-infused tequila).

name: Vante
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE cardamom in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Bold, aromatic and fruit with generous vanilla and pineapple.
history: Created in 2003 by yours truly in London, England.

name: Verdant Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf.
review: What this cocktail lacks in verdant colour is made up by its grassy green favour.
history: Created in 2003 by yours truly.

name: Vodka Espresso
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of float 3 coffee beans in the centre of the drink, arranged in a flower petal formation..
review: Vodka and coffee combine in this tasty wake-up call.
history: Created circa 1983 by Dick Bradsell while bartending at the Soho Brasserie on Old Compton Street, London who created his Vodka Espresso for a woman, said to be an "American model," who famously asked for "Something that's gonna wake me up, then fuck me up."

name: Vodka Gimlet
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: Dry grainy vodka, delicately flavoured and softened by subtly sweetened lemon and lime juice.
history: A vodka-based riff on the classic gin-based Gimlet. Recipe by yours truly, London, England.

name: Vodkatini / Vodka Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR (or shake) all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lemon zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish &/or adorn with skewered olive.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist &/or skewered chilled green olive.
review: Temperature is key to the enjoyment of this modern classic. Consume while icy cold, and you'll enjoy a clean and crisp Martini.
history: Exactly where the Vodkatini fits in the vexed question of the origins of the Dry Martini are unknown but it's certain that 'Vodka Martinis' were being served in New York after the repeal of Prohibition in 1933. Its first known print appearance is in a 1935 Smirnoff marketing pamphlet:

name: Waltzing Matilda
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
Cut passion fruit in half and scoop out flesh into shaker.
Add next 3 ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
STRAIN (fine strain if you don't want passion fruit seeds in cocktail) into ice-filled glass.
TOP with ginger ale and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice wheel.
review: Passion fruit, gin, wine and ginger ale all combine well in this fruity, yet dry, refreshing drink.
history: Adapted from a recipe from David Embury's 1948 book, The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks.

name: Watermelon Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of fresh watermelon wedge on rim or watermelon slices sweets/candy.
review: Watermelon fruit shines in this medium-dry riff on a Vodka Martini.
history: Created in May 2020 by yours truly.

name: Dry Martini (2:1 ratio) Wet
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Express lemon zest twist over cocktail &/or garnish with skewered olive.
review: Reputed to be a favourite of King Charles III.
history:
A generous measure of vermouth to two of gin, hence the name 'Wet' Martini.

See: Dry Martini history.

name: White Lady
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review:
A silky and delicious gin-laced classic with a sour finish.

My White Lady recipe is authentic to the proportions in Harry Craddock's 1930 recipe (see below) in using 2 parts dry gin, 1 part triple sec and 1 part lemon juice, but the sugar in modern-day triple secs (such as Cointreau) fails to balance lemon's citrus acidity (many triple sec liqueurs have become drier post-2000) so, depending on the sourness of your lemons, it is desirable to add a dash or even splash of sugar syrup.

Led by The Savoy's American Bar itself, where Peter Dorelli always made his signature White Lady with egg white, this addition has become the norm in most countries. However, while the Japanese enjoy raw fish, the trend of adding raw egg to sour cocktails has yet to catch on there, with Japanese bartenders such as Hidetsugo Ueno San vehemently opposed to using egg white in any cocktail.

I believe the Japanese omission of egg white is more a cultural judgement than one of taste, and egg white rounds a White Lady. It also makes it white. Beneath the attractive fluffy white foam produced by aerating the egg lies a dirty yellow drink that hardly befits the name. Those against the use of egg white argue that the drink is not named after its colour but the numerous ghostly white ladies of mythology. Maybe, but dirty yellow is not as visually appealing as a fluffy white head, and the drink's better with it!
history:
Like so many cocktails, the history of the White Lady is debatable. However, the recipe we recognise today originated in the late 1920s, probably created by Victor Cabrin at London's Grosvenor House Hotel, and was popularised by Harry Craddock, who included it in his 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

Harry MacElhone, from Dundee, Scotland, created the first White Lady cocktail while working at London's Ciro's Club in 1919. However, this consisted of equal parts brandy, crème de menthe, and Cointreau, shaken and strained into a cocktail glass, so very different from the White Lady we know today with gin, triple sec, and lemon juice. 

Harry's brandy-based crème de menthe recipe appears in his 1922 Harry's ABC of Mixing Cocktails and is repeated the following year as in Harry of Ciro's ABC of Mixing Cocktails.

name: Wibble
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: As Dick Bradsell once said to me, "It may make you wobble, but it won't make you fall down." Dick named this cocktail after the 1970s' Weebles children's roly-poly toys which were advertised with the catchphrase, "Weebles wobble, but they don't fall down."
history: Created in 1999 by Dick Bradsell at The Player, London, England for Nick Blacknell - a conspicuous lover of gin.

name: Widow's Kiss
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR (or shake) all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherries.
review: Herbal with hints of apple, mint and eucalyptus. This classic is also often made with green Chartreuse, but we prefer, as per classic recipes, with half Yellow Chartreuse and half Bénédictine.
history: Created by George J. Kappeler at New York City's Holland House Hotel. This recipe is adapted from his 1895 book Modern American Drinks.

name: Wink
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR absinthe into ice-filled glass.
TOP with chilled water and set to one side.
Separately, SHAKE other ingredients with ice.
DISCARD contents of glass (absinthe, water and ice).
FINE STRAIN cocktail into empty absinthe-coated glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Reminiscent of a Sazerac but with gin and triple sec. Delicate, remarkably fruity and very sippable.
history: Created in 2002 by Tony Conigliaro at The Lonsdale in Nottinghill, London, England.

name: Wonky Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express pink grapefruit zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare pink grapefruit zest for garnish.
review: A sweet, wet Vodkatini invigorated with vanilla and grapefruit.
history: Created in 2003 by yours truly, London, England.

name: Yellow Belly
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Lemon, lemon and lemon, but well-balanced, refreshing and tasty. A great early summer's evening cocktail.
history: Originally named Yellow Belly Martini, this was a popular neo-martini in London during the 1990s.

name: Yellow Bird
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with carved banana or skewered pineapple wedge, maraschino cherry and half orange slice.
review: With its sunny colour and tropical fruity flavour and rum base, the Yellow Bird certainly evokes the Caribbean. Two rums and three fruit juices, but the ripe banana and peppermint freshness of the two liqueurs that most influence this lightly Tiki cocktail.
history: Perhaps simply named due to its golden yellow colour or possibly after the 19th-century Haitian tune Choucoune, rewritten with English lyrics as Yellow Bird in the mid-1950s. The song was first recorded with his calypso-style arrangement by Norman Luboff and his choir in 1957. A 1959 recording by the Mills Brothers became a minor hit but it was Hawaiian singer Arthur Lyman's recording that rose to No.4 on the Billboard charts in July 1961. Some say that this record was regularly played at Shell Bar in The Hawaii Village so inspiring the creation of this cocktail there.

name: Z Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of blue cheese stuffed olives.
review: Grainy vodka with dry, wine-like notes. The garnish makes the drink.
history:
Discovered in 2004 at Les Zygomates, Boston, USA.

Our Martini cocktail page details its evolution and other common variants.

name: Zamboanga 'Zeinie'
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: I described our previous version of this classic as "Reminiscent of a tropical Sidecar." The recipe above is more fitting for this classic, but if you preferred the tropical version, it comprised 60ml cognac, 7.5ml maraschino cherry syrup, 30ml pineapple juice, 15ml lime juice, and 2 dashes of aromatic bitters.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Charles H. Baker, Jr.'s 1939 The Gentleman's Companion volume II – An Exotic Drinking Book. He describes this as "another palate twister from the land where the Monkeys Have No Tails. This drink found its way down through the islands to Mindanao from Manila..."

name: Zeus Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
RINSE glass with fernet (swirl around to coat and discard excess) and set to one side.
MUDDLE raisins with brandy/cognac in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into fernet-rinsed glass.
garnish: Garnish with 3 coffee beans floated on surface.
review: Rich, pungent and not too sweet.
history: Adapted from Dr Zeus, a cocktail created by Adam Ennis in 2001 at Isola in London, England.

name: Zombie
ingredients: 20 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill a ZOMBIE OR COLLINS GLASS.
DRY BLEND (without ice) first 10 ingredients (all but Xanthan gum) on a slow setting and then gradually add Xanthan while continuing to blend.
Add 7oz scoop of crushed ice and BLEND some more.
POUR into chilled glass.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: Powerful funky rum and delicate cinnamon spice partially tamed by rich fruit and sweetened clove.
history: Created by Don Beach in 1934 at Don the Beachcomber's in Hollywood, USA. This recipe is adapted from one published in Jeff Berry's Sippin' Safari, which Jeff sourced from the 1937 notebook of Beachcomber's waiter Dick Santiago.

name: Zoom
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with cocoa powder.
review: Cognac is smoothed with honey and softened with milk and cream in this classic cocktail.

name: Alice Mine
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Medium-dry with luscious orange notes. The orange zest twist is essential to the balance of this late-night sipper.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Stanley M. Jones' 1977 Jones' Complete Barguide.

name: Bacardi Special
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: Hit the perfect proportions and you will strike a wondrous balance of flavoursome rum, gin botanicals, limey sourness and fruity sweetness.
history: This vintage adaptation of the Bacardi Cocktail was first published in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book, were he says it was "Made famous by Karl K. Kitchen, the well-known New York Newspaper Columnist."

name: Bee's Knees
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review:
The combination of honey and lemon suggests flu relief but don't wait for an ailment before trying this soothing concoction.

The beneficial addition of a splash of orange juice to a Bee's Knees (classically it's just gin, lemon and honey) comes from David Embury's 1948 book The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks where he says, "The addition of a small amount of orange juice makes for an interesting variation".
history: For years, thanks to David A. Embury,  the Bee's Knees was thought to have emerged in America during Prohibition when it was said the lemon juice and honey in this cocktail masked the poor quality of gin. However, the Bee's Knees actually originated on the other side of the Atlantic in Paris, France.

name: Brandy Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Subtle mint and citrus lightly flavour the cognac.
history: One of the earliest cocktails, the Brandy Cocktail first appears in Jerry "The Professor" Thomas' 1862 The Bar-Tender's Guide & Bon Vivant's Companion.

name: Daiquiri No. 2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A Daiquiri with subtle orange notes, but far from being a mere Orange Daiquiri.
history: Created circa 1915 by created by Constantino "Constante" Ribalaïgua Vert, who owned Havana's famous El Floridita from 1918 to 1952, this is the second of four numbered daiquiris in the 1934 1st edition Bar La Florida Cocktails, menu book.

name: El Presidente (with rhum agricole)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf or sprig tip floated or pegged to rim.
review: Rhum agricole adds distinctive grassy notes to this delicate Cuban classic.
history:
El Presidente
El Presidente (Sloppy Joes)
El Presidente (sans grenadine
El Presidente Menocal Special

name: Fair and Warmer No. 2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Sure to warm and fairly good.
history: Adapted from Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Fairbanks No.1
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: Apricot liqueur dominates this cocktail but dry vermouth, dry gin, lemon juice, and dilution save it from excessive sweetness.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Fancy Brandy
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: This appropriately named brandy-based cocktail benefits from dilution, hence my addition of a splash of water. Modern bartending convention might lead you to stir rather than shake this cocktail, which would be a shame, as it's better shaken!
history: Adapted from a recipe by Charles Schumann, Munich, Germany, and published in his American Bar. Very similar to Jerry Thomas' Fancy Brandy Cocktail, published in the 1862 edition of his The Bar-tenders Guide.

name: Dry Martini (1:1 ratio) Fifty-Fifty
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered olive.
review: A very 'wet' but wonderfully Dry Martini which demands an olive, not a twist. Before you start - Craddock calls for it to be shaken, but it's better stirred. Shaking tends to suit Martinis with a low vermouth ratio.
history: First published in Hugo R. Ensslin's 1917 Recipes for Mixed Drinks and then repeated, like so many of Ensslin's cocktails, in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book. Both Ensslin and Craddock call for the "Fifty-Fifty Cocktail" to be stirred and both also list a Dry Martini cocktail with the addition of "1 dash orange bitters." However, while Ensslin's 2:1 Martini is stirred, Craddock's 1:1 Martini is shaken, so according to Craddock, a Fifty-Fifty should be stirred while an equal parts Dry Martini is shaken. Remember, this is 23 years before Fleming's first Casino Royale Bond novel.

name: Gin Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Bready genever with a hint of orange curaçao  adding flavour and the merest touch of sweetness balancing Boker's Bitters. If you don't have Boker's to hand then use the more widely stocked Peychaud's Bitters which produce a different but very tasty drink. I'd go as far as to suggest that Peychaud's produces better cocktail, one with an attractive pale shade of salmon pink – if you like that kind of thing.
history: A close relation to the Martinez, and so by default also the Martini, this cocktail is adapted from one of the oldest known cocktail recipes. Some say this recipe dates back to 1824, coincidentally the same year Dr Siegert created Angostura Bitters. However, the first known recipe appears in Jerry Thomas' 1862 The Bartender's Guide.

name: Gin Punch No. 1
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
review: Light and refreshing, akin to alcoholic real lemonade (not fizzy.
history: This is a version of the cocktail for which Limmer's Hotel in London was most famed: a Captain Gronow recalled it in his 1860s memoirs as one of the top, if filthy and seedy, sporting hangouts of 1814, thanks in part to its 'famous gin-punch'. A bartender named John Collins worked there later in the 19th century, and was famous enough to inspire a limerick. Hence, many believe he created the Collins, which is similar to gin punch.

name: Golden Bronx
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A Bronx made 'golden' by the addition of egg yolk.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Stanley M. Jones' 1977 Jones' Complete Barguide.

name: Honolulu Cocktail No. 1
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim skewered maraschino cherry.
review: Gin is hardly Hawaiian, but its botanical bite sits well in this tropically fruity cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Larchmont
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: I share Embury's appreciation of this fine, delicate Orange Daiquiri. However, I've tweaked his formulation as we found the original somewhat rich and over citrusy.
history: Created by David A. Embury, who in his 1948 Fine Art of Mixing Drinks writes of this drink: "As a grand finale to cocktails based on the Rum Sour, I give you one of my favorites which I have named after my favorite community." Larchmont is a village in Westchester County, New York where he lived for many years.

name: Mint and Honey Daiquiri
instructions: STIR honey and rum in base of shaker until honey dissolves
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: A fresh-breath-tastic riff on the classic Daiquiri.
history: Created in 2006 by yours truly (Simon D).

name: Sir Walter Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Spirit-forward with faint zesty tartness balanced by delicate rich red fruit sweetness.
history:
The Sir Walter (or Swalter) first appears in Harry MacElhone's 1927 Barflies and Cocktails and is repeated in other regarded tomes such as The Savoy Cocktail Book (1930).

Whether this cocktail honours Sir Walter Raleigh or Sir Walter Scott is a matter of conjecture, but as MacElhone, who heralded from Dundee, Scotland, is thought to be the drinks' creator, many favour Scott as being the protagonist.

name: Tahitian Honey Bee
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Basically a lemon honey Daiquiri or a lemon Canchànchara - very tasty it is too.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Victor Bergeron's 1972 Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide (Revised Edition).

name: TNT (Tequila 'N' Tonic)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
STIR.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A simple but very tasty way to enjoy tequila.
history: Adapted from Victor Bergeron's Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide (1972 revised edition).

name: Toreador
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge on rim.
review:
Apricot liqueur replaces triple sec in what otherwise follows a broadly classic Margarita recipe. As with my preferred Margarita recipe, my previous recipe was based on reposado tequila with half bar spoon of agave syrup, but a Toreador better suits the clean fresh notes of a blanco tequila with a spoon of sugar syrup.

I updated this recipe in January 2025. The Previous recipe: 50ml (1⅔oz) reposado tequila, 25ml (5/6oz) apricot brandy liqueur, 25ml (5/6oz) lime juice, 2.5ml (½ barspoon) agave syrup and 4 drop margarita bitters.
history: This variation on the Margarita was first published in William J. "Billy" Tarling's 1937 Café Royal Bar Book.

name: Amarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of length of lime peel.
review: The name of this bitter bittersweet and sour Margarita riff references amaro (meaning bitter) and the use of an Italian bitter liqueur. Many will want to add 5ml (1 bar spoon) of agave syrup to rebalance this cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2007 by Neyah White in San Francisco, USA.

name: Amber Room No. 2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A subtle, delicately floral Martini.
history: Created in 2007 by Stephan Berg, Munich, Germany.

name: Ampersand Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A boozy brandy-influenced 'wet' and 'sweet' Martini with a hint of orange. The zesty orange notes help the cleansing attributes of this spirit-forward cocktail, so making it equally suited to the pre-dinner and after-dinner occasions.
history: First published in Albert Stevens Crockett's 1934 Old Waldorf Bar Days.

name: Anejo Highball
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass half filled with half ginger beer.
TOP with rest of ginger beer.
garnish: Garnish with orange and lime slice wheels.
review: Orange and rum with a hint of ginger spice. Long and thirst-quenching.
history:
The Añijo Highball was created in the late 1990s by Dale DeGroff at The Rainbow Room, New York City, USA.
Highball cocktail history

name: Apple Blossom
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A Gin & Juice with a little extra interest courtesy of a splash of apple brandy.
history: In W. J. Tarling's 1937 Café Royal Cocktail Book the invention of this cocktail is credited to one R. G. Buckby.

name: Bamboo (Savoy's 1930 recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of toasted almonds.
review: The simplest, one of the best and dryest renditions of the Bamboo cocktail. It may be simple but the combination of vermouth and fino makes for a fabulously complex cocktail.
history: Adapted from Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Bat Bite
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: All too easy - light and fruity, lightly fortified with rum character.
history: Adapted from a cocktail promoted by Bacardi.

name: Batida Rosa
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR soda water into ice-filled glass.
SHAKE other ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into the ice-filled soda-charged glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Fruity and tropical. A tall refreshing drink for a hot evening or an afternoon beside the pool.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2008 by Jeffrey Morgenthaler at Bel Ami Lounge, Oregon, USA.

name: Biggles' Aviation
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with fresh ginger and lime slices.
review: A ginger-influenced Aviation named after the fictional book Biggles in Borneo about WWII flying heroes Ginger, Algy, and Biggles. Originally inspired by the ginger used in Canton Ginger Liqueur being sourced from Borneo.
history: A riff on the classic Aviation created in 2008 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England

name: Biggles' Sidecar
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with fresh ginger and lemon slices.
review: The idea was to make a riff on an Aviation (see Biggles Aviation) but ginger cries out for cognac so I also ended up with this ginger Sidecar.
history: Created in 2008 by yours truly at The Cabinet Room, London, England. As a kid, I was an avid reader of the Biggles books.

name: Blood Sage
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with sage leaf.
review: Sage combines wonderfully with gin and orange in this well-balanced sour.
history: Adapted from a drink created by Ryan Magarian, Seattle, USA.

name: Bloody Bronx Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of dehydrated blood orange slice or orange zest twist.
review: A Bronx made 'bloody' by the use of blood oranges. Fruity yet dry with botanical notes both from the vermouth and the gin.
history: See: Bronx cocktail history

name: Brandy Daisy
instructions: STIR first 4 ingredients with ice and strain into glass filled with crushed ice
TOP with a splash of soda and briefly stir.
review: Chartreuse notes show strongly in this short refreshing cocktail, with cognac providing the base and lemon juice contributing to freshening citrus balance.
history: The Brandy Daisy appears in the 2nd (1887) edition of Jerry Thomas' Bar-Tender's Guide and then the 2nd (1888) edition of Harry Johnson's Bartenders Manual. Tellingly, both bartenders omit the Brandy Daisy, and indeed Daisies in general, from their first editions. I prefer Johnson's recipe.

name: Breakfast Club
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR honey and marmalade with rum in base of shaker until dissolved.
Add tea, and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Slightly smoky with incredible depth of flavour. A perfectly balanced delight.
history: Recipe courtesy of DrinkBoy.com in 2008.

name: Casino
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: This recipe is a broad interpretation of Jacob A. Didier's 1909 original and the Casino recipes that follow in Hugo R. Ensslin's 1916 book and other subsequent tomes. Although, as Didier merely specifies "dashes" of lemon juice, his recipe may not be so far removed from mine.
history: Until March 2014, when Henry Duke, a fellow discerning Drinker, made me aware that the Casino first appeared in Jacob A. Didier's 1909 book The Reminder: Up-To-Date Bartender' Vest Pocket Guide [thanks, Henry], I and most others credited Hugo R. Ensslin for debuting the recipe in his 1916 Recipes For Mixed Drinks, written when he was head bartender at New York City's Hotel Wallick which stood at the corner of Broadway and 43rd Street. It would appear that Ensslin copied Didier as the recipes in both books are almost identical.

name: Chartreuse Swizzle
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into chilled glass and two-thirds fill with crushed ice.
SWIZZLE and then fill the glass with more crushed ice.
Add more crushed ice to fill glass and SWIZZLE some more.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: A swizzle for people like me - Chartreuse lovers. Best enjoyed on a hot summer's evening.
history: Created in 2002 by Marco Dionysos at Tres Agaves in San Francisco, California.

name: Chrysanthemum
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Herbal and aromatic, this benefits from the dilution that comes with a good long stir.
history: The Chrysanthemum appears in Hugo R. Ensslin's 1917 Recipes for Mixed Drinks (and may well have been in his 1916 First Edition).

name: Clairvoyant
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A hazelnut flavoured variation on the classic Coronation Cocktail
history: Created in 2008 by yours truly in Jerez, Spain and named after Claire Hu, a fellow drinks hack on the same trip who was a fan of hazelnut liqueur.

name: Clara Astié Cocktail
instructions: STIR jam with rum until jam dissolves
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Tangy apricot with hints of maraschino dried by vermouth and fortified by light rum.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2008 by Ian McLaren of Bacardi. The name is in tribute to Doña Amalia's Godmother whose bequest revived the fortunes of Don Facundo Bacardi Massó allowing him to establish the rum brand we know today.

name: Clover Club
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with raspberry floated in centre of cocktail.
review:
Gin-laced, sweet 'n' sour with a fruity blast of rich raspberry toned by dry vermouth.

Depending on your raspberry syrup, the finished cocktail may range in colour from a shade of pale yellow to a tinge of pink through to pink red (the cocktail above was photographed made with homemade syrup). You may need to vary the measure of syrup from 12.5ml to as much as 20ml (2/3 oz): this is as much dependent on personal taste as your choice of raspberry syrup.
history:
The Clover Club cocktail is eponymously named after a club founded on 29th January 1882 by a group of prominent Philadelphia journalists who, from January 1883 to January 1897, met each Thursday at Philadelphia's Hotel Bellevue. Over time, the club's membership expanded to include lawyers, bankers and prominent businessmen. [Many thanks to Currer Bachman for sharing this link to the Clover Club's records via spiritsandcocktails.community.]

The first reference to a Clover Club (unearthed by David Wondrich) is in a 23 June 1901 piece in the New York Press where Michael Killackey (or "Killacky"), "the head wine man of the Walforf-Astoria" lists "delicious summer beverages" with recipes, the fourth being the "CLOVER CLUB COCKTAIL – White of one egg, juice of one-half lemon, dash raspberry syrup, one drink Plymouth gin, frappé."

The Clover Club Cocktail appears in Paul E. Lowe's 1909 book Drinks: how to mix and how to serve but with the lemon juice omitted from the recipe, although this is thought to be a transcription mistake. The cocktail does not feature in Lowe's earlier 1904 Drinks as they are Mixed, but a toast from the club does feature in the book.

name: Clover Club (D's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE raspberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with raspberry floated in centre of cocktail.
review: It's a bit of a faff to make, but it's worth the clogged sink drain from the raspberry shrapnel. Using raspberry syrup and especially raspberry jam in a Clover Club introduces cooked "jammy" notes, while this can be delicious (see Julie Reiner's Clover Club), I like to retain and hero fresh berry flavours.
history: My version of a classic .

name: Coffee Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a WINE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: As the 1887 edition of the Professor's book says, "The name of this drink is a misnomer, as coffee and bitters are not to be found among its ingredients, but it looks like coffee when it has been properly concocted."
history: Adapted from a recipe in the posthumously published 1887 Jerry Thomas' Bar-tenders Guide (Jerry "The Professor" Thomas died 15th December 1885).

name: Colony
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: Grapefruit and maraschino balance each other in this gin-based classic.
history: Created during the Prohibition period at New York's Colony speakeasy, possibly by bartender Marco Hattem. The Colony attracted an upmarket clientele, including the Vanderbilts and Windsors. The liquor was kept in an elevator which would be sent to either the attic or the basement if federal agents raided.

name: Corpse Reviver No.2 (Contemporary Recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Yellow Chartreuse replaces triple sec in this riff on the classic Corpse Reviver Cocktail No. 2.
history: A riff on the classic Corpse Reviver No. 2, this recipe was popular with London bartenders during the late noughties.

name: Cornwall Negroni
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail, then use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A Negroni-style cocktail served up. Heavy on the gin and relatively light on bitter liqueur and vermouth. It's a formula that works but like us, you may prefer yours served on the rocks.
history: Created in 2006 by Philip Ward, New York, USA after attending Gary "gaz" Regan's Cocktails in the Country workshop in Cornwall-on-Hudson.

name: Daiquiri Authentico
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A lightly peppermint spiced influenced Daiquiri.
history: A simplification of a drink created in 2008 by Erik Lorincz at Purple Bar, Sanderson Hotel, London, England.

name: Daiquiri No. 4
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge on rim.
review: Maraschino liqueur adds delicate notes to this finely balanced Daiquiri.
history: Created by Constantino "Constante" Ribalaïgua Vert, who bartended at Havana's famous El Floridita (originally Bar Florida) from circa 1914 to his death in 1952. (He purchased the bar in 1918.) This is the fourth of four numbered daiquiris in the 1934 1st edition Bar La Florida Cocktails menu book from his bar.

name: La Dolce Vita
instructions: MUDDLE grapes in base of shaker
Add vodka, honey and bitters, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with prosecco.
review: Complex, yet easy to quaff, with a touch of fizz adding some sparkle.
history: Created in 2002 by Tony Conigliaro at Isola, London, England

name: Dry Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail, then use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist (flamed).
review: Passion fruit syrup is flavoursome stuff, and its fruity richness is used sparingly to greatly enhance this bittersweet cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created circa 2005 by Kevin Armstrong at Match Bar in London, England. The Dry Daiquiri started its course towards becoming a contemporary classic cocktail in 2006/7 when it was added to the menu at Trailer Happiness over in London's Notting Hill.

name: Dutch Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review:
A Dry Martini with a Dutch influence courtesy of genever and orange curaçao.

(Genever, considered the precursor to modern gin, is the traditional juniper-flavoured spirit from the Netherlands and Belgium.
Curaçao, the island where the oranges used to make the liqueur originate, is a constituent island country within the Kingdom of the Netherlands, located in the southern Caribbean Sea.)
history: Created by yours truly in 2010. Inspired by the Martini Cocktail in Harry Johnson's 1882 New and Improved Bartender's Manual, which calls for "a dash of curaçao or absinthe, if required."

name: Eighteen '97
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE kumquats in base of shaker.
Add next 3 ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Carefully FLOAT wine.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with skewered grape.
review: Looks great and, with the citrus freshness of kumquat combined with the oily character of grappa, it also tastes great.
history: Created in 2008 by Raffaello Dante at the short-lived Salvatore's at Fifty in London, England.

name: Fallen Angel
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with a mint leaf or float the tip of a mint sprig.
review: Gin-laced zesty citrus with subtle freshening mint with aromatic bitters adding depth of flavour and complexity.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Fitzgerald
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice wheel.
review: A Tangy and fresh Gin Sour without the egg white. A juniper heavy gin and dashes of bitters are key to the success of this sour.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in the early 1990s by Dale DeGroff at the Rainbow Room in Manhattan, New York City.

name: Flipping Good
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SOUR OR MARTINI/COUPETTE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: Simply an aged rum flip with falernum replacing sugar syrup and egg white omitted.
history: Created in December 2008 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Four Leaf Clover
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE clove in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with physalis (cape gooseberry) on rim.
review: Originally based on vodka, but seemed a shame not to try it as a Daiquiri.
history: Created in 2008 by Denis Broci at Maze bar & Restaurant in London, England.

name: Hoyt's Daiquirí
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
Sear two halves of lime with a chef's blow torch and squeeze into a shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with seared lime slice.
review: Most unusual and interesting. Recognisably a Daiquiri but with a creamy mouthfeel and great depth of flavour.
history: Created in 2008 by Meimi Sanchez at Bramble in Edinburgh, Scotland.

name: Ideal
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of serve with almonds on the side.
review: On the dry, tart side of bittersweet with hints of freshening zesty grapefruit and dry gin botanicals which cut through heavier vermouth notes. The maraschino is subtle but key.
history: The first known appearance of this classic cocktail is in Hugo R. Ensslin's 1917 Recipes for Mixed Drinks (2nd Edition) (It may have also appeared in his 1916 first edition.)

name: Jalisco Flower
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine and briefly stir. (Or pour half the sparling wine into the glass first to help reduce foaming and eliminate need to stir.)
garnish: Garnish with grapefruit zest twist &/or edible white flowers.
review: Subtle, fruity and one of my favourite champagne/sparkling wine cocktails.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2008 by Vincenzo Marianella at Doheny in Los Angeles, USA. (Originally served straight-up in a flute.)

name: Kangaroo
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Temperature is key to the enjoyment of this modern classic. Consume while icy cold.
history: Bartending legend has it that "Kangaroo" was the original name for a Vodka Martini. It wasn't: the first known printed reference to a Vodka Martini predates that of what was originally called the "Kangaroo Kicker" in Oscar Haimo's 1943 Cocktail Digest by ten years.

name: Lorraine
instructions: Select a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: Sweet and sour with fruity, herbal notes.

name: Martinez (London spec. with genever)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Bready notes from genever sit brilliantly with the herbal notes of the vermouth. Maraschino notes are subtle but essential to the balance of flavour.
history:
This formula for the vintage classic emerged and became popular in London in early 2008 with the introduction of Antica Formula.
See Martinez cocktail history

name: Matador Margarita (Tommy's Style)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge.
review: A Pineapple Margarita with agave syrup in place of a sweet liqueur.

name: Mayfair No. 2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE cloves in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Gin-laced with clove spice, apricot and orange fruitiness.
history: Unknown.

name: Metropole
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist expressed and discarded. Adorn with Skewered Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: A Cognac Manhattan mellowed by a dash of sugar.
history: The Metropole first appears in George J. Kappeler's 1895 Modern American Drinks and was the signature cocktail of the Hotel Metropole, located just off Times Square at Broadway and 42nd Street. It is a subtle adaptation of the 1884 Metropolitan Cocktail.

name: The Montford
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A wonderfully wet Martini with the addition of Lillet and orange bitters.
history: Adapted from a drink created by Dan Warner at the Beefeater Distillery in London, England.

name: Moonlight Cocktail (Gaz Regan's)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Basically an Aviation but with triple sec in place of maraschino and lime instead of lemon juice. Gaz used lime juice because he didn't have any lemons but in this adaptation of his recipe I've mellowed the citrus sourness by using a combination of both lime and lemon juice.
history:
This riff on the classic Aviation was created by Gary "gaz" Regan and first published in his The Cocktailian column in the San Francisco Chronicle on 27 September 2007. Gaz wrote, "After making my new baby I took it for a stroll around my deck and gazed up at the moon. It was off-white that evening, but it had a faint purplish hue to it. Just like my drink. The Moonlight Cocktail was born."

For the story behind the origins of the Aviation and its many other variations see our Aviation cocktail page.

name: Mulata Daisy
instructions: MUDDLE fennel seeds in the base of shaker
Add lime juice and powdered sugar and stir until sugar is dissolved
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Delicately spiced and subtly chocolaty.
history: Created in 2008 by Ago Perrone at The Connaught, London, England. This drink, which is named after the classic Cuban Mulata Daiquiri, won the 2009 Bacardi Legacy.

name: Myrtle Bank Special Rum Punch
ingredients: 45 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: A rich, fruity and spirituous Daiquiri.
history: Originally made at the Myrtle Bank Hotel in Kingston, Jamaica. Built in the mid-1800s, the Myrtle Bank was converted from a shipyard into a select boarding house. After the hotel was destroyed in the 1907 earthquake it was reconstructed in 1918 and was then the largest hotel in Jamaica, with 205 rooms and a filtered saltwater pool.

name: Negroni Spumante
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR first 3 ingredients into ice-filled glass.
STIR.
TOP with sparkling wine.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A Negroni invigorated with a splash of sparkling wine. Every Negroni drinker should try this variation.
history: The history and other recipes for the Negroni cocktail can be found on our Negroni cocktail page.

name: Old Cuban
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf floated on the cocktail or pegged/stuck with spot of honey to the rim.
review: A luxurious, minty Daiquiri topped with a splash of fizz.
history: Created in 2004 by Audrey Saunders, New York, USA.

name: Orchard Crush
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with seasonal berries.
review: Apple brandy and damson jam combine brilliantly in this tangy but not at all sweet cocktail.
history: Created in 2008 by Jeffrey Morgenthaler at Bel Ami Restaurant & Lounge in Eugene, Oregon, USA.

name: Perfect Regent
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered seasonal berries.
review: Fruity and easy, perhaps not what you would expect.
history: Recipe adapted in 2008 by yours truly.

name: Pernelle
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
TOP with soda and briefly stir.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Garnish with rosemary sprig.
review: This long clear drink has a grassy, alpine aroma and a fresh pine finish.
history: Created in 2007 by Colin Asare-Appiah, London, England. Colin named this drink after the wife of the 14th-century alchemist Nicolas Flamel, who supported her husband in his search for the Philosopher's Stone.

name: Prestige
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Aged rum, pineapple, and lime are used with delicate but enlivening clove spice.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2002 by Dale DeGroff, New York City, USA.

name: Ranglum
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Estery rum with zesty lime and sweet clove spice. A Tiki-style cocktail for a full-on sunny afternoon or to lift a dull winter's day.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2006 by Gonçalo de Sousa Monteiro at Victoria Bar, Berlin, Germany. Gonçalo created this drink whilst listening to Ernest Ranglin, so he used a combination of the guitarist's surname and falernum to name his new libation.

name: Reversed Vesper and Tonic 'Martini'
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE first 7 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with tonic water.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Bittersweet and Martini in style but with the hard edges smoothed and a hint of eastern spice added. Perhaps more a digestivo than an aperitivo.
history:
Created in 2008 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

James Bond named his favourite style of Martini (Vesper) after the beautiful Russian agent Vesper Lynd. This version is 'Reversed' due to the dramatically increased ratio of aromatised wines. Fittingly, my cocktail mixes East and West European ingredients with the introduction of Becherovka. And where there is Becherovka, there should be tonic water (see Be-ton).
