I originally made this with 5ml of homemade pomegranate syrup to add much-needed sweetness (to counter kina bitterness) as a fitting blood-like syrup. Swapping to commercial grenadine added too much colour and flavour; hence, now equal parts sugar and grenadine syrup.

name: Rose-Hyp Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
THROW all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with rose petal, edible flower or orange zest twist.
review: Dry (but far from bone dry), aromatic and floral.
history: Created in 2006 by yours truly in London, England.

name: Rum Amandine
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: A classic Daiquiri benefiting from the influence of maraschino, almond and absinthe.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Marcis Dzelzainis at Quo Vadis in London, England.

name: Sake Martini
instructions: STIR all ingredients with ice and fine stain into chilled glass.
review: Dry, subtle, and depending on your choice of sake, possibly amazing.
history: Our Martini cocktail page details its evolution and other common variants.

name: Shark's Tooth (by Don Beach)
instructions: BLEND first 5 ingredients with 4oz scoop of crushed ice and pour into an old-fashioned glass
POUR Jamaican rum into shot glass and serve alongside drink, instructing drinker to pour rum into cocktail prior to sipping (without straw).
review: This cocktail should be blended so the ice is dissolved to chill and dilute the drink which should end up with a long-lasting frothy head.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Don Beach in the 1960s.

name: Sherry Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: The huge flavour of sherry is freshened by lemon juice and rounded by egg white.
history: Adapted from a cocktail discovered in 2007 at Suba in New York City, USA.

name: Sol Ardiente
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: MUDDLE peppercorns in base of shaker
Add next four ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with splash soda.
review: Red pepper and pineapple influences this twisted Daiquiri.
history: Created in 2008 by Anthony Farrell, San-greal Bartending Ltd, Belfast, Northern Ireland. The name translates from Spanish as 'Burning Sun'.

name: Tenner Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: Very wet, aromatic Martini.

name: Valencia Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A Martini-style drink but using fino sherry instead of vermouth. Crisp and dry.
history: See Martini cocktail and its evolution

name: Waldorf Cocktail No.2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Lime works better than lemon but like the Astor, using a combination of lime and lemon produces an even better drink.
history: This is the later of the two versions of this vintage cocktail from New York's Waldorf-Astoria Hotel [Waldorf No.1]. It appears in the 1939 Café Royale book and in the 1955 United Kingdom Bartender's Book. Both books offer a choice between using lemon or lime juice and the original recipe calls for two parts Swedish punch to one part gin but with most modern-day Swedish punches this makes for an overly sweet drink.

name: White Cargo
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: A delicious dessert-style drink.
history: Adapted from Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Wild Blossom
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: Aptly named - it is indeed blossom-like with subtle floral elderflower, yet also balanced with assertive gin notes.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2007 by James Scarito at BLT Market in New York City, USA.

name: Will Of Alan
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Fantastic, spiced, tiki-style twisted Daiquiri.
history: Discovered in 2008 at Tonic, Edinburgh, Scotland.

name: Corpse Reviver No.2 (Duffy's/Gaige's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: For an equal-parts recipe, this Corpse Reviver variation is brilliantly balanced and delicious.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Patrick Gavin Duffy's 1956 Official Mixer's Manual. This recipe features Swedish Punch, whereas the recipe in his 1934 first edition calls for Kina Lillet. Duffy's recipe change may have been influenced by Crosby Gaige's 1941 Cocktail Guide and Ladies' Companion, where this Swedish Punch version first appears.

name: Daisy Cutter Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf &/or edible daisy pegged to rim.
review: Dry, floral, minty and herbal. A palate cleansing Vodkatini. The few tiny shrapnels of mint that make it through the fine strainer add to this cocktail's appearance
history: Named not for the bomb but after the English cricketing term for a ball bowled so incompetently that it skims along the ground. Created in 2006 by yours truly and reformatted with ginseng bitters added in April 2026.

name: Elderflower Martini No.2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.

name: English Breakfast Cocktail

SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Gin's botanicals and rich floral elderflower are the foundation overlayed with zesty sour citrus freshness and drying black tea tannins.
history: Created in 2006 by yours truly, London, England.

name: English Channel
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: The earl grey tannins balance the spice and orange in the liqueurs to make a harmonious aperitivo.
history: Adapted from a drink discovered in 2005 at Bellini, Auckland, New Zealand.

name: Fallen Leaves
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Suitably autumnal in colour. Two vermouths and two brandies presented in harmony.

name: Fat Tire
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: This flavourful, bittersweet fruity aperitivo won't be to everyone's taste but it's certainly to ours.

name: Feather Duster Crusta
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: In Gregor's own words, "based on the father of the Sidecar, the granddad of the Margarita, Laydeez an' Gennulmen! The Brandy Crusta."
history: Created in 2006 by Gregor de Gruyther at Ronnie Scott's in London, England. It is "quite a light Crusta", hence the name.

name: Gun Club Punch No.1
instructions: Select and pre-chill a CARTRIDGE MUG OR COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into mug or glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: Rum-laced fruit, as with most Trader Vic classics, balanced rather than sweet.
history: Victor Bergeron specified that this cocktail should be served in one of his bespoke green cartridge mugs (pictured here). This recipe is adapted from Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide (1972 revised edition).

name: Gypsy No. 1
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: Dominic Venegas, this cocktail's creator, describes it as "a little homage to the Last Word cocktail".
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2007 by Dominic Venegas at Bourbon & Branch in San Francisco, USA.

name: Hearst Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A Fifty-Fifty Sweet Martini with orange and aromatic bitters.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Jacques Straub's 1913 Straub's Manual of Mixed Drinks. Supposedly a favourite of hacks who worked for the American newspaper magnate, William Randolph Hearst, and believed to have been created at New York's Waldorf-Astoria. It is nicknamed "The Disgruntled Journalist", perhaps in reference to the Journalist cocktail.

name: Italian Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A liberal dash of amaretto adds very Italian subtexts of apricot and almond to a classic Margarita.
history: Discovered in 2005 at the Club Bar in The Peninsula Beverly Hills, USA.

name: Air Mail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into an ice-filled glass (preferably a column of ice).
TOP with sparkling wine and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with lime zest twist (not expressed) or mint sprig tip.
review:
Lightly aged rum invigorated with brut sparkling wine, freshened by a touch of lime and balanced by honeyed richness.

Described by some as being a rum-based French 75, and like the French 75, there is debate over the correct glass and whether to serve with ice. The truth: the Air Mail is best as originally intended, served as a highball in a highball glass (max capacity 10 oz/ 300 ml).
history:
This is a potent little cocktail's name likely references airmail as the quickest way to get a letter from A to B., and it probably dates to the early days of airmail, which started on 15th May 1918 with the world's first scheduled airmail route between New York and Washington D.C.. However, the earliest known reference to this cocktail is a 1930s promotional pamphlet, Bacardi and Its Many Uses.

The Air Mail (spelt as two words) most notably appears in W.C. Whitfield's 1941 wooden cover-bound Here's How, accompanied by the note "It ought to make you fly high."

name: Parisien Mojito
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE mint (just to bruise) in base of glass
Add other ingredients, half fill glass with crushed ice and CHURN (stir) with bar spoon
Fill glass to brim with more crushed ice and churn some more
Serve with straws.
review: Those with a sweet tooth may want to add a dash of sugar syrup.
history:
Created in 2006 by yours truly.
See: Mojito Cocktail history

name: Nuclear Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge on rim.
review: A great way to inflict mutually assured destruction, although there will be fallout the morning after.
history: Created in 2005 by the late great Gregor de Gruyther at LAB bar in London, England.

name: Obituary
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with green olive.
review:
What a way to go. A Martini with a splash of the green fairy. I like to use old tom gin to make an Obituary as it sits so much better with the absinthe than London dry gin. I also prefer bianco over the dry vermouth traditionally used to make an Obituary. However, If I only have dry gin to hand, this is my recipe:  
60ml (2oz) Dry Gin (from freezer) 
7.5ml (¼oz) Dry vermouth
2.5ml (½ spoon) Absinthe
history: This absinthe-laced Dry Martini was popularised during the 1940s at Lafitte's Blacksmith Shop in New Orleans and is regarded as one of the Crescent City's cocktails.

name: Old Flame
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail, then use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: As Dale DeGroff, this cocktail's creator, says, it's a Negroni softened by fresh orange juice and a splash of triple sec.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2001 by Dale DeGroff in New York, USA.

name: Pear Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pear wedge on rim.
review: Vermouth adds depth of flavour to aromatic pear vodka and floral elderflower liqueur.

name: Pear Tree Cocktail
ingredients: Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE first 2 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: Garnish with pear wedge on rim.
review: Aromatic pear vodka and elderflower liqueur paired with biscuity sparkling wine.

name: Pegu Club Cocktail (D's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge on rim.
review:
Whatever you think of modern-day Rose's Lime Cordial (other brands are available as well as homemade recipes), as with the Gimlet, I believe lime cordial is an essential part of this cocktail's DNA so should be included. Also, in keeping with the Gimlet, I like to use fresh lime juice to add fresh citrus zing alongside the 'cooked' notes of the cordial.

The other moving part of a Pegu Club is the orange curaçao liqueur. A dry 40 alc./vol. versus a very sweet 20-27% orange curaçao will impact the overall alcohol and sugar content very differently. I use a dry 40% curaçao so this dictates the need to balance with a smidgen of sugar syrup.

Strike the perfect balance and a Pegu Club is citrusy, bracing, and refreshing with the gin base providing the backbone while rich zesty orange curaçao, accentuated by tart lime, drives the flavour.
history:
Created in the 1920s at the Pegu Club, an ex-pat gentlemen's club in British colonial Rangoon, Burma. 

The Pegu Club first appears in Harry McElhone's 1923 "Harry" of Ciro's ABC of Mixing Cocktails and notably in Harry Craddock's seminal 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book where he notes, "The favourite cocktail at the Pegu Club, Burma, and one that has travelled, and is asked for, round the world."

Despite The Savoy's influence, it's the recipe published just months before Craddock's, in Cocktails by Jimmy Late Of Ciro's London, written by the mysterious "Jimmy" (like McElhone another bartender who worked at Ciro's in London), which calls for fresh lime rather than cordial that's nearer to most other modern-day interpretations of this venerable classic. [Mine respects the use of lime cordial so is perhaps a tad more authentic!]

name: Periscope
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: Fabulously light, almost creamy, and floral.
history: Created by Matt Gee at Milk & Honey in New York City, USA.

name: Peruvian Elder Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: This tasty sour combines the aromatics of pisco and elderflower in an intriguing variation on the Margarita. Consider smoothing with fresh egg white.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2006 by drinks writer Gary "gaz" Regan</a in New York, USA.

name: Peto
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: APerfect Martini with a splash of orange juice and a touch of maraschino liqueur.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: The Puritan
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Gin contributes botanical strength, vermouth enhances the aromatics, while Chartreuse adds sweet herbal complexity.
history: An often overlooked classic which is thought to have originated at the end of the 19th century.

name: Queen's Park Swizzle
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE mint in base of shaker (just to bruise).
Use a bar spoon to raise the bruised mint up and around the inside of the glass to coat with fragrant mint oils.
Add other ingredients.
Two-thirds fill glass with crushed ice and SWIZZLE.
Fill glass with more crushed ice and briefly CHURN.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: This close relation to the Mojito is drier, more complex and tad less minty than its sibling. Some like to finish this cocktail with a hefty dose of bitters to colour the crushed ice atop the cocktail but as Martin Cate says in his 2016 book Smuggler's Cove, "even though the bitters look pretty when floated on top, the drink tastes much better when they are swizzled with the rest of the ingredients." Two dashes integrated with the other ingredients is plenty.
history:
Created at the Queen's Park Hotel, Port of Spain, Trinidad. The hotel opened on the 15th of January 1895 and it is likely that this cocktail emanated soon after. This luxury hotel was a fashionable stop for Hollywood types and well-heeled vacationers who spread the legend of its tall swizzled signature cocktail. The hotel was also famed for its Queen's Park Hotel Super Cocktail.

In this 1946, Trader Vics Book of Food and Drink, Victor Bergeron describes the Queen Park Swizzle as "the most delightful form of anesthesia given out today." A year later he also includes this swizzle in his Bartender's Guide By Trader Vic, adding, in the 1972 revised edition, "If you like to make and drink a real doozer of a rum drink that really is a rum drink, try this. It's from the Queen's Park Hotel in Trinidad."

name: Elderflower Spritz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR wine and then elderflower liqueur into an ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda.
garnish: Garnish with lime wheel.
review: A long, easy-drinking summer cooler.
history:
Created in 2006 by yours truly ahead of the launch of St-Germain elderflower liqueur. It went on to become the brand's signature cocktail.
See: Spritz cocktail history

name: Saúco Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge on rim.
review: The floral notes of elderflower combine wonderfully with the herbaceous tequila and citrusy lime.
history: Created in 2006 by yours truly and named after 'flor saúco', which is Spanish for elderflower.

name: The Bird Is The Word No. 2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: A grappa-based Last Word by way of Denmark.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2009/10 Mikael Melzer Nilsson while a bar manager at Salon 39 in Copenhagen, Denmark.
See: Last Word history

name: Vancouver
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish (&/or garnish with skewered cherry).
garnish: Orange zest twist (&/or skewered Luxardo Maraschino Cherry).
review: This cocktail, which is popular in the city from which it takes its name, was originally made with French dry vermouth but since the turn of the century has been made with Italian sweet vermouth. I've opted for the 'perfect' solution with 50% dry and 50% sweet vermouths.
history: Recipe adapted from the About Town Cocktail Book which featured popular food and drink recipes in Vancouver and was published in 1925 by Mitchell Publishing, a local publishing company.

name: Wisecrack Fizz
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: SHAKE first four ingredients with ice and strain into an ice-filled glass
TOP with soda and serve with straws.
review: Light, balanced and refreshing. The pisco character shines through.
history: Created in 2007 by Matt Gee at Milk & Honey, New York City, USA.

name: Apple Fool
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of float dehydrated apple slice.
review: There's no gold in 'Fool's Gold', a mineral called Iron Pyrite (FeS2) that looks like gold. Similarly, there's no apple in this Apple Fool although its crisp enlivening freshness is reminiscent of a good apple.
history: Adapted from a drink created by Aisha Sharpe in New York City, USA.

name: Apple Martini (with apple schnapps)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with apple wedge on rim.
review: There are as many different recipes for this drink as there are varieties of apple and brands of apple liqueur: this one was popular in the UK during the Noughties.

name: Chanticleer
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Clean, crisp, and fresh - gin-laced orange zesty citrus with herbal vermouth mellowed by smoothing egg white.
history:
Recipe adapted from Albert Stevens Crockett's 1935 The Old Waldorf-Astoria Bar Book which originally calls for "orange gin" rather than an orange liqueur. Crockett says the drink "celebrated the local opening of Edmond Rostand's Chanticler".

Edmond Rostand was a French poet and dramatist, best known for his play Cyrano de Bergerac. First released in 1910, Chantecler (correct spelling) is a story where the characters are based on barnyard animals, and whose eponymous protagonist is a rooster who believes that his song makes the sun rise. 

By all accounts, the cocktail is rather better than the play. Incidentally, the Chantecler is actually a breed of chicken developed in the early 20th century by Brother Wilfred Chantelain, a Trappist monk at the Abbey of Notre-Dame du Lac in Canada.

name: Cuban Liberal
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Light white rum mellowed by Italian vermouth and made interesting with bittersweet French Amer Picon liqueur.
history: Adapted from a drink featured in the La Florida (El Floridita) Cocktails book, this is a riff on El Presidente.

name: Culross Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Relatively low in alcohol and suited to the aperitivo moment, your choice of quinquina will make or break this delicate aromatised rum sour.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Defender Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: One of those drinks where the bitter oils from the orange zest twist are crucial to its fine balance.
history: Adapted from Albert Stevens Crockett's 1931 Old Waldorf Bar Days which explains, "The name of an American yacht which took care of one of Sir Thomas Lipton's early but seemingly endless Shamrocks". [Shamrock being the name of Sir Thomas Lipton's fleet of America's Cup racing yachts.]

name: Fernando
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
review: An unusual, vermouth-based herbal cocktail - very fresh and aromatic.
history:
I came across this cocktail in 2010 at Employees Only in New York City, its birthplace. The recipe I took away from that night called for 30ml (1oz) dry vermouth, 30ml (1oz) rosso/sweet vermouth, 7.5 Galliano, and a mere 5ml (1/6oz) fernet, but I noted: "Originally based only on bianco vermouth with no dry vermouth." I blame their hospitality as it appears their actual recipe for a Fernando is more in keeping with the proportions above.

For all those who liked my previous incarnation, the recipe is 30ml (1oz) dry vermouth, 30ml (1oz) sweet/rosso vermouth, 7.5ml (¼oz) Galliano, and 5ml (1/6oz) fernet.

name: Four Aces
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
MUDDLE grapes in base of shaker.
Add next 5 ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda water and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with basil leaf.
review: A summery, light, superbly balanced herbal fruit cocktail.
history: Created in 2009 by Damian Windsor at The Roger Room in Los Angeles, California. Inspired by Table 8 in Los Angeles.

name: Greyhound's Tooth
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Fresh grapefruit flavours form the backbone of this summer-fresh cocktail.
history: Created in 2010 by Brandon Clements, San Francisco, USA.

name: Gypsy Queen
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Vodka both fortifies this complexly spiced honeyed and herbal cocktail.
history:
Drinks historian David Wondrich discovered the earliest known reference to the Gypsy Queen in a 1938 booklet published by New York City's famed The Russian Tea Room. This makes it one of the earliest vodka-cocktails in the USA.

In his seminal 1948 The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks, David Embury says, "The original recipe calls for a proportion of 1 to 2. This, however, is rather sweet. About 5 top 6 parts vodka to 1 of Benedictine makes a much better drink." [The above recipe is 3 to 1.] 

Embury's "Gypsy or Gypsy Queen" recipe of 1-part Benedictine and 2 to 6 parts Vodka stipulates "1 dash Orange Bitters to each drink" but Angostura or similar aromatic bitters produce a rounder cocktail.

name: The Hac
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE cardamom in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Perhaps better billed as being a 'Pineapple and Cardamom Margarita'.
history: Discovered in 2009 at Cloud 23, Manchester, England.

name: Highland Sazerac
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR Chartreuse into glass and twirl to coat inside with liqueur.
Fill glass with ice and set aside.
Separately, STIR other ingredients with ice in mixing glass.
DISCARD contents of glass (Chartreuse and ice).
FILL Chartreuse-coated glass with fresh ice and STRAIN contents of mixing glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A dash of malt gives a Highland influence to the Sazerac, also given a herbal note due to a generous splash of Chartreuse.
history:
Adapted from a recipe crearted by Don Lee at PDT in New York City, USA. Don's original recipe calls for yellow Chartreuse.
Sazerac cocktail history

name: Mr Kappes
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Kirschwasser, aromatised wine and sherry sit together harmoniously. I amended, to reduce the volume of serve and make a tad drier in January 2022. The previous recipe, served in a coupe: 60ml kirschwasser, 30ml aromatised wine, and 30ml PX sherry.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2009 by Diana Haider in Frankfurt, Germany.

name: Lady's Sidecar
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: The addition of orange juice and an orange zest twist supposedly gives this otherwise classic Sidecar a feminine twist. Classically, this cocktail does not contain sugar syrup, so it is a touch sourer. Hence, if you have a dry palate, omit the sugar, and if you have a sweet palate, consider increasing the sugar syrup to 5ml (1/6oz).
history: Sidecar cocktail history

name: Lazy Man Flip
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SOUR OR MARTINI/COUPETTE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: Indulgent creamy mouthfeel but not too sweet, with a great balance between the port and the calvados.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2007 by Chris Jepson at Milk and Honey in London, England. Chris' original recipe also contained 3/4 shot of cream. However, strictly speaking, flips contain egg but not cream.

name: Money Penny
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: Dry gin soured with a splash of fresh grapefruit juice and balanced with the merest touch of sugar.
history: Adapted from a recipe discovered in 2010 at the Blue Owl in New York City, USA.

name: Negroni Tredici
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with orange wedge.
review: A amaro riff on the Negroni.
history:
Adapted from a drink created in 2009 by Toby Maloney of Alchemy Consulting, USA.
Negroni cocktail history and recipe variations

name: Nick and Nora
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered olives.
review: A classic Three-to-One Dry Martini served in bijou proportions.
history:
Created in circa 1934 and named after Nick and Nora Charles, the flirtatious married couple at the centre of Dashiell Hammett's 1933 The Thin Man story serialised in Redbook Magazine. Published as Prohibition was ending, Nick is a hard-drinking retired detective and his wife Nora a wealthy heiress, both embrace cocktail culture. The Thin Man was published as a novel in 1934 and then a series of movies.

In the novel, Nick and Nora shake their Martinis and Nick tells a bartender, "The important thing is the rhythm. Always have rhythm in your shaking. Now a Manhattan you shake to fox-trot time, a Bronx to two-step time, a Dry Martini you always shake to waltz time." Hence, some insist a Nick and Nora Martini should be shaken rather than stirred.

In the movie, the detective duo drink from what has become known as a Nick & Nora glass so this has become the default for this scaled-down to fit Dry Martini.

name: Precursory Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with quarter orange slice on rim.
review: The winning cocktail from CLASS magazine Bartender of the Year 2009.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2009 by Tim Philips at Milk & Honey in London, England.

name: Roosevelt Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Hit the right proportions with freshly squeezed orange juice, and this drink is sublime.

name: Ruby Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit wedge .
review: A well-balanced, almond-influenced Daiquiri.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2008 by Wrigley "Oz" Osbourne at Goldbrick House in Bristol, England.

name: Starry Night
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with star anise floated on surface.
review: Delicate pear and aromatic maraschino over a dry wine base.
history: Created in 2008 by Jamie Boudreau at Vessel bar in Seattle, Washington.

name: Velvet Threesome
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A trio of brandies with a dash of herbal vanilla, rounded with vermouth.
history: Created in 2010 by yours truly.

name: Bohemian Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of caperberry.
review: A regular Five-to-One Dry Martini garnished with a caperberry.
history:
Created circa 2002 by Tim Stones and Ross Simon while bartending at LAB Bar on Old Compton Street in Soho, London, England. The name comes from Café Bohéme, the late-opening restaurant practically opposite, where the LAB crew would go for sustenance after a shift.
Martini history

name: Buckeye
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of black olive.
review: A standard Dry Martini - but a Buckeye is always garnished with a black olive instead of a green one.

name: Hollywood
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: This drink is deliciously different. Rum, grapefruit, and pomegranate are smoothed with egg white and spiced with nutmeg.
history: Adapted from a recipe in William "Cocktail Bill" Boothby's 1930 Swallows : World Drinks and How to Prepare Them.

name: Improved Holland Gin Cocktail
instructions: STIR all ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass
[To measure 1.25ml and how many dashes in 1.25ml, please see <a href="/g/1150/how-to-make-cocktails/measuring-cocktail-ingredients">Measuring cocktail ingredients</a>
review: Salmon-pink with subtle juniper, rounded by maraschino liqueur, aromatic bitters and absinthe.
history: The classic Holland House "improved" due to the use of genever.

name: King's Jubilee
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist (discarded) and skewered maraschino cherry.
review:
If there is such a thing as a 'Rum Aviation', then this is surely it.

The proportions of the original 1935 recipe stipulate equal amounts of maraschino and lemon juice but even my recipe with a third less lemon is on the tart side. Don't be tempted to drop the cherry garnish into the drink or both appearance and balance will be muddied. Skewer the cherry and reserve for a sweet treat at the end. 

My original take on this classic (up until July 2020) was 60ml light rum, 22.5ml maraschino liqueur and 15ml lemon juice but this packs a whopping 80ml of alcohol. The above recipe scales this back by a third to still pack a double shot punch, but in a drink that hopefully won't be a killer blow to the night.
history:
Created in 1935 to celebrate the Silver Jubilee of King George V by Harry Craddock, then head bartender of The American Bar at London's The Savoy Hotel. Craddock was also President of the United Kingdom Bartenders' Guild and this cocktail appears in the U.K.B.G. Approved Cocktails book published in 1937, as well as William James "Billy" Tarling's Cafe Royal Cocktail Book – Coronation Edition, published the same year.

Tarling was also in the top echelon of the Guild and the royalties of his book were equally divided between the U.K.B.G. Sickness Benefit Fund and The Café Royal Sports Club Fund.

name: Melba Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of float mint leaf or peg to rim.
review: This would be better named, "Melba Daiquiri", as it is a Swedish punsch, absinthe, and grenadine influenced Daiquiri. I've dramatically changed the equal parts rum and Swedish punsch specified in The Savoy Cocktail Book original, and upped the grenadine, but hopefully for the better.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Porn Star Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SCOOP out the seeds and flesh from 3 of the passion fruit halves into your shaker.
Add the next 4 ingredients (all but the sparkling wine).
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Optionally, separately POUR the sparkling wine into a chilled shot glass to serve alongside the cocktail.
review: A passion fruit and vanilla crowd-pleaser that's balanced with zesty lime, often served with sparkling wine on the side.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2002 by Douglas Ankrah at The Townhouse bar in Knightsbridge, London. Douglas also founded London's LAB bar which is perhaps more associated with this cocktail.

This cocktail was originally named the Maverick Martini after a nightclub in Cape Town, South Africa which Douglas frequented, a story he recounts in his 2004 book, Shaken & Stirred.

name: The Quad
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Four different, orange-flavoured ingredients combine in this complex bittersweet citrusy cocktail.
history: Created in 2010 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Roosevelt Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered olives.
review: A regular Martini, but garnished with two olives instead of one.
history:
Named after Franklin Delano Roosevelt (1882-1945), the 32nd President of the United States. Roosevelt is remembered for leading his country into World War II to fight alongside the Allies against Germany and Japan: he died just as victory was in sight.

Also see: Dry Martini

name: Sweet Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: A 5:1Dry Martini made with sweet vermouth - sweet in name but drier than the cherry garnish might indicate.

name: Tijuana Brass
instructions: SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
Top with cucumber foam
(Top make cucumber foam: fill a cream whipper two-thirds full with equal parts cucumber juice, 1:1 simple syrup and egg white, then charge with two nitrous oxide (N2O) cartridges and chill.)
review: Pronounced "Tea-You-One-Na" this is basically a Tommy's Margarita topped with cucumber foam.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2009 by Damian Windsor at The Roger Room in Los Angeles, USA.

name: Tip Top Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: The lemon zest twist is essential to the fine balance of this vermouth (note; no spirit) cocktail.
history: Recipe adapted from Albert S. Crockett's 1935 Old Waldorf Bar Days.

name: Green Deacon
instructions: MIST chilled glass with spray or rinse of absinthe to coat the inside and set aside
SHAKE other ingredients with ice and fine strain into absinthe-rinsed chilled glass.
review: Actually, a shade of orange-red rather than green, the name is a reference to the absinthe in this unusual combination of ingredients. The absinthe-rinse is a subtle addition to the Rosy Deacon but propels this classic into a more interesting place.
history: Created in 2009 by Jim Meehan at PDT in New York City, USA, this is a riff on the Rosy Deacon in Stanley M. Jones' 1977 Jones' Complete Barguide.

name: London Gypsy
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: The botanicals in the gin and the herbal Bénédictine combine well in this cocktail, which is balanced by the addition of bitters.
history: A riff on the classic Gypsy Queen by yours truly and then revisited in January 2023 thanks to feedback from fellow Discerning Drinkers and a tweak to my formulation by John Hinojos.

name: Sherry Cobbler
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge, orange slice, mint sprigs bouquet and seasonal berries.
review: This age old cocktail is said to have been the drink for which the waxed paper straw was invented. To quote Harry Johnson, from his 1882 Bartender's Manual, "It is a very refreshing drink for old and young."
history:
The drink of choice in American taverns from the early 18o0s was the Mint Julep (then called a Mint Sling), but its domination was usurped by the Sherry Cobbler which emerged during the 1830s and, by the end of that decade, dominated.

Cocktail historian David Wondrich unearthed the earliest known reference to a Cobbler in the 1838 diary of Katherine Jane Ellice, the wife of a Canadian government official who discovered the cocktail while touring upstate New York resorts, describing it as "delicious and easy of composition."

The popularity of the Sherry Cobbler was not limited to the USA, and it is this cocktail that drove "American bars" to open in cities around the world throughout the second half of the 1800s. The Sherry Cobbler is also frequently referenced in literature and cartoons during the period, from  Charles Dickens' 1843–44 The Life and Adventures of Martin Chuzzlewit and Jules Verne's 1865 From the Earth to the Moon (De la Terre à la Lune), to a 1847 illustration in the satirical Punch magazine by artist John Leech.

The Sherry Cobbler also notably appears in the first known book specifically aimed at bartenders, Jerry Thomas' 1862 The Bar-Tender's Guide. "Like the julep, this delicious potation is an American invention, although it is now a favorite in all climates." As Jerry Thomas says, "We give an illustration showing how a cobbler should look when made to suit an epicure." As with other illustrations from the time, this depicts the cocktail served with two straws.

Although the oldest known drinking straw, found in a Sumerian tomb, dates from 3,000 BC, the first straws used to lessen ice contact with the sensitive teeth while drinking Sherry Cobblers were just that, 'straw', usually rye, or even hollow pasta (macaroni or vermicelli). The hand-rolled paper straw wasn't patented until the 3rd January 1888, and as David Wondrich says in his Imbibe Revised Edition, "the Sherry Cobbler was the killer app that brought it into common use." That and the poor dentistry of the time!

The use of ice in drinks was little older than that of the straw, first widely used in the 1820s, a mere decade before the Sherry Cobbler emerged, and it is theorized that the Cobbler is named after the small cobbles of ice it was chilled with, in an age before the advent of machines producing finer crushed or even snowy ice.

Emperor Napoléon III's 1867 Exposition Universelle in Paris attracted seven million people to experience exhibits from countries world over. Along with machinery projecting engineering prowess, the American stand featured a bar serving American cocktails such as the Mint Julep, Brand Smash and, most popular, the fashionable Sherry Cobbler. The exhibition ran for seven months, with the bar purportedly going through five hundred bottles of sherry a day.

The Sherry Cobbler's popularity continued to the end of the 1800s and beyond, with Harry Johnson writing in his 1888 New and Improved Bartender's Manual, "This drink is without doubt the most popular beverage in this country, with ladies as well as with gentlemen. It is a very refreshing drink for old and young."

Sadly, like so many great things in American cocktail culture, Cobblers never really emerged from Prohibition as a popular category.

name: Submarine Kiss
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
POUR liqueur into base of chilled glass.
SHAKE other ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
Carefully FINE STRAIN into the liqueur-primed glass so contents of shaker float over liqueur.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Looks stunning and gives the drinker the option to sip or swirl to mix the sweet liqueur base into the body of the drink.
history: This cocktail is mentioned in O. O. McIntyre's New-York-Day-By-Day column in The Washington Herald on the 24th November 1915, "Sailors who come to Broadway for a touch of high life have invented a new drink called the Submarine Kiss." "The liquid combination is a milky white above and purple below and the submarine effect is secured after drinking about three."

name: Palomino Flor
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with celery stalk.
review: Celery imparts subtle vegetal notes in this unusual aperitivo cocktail.
history: Jody Monteith at The Liquorists, Manchester, England.

name: The Hee Bee Jee Bees
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A Margarita with a touch of honey vodka and agave enriched orange liqueur.
history: Created in 2010 by yours truly (Simon D) at The Cabinet Room, London, England and named after Julio Bermejo & Jacob Briars. Julio is a tequila ambassador and Jacob was promoting 42 Below honey vodka.

name: Bernice
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf pegged to rim or float the tip of mint sprig.
review: Best described as "A vodka daiquiri with a hint of herbal peppermint courtesy of Galliano."
history: Created by "C. S. Berner", this cocktail first appears as the winning cocktail at a 1950 bartender's competition in The U.K.B.G. Guide to Drinks 1st edition published 1953 [and not in Ted Saucier's 1951 Bottoms Up! as we were previously led to believe. Having checked, I can't see it in Saucier's book.]

name: Angel's Draft
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf floated on the cocktail or pegged/stuck with spot of honey to the rim.
review: A refreshing, minty, light cocktail herbal cocktail.
history: Adapted from the Bacardí Legacy 2010 winning cocktail, created in 2009 by Matthew Dakers at The Hoxton Pony in Shoreditch, London, England.

name: Aperol Spritz / Aperitivo Spritz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a WINE GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into chilled glass.
FILL glass with ice.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review:
Slightly sweet, herbal, refreshing and quaffable.

The good folk at Aperol, the aperitivo liqueur brand most identified with the Spritz, recommend equal parts prosecco and liqueur topped with a splash of soda. However, we've stuck with our preferred 3:2:1 ratio, which is a little lighter on aperitivo liqueur and noticeably fizzier. Hence, along with specifying how you want your steak done and preferred Dry Martini ratio, perhaps also stipulate your Spritz ratio.
history: Spritz cocktail history (and other recipes)

name: Daiquiri No. 1 (countdown 3:2:1 formula)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Made to the easy-to-remember 3:2:1 Daiquiri "countdown" formula (3 parts rum, 2 parts lime & 1 part sugar), this balanced and limey daiquiri suits a more characterful light rum.
history:
I'm indebted to Roger Vilalta who in 2010 at Banker's Bar in Barcelona, Spain made yours truly an excellent daiquiri using this formula.

See: Daiquiri Cocktail history

name: Gin Twist
instructions: POUR ingredients into pre-warmed glass and STIR.
review: Gin botanicals flavour this delicate, citrusy, warming drink.
history:
This classic English cocktail dates back to at least the early 1800s and was so popular at the time that it is frequently referred to in novels and periodicals from the time. In Sir Walter Scott's 1823 novel St Ronan's Well, the character Captain MacTurk says, "Sir Binco, I will beg the favour of your company to the smoking-room, where we may have a cigar and a glass of gin-twist; and we will consider how the honour of the company must be supported and upholden upon the present conjuncture."

William Makepeace Thackeray mentions the Gin Twist in his 1869 Miscellanies: The Book of Snobs. Sketches and Travels in London. "About eleven men in white neckcloths drop in from dinner-parties, and show their lacquered boots and shirt-studs with a little complacency - and at midnight, after the theatres, the young rakes and viveurs come swaggering in, and call loudly for Gin-Twist."

name: Zubrowka Cocktail
instructions: POUR absinthe into ice-filled glass, top with water and stand to one side
Separately STIR other ingredients with ice
Dump contents of glass and strain stirred drink into the absinthe washed glass.
review: The grassy notes of Zubrówka and delicate cinnamon shine through in this well-balanced and well-integrated delicately spicy vintage cocktail.
history: Adapted from W. J. Tarling's (Bill Tarling) 1937 Café Royal Cocktail Book Coronation Edition in which the invention of this drink is credited to S. T. Yakimovitch.

name: Tres Amigos Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Actually this is a Navy rum influenced Margarita made to Daiquiri proportions, but whatever it's called, it's damn good.
history: Adapted from Craig Toone's (8:2:1) interpretation of a cocktail that was doing the rounds in Manchester in 2003, originally under the name 'Triple Rum Daiquiri'.

name: Swedish Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A Swedish punch influenced Margarita. Tangy and oily.
history:
Created in 2010 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England. 
See: Margarita cocktail history

name: Strawberry on Acid
instructions: MUDDLE strawberries in base of shaker
Add next 4 ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with champagne.
review: Rich fruity strawberry, balanced and made interesting by vodka, balsamic vinegar and black pepper tingle.
history: Adapted from a cocktail discovered in 2010 at Bordeaux Quay in Bristol, England.

name: Francophile Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: A simple variation on the vodka-based French Martini adding gin and vermouth to help justify its "Martini" name.
history: Created in June 2011 by yours truly at The Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: French Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: A simple riff on the French Martini, swapping the usual vodka for the more French cognac. Fruity and easy drinking.
history: Created in June 2011 by yours truly at The Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Millennium Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist and dust with grated nutmeg.
review: Somewhat reminiscent of a cognac-laced Hawaiian pizza. Yum!
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Dale DeGroff, New York City, USA.

name: Tequila Basil Lemonade
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda and gently stir.
review: Just as it says on the tin, tequila charged with lemonade and flavoured with basil.
history: Created in 2011 by Chris Stave.

name: Beuser and Angus Special
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
FLOAT half of orange flower water.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice or wedge.
review: A floral, aromatic and herbal Chartreuse flip.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2007 by Gonçalo de Sousa Monteiro at Victoria Bar in Berlin, Germany. Gonçalo was inspired to make this cocktail for two fellow 'Travelling Mixologists', Bastian Heuser and Angus Winchester.

name: Hi Ladies!
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: This tasty cocktail is basically a Vodka Mojito served straight-up.
history: From Ted Saucier's 1951 book, Bottoms Up!.

name: Abbey
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A dry, orangey, herbal, gin-laced aperitivo, closely related to the better known Bronx.
history:
Adapted from a recipe first published in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book and repeated in William James "Billy" Tarling's 1937 Café Royal Bar Book.

Although one of the earliest cocktails on our database (the third), the Abbey was brought to our attention for a long overdue update by by J. E. Clapham of Clapham Cocktails, who recommends making it with Cocchi Americano as a substitute for the now sadly discontinued Kina Lillet used in the 1930 recipe.

name: Oh Cecilie!
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: A tasty Danish riff on the Negroni.
history: Created in 2010 by Carl Wrangel at the Oak Room, Copenhagen, Denmark and named after his girlfriend.

name: Oaxaca Old-Fashioned
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR tequila with three ice cubes in a glass.
Add other ingredients and STIR with more ice.
Top up ice and STIR some more.
EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail, then use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist (flamed).
review:
An agave-based old-fashioned with a wonderful hit of smokiness from the mezcal.

Like many cocktails, Philip's original recipe (of 45 ml Reposado tequila, 15 ml Mezcal, 7.5 ml Agave syrup and 1 dash Angostura bitters) has morphed with chocolate bitters now favoured by many in place of Angostura (as per the recipe above). If using a "sweeter" style glycerine-based (rather than alcohol-based) chocolate bitters, such as Fee Brothers, or you have a bone-dry palate, then consider reducing the agave syrup to 5ml.
history:
Created 2007 by Philip Ward at Death & Co., New York City, USA.
See: Old-Fashioned cocktail history

name: Rhine Wine Cobbler
instructions: Select and pre-chill a GOBLET GLASS.
MUDDLE grapes and pineapple in base of shaker.
FINE STRAIN (without shaking) into glass filled with crushed ice.
POUR other ingredients into glass.
COBBLE (churn/stir) with barspoon to thoroughly mix.
garnish: Garnish with grape, orange slice and pineapple wedge.
review: Light, fruity and very refreshing. A fruity alternative to a spritz.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Johnson's 1882 New And Improved Bartender's Manual.

name: Sourise
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
DRY SHAKE all ingredients (without ice) to emulsify.
SHAKE again with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with raspberries.
review: Almond, cognac and raspberry harmoniously sit together to flavour this sour-style cocktail.
history: Created in 2010 by David Wondrich and named 'Sourise' after the French for 'smile'.

name: Corn 'n' Oil
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
(Optionally: FLOAT dark rum on cocktail's surface.)
garnish: Garnish with a lime wedge.
review:
Rum, slightly sweetened and flavoured with the lime and clove flavours of falernum.

There is much debate over the Corn 'N' Oil, particularly the type of rum used, or even if a proportion of the base spirit should be brandy. The style of dark "black strap" rum is now often floated to give this cocktail an oil slick-like dark top, but this is not traditional in Barbados; this cocktails' home, but it does aid its appearance.

To be authentic to its origin, you should use a Bajan rum and forego the oil slick. There does seem to be a consensus that whatever rum or combination of rums (and brandy) are used, the base spirit is usually a 60ml (2oz) measure mixed with 15ml (½oz) falernum.  Once you decide on the base spirit, you have a choice of whether to add "red bitters" (aromatic bitters such as Angostura) or "green bitters" (absinthe) and how many dashes. Finally, you have a choice between cubed or crushed ice.

As with all cocktails, I advise making your Corn 'N' Oils to suit your tastes or your guests'.
history:
The Corn 'n' Oil is a traditional Barbadian planters' drink which, over the course of the twentieth century, morphed into the cocktail we know today.

The 'Corn and Oil' name is said to have derived from the Book of Deuteronomy, the fifth book of the Christian Old Testament. Chapters 1–30 are of sermons delivered to the Israelites by Moses on the plains of Moab, shortly before they enter the Promised Land. The salient verse being, "That I will give you the rain of your land in his due season, the first rain and the latter rain, that thou mayest gather in thy corn, and thy wine, and thine oil."

This cocktail's notoriety was helped by its appearing on the back label of John D. Taylor's Velvet Falernum, the best-selling brand of falernum:

name: El Presidente (Sloppy Joe's)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with skewered maraschino cherry.
review: A splash of lime adds zesty citrus balance to this delicate rose-pink tinted rum and vermouth cocktail with a touch of pomegranate contributing fruity sweetness.
history:
El Presidente is one of the best-known classic Cuban cocktails to enter the world's drinking lexicon and during Cuba's 1930s heyday, Sloppy Joe's was one of Havana's most famous bars.

In his 1939 The Gentleman's Companion, Charles H. Baker says (page 99) "Sloppy Joe's own Special is merely the Presidente with the juice of a small lime added, and the twist of lime peel."

name: Satan's Whiskers (Enroulée)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Enroulée is the French word for 'curled' and this version of the classic Satan's Whiskers is "curled" by the use of mandarine liqueur in place of the Grand Marnier found in the 'straight' version.
history: Created in 2011 by Gary "gaz" Regan, New York, USA.

name: Satan's Whiskers (Marcelled)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: This riff on the Satan's Whiskers (in turn a Bronx variation) calls for it not to be 'curled' (as is the case when you use orange curaçao), but to be "marcelled", apparently a deep soft wave in one's hair (for those that still have some) created with curling tongs and popular in the 1920s and 1930s.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2011 by William "Chili Bill" Eichinger at Finnegan's Wake in San Francisco, USA, and courtesy of Gaz Regan's Ardent Spirits.

name: Satan's Whiskers (Curled)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: This variation on the 'Straight' Satan's Whiskers is made curly by the use of orange curaçao liqueur. It seems strangely counter-intuitive as you'd think the slightly richer Grand Marnier would be what made it curly.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book. (A variation on the classic Bronx.)

name: Applejack Rabbit
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Apple brandy, orange and tart lemon enriched with maple syrup. Strike the perfect balance and this is a delicious combination. This cocktail's name alone suggests making it with American applejack brandy rather than French calvados, and when made with 45ml (1½oz) straight bottled-in-bond 50% alc./vol. applejack in place of the calvados, it's arguably a better cocktail.
history: The Apple Jack Rabbit appears in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book and in David A. Embury's 1948 The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks where he tells us "This drink is also sometimes, for no reason at all, called the Applejack Dynamite. The same cocktail made with a gin base plus a dash of Angostura is called the Old Vermont."

name: Vodka Daisy
instructions: Select and pre-chill a GOBLET GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
CHURN (stir) using a bar spoon.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: Dependent on your raspberry syrup (homemade is best) you'll either have a beautifully fruity and refreshing drink, or one that's a tad on the sweet side. Adjust to taste.

name: Love me flip
instructions: BEAT egg yolk with sugar in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: The grated tonka bean adds a vanilla aroma to the surface of this drink.
history: Created in 2010 by Erik Lorincz at The Connaught Hotel, London, England.

name: Fino Flip
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of 3 drops of Angostura aromatic bitters.
review: Salty tang of fino balanced and given extra richness by the egg.

name: The Business
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: As the name suggests, The Business is a riff on a classic Bee's Knees with lime juice. It's snappier with lime instead of lemon juice.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created by Sasha Petraske at his famous Milk & Honey bar in Manhattan, New York City, USA.

The Business features in Georgette Moger-Petraske's 2016 Sasha Petraske Regarding Cocktails.

name: Dutch Count Negroni / Red Light Negron
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
STIR.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Genever's flavoursome character adds malty notes to an otherwise traditional Negroni.
history:
Discovered in 2011 at Vesper Bar in Amsterdam, Netherlands, under the name Dutch Count Negroni. Due to its origins in Amsterdam, a city famous for its red light district, this cocktail is now better known as the Red Light Negroni.

See: Negroni cocktail history.

name: Brandy Crusta
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
Prepare the glass with citrus peel and sugar CRUSTA by first finding a whole lemon that fits snugly inside the top of your glass with its equator in contact with the rim of the glass.
Cut off both ends of the fruit to leave a central slice a little more than 25ml (1 inch) thick.
Carefully remove the pulp from this central slice to leave a ring of skin and push this into the top of the glass.
Moisten the outside rim of the glass and exposed fruit with citrus juice and dip in white caster sugar to heavily frost the edge of both peel and glass.

Leave for a couple of hours to form a hard crust that helps secure the peel. (The ring of citrus peel should become a waterproof extension to the top of the glass.) Place in a freezer to chill the glass ready for the cocktail.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into pre-prepared glass.
review: This finely balanced, brandy-based classic zings with fresh zesty lemon and layers of complexity added by orange curaçao, maraschino liqueur and aromatic bitters. Balanced to be a smidgen on the sour side to allow for minimal additional sweetness introduced by a crusted rim.
history:
Created in the 1850s by Joseph Santini, an Italian from Trieste, in New Orleans, Louisiana, either at the City Exchange in the French Quarter, or at his Jewel of the South saloon on Gravier Street in the American Quarter, which he opened in 1855. The name refers to the thick crust of sugar around the rim.

Regarded by many as the forerunner to the Sidecar
 and, by extension, the Margarita, crusted sugar rim and all. The Brandy Crusta is a veritable member of cocktail royalty, so much so that it proudly takes its place as the fourth drink to be illustrated in the world's first cocktail book, Jerry Thomas' 1862 Bar-Tender's Guide, where, under entry for the Dry Punch, Santini is wrongly credited as being of Spanish origins and spelt "Santina".

name: Brandy Fix
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: This wonderful classic is slightly on the tart side of well-balanced.

name: Holland House (Craddock's London dry gin-based)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Without the optional addition of sugar syrup, this cocktail is very dry. Even with sugar, this cocktail is on the dry side and makes for a great aperitivo.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Dry Martini (3:1 ratio)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered olive or lemon zest twist.
review: Three to one may be unfair odds in a fight, but vermouth shines in this stirred off-dry Martini. Through experimentation, I've found that 3:1 Martinis are better stirred rather than shaken. (Conversely, 15:1 Martinis are better shaken rather than stirred.)
history: There is considerable debate around the origin of the Martini and the best way to make this cocktail.

name: Jenever Punch
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP up with soda.
review: A more complex riff of a Collins with malty genever and brown sugar flavours considerably adding to the drink.

name: Christmas Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Rosemary sprig spiked with dried cranberries, or float a dehydrated orange slice.
review: The Cosmopolitan meets the Margarita in this gloriously pink cocktail.
history:
A Christmas cocktail created in October 2011 by yours truly and then revisited in November 2020.

Christmas Margarita (2011 recipe)
Glass: Coupe
Garnish: Orange Zest twist
Method: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
45ml / 1½oz Reposado tequila
45ml / 1½oz Cranberry juice
15ml / ½oz Grand Marnier Cordon Rouge
15ml / ½oz Lime juice (freshly squeezed)

name: Brandy Flip
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: A serious alternative to advocaat for those without raw egg inhibitions.
history: A forgotten classic.

name: Brandy Milk Punch
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: This traditional New Orleans hangover cure beats your bog-standard vanilla milkshake. (Don't dry shake a Milk Punch as this produces something redolent of an Egg Nog.)
history: A classic which was popular during America's Prohibition era and dates back to colonial times. It is still widely served in New Orleans where it is commonly consumed with Sunday brunch.

name: Brandy Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist (discarded) and garnish with a skewered lemon slice wheel and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry sail.
review: After the Whisky Sour, this is the most requested sour. Try it and you'll see why - but don't omit the egg white.

name: Lychee Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered lychee.
review: As the name promises, lychee fruitiness shines throughout this dry (fruity but not sweet) Martini.
history: Recipe by yours truly in May 2025.

name: Green Goddess
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with small mint leaf floated on surface.
review: This deep olive-green, minty, bittersweet herbal cocktail is perhaps the ultimate digestivo. If you're stuffed/flagging after an overly indulgent, multi-course meal, then this might well be your salvation.
history: A riff on the classic Devil's cocktail, created in January 2026 by yours truly.

name: Breakfast Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR marmalade with gin in base of shaker until it dissolves.
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with a slice of toast on the rim.
review: The success or failure of this tangy drink is partly reliant on the quality of marmalade used.  For ease of use, choose "fine cut" or even "no peel"/"shredless" orange marmalade.
history:
This neo-Martini was created in 1996 by bartender and long-term President of the United Kingdom Bartender's Guild, Salvatore Calabrese.

Being of proud Italian descent, Salvatore usually has little more than a swift espresso for breakfast. However, one morning, Sue, his English wife, insisted he sit down for breakfast and served him toast and marmalade. Salvatore came up with the idea for his Breakfast Martini while enjoying the tangy preserve-covered toast and took the jar to work with him. Later that day, at London's Library Bar in the Lanesborough Hotel, he perfected his signature cocktail.

Coincidentally, Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book includes a recipe for a "Marmalade Cocktail" that's very similar to Salvatore's Breakfast Martini. However, Salvatore says that the inspiration for his drink was the hearty English breakfast and not the classic English bartending book.

name: Sharman-Cox Daiquiri
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and fine strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE (without ice) and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: A foam-topped blue riff on two classic cocktails which honours two men named Cox as well as our friend Hannah Sharman-Cox.
history:
Adapted from a drink created in 2013 at the Rum Kitchen, Notting Hill, London. The name 'Cox' is a common theme to this drink:

1. It is inspired by the Perfect Lady, a classic cocktail created in 1936 by Sidney Cox, a bartender at the Grosvenor House, London for The British Empire Cocktail Competition where it took the 1st Prize.

2. The original Daiquiri, said to be created in 1898 by Jennings Cox, an American mining engineer working at a tin mine near the Cuban town of Daiquiri.

3. Most importantly, this drink was created and named to honour the fabulous Hannah Sharman-Cox of London Cocktail Week fame.

name: Kopstoot
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SHOT AND BEER GLASS.
POUR genever into chilled shot glass and beer into beer glass.
To enjoy, sip ice-cold genever then follow with a sip of beer. And repeat.
review: In the worthy pursuit of responsible drinking I must stress that when enjoying a Kopstoot you should repeatedly take a sip from each glass – savour the combination rather than shooting the genever in one gulp.
history:
The traditional Dutch way to drink genever, Kopstoot (pronounced 'Cop-Stoat') translates as 'headbutt'.

The genever is served straight from a fridge or freezer in a small tulip-shaped glass and poured at the bar to fill the glass to the brim. The glass is filled to the extent that it can't be moved without causing spillage, and tradition dictates that the drinker bends down and takes the first sip of genever without touching the glass.

name: Death Flip
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: With a whole egg, a shot of tequila, bittersweet liqueur and Chartreuse, this is both a Death Flip and perhaps your last meal, for a while at least. Challenging herbal and bitter complexity yet with endearing sweet creaminess.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2010 by Chris "Hasselhoff" Hysted Hysted-Adams at the Black Pearl in Melbourne, Australia, where it was an immediate hit on the menu, despite its being listed without ingredients and the warning, "You don't wanna meet this cocktail in a dark alley."

name: Betsy Ross
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Deep burgundy red and boozy. Based on the classic combination of port and brandy, this is a cocktail with which to end a night. (Benefits from some dilution, so stir well.)
history: This combination of brandy, port and curaçao is also known as the "Imperial": indeed, it first appears under that name in William "Cocktail Bill" Boothby's posthumously published 1934 World's Drinks And How To Mix Them.

name: Bronx
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review:
A fairly dry, complex cocktail. Generous sweet vermouth and orange juice make the Bronx less bitter and fruitier than many of its era, but still challenging to delicate modern palates. A classic that needs a trio of something extras to give it zing;
1. Freshly squeezed orange juice
2. Orange bitters
3. Orange zest twist
It is often claimed that the Bronx is "the first cocktail to use fruit juice" other than citrus, but this is just another muddy 'fact' in the Bronx's history.
history:
