One of the classic cocktails named after New York's five boroughs, probably more directly after the then newly opened Bronx Zoo. There are numerous variations on the recipe, but the ingredients classically comprise dry gin, dry and sweet vermouth, and orange juice.

The Bronx Zoological Park and neighbouring Gardens opened on the 8th of November 1899, and it is presumed a bartender created this eponymous cocktail after being inspired by his visit to the newly opened zoo. The first known reference appeared on the 15th of February 1901 in the The Virginia Enterprise newspaper, which, in a list of Bartender's Association committee members, says, "J. E. O'Connor of the Waldorf-Astoria, inventor of the "Bronx cocktail.""

name: French Maid
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
MUDDLE cucumber in base of shaker.
Add next 5 ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with ginger beer.
garnish: Garnish with cucumber slice speared with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: As Jim Meehan said when he gave us this recipe, "This hybrid of Audrey Saunders Gin Gin Mule substitutes cognac for gin and adds falernum and cucumber. Milk & Honey barman Sam Ross has a similar drink made with Bourbon dubbed the Kentucky Maid: consider this her spicy French sister."
history: Created in autumn 2007 by Jim Meehan at PDT in New York City for an art gallery opening hosted by John Pellaton of Hine Cognac.

name: Green Park
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE basil in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with a basil leaf.
review: Lime green in colour under a frothy head, lemon fresh and botanical with gin, basil and a hint of celery.
history: Created in 2011 by Erik Lorincz white head bartender at The American Bar, The Savoy Hotel, London, England.

name: The Salty Bird
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with dehydrated pineapple.
review: Salty Bird hardly suggests a sweet fruity drink but perfectly befits this bittersweet rum-laced pineapple sipper.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Lauren Schell at The Everleigh in Melbourne, Australia and promoted by Campari in 2013.

name: Bijou Cocktail (D's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review:
It is not uncommon to see Bijou recipes with equal parts gin, Chartreuse and rosso vermouth, as called for in Harry Johnson's Bartender's Manual. Indeed, that's the norm. Equal parts works, but it makes for a cocktail strong enough to drop a rhinoceros with Chartreuse overly dominant (and I love Chartreuse).

The word 'bijou' is French for 'jewel', and this cocktail's name is said to have been inspired by the jewel-like colours of the ingredients: diamond for gin, ruby for rosso vermouth and emerald for Chartreuse Verte.
history: A cocktail named Bijou first appeared in C. F. Lawlor's 1895 book The Mixicologist as equal parts gin, Grand Marnier and sweet vermouth. This recipe is adapted from Harry Johnson's 1900 Bartender's Manual, which calls for "1/3 wine glass Chartreuse (green); 1/3 wine glass vermouth (Italian); 1/3 wine glass of Plymouth gin; 1 dash orange bitters".

name: Emerson
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: The botanical notes of old tom with herbal vermouth, zesty citrus and sweet maraschino. Reminiscent of a Last Word without Chartreuse.
history: Recipe adapted from Jacques Straub's 1913 Straub's Manual Of Mixed Drinks.

name: Gin Basil Smash
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
MUDDLE basil sprigs (no need to pluck leaves from stem) in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with basil sprig bouquet.
review: Jörg, the creator of this cocktail, says, "Better too much than not enough basil in this drink". He also specifies that the basil is muddled rather than just broken by the action of shaking as this produces a greener drink. He adds "Keep in mind:  5 cl Gin is not enough; 6 cl Gin is the recipe; 7 cl Gin is LOVE!"
history: Created in the summer of 2008 by Jörg Meyer at Le Lion • Bar de Paris, Hamburg, Germany and originally named Gin Pesto. Jörg blogged about his new creation on the 10th of July 2008 and from that point, it started to quickly grow in popularity to become internationally known, also becoming better known as the Gin Basil Smash.

name: Hocus Pocus
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Bittersweet with gin's botanical complexity and orange zest freshness.
history: Adapted from a drink discovered in 2010 at Forty Four, New York City, USA. Hocus Pocus by the Dutch rock band Focus was a chart-topper both sides of the Atlantic in 1971. Famous for its yodelling, whistling, accordion, and flute, this is a tune that has to heard to be believed. We're fans!

name: The Lone Ranger
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Very slightly sweet tequila and lemon, lengthened and dried with brut sparkling wine.
history: Created in 2012 by Jeffrey Morgenthaler, Oregon, USA.

name: Mezzo E Mezzo
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Slightly on the bitter side of bittersweet, this classic aperitivo is light and palate cleansing. Best made with equal parts Nardini Rosso, Nardini Rabarbaro and soda.
history: Originally and properly made with equal parts Nardini Rosso, Nardini Rabarbaro and soda water, this drink originates from the famous Grapperia Nardini on the Ponte Vecchio where Nardini Rosso was first served with soda water in the 1920s. In the 1970s a drinker tried Nardini Rosso with a dash of Nardini’s rhubarb liqueur. The combination became increasingly popular, and as its popularity grew, so the dash became larger until the mixture reached equal parts Nardini Rosso and Rabarbaro.

name: Monet's Moment
ingredients: 30 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: STIR all ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass (preferably with one large hand-cracked cube)
review: Cognac and aromatised wine lifted with a splash of absinthe. Complex and refreshing.
history: Created in 2013 by Erik Lorincz, then Head Bartender at The American Bar, The Savoy Hotel, London. Erik was inspired after seeing a work by Claude Monet that hung in the hotel's Monet Suite, the room where, in 1901, the French impressionist originally sketched the pastel work entitled Waterloo Bridge.

name: My Sherry Amore
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: A bittersweet, salmon-pink, light, delicate balance of gin, herbal complexity, grapefruit and dry sherry.
history: Created in 2011 by Nick Van Tiel and named after the 1969 soul classic by Motown singer-songwriter Stevie Wonder. Originally titled Oh My Marcia, the song was originally about Wonder's girlfriend whilst he was at the Michigan School for the Blind in Lansing, Michigan. After they broke up, the lyrics and title were changed to 'My Cherie Amour'.

name: Paloma (Simple)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with grapefruit wedge.
review: Long, fruity and refreshing.
history: Paloma is Spanish for 'dove', and this well-known cocktail in Mexico is rumoured to have been created by the legendary Don Javier Delgado Corona, owner/bartender of La Capilla (The Chapel) in Tequila, Mexico. Don Javier notably stirred his drinks with a huge knife.

name: Rhubarb Lucca Negroni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with rhubarb as stirrer.
review: A rhubarb-influenced rift on the Negroni, which takes the classic from an aperitivo to a digestivo.
history:
Adapted from a cocktail created in 2011 by Jonathan Abarbanel at Quo Vadis, London, England. Jonathan originally used Rhubarb-infused London dry gin.
See: Negroni cocktail history

name: Rocky Left Bank
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: A slightly sweet, hopefully crowd-pleasing, combo of gin, elderflower and white wine.
history: Created in 2006 by yours truly at The Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Gin Sangaree (Sangre)
instructions: SHAKE gin and sugar with ice and strain into chilled glass
Lastly POUR the port wine which will sink through drink and mix, leaving a thin clear layer at the top.
review: Either drink through the layers or stir before consuming. Fabulously old-school in style, this sweetened port and old tom gin drink resembles chilled mulled wine.
history: The Sangaree takes its name from the Spanish word for blood, Sangre. The drink's origins date back to the early 1700s and it first appears in writing in a 1736 issue of the British Gentleman's Magazine, "... a punch seller in the Strand had devised a new punch made of strong Madeira wine and called Sangre".

name: Voyager Vodka Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of frozen green grape on stick (place on stick then freeze).
review: Why dampen your Martini with just vermouth when the Japanese and the Peruvians have so much more to offer?
history:
Created in 2013 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

See Martini cocktail and its evolution

name: Waldorf Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE celery in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with a walnut, apple wedge and chunk of blue cheese.
review: Walnut, celery and apple are essential elements to both a Waldorf Salad and this tasty Daiquiri.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2010 by Julian de Feral at Lutyens Bar in London, England.

name: Reality Check
instructions: MUDDLE raspberries in base of large shaker tin
Add ice and other ingredients then THROW (strain from a height into a second strainer)
Repeat four times and then strain into ice-filled glass.
review: Raspberry fruit, herbal complexity and dry hoppy beer with a splash of zesty lime. Fabulously refreshing.
history: Created in 2013 by Simone Caporale at the Artesian Bar, Langham Hotel, London, England. One of five finalists in the D Beer-tail Competition held 9th August 2013 at Simon D's Cabinet Room in London.

name: Tomatini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE tomatoes in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with grind of black pepper and garnish with a cherry tomato cut to sit on glass rim.
review: A sweet and sour sipper that has to be tried. Delicate and yet assertive.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2011 by Jimmy Barrat at La Petite Maison Dubai (LPM). This was also the signature cocktail Jimmy made for the 2012 World Class Bartenders' competition.

name: Elle Loves
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with rose petals floated on surface.
review: Fruity, very slightly sweet and suitably pink in colour. Best made with a light white rum infused with a vanilla pod, rather than a commercially produced vanilla-flavoured rum.
history: Created 30th January 2014 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room as a Valentine's Day cocktail for Elle Magazine.

name: Ramos Chocolate Fizz cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
SHAKE first 6 ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine slowly poured into glass from a height to create and raise thick foamy head.
garnish: Garnish with chocolate truffle on rim.
review: Creamy chocolate vanilla fudge with faint orange and a hint of gin.
history: Created 4th February 2014 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Red Gemma
instructions: STIR first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass
TOP with sparkling wine and briefly stir.
review: Red berry flavours with light red wine tannins, liqueur sweetness and botanical depth courtesy of gin, all refreshed with fizz. Berry fizz!
history: Created 5th February 2014 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England and named after Gemma Monaghan, who incidentally is a Mancunian who supports Liverpool FC.

name: Manky Panky
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Red berries, red wine, and ruby orange are subtly fortified with gin and the underlying herbal notes of fernet liqueur.
history: Created 5th February 2014 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England. A fruity riff on Ada 'Coley' Coleman's classic Hanky-Panky.

name: Sangre Dulce Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Mezcal, cinnamon and bittersweet Fernet are big-hitting flavours which combine brilliantly in this characterful yet harmonious cocktail.
history: Created in 2013 by Regina Butler at Blackbird Bar in San Francisco, USA. Regina's original recipe calls for Fernet Vallet, a Mexican amaro that's sadly not available in the UK.

name: Blood Orange Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with blood orange slice.
review: Notes of tequila, orangey triple sec, bittersweet liqueur, blood orange and lime juice all sit in perfect kilter in this tasty riff on the classic Margarita.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2000 by Ben Davidson in Sydney, Australia.
Margarita cocktail history.

name: Fruit Cup Spritz
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of half orange slice, lemon slice and mint sprig.
review: A light summery spritz with flavours reminiscent of Pimm's.
history:
Created in 2013 by yours truly for Jamie's Italian Bar.

Also see Fruit Cups and Pimm's

name: Affogato Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
BLEND all ingredients with three cubes of ice.
POUR into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with 3 coffee beans floated in a petal formation in the centre of the cocktail's foamy head.
review: Based on the eponymous classic Italian dessert, this after-dinner cocktail blends coffee and chocolate with ice-cream, appropriately fortified with a splash of grappa.
history: Created in 2013 by yours truly for Jamie Oliver's Jamie's Italian bars.

name: Berry Blast
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of mint sprig and lemon slice.
review: As the name suggests this non-alcoholic long drink is loaded with berry fruit, presented over the light tannins of a black tea base with a splash of passion fruit syrup adding flavour and a hint of balancing sweetness.
history: Adapted from a drink created at Jamie's Italian Bars.

name: Gennaro's Sidecar
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: My version of the established Jamie's Italian Gennaro's Sidecar is a tad boozier than the original, but retains the balance between sweet limoncello liqueur and tart lemon juice.
history:
Adapted from a popular drink on the menu of Jamie's Italian restaurant and bar chain.
See:  Sidecar cocktail history

name: Ginger No-jito (Temperance Ginger Mojito)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR first 2 ingredients into glass and half fill with crushed ice.
ADD mint leaves and CHURN.
Fill glass with more crushed ice.
TOP with ginger beer.
Briefly CHURN to combine.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: This Mojito may lack alcohol; indeed, it's alcohol-free -but it's refreshing, balanced, and tasty nonetheless.
history:
Adapted from a popular drink on the menu of Jamie's Italian restaurant and bar chain circa 2014. I discovered and borrowed the No-jito name from Wagamama restaurants in January 2023.
Mojito Cocktail history.

name: Jamie's Mojito
instructions: POUR first 4 ingredients into glass with lime wedges and mint
Half fill glass with crushed ice and CHURN (stir)
Fill glass with more crushed ice and TOP with Prosecco.
review: A light mojito made more complex with bianco vermouth.
history:
Adapted from a drink found on the menu of Jamie's Italian restaurant and bar chain.

Mojito cocktails, tips and history

name: Flying Frenchman (Absinthe Espresso Martini)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of star anise floated on surface.
review: Best described as an energy drink on steroids - equal parts absinthe, coffee liqueur and espresso. However, unlike most caffeine-laden energy drinks the Flying Frenchman is very tasty.
history:
Adapted from a drink created in 2014 for La Fée Parisienne by Andy Mil at the London Cocktail Club. Andy's recipe originally called for Patron XO Café and an additional 15ml of sugar syrup.
Espresso Martini history

name: The Heritage
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of star anise floated on surface.
review: Vodka fortifies and supports a well-balanced blend of orange zest, apple, lemon and star anise.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in May 2014 by Danish bartender (and winner of Diageo World Class 2012 Western Europe Final), Hasse Johansen from Apothex Bar. This cocktail was first made to celebrate the birthday of the creator of Ketel One Vodka, Carolus Nolet. If you'd like to make one and toast Carolus, his birthday is on the 15th of May.

name: Hi Life Spritz
instructions: POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda and briefly stir.
review: Borrowing its name from both BA High Life magazine and the highball glass it is served in, this refreshing long drink combines the botanical complexity of gin with honeyed elderflower liqueur balanced by white wine acidity.
history:
Created 30th May 2014 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England for British Airways' BA High Life magazine.
Spritz history

name: Cosmopolitan cocktail (Toby Cecchini's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: This 2:1:1:1 original Cosmopolitan is on the tart side of balanced with the citrus-flavoured vodka shining over the other ingredients. Indeed, Toby describes his Cosmo  as being "kind of a brutal sour, it's very very tart."
history:
The original Cosmopolitan cocktail recipe as created in 1988 by Toby Cecchini at The Odeon in New York City. Toby's recipe used the then newly launched Absolut Citron and Cointreau triple sec.
Cosmopolitan cocktail history and development

name: Surf and Turf
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of skewered caperberry.
review: Dry sherry adds a slight salty 'surf' note to this riff on the classic Turf Club Cocktail.
history:
Created 8th September 2014 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England. I've tweaked the recipe in recent years, the original:
1½ Old Tom gin
¾ Dry vermouth
½ Fino sherry
¼ Orange curacao
1 dash Aromatic bitters
½ chilled water

name: Turf Club (1895, George Kappeler)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Reminiscent of a Martini but sans vermouth, sweetened with dash of sugar and balanced by heavy use of bitters.
history: Adapted from a recipe in George J. Kappeler's 1895 book, Modern American Drinks.

name: Turf Club (1900, Harry Johnson)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Despite the maraschino liqueur, this Martini-style cocktail is dry and punchy.
history: Harry Johnson, tellingly, doesn't list the Turf cocktail in the 1882 first edition or the 1888 second edition of his Bartenders' Manual but does include it as the last drink in his revised 1900 New and Improved edition. Johnson reflects on changing tastes and fashions by calling for Plymouth gin rather than old tom gin in his turn-of-the-century recipe.

name: Turf Club (1935, Albert Stevens Crockett)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Albert Stevens Crockett's original recipe calls for two-thirds genever to one-third sweet vermouth, but this produces an overly dry cocktail so I've upped the proportion of vermouth to genever. Made with a quality oude genever, this cocktail is almost nutty and makes for an interesting aperitif.
history: Recipe adapted from Albert Stevens Crockett's 1935 The Old Waldorf-Astoria Bar Book.

name: Gin Colheita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
THROW all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: The quality and style of your madeira is crucial to the success and balance of this cocktail - don't skimp – buy a colheita.
history: Created on 20th August 2014 by yours truly to accompany 'Beef, Juniper and Pistachio Canapés' for the EBLEX Quality Standard Mark Scheme for beef and lamb. Tasty meat and a tasty cocktail.

name: Thai Green Cocktail
instructions: MUDDLE first 3 ingredients in base of shaker
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass.
review: Extracting the juice from the stems and leaves of the coriander is key to both the colour and flavour of this fresh-tasting cocktail, inspired by some of the flavours of a Thai Green Curry - only without the chilli heat.
history: Created in September 2014 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England to accompany Spiced Beef and Chilli Blinis.

name: Paddington Bear Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Tangy orange marmalade with gin and pisco rounded with bianco vermouth.
history: Created on the 28th November 2014 by yours truly to celebrate the premiere of Paddington Bear the movie. Paddington is a Peruvian bear who has a penchant for marmalade sandwiches – hence the use of both marmalade and Peruvian pisco in this Martini.

name: Champagne Snowball
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
POUR sparkling wine into chilled glass.
SHAKE other 3 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN over sparkling wine in the glass.
Express lime zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Dust with cacao powder.
review: Deliciously silky smooth, indulgent, and decadent. Rich, thick, and creamy advocaat freshened with dry sherry, zesty lime cordial, and invigorated with fizz.
history: The Snowball cocktail was huge in the 1970s and is now enjoying a well-deserved renaissance. This fizz-enhanced version is by yours truly.

name: Christmas Cheer-re
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Genever provides the yuletide spirit base, sweetened and flavoured by cherry liqueur, with chinato drying and adding wine notes while gin adds freshness and botanical complexity. Maraschino and orange bitters complete this festive tipple.
history: Created in December 2014 by yours truly and christened by Paloma.

name: Christmas Special No. 1
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Garnish with half walnut balanced on rim or rest on the ice.
review: A great after-dinner cocktail any time of year but particularly appropriate over the festive period due to its ingredients – Pedro Ximénez sherry tastes like Christmas pudding, mandarins are not just for Christmas and walnuts are best cracked whilst watching a Christmas special.
history: Created in December 2013 by yours truly and Paloma Alos at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: White Christmas
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mince pie.
review: As the name suggests, this is a Christmassy riff on a White Russian cocktail– the festive feel courtesy of mincemeat and gingerbread syrup.
history: Created in December 2014 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Randy Old-fashioned
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered maraschino cherries.
review: Port and brandy (rhymes 'Randy') are traditionally consumed at Christmas and used to be a popular British pub combo. I've used maple syrup in place of brown sugar syrup to lightly sweeten this festive after-dinner combo. The Sazerac was the inspiration for the choice of bitters and use of absinthe.
history:
Created December 2014 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.
Old-Fashioned cocktail history and recipes

name: Saison l'Hiver
instructions: MUDDLE clove in base of shaker
Add next 3 ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with champagne.
review: The rich wintery flavours of sloe gin and honey are balanced by lemon juice with clove providing seasonal spice and rosé champagne lifting and refreshing all.
history: Created in September 2014 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Saint-Marc Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime slice or wedge on rim.
review: It's a Daiquiri with monastic Bénédictine and decadent absinthe influences. Perhaps it's the saffron in the Bénédictine or the anise in the absinthe-but the result is truly a spiritual experience. It's simply divine.
history: Adapted from a drink discovered in 2014 at Bastille Cafe & Bar in Seattle, Washington, USA.

name: Absinthe Makes The Heart Grow Fonder
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail, then use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Absinthe and gin delicately lead this subtle cherry-flavoured cocktail. Bitter liqueur adds more colour than flavour, while pink grapefruit freshens.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created by Scott Diaz in Seattle, Washington.

The phrase "absence makes the heart grow fonder" is first attributed to English poet Ernest Dowson (1867-1900) as part of a February 1899 letter to Arthur Moore. However, it was another poet, Thomas Haynes Bayly, who popularised the phrase in his poem Isle of Beauty published in his two-volume 1844 Songs, Ballads, and Other Poems.

name: Fairy Cream
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: Absinthe sits majestically with cream and chocolate in this indulgent after-dinner cocktail.
history: Created in 2010 by bartending legend Dick Bradsell in London, England.

name: White Negroni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
STIR.
Express pink grapefruit zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare pink grapefruit zest for garnish.
review: Wayne Collins, this cocktail's creator, originally used equal parts as is usual for a classic Negroni, and after trying various other formulations, I've found this works best. Wayne also specified a grapefruit zest twist, and this is essential to the recipe's success. However, be warned, this is a cocktail for aficionados of bittersweet aperitifs and gentian.
history: Created in 2001 by Wayne Collins at VinExpo. Bordeaux, France using  Plymouth Gin for Nick Blacknell, then brand director of Plymouth Gin. Later, Simon Ford, who was the Plymouth Gin ambassador, introduced the cocktail to Audrey Saunders in the early days of her Pegu Club, where it became a hit on the menu.

name: Charente White Negroni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
STIR.
Express pink grapefruit zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare pink grapefruit zest for garnish.
review: A riff on the White Negroni with the brandy replacing half of the gin. Fittingly, 'Charente' references the Cognac area of France.
history: Created in January 2014 by Matthias Lataille and yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Green Tea Punch
instructions: POUR all ingredients into glass and then fill with ice.

name: Velvet Maker
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express pink grapefruit zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare pink grapefruit zest for garnish.
review: Originally made with home infused vanilla vodka, zesty grapefruit, and dry vermouth which adds balancing botanical complexity.
history: Created by yours truly and named after Velvet Maker, the filly who won the 15:05 at the wonderfully named Fairyhouse racecourse in Ireland on 31st January 2015 - the day I first made this drink.

name: Bloody Negroni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
Briefly STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with blood orange slice.
review: A classic Negroni consists of equal parts gin, Italian red bitters and sweet vermouth, garnished with an orange slice. When in season, a splash of blood orange juice adds a refreshing citrusy tang.
history:
Made by yours truly in February 2014 at the Cabinet Room, London, England. (Although I'm sure someone came up with this combo years before.)
Negroni cocktail history and recipe variations

name: Buttercup
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lemon zest (not expressed as the oils overpower the butterscotch) across rim on which to nestle a Werther's Original.
review:
When made to Lucas Bols original specification: 1 shot Bols Genever, 1 shot Bols Butterscotch liqueur and ¾ shot sweet vermouth, this drink has a great flavour but lacks balls (or perhaps I should say Bols). Hence, the above formula is heavy on the genever with equal parts butterscotch liqueur and sweet vermouth. It's even better with an oude genever rather than a jonge as this amplifies the butterscotch notes.

WARNING: Buttercup flowers are poisonous, so don't use them as a garnish. They are also poisonous when eaten by livestock, but their acrid taste and the blistering of the mouth they cause mean that animals don't tend to eat them. Interestingly, the toxins degrade when dried, so hay containing dried buttercups is safe for animals to eat.
history: My adaptation of a recipe I discovered in January 2014 at The House of Bols in Amsterdam, Netherlands.

name: Heisenberg Cocktail
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
(Most appropriately served in a science beaker.)
review: Like a suitably complex chemistry experiment, this Margarita-like cocktail has seven ingredients with mezcal adding smoky notes to tequila and a trio of liqueurs balancing lime sourness.
history: Adapted from a drink created in September 2013 at Mark's Bar, Soho, London to celebrate the last episode of Breaking Bad inspired by the show's blue crystal meth.

name: Latest Word
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: A Last Word made with genever in place of London dry gin. The distinctive flavour of genever stands up well to the other very characterful ingredients.
history: Last Word history

name: Other Word
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: Mezcal fortifies this Californian riff on the Last Word.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created by Eric Alperin at The Varnish in Los Angeles, California. Eric's original recipe, as published in his 2020 book Unvarnished, calls for:
60ml (2oz) Mezcal
30ml (1oz) Lime juice
7.5ml (¼oz) Yellow Chartreuse
7.5ml (¼oz) Maraska Maraschino Cherry Liqueur
5ml (barspoon) Agave syrup.

name: Closing Argument
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: A mezcal-based Last Word. It works! End of argument.
history: Last Word history

name: Very French Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered raspberry.
review: Brandy, pineapple juice and black raspberry liqueur. OK, so France is not known for its pineapples, but as I said, "very" not "totally" French. And, yes, I know it's not really a Martini but it's fruity, tasty and quaffable.
history: A riff on the French Martini created in January 2015 by yours truly and Matthias Lataille at the Cabinet Room, London, England. I subsequently found I'd already created an almost identical cocktail, the French Cocktail, back in 2011. However, I prefer the Very French Martini name - even though it's far from being a "Martini".

name: Lemon Tart
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with lemon or lime slice on rim.
review: This bittersweet aperitif harnesses the herbal bitterness of gentian root with gin's botanical notes.

name: English Marmalade (a.k.a. Hawksmoor Marmalade Martini)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR 4 flat barspoons marmalade with other ingredients in base of shaker to dissolve the marmalade.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: The addition of bitter liqueur and aromatic bitters to the classic 1930 recipe from The Savoy Cocktail Book, lifts the Marmalade Cocktail to a new level of bittersweet complexity.
history: Adapted from a recipe discovered in 2013 at Hawksmoor in Spitalfields, London, England. Huw Gott, Hawksmoor's co-founder, says the cocktail went on the menu soon after the restaurant and bar opened in 2006.

name: Marvin's Last Word
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: The distinctive notes of mastiha and Chartreuse shine in this Greek riff on the classic  Last Word.
history:
Created by Christos Chouseas at 42 Bar in Athens, Greece – a bar where The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy and the work of Douglas Adams influences many of the drink names.

In The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy book, the number 42 is the "answer to the ultimate question of life, the universe, and everything", while 'Marvin' is a paranoid, depressive android.

See also Last Word history

name: La Poire des Bénédictines
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pear wedge speared with cube of Roquefort cheese.
review: Pear and cognac are a match made in heaven. Bénédictine adds honeyed sweetness and herbal complexity to this after-dinner cocktail.
history: Adapted from a drink promoted by Grey Goose in 2013 and originally using their La Poire vodka.

name: Club Cocktail (Whitfield's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: Pineapple juice adds fruity tropical freshness and a foamy head to this cocktail. Fortified with cognac and aromatised with a splash of maraschino cherry liqueur and a couple of dashes of New Orleans bitters.
history: The Club Cocktail first appeared in W.C. Whitfield's 1939 Just Cocktails book (although does not appear in his 1941 Here's How Mixed Drinks by the same Three Mountaineers Inc. publishers).

name: Club Cocktail (D's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge and maraschino cherry.
review: Cognac and pisco brandies with maraschino and pineapple adding fruity tropical freshness and a foamy head.
history:
I merely added a splash of pisco to the classic Club Cocktail, which first appeared in W.C. Whitfield's 1939 Just Cocktails' book to arrive at this delicious recipe:
60 ml Cognac
7.5 ml Pisco
15 ml Maraschino liqueur
22.5 ml Pineapple juice
2 dash Peychaud's/Creole-style bitters
While delicious, this recipe is boozy, so in June 2020, I rained this back to the recipe above.

name: Love Heart
instructions: Select a FLUTE GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into chilled glass.
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Garnish with strawberry slice on rim and peg Love Heart to rim.
review:
This lavender pink cocktail is everything you (or your partner) might want for a Valentine's tipple. It is the right colour, includes parfait amour liqueur (meaning perfect love) and the all-important fizz-charged sparkle. Good luck.

In case he/she asks, parfait amour is a lilac coloured curaçao (orange) liqueur flavoured with rose petals, vanilla pods and almonds.
history: Created in February 2014 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London in England.

name: Byrrh Special
ingredients: 45 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: The botanicals in gin combine brilliantly with Byrrh to create a cocktail that works as well as an apéritif as it does a digestif.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Double Grape
ingredients: 52.5 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist and 2 red grapes..
review: The aromatics of the distilled wine of pisco combine harmoniously with the aromatised wine's sweet grapey notes.
history: Created by yours truly (Simon D) at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Negroni and the Goat
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Negroni in name and Negroni in style with the sweetness of Aperol countered by the use of amaro.
history: This riff on a Negroni was originally served straight-up in a coupe and comes from The Girl And The Goat restaurant in Chicago, USA where it was created in 2014.

name: Right Hand
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Express orange zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish, or discard and garnish with orange slice wheel.
review: This rum-based Negroni may make you wonder why you've been drinking gin Negronis all these years. The Right Hand is usually served straight-up in a coupe but I prefer it tamed by serving on-the-rocks in an old-fashioned glass.
history: Adapted from a recipe created 2007-8 by Michael McIlroy at Milk & Honey in New York City, USA.

name: Londoner (Ward's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail, then use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: As a born and bred Londoner, I'm slightly miffed that a New Yorker has applied the name of our great city to a drink using French and Italian products, but as it's Phil Ward and a great gin-based drink, I'll forgive him and mix up another.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Philip Ward while at Pegu Club, New York City, USA and brought to our attention by the super imbiber and scribe Gary "gaz" Regan, RIP.

name: Give Me Fever
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE first 6 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with tonic water.
EXPRESS flamed lemon zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist (flamed).
review: This drink combines the fever quenching G&T with medicinal lavender and flavoursome mezcal from the Mexican territories where us Brits are susceptible to "fever... give me fever. What a lovely way to burn."
history: Created 3rd May 2014 by yours truly while Fever by Peggy Lee was playing.

name: Cable Car
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into pre-prepared glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of coin-sized orange zest twist.
review: A spiced-rum-based riff on a Sidecar. Vanilla and spice from the rum interact with the orange liqueur in this citrusy sour cocktail.
history:
Created in 1996 by Tony Abou-Ganim at the Starlight Room, a nightclub and cocktail lounge atop San Francisco's Sir Francis Drake Hotel (now the Beacon Grand Hotel). The Nob Hill cable cars pass by the bar, hence its catchphrase, "between the stars and the cable cars," and this contemporary classic cocktail's name.

Some say that Cory Reistad created the Cable Car, but Tony says of such claims, "Cory Reistad did indeed work at the Starlight Room with me although not as a member of the opening team. All the menus were created by myself and yes other bartenders were encouraged to participate but the Cable Car was one of my creations which did indeed go on the menu and was created in 1996."

Tony's original 1996 recipe: 1½ oz Captain Morgan Spiced Rum, ¾ oz Marie Brizard orange curaçao and 1½ oz fresh lemon sour mix (which is made with 2 parts freshly squeezed, filtered lemon juice with 1 part simple syrup).

name: Pisco Kid
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with Nestle's Milkybar or other white chocolate.
review: This short pisco-based spirituous stirred cocktail was inspired by memories of Nestle's Milkybars and the 1980s British TV ads featuring the Milkybar Kid promoting the white chocolate bars. White crème de cacao's sweetness is balanced by dry sherry and the acidity of Sauvignon Blanc wine, which also adds grassy floral notes. The discarded orange zest twist makes the first sip a white chocolate orange experience.
history: Created by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Mediterranean Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: This tasty sour features the distinctive flavour of mastiha.

name: Fresh Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf.
review: The perfect digestif Martini. Mastiha and white crème de menthe - both renowned for their digestive properties - add cooling freshness to this wet, lightly sweetened Martini.
history:
Created 19th April 2015 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England. 

See: Martini Cocktail and its evolution

name: Milano Torino (Mi-To)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Garnish with orange slice or wedge.
review: The cocktail that led to both the Negroni and the Americano. Pause and enjoy its simplicity before progressing.
history: Created in the 1860s by Gaspare Campari at his Caffè Campari, on Milan's Piazza del Duomo and named after the origins of its two ingredients: Campari from Milan and rosso vermouth, traditionally from Turin. It is said that later, soda water was added to the Milano-Torino to create the Americano. The Milano-Torino also morphed into the Negroni with gin added.

name: Cachaça Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Might not look like a Caipirinha but tastes like a Caipirinha - a very sophisticated and refined Caipirinha.

name: Strawberry Fields
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE strawberry in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with 5 drops balsamic vinegar and dust with freshly ground black pepper.
review: This refreshing vodka-based strawberry cocktail balances tart lemon juice with rich honey. Drops of balsamic and a dusting of black pepper add complexity.
history: Created by Trina Nishimura in 2014.

name: The Other Woman
ingredients: 50 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: MIST with 2 sprays of absinthe.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Lucy Horncastle at Nola Bar, London, England.

name: Fly Me The Flip To France
ingredients: 45 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A bittersweet flip best enjoyed as a wholesome aperitivo.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Ben Campbell at the Alchemist in Manchester, England.

name: Le Grand
ingredients: 50 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: STIR first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass
TOP with sparkling wine.
review: Aromatised wine plays a lead role in this cognac-charged apéritif.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Samuel Rose at Panda & Sons in Edinburgh, Scotland.

name: Rebujito cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
STIR.
garnish: Garnish with lemon and lime slice wheels.
review: Dry characterful sherry with sweet zesty lemonade – a combo made for a hot summer's day.
history: Named after the Spanish word 'arrebujar' meaning to mix, the recipe for a Rebujito changes from region to region in Spain and is often as simple as equal parts fino sherry and soda or equal parts fino sherry and Sprite or Seven-up. It is popular in Seville and the south of Spain, particularly during April's Seville Fair (La feria de Abril).

name: Petanque cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: The game this drink is named after is French while the main ingredients are Spanish and Italian, but they do play Pétanque in the Basque region of Spain. However, both the creator of this drink and the Creole-style bitters used originate from the USA – perhaps it took a non-European to bring these ingredients - fino sherry and amaretto - harmoniously together.
history:
Adapted from a drink created in 2014 by Andrew Bohrer at Mistral Kitchen, Seattle, USA, and named after the French game where hollow metal balls are thrown as close as possible to a small wooden jack ball.

Inspired by playing Petanque in France whilst sipping on pastis, I tried Andrew's original spec but with an additional float of absinthe. To be honest, I tried pastis first but the liquorice notes detracted from the cocktail. A couple of dashes of absinthe merge with the body of the cocktail to add harmonious additional complexity. Hope you agree.

name: Cactus Banger
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A "bangin", golden, sunny-looking and sunny-tasting cocktail.
history:
I don't have any notes as to the origin of this version of the Cactus Banger but as one of the older recipes on my database, its origin is pre-late 1990s.

A Cactus Banger is perhaps better known as being a simple Harvey Wallbanger based on tequila rather than vodka and it is this version that appears in books such as Stanley M. Jones' 1977 Jones' Complete Barguide.

name: Bending Blades cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Tequila-based, zesty and bitter before turning bittersweet. In March 2022, I cut the volume of gentian liqueur from 15ml (½oz) back to 7.5ml (¼oz) so fans of bitter gentian my want to stick with the original spec.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Christopher Day at General Lee's in Los Angeles, USA.

name: Bitter in Brazil
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Cachaça based and bittersweet with orange accents and Fernet Branca contributing subtle bitterness.
history: Adapted from a recipe created at The Citizen Wine Bar in Worcester, Massachusetts.

name: Brazil 66
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Reminiscent of an orange-influenced Caipirinha.
history:
Adapted from a drink created at Absinthe Brasserie & Bar, San Francisco, USA.

The name perhaps refers to Sérgio Mendes' band Brazil 66, best known for their 1966 hit Mas Que Nada. One of the best-known Brazilian songs, the name translates as a sarcastic 'Yeah, right!' and reached 47 on the US Billboard Hot 100 and six on the Official UK Top 40.

This cocktail name may also remember 1966 when Brazilians had much to be bitter about. The country's team had done well in the two previous World Cups, and during their first match in the 1966 Cup against Bulgaria, Pele, the world's most famous footballer, became the first player to score in three World Cups with a goal from a free kick. The Bulgarians spent the rest of the match fouling Pele, making him unable to play in Brazil's next game against Hungary. Without their star player, Brazil lost, meaning they needed a win in their next and final group game against Portugal. The Portuguese players followed the bad example set by the Bulgarians and repeatedly fouled Pele, resulting in him hobbling through much of the game. The Bulgarians won, and Brazil were eliminated from the World Cup at the group stage.

name: Caneflower cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Rust coloured and bittersweet with cachaça and elderflower.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2009 by Jeffrey Morgenthaler in Portland, Oregon, USA. Jeff's original recipe calls for a "generous twist of lemon" rather than orange. On JeffreyMorgenthaler.com, he says this cocktail was "Inspired in part by a gorgeous cocktail created by my good friend Gonçalo de Souza Monteiro, the Comte de Sureau."

name: Orinoco cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with cacao powder.
review: Dry sherry, chocolate and almond add subtle nuances to a base of cachaça and apple juice.
history: Adapted from an originally blended drink created in 2015 by Sergio Padilla at Boca Chica in Barcelona, Spain.

name: Sin Cyn
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A single malt whisky laced twist on a Boulevardier with Cynar replacing the familiar Italian red bitter liqueur.
history: Created by Paul Dellevigne at the Red Owl Tavern, Philadelphia, USA.

name: New Orleans Minute
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
RINSE glass with mezcal (swirl around to coat and discard excess) and set to one side.
STIR other ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into mezcal rinsed glass.
Express lemon and orange zest twists over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Garnish with 3 coffee beans floated on the surface to represent health, wealth and happiness.
review: Perhaps not a Bourbon Street staple, but characterful nonetheless with tequila, mezcal, aromatised wine, maraschino and orange bitters.
history: Created by Sune Urth at Ruby bar in Copenhagen and adapted from the original New York Minute created by Luke Whearty at Der Raum bar in Melbourne.

name: Bitches' Brew cocktail
ingredients: 30 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: Beer has never tasted so complex - sweet aperitif, dry hoppy IPA beer, bready jenever and cleansing vodka.
history: Created by yours truly on 21st February 2015 at the Cabinet Room, London, England and so named due to its ingredients including beer and Byrrh aperitif (also pronounced 'beer') so "brew" seemed appropriate, helped by the track Bitches Brew on the brilliant 1961 Bitches Brew studio album by Miles Davis, the American jazz trumpeter, composer and bandleader.

name: Chanbanger
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Hard to classify, a hint of Singapore Sling oriental cherry with some Cuban aged rum Daiquiri and a splash of Papa's grapefruit citrus sourness.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2014 by Barney Chan at The Mayor of Scaredy Cat Town in London, England.

name: Pastry War Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Mezcal, amaro and elderflower liqueur all work together to set this Margarita apart.
history:
Adapted from a recipe discovered at Bastille Cafe & Bar in Seattle, USA.

The Pastry War of 1838–39 between Mexico and France was fought after a French pastry chef living in Tacubaya, near Mexico City, claimed some Mexican army officers had damaged his restaurant. The chef was one of a number of foreign nationals who claimed they'd suffered losses as a result of civil disturbances, and their respective governments had unsuccessfully sought compensation on behalf of their nationals from the Mexican government. The chef's grievances pushed the French to the war to support his claim for 600,000 pesos and sent a fleet to the Mexican port of Veracruz. After bombarding the fortress of San Juan de Ulúa and occupying the city, thanks to a British-brokered peace, the French secured payment and withdrew their fleet.
See: Margarita cocktail history

name: Chutes and Ladders cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Citrusy tequila-led with Swedish punch and Cocchi Americano rounding and adding their distinctive flavours.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2014 by Frederic Yarm at Russell House Tavern in Cambridge, Massachusetts.

name: Edison's Medicine cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with basil leaf boat floated on cocktail.
review: Cachaça leads with basil and Chartreuse herbal notes, zesty lime and agave richness.
history: Adapted from a 2011 recipe created by Pete Gugni at The Bedford in Chicago, Illinois.

name: Roman Highball
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda.
garnish: Garnish with skewered crystallised ginger.
review: Refreshing and Pimm's Cup-like. A great long drink to come home to.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created by Danny Gil at Weather Up, New York City, USA.

Information on Highball cocktails, their history and tips on the perfect serve can be found on our page.

name: Sloane Square cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: An unusual pairing of single malt scotch whisky and tequila with herbal complexity courtesy of Bénédictine liqueur, freshened with orange zestiness.
history: Adapted from a 2013 recipe by Paige McCune of AroundOrTwo.com

name: Spanish Eyes
ingredients: 22.5 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with star anise floated on cocktail.
review: Reminiscent of a Mexican Blood & Sand, refreshing and complex.
history: Created in August 2015 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England. Why Spanish eyes? Tequila distillers speak Spanish, Byrrh is made close to the Spanish boarder, pacharan is very Spanish, as are the best oranges. Plus my missus is half Spanish, so has brown eyes, and this drink is brownish.

name: Claudine cocktail
ingredients: 10 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Dry and aromatic, the combination of Byrrh and armagnac reveals interesting favours of dried apricot and chocolate.
history: Created in 2015 by Matteo Malisan at 69 Colebrooke Row, London, England.

name: To Be Oranged
ingredients: 37.5 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest (discarded) and orange wedge on rim.
review: To be arranged - In the meantime shake your tangerine with this pleasing earthy tequila and zesty fruity number.
history: Created in August 2015 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Greek Celery Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grind of pepper and celery stick.
review: Celery and mastiha pair well, while gin adds a spirituous bite and botanical complexity. A small grind of pepper to finish adds a nuance of spice.
history: A simplified version of a drink promoted by Skinos mastiha by yours truly in August 2015 at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Gun Metal Blue
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and float as garnish.
review: Orange, peach and cinnamon delicately flavour this mezcal-based Margarita riff.
history: Created in February 2015 by Mike Shain and Nicholas Bennett at Porchlight in New York City. At Porchlight, the original is made with house-made cinnamon syrup infused with gentian root to add a slight bitterness. Instead, I've added a little gentian liqueur to the above recipe.

name: Greek Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf or sprig tip floated or pegged to rim.
review: Mastiha (which after all is a botanically flavoured spirit) combines wonderfully with gin and dry vermouth. A splash of ouzo adds a touch more Greek personality.
history: Created by yours truly in August 2015.

name: Aristotle cocktail
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE mint in base of stirring glass
Add other ingredients, STIR with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: The thinking man's mixed drink, this is based on gin with the distinctive flavour of Greek mastiha and lengthened with birch water.
history: Created in August 2015 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Woody cocktail
instructions: MUDDLE mint in base of stirring glass
Add other ingredients, STIR with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Named not after the cheery chap in Cheers or even the Beatles’ Norwegian Wood but due to this particularly Woody cocktail containing birch water and mastiha.
history: Created in August 2015 by yours truly (Simon D) at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: South of Heaven
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Express lemon zest twist over cocktail and discard. Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: Bittersweet and refreshing with the very distinctive flavours of mastiha and Fernet Branca.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2013 by George Megalokonomos at Food Mafia, Athens, Greece. This was the winning cocktail in the Mediterranean Cocktails Challenge 2013.

name: Eccentrica Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Daiquiris are refreshing but I believe this mastiha and birch water influenced example is particularly so.
history: Created in August 2015 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England. Named after a character in Douglas Adams' The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy by the name of Eccentrica Gallumbits.

name: Smoky Tears cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit wedge.
review: Best described as being a mezcal, mastiha and pink grapefruit Margarita. It’s a great combo. Mastiha freshness with a hint of smoky mezcal is balanced by slightly acidic pink grapefruit juice.

name: Minty Pentones cocktail
instructions: STIR all ingredients with ice and strain into glass filled with crushed ice.
review: Gin's botanical complexity combined with minty and mastiha freshness and a pink hint of cranberry fruitiness makes for a cleansing after-dinner digestif.
history: Created by yours truly in August 2015 at the Cabinet Room in London, England and named after one of my favourite New Orleans' bands, Mark and the Pentones, whose album was playing at the time of the drinks creation.

name: Malecon Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Use tongs to place  large ice cube/ball or chunk or block ice in cocktail.
review: Best described as a fortified wine-rich Daiquiri.
history: Created in 2011 by Erik Lorincz at  , London, England.

name: Apple Black Royal
instructions: SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass
TOP with champagne.
review: Richly flavoured apple and blackberry fruit liqueurs, bolstered and fortified with calvados, charged and given character with rosé champagne.
history: Created in 2015 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Thuir Sour
ingredients: 30 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Perfectly balanced sweet and sour apricot with faint red wine notes.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2015 by Stuart Bale of 69 Colebrooke Row, London during a visit to Thuir in the South of France where Byrrh has been made since the 1870s. When Stuart asked what Thuir was famous for, apart from Byrrh, the locals claimed their apricots were the best in the world. Hence Stuart’s use of Byrrh and crème d’apricot in this cocktail.

name: Copenhagen Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Bready notes in the genever combine with the cherry brandy and the lime juice to produce a complex bittersweet, jammy cocktail.
history: In August 2009, Cherry Heering, a liqueur from Copenhagen, challenged local bartenders to create a signature cocktail for their city. This winning recipe was created by Gromit Eduardsen at Bar 1105 in Copenhagen, Denmark.

name: Dried Meadow Flower
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda.
garnish: Garnish with lemon wheel (& preferably a bouquet of edible flowers).
review: Floral elderflower and tequila with bittersweet gentian and refreshing lemon.
history: Created in August 2015 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Sunny Disposition cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Slightly on the bitter side of bittersweet with gentian, jammy apricot and lemon juice over a tequila base.

name: Zuzu's Petals cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Bittersweet with fragrant bison grass vodka and fruity Maraschino cherry flavours supported by bitter gentian, rich elderflower and lime juice.
history: Chaim Dauermann at The Up & Up, Greenwich Village, NYC, USA. The name references It's A Wonderful Life.

name: Reggatta de Blanc
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of skewered green grapes.
review: Dry and spirit-forward.
history:
Created in August 2015 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

Reggatta de Blanc (White Reggae) was the second, and seminal, album by the English rock band The Police. The album's French name reflects the heavy influence of the country's products in this cocktail and happily, for approval by our naming committee (my missus), most of the ingredients are also decidedly "blanc".

name: Reverse Vesper Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: More a subtly fortified wine than a true "three and under the guest" Martini, but delightfully so with sherry-like wine notes and faint botanical influences. Some will say this should be stirred despite the 'Vesper' name. Shake boldly on, but with good ice.
history: A riff on James Bond's preferred Vesper Martini by yours truly.

name: Call Me Old-Fashioned
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: An Old-Fashioned made with cognac instead of whiskey. Works well.
history: A Cognac Old-Fashioned cocktail created in 2001 by yours truly, London, England.

name: Andean Dusk
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
MUDDLE grapes in base of shaker.
Add next 3 ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: Garnish with grape dropped into cocktail.
review: Dry, biscuity champagne adds sparkle to sweet red grapes while pisco fortifies and adds complex distilled grape flavours.
history: Adapted from a recipe created circa 2011 by Meaghan Dorman at Raines Law Room in New York City. Named due to the use of Peruvian pisco and the cocktail hue due to rosé sparkling wine.

name: Minty White Lady cocktail
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE (without ice) and strain into chilled glass.
review: White crème de menthe and peppermint bitters bring minty freshness to this lady in white.
history:
A minty riff on the classic White Lady created by yours truly in August 2015 at the Cabinet Room in London, England.
White Lady cocktail history

name: Sailor's Swizzle
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into chilled glass.
Two-thirds fill glass with crushed ice and SWIZZLE.
Add more crushed ice to fill glass and SWIZZLE some more.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: Port wine is further fortified with golden rum and delicately spiced with pimento dram in this swizzled punch.
history: Created in 2012 by Erik Lorincz at  , London, England.

name: Miss Moonshine
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Rosemary sprig.
review: Gentian, elderflower and rosemary combine harmoniously, over a Poitín base with aromatised wine.
history: Created in 2014 by Louis Lebaillif at Hawksmoor Air Street, London, England.

name: Boozy Suzie
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pink grapefruit zest twist (discarded) and 3 spots of Peychaud's bitters drawn through foamy head.
review: Bittersweet gentian is the prevalent flavour (hence the 'Suzie' name), toned down by bianco vermouth and freshened with pink grapefruit over a fortifying grainy vodka base. Peychaud's subtly adds its distinctive flavour.
history: Created in August 2015 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Salvatore Meets
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
review: A refreshing zesty lemon Daiquiri topped with a splash of fizz.
history: Created in 2015 by Salvatore Calabrese at his bar in the Playboy Club, London, England.

name: Ironbound
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Who'd have thought cachaça and apple brandy would make such good bedfellows with herbal liqueur and Creole-style bitters.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Mayur Subbarao of New York, USA. Mayur's original recipe called for bottled-in-bond applejack rather than calvados. The extra punch of applejack dries this cocktail so if using calvados consider reducing the sugar syrup.

name: Pearl Button
instructions: SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with lemonade.
review: Cachaça, aromatised wine, and citrus combine in this dry, refreshing drink.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2013 by John Deragon at PDT, New York City, USA. John's original recipe calls for San Pellegrino Limonata in place of lemonade.

name: Ambrosio Cup
instructions: SHAKE first 6 ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with soda water.
review: Cachaça, Bison Grass vodka and manzanilla sherry combine harmoniously and are freshened with mint and lime in this refreshing cocktail.
history: Created in 2013 by Ann Robinson at PDT, New York City, USA

name: Picotin
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Bright yellow and on the bitter side of bittersweet with aquavit, lemon, gentian and maraschino liqueurs.
history: Created in 2015 by Florian Dubois at Joyeux Bordel in London, England.

name: Sakura Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with cherry blossom or skewered olive.
review: Sake replaces vermouth in this Martini with maraschino liqueur adding deliciousness, enhanced by faint salinity.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Kenta Goto. This is the house Martini at his Bar Goto in Manhattan, New York City, where it is garnished with a salted cherry blossom.

See: Martini cocktail and its evolution

name: Queen's Park Hotel Super Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: It is perhaps best described as a Trinidadian Daiquiri due to its heavy use of Angostura Bitters. According to Owen Rutter, it was also originally made with Trinidad gold rum, Canning's Caroni Rum. (If using homemade 2:1 pomegranate syrup rather than grenadine, use 15ml (½oz) and omit sugar syrup.)
history: Recipe adapted from If Crab No Walk: A Traveller in the West Indies, a 1932 book written by British travel writer Owen Rutter, who writes of the Queen's Park Hotel, Port of Spain, Trinidad, "The cooking is good; the servants, though slow, are attentive; there is an excellent ballroom, a first-rate band, and a good bar: 'the Long Bar with the Brass Rail,' where you may drink the Queen's Park Hotel Super Cocktail."

name: The Angel Wore Red
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
STIR first 3 ingredients with ice.
POUR beer into ice-filled glass.
STRAIN stirred ingredients over beer and briefly stir.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A most unusual combination of classic Italian red bitter liqueur, bianco vermouth, crème de menthe and lager combine brilliantly in this refreshing aperitivo.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2015 by Dominic Whisson at The American Bar in The Savoy Hotel, London, England.

name: Mr Simon
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Your choice of genever and orange curaçao greatly effects the balance of this cocktail. Salvatore uses Rutte Old Simon which is not only a fantastic genever with an attractive nutty character but also befits the name. The choice of a drier, high alcohol curaçao obviously produces a correspondingly drier drink.
history: Created in 2015 by Salvatore Calabrese at his bar in London's Playboy Club. Salvatore was inspired to make this drink both by the vintage 'Gin Cocktail' - perhaps the first ever gin cocktail - and Old Simon Rutte genever, which was being released across Europe at the time.

name: Eastside Gimlet
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE cucumber in base of shaker.
Add mint and lightly MUDDLE (just to bruise).
