Add gin, lime juice and sugar syrup, and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with soda.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf pegged to rim and cucumber slice floated on surface.
review: A riff on the Southside and the Gimlet using cucumber, an ingredient inspired by Hendrick's Gin having recently launched when this gin cocktail was first created.
history: Created in 2004 by George Delgado at Libation in Manhattan, New York City, originally as a long drink. Christy Pope took the Eastside to Milk & Honey, where this popular straight-up version was created.

name: Waterloo
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with pickled almond.
review: A dry, lightly bitter, aperitif cocktail highlighting the bready notes in genever and the bright zesty freshness of mandarin liqueur.
history:
Created by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

Named after the Battle of Waterloo, which was fought on Sunday 18th June 1815 near the town of Waterloo in present-day Belgium, then part of the Netherlands. The French army commanded by Napoleon Bonaparte was defeated by an Anglo-Allied army commanded by the Duke of Wellington fittingly alongside a Prussian army commanded by Gebhard Leberecht von Blücher, Prince of Wahlstatt. Napoleon's defeat at Waterloo brought his reign as French Emperor to a sudden end.

name: Division Bell
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: A mezcal inspired variation of the Last Word with a refreshing, spirit-forward blend of ingredients.
history:
Created in 2009 by Phil Ward at Mayahuel in Manhattan, New York City.
Last Word history

name: Joy Division
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange (or lemon) zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange (or lemon) zest twist.
review: A 2:1 Dry Martini with a slug of triple sec and dashes of absinthe contributing zesty orange and subtle aniseed respectively.
history: Recipe created in 2008 by Phil Ward at Death & Co., New York, USA.

name: Juliet and Romeo
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE cucumber with salt in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Float 3 drops rose water and 3 drops of aromatic bitters, and peg mint leaf to rim.
review: You smell rose water but taste gin, lime, cucumber and mint. A refreshing, summery cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created circa 2007 by Toby Maloney at The Violet Hour in Chicago and featured in his 2022 book The Bartender's Manifesto.

name: Medicina Latina
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
FLOAT mezcal.
garnish: Garnish with skewered crystallised ginger.
review: A ginger spiced and honeyed Tequila Sour.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2009 by Marcos Tello at The Varnish in Los Angeles, USA.

In January 2022 I updated the recipe above. The previous after-dinner version comprised:
60ml (2oz) Blanco tequila
10ml (1/3oz) Honey syrup (3:1)
10ml 1/3oz) Ginger syrup
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled old-fashioned glass. Garnish with candied ginger and spray of mezcal.

name: Moon Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Gin and sherry combine harmoniously in this stirred boozy cocktail with a  honeyed touch of jammy peach.
history: Created in 2008 by Thomas Waugh at Death & Co., New York City, USA.

name: Alcatraz
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Dry and lightly bitter, this tequila-based sipper makes for a great apéritif cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Christin Wagner at La Petite Grocery, New Orleans, USA. Recipe courtesy of Gary "gaz" Regan's Ardent Spirits.

name: Bennett Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of float dehydrated lime wheel.
review: The judicious dashing of bitters makes or breaks this Gimlet-like cocktail which tastes good when made with a dry gin but better when made with an old tom gin, as per the original 1922 recipe. (If your old tom gin is sweet, reduce the sugar syrup.)
history:
The Bennett Cocktail was first published in Robert Vermeire's 1922 Cocktails: How to Mix Them and then repeated in the 1930 book Cocktails by "Jimmy" Late of Ciro's London written by the mysterious Jimmy. Both recipes are based on old tom gin and without the addition of sugar.

Most modern versions of the Bennett, including that made in 2015 by Meaghan Dorman at The Bennett in New York City, USA call for dry gin rather than old tom.

name: Bijou Cocktail (inspired by Lawlor's 1895 recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review:
A boozy sipper of a cocktail that's medium dry with honeyed richness. Gin-based with zesty orange freshness and sweet vermouth adding deep herbal complexity. A great after-dinner cocktail that also works well on-the-rocks.

This should not be confused with the more commonplace equal parts gin, Chartreuse and vermouth Bijou which first appeared in Harry Johnson's 1900 Bartender's Manual.
history: Adapted from the original equal parts gin, Grand Marnier and sweet vermouth recipe in C. F. Lawlor's 1895 book The Mixicologist in which he describes this as "a delicious drink." Handily adding, "Grand Marnier can also be served in a pony-glass like any liqueur." Remember, Louis-Alexandre Marnier-Lapostolle only created Grand Marnier 15 years earlier and it must have taken a while for the new liqueur to find its way from France to the Grand Hotel in Cincinnati where Lawlor worked, so he was commenting on what was then an exciting new product.

name: Mediocre
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge.
review: Ripe pineapple, rich vanilla and honey laced with rum. The use of mead in this drink inspired the 'Mediocre' name. Best made with a light white rum infused with a vanilla pod, rather than a commercially produced vanilla-flavoured rum.
history: Created in 2016 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Canary Flip
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A delightful balance of egg, spirit and wine. Looks creamy and sweet but is sharp and citrusy.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2002 by Alex Kammerling, London, England.

name: Pinecone
instructions: MUDDLE cloves in base of shaker to crush
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Honey and pineapple, laced with aromatic pisco and spiced with clove.
history: Created by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Well-fortified
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A Bamboo meets a Vodka Martini in a vodka-laced aromatic aperitif cocktail. So named due to its packing a trio of fortified wines.
history: Created in 2016 by yours truly at The Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Tribute
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
SPRAY violet liqueur onto inside surface of glass and set aside.
STIR brandy with ice and strain into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: Garnish with edible flower.
review: Originally made with Martell Cordon Bleu, this sophisticated champagne cocktail has delicate floral notes.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Matthias Lataille at The Beat, London, England.

name: The Savelberg
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Boozy with bready genever, pear, citrus and faint rich honey.
history: Created in 2016 by Jan Halfpap at Bar Le Lion in Hamburg, Germany. This riff on the classic Journalist cocktail is named after a Dutch journalist called Robert Savelberg, who lives and works in Berlin. Savelberg became famous in Germany due to his critical questioning during a press conference held over the coalition agreement of the new Federal Government in October 2009 and a subsequent satirical movie made by ZDF.

name: Pour Me Another
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Raisiny pedro ximénez sherry adds subtle richness to the cognac with hints of flavour from the absinthe and lemon zest. This drink benefits from generous dilution so consider adding a splash of water.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Noel Ekstrand at Astrum Grill & Raw Bar, Trondheim, Norway.

name: Trade Winds
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wheel.
review: Coconut water adds dilution and subtly flavours this brandy-based cocktail with sweet vermouth and subtle notes of chocolate and aromatic bitters.
history: Created in 2015 by Daniel Bovey at Be At One in Bristol, England.

name: Je suis l'Amour
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Cognac and cask-aged dry vermouth form the base of this refined cocktail with complex ginger, pink grapefruit, and chocolate notes.
history: Adapted from a drink created by Walter Gosso at The Mad Dog Speakeasy, Turin, Italy.

name: Élysée Treaty
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Cognac-laced, zesty and bittersweet. Delicious as a late-night after-dinner digestive.
history: Created in 2015 by Ivan Mandaric at Oxbo Urban Bar & Grill, DoubleTree by Hilton Hotel, Zagreb, Croatia.

name: Indimenticabili Momenti (Unforgettable Moments)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Slightly sweet with vanilla, cherry and cinnamon, along with complex peppermint and herbal notes from Strega interlaced with cognac.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Renato Pinfildi at presso Bottiglieria, Italy.

name: The Cab
instructions: SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with champagne.
review: A minty limey cognac sour invigorated with champagne.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Andres Walters at The Lobo Plantation in Sydney, Australia.

name: Mon Dernier Mot (My Last Word)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered crystallised ginger.
review: Tangy lemon and cognac with sweet herbal and ginger notes. An interesting riff on The Last Word.
history:
Created in 2015 by Edward Godard at Berlin Bar in Melbourne, Australia.
Last Word history

name: Banana Split
instructions: STIR first 5 ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass
Shake a sealed container of cream and carefully POUR over the back of a spoon to form a small white spot floating in the centre of the drink.
review: A banana-flavoured after-dinner sipper of cocktail with coconut water subtly flavouring and smoothing cognac.
history: Created in April 2016 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Art de Vivre (Art of Living)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Cognac freshened and flavoured with Mastiha, Fernet Branca and chocolate bitters.
history: Adapted from a drink created by Thanos Prunarus at Baba Au Rum, Athens. Originally with 50ml Martell Cordon Bleu cognac.

name: La Garde de Nuit (The Night's Watch)
ingredients: 45 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of Fragata Green Olive and cucumber slice.
review: Cognac with red berry and red wine notes with faint honeycomb.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Matthew Long at Lulu White, Paris, France. Matt's original recipe called for Chouchen Hydromel mead wine from Brittany and Gentiane de Lure.

name: Lovelight
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with dark chocolate.
review: Reminiscent of a cognac-based Negroni with a hint of cinnamon spice.
history: Created in 2015 by John Stavroulakis at Zaxari kai Alati Bistrot Bar in Athens, Greece

name: L'Indochine
instructions: STIR all the ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
review: This stirred cognac-based cocktail is reminiscent of both an old-fashioned and a Godfather with notes of citrus, coffee and tea.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Trinh Quan Huy-Philip, Vietnam.

name: Celebration
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: One of our most popular cocktails from Thanksgiving through to the end of the December holiday period. Reminiscent of a brandy-based Negroni served straight-up, this is an appropriate cocktail for any celebration. (Your choice of vermouth will make or break this cocktail, which was originally made with a rich, expressive rubino vermouth.)
history: Created in 2015 by Devis D'Ercole at Imperial Beach in San Benedetto del Tronto, Marche, Italy. Originally with Martini Rubino vermouth.

name: Parmenides
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
review: A sour-style cocktail served straight-up combining brandy, Green Chartreuse, white wine and lemon.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Fabiano Latham when he was at The Natural Philosopher in London, England. Parmenides was a 6th century BC Greek philosopher, appropriate considering the name of Alban's bar.

name: The Comeback
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: A complex cocktail with apricot fruit at the forefront and underlying fernet bitterness.
history: Created in 2015 by Constanca Cordeiro at Peg + Patriot, London, England.

name: A Rose By Any Other Name
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with Turkish Delight and dark chocolate grated over cocktail.
review: A dry spirit-led sipper with faint floral and chocolate notes. Perhaps forgo the grated chocolate garnish in favour of a square of dark chocolate balanced on Turkish Delight.
history: Created in 2015 by Sammy Abdalla at The Suburbs, Hoole, Chester, England. Sammy's original recipe calls for Martell Cordon Bluu cognac and La Quintinye Vermouth Rouge.

name: Revolutionary Flip
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE without ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: "Full-bodied" both describes this cocktail and how you'll feel after consuming this very tasty meal of a drink.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Markus Olsen at Ziloufs, Islington, London, England.

Markus' original recipe uses equal parts molasses syrup and simple syrup in place of Demerara sugar syrup. It also contained 15ml/ 1/2 oz cream.

name: The French Canadian
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE cucumber in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of cucumber slice.
review: Dry and aromatic with cucumber adding freshness. A good apéritif cocktail.
history: Created in 2015 by Dan Corum Fisher at Hawksmoor in Air Street, London, England.

name: C'est la Vie (That's Life)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with dehydrated apple slice and star anise floated on surface.
review: A tasty apple and cognac sour with delicate herbal and cinnamon spice.
history: Created in 2015 by Saverio Vicari at The Rib Room Bar at The Jumeirah Carlton Tower Hotel in London, England.

name: The Devil In Me
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Plum sake, chilli spice and maraschino cherry delicately flavour cognac in this stirred, boozy after-dinner cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Grant Murray at Bar One, Inverness, Scotland.

name: Excuse My French
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
Prepare the glass with citrus peel and sugar CRUSTA by first finding a whole lemon that fits snugly inside the top of your glass with its equator in contact with the rim of the glass.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into pre-prepared glass.
review: Raspberry flavours characterise this cognac sour which is served crusta-style.
history: Created in 2015 by Daniele Francini at Salvatore's Bar at Playboy Club in London, England.

name: Le Commerçant (The Merchant)
ingredients: 25 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass
TOP with prosecco.
review: A balance between citrus and berry sweetness forms the heart of this cognac-based cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Mark Velasquez at The Merchant Hotel, Belfast, Northern Ireland.

name: Butler's Tipple
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Almost creamy with vanilla, bittersweet rootiness and cognac.
history: Created by Nicholas Gilmour at The Tippling House, Aberdeen, Scotland.

name: Derniere Minute (Last Minute)
ingredients: 22.5 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Cognac stirred with rich herbal and heather honey Drambuie, along with aromatised wine. Aromatic bitters add complexity depth.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2015 by Jakub Bobcek at The Refectory Bar, Plymouth, England.

name: The Beloved
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: A wonderfully complex marriage of cognac, distilled chocolate, rich Pedro Ximénez and banana liqueur with aromatic bitters and a dash of brine.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by James Wall at Hawksmoor, Air Street, London, England.

name: Three Hundred Poems
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Blackcurrant jam adds rich concentrated berry notes to this attractive crimson red cognac laced cocktail.
history: Created in 2015 by Konrad Kaczmarzyk at ECC Chinatown, London, England.

name: MGM Grand
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Nail the perfect balance and this cognac-based cocktail sings on the palate with cognac, richly subtle cherry, and zesty lemon acidity.
history:
A riff on the classic Casino Cocktail, hence the name referencing one of the most famous Las Vegas resort casinos. 

Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Andrew Loudon at Satan's Whiskers in London, England.

name: Titre Provisoire (Provisional Title)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Characterful cognac, herbal Bénédictine and rich fig liqueur are harmoniously balanced by dry vermouth and cider vinegar. This drink is finely balanced but when achieved it sings.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Matt Robertson at Hawksmoor Spitalfields in London, England. Matt's original recipe calls for Martell Cordon Bleu cognac.

name: The Addington
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A beautifully balanced, spirit-forward, stirred, cognac-based cocktail with Vermouth di Torino and herbal liqueur.
history: Created in 2015 by Guy Butler at Muddlers in Sydenham, London, England.

name: Terroir
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Chablis' white wine acidity balances rich nettle cordial in this sweet 'n' sour cognac based cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Clotilde Lataille at Hawksmoor Spitalfields Bar, London, England.

name: Repose' 1912
ingredients: Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: STIR first 5 ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass
Top with sparkling wine.
review: Cognac with sharp balsamic notes and hints of chocolate, enlivened with champagne.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Simone Ditadi at Hilton Birmingham Metropole, Birmingham, England.

name: Mart-Inez
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Grape and grain combine harmoniously in this sipping cocktail.
history: Created in 2015 by Matteo Rebuffo at China Tang at Dorchester Hotel, London, England, using Martell Cognac – hence the name.

name: Carnaghino
instructions: STIR all ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass (preferably block ice).
review: Cognac, luxurious chocolate and pistachio. What’s not to like?
history: Created in 2015 by Gabriele Carnaghi at Sky Bar Birmingham Resort World, England.

name: Le Coq d'Or (The Golden Rooster)
instructions: Saturate sugar cube with Peychaud's in base of shaker and MUDDLE thoroughly
Add cognac and stir to ensure sugar is dissolved
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE (without ice) and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Perhaps substitute ¼ shot demerara sugar syrup for the sugar cube and all that muddling. Whether runny or dry sugar, this flip-style cocktail is balanced and complex.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Timothy Eshing who says "'Le Coq d'Or' means 'the golden rooster' (hence the use of 'golden' ingredients); it is the title of a 1907 opera by Nicolai Rimsky-Korsakov, after a story by Pushkin, itself after a story by Irving. Outside Russia, the work is usually performed in French (hence the name and reliance on French ingredients). Each major ingredient is associated with a major character in the opera: the cognac and liqueur for the Tsar and Tsaritsa, the fruit juices for the Tsar's two doomed sons, the yolk for the golden rooster, and the bitters for the astrologer."

name: Don Pone
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
RINSE glass with maraschino liqueur (swirl around to coat and discard excess) and set to one side.
STIR other ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into maraschino rinsed glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered crystallised ginger and dust with grated dark chocolate.
review: Maraschino cherry aromas strike you first as you enjoy this cognac-laced, subtly chocolate and ginger after-dinner cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Pascal Pone at W Hotel, Verbier, Switzerland.

name: L'Heritage
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express orange zest twist over cocktail and discard. Garnish with maraschino cherry.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: Also, try this apéritif-style, bittersweet cocktail served on the rocks.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2015 by Pierre-Marie Bisson at Basement Sate in London, England.

name: Triomphe d'Anis (Triumph of Aniseed)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
RINSE glass with absinthe, discard excess and set to one side.
Separately, STIR other ingredients with ice in mixing glass.
STRAIN into absinthe-rinsed glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with star anise floated on surface.
review: A boozy stirred sipper, perhaps an after-dinner brandy-based cocktail, with hints of anise coffee and dry sherry complexity.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Isaac Windsor at The Milk Thistle in Bristol, England.

name: The Good, The Bad and the Ugni
instructions: SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass (preferably block of ice)
Lastly, FLOAT Chartreuse.
review: Perhaps it's the holier-than-thou Chartreuse that is playing the part of the bad guy in this tasty drink. The cognac (made with Ugni Blanc grapes) play the strong, characterful part.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Alex Mills at Lab 22 in Cardiff, Wales. Alex's original recipe calls for Martell Cordon Bleu.

name: Love On Sale
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Spiritous and fresh – if you like Natural Daiquiris then perhaps try this. The unaged rye spirit sits brilliantly with the vermouth and maraschino with the lemon enlivening the drink.
history: Created by Dirk Gueldner at the Pine Room, Bar de Lion, Hamburg.

name: White Old Fashioned
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: Joerg makes this cocktail using Bacardi Neo but it also works well with the easier to obtain Bacardi Carta Blanca. If you haven't any Sexy Bitters then try with a dash of orange bitters.
history:
Created in 2016 by Joerg Meyer at Le Lion - Bar de Paris, Hamburg, Germany.

For a more detailed history of the Old-Fashioned cocktail, along with additional recipes, see our page.

name: Gin Old Fashioned
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A couple of shots of bready genever with a spoon of sugar and a couple of dashes of bitters - incredibly simple, equally classic, and with the right genever, incredibly tasty.
history: For a detailed history of the Old-Fashioned cocktail, along with additional recipes, see our page.

name: Smoke and Mirrors (by The Bon Vivants)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: Genever and mezcal sit surprisingly well together in this very refreshing drink with mint and lemon.
history: Created in 2016 by The Bon Vivants, USA for Rutte Old Simon Genever.

name: Apairoteef
instructions: STIR all ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass (without ice)
(Note the sparkling wine should be flat.)
<strong>*Special ingredient #1</strong> = Cardamom and Pear Shrub made as follows:
400ml Merlet pare
300ml Gewürztraminer, Germain
7g Cardamom pods
5ml Vanilla curd
75ml White balsamic
10g Citric acid
10g Tartaric acid
150g Caster sugar
100g Water
Blend for 5 minutes and super strain.
review: Invigorating, refreshing and very amusing – we do like a pun.
history: Created in 2016 by Andy Mil at The Cocktail Trading Co., London, England

name: Yuliya Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: It's surprising what influence just half a barspoon of crème de pêche and a couple of dashes of absinthe has on this subtly flavoured vodka-based Martini.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Andrii Osypchuk at Gaucho, Dubai, UAE. The same year, Gary Regan selected the Yuliya Martini as one of his 101 Best New Cocktails. The original cocktail is shaken, not stirred, and topped with a splash of soda water.

name: Floral Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
RINSE chilled glass with violet liqueur, discard excess and set to one side.
MUDDLE blueberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into lavender-rinsed glass.
garnish: Garnish with lavender sprig pegged to rim.
review: A floral riff on a classic Daiquiri with a hint of berry fruitiness and a lavender pink hue courtesy of a blueberry threesome.
history: Created in 2016 by Andrii Osypchuk at Gaucho, Dubai, UAE.

name: Sleepy Smoker
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Smoked vodka delicately fortifies dry faintly salty manzanilla sherry with curaçao adding a touch of sweetness and zesty orange freshness.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2016 by Amelia Castro at Polite Provisions, San Diego, CA, USA.

name: Eight Foot Fizz
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: SHAKE first 6 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda water poured from a height to create a frothy head
Serve with straws
<strong>Special ingredient #1</strong> = Lemon sherbet
Blend the following for a couple minutes and fine strain
600g Caster sugar
600g Super strained lemon juice
20g Lemon zest (removed from fruit with Microplane grater)
5g Maldon sea salt
<strong>Special ingredient #2</strong> = Rose water and kiwi syrup
Blend the following and bottle
5g malic acid
100ml Catron kiwi liqueur
100ml Merlet pear liqueur
100ml Money musk coconut
25ml Absinthe
5g Maldon sea salt
100ml Orgeat syrup
50ml Campari
200ml Sugar syrup (1:1)
history: Created in 2016 by Andy Mil at The Cocktail Trading Co., London, England.

name: Rabo-de-Galo
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
THROW OR STIR (or even shake) all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: The bittersweet herbal notes from the vermouth and Cynar combine harmoniously with the cachaça. Depending on how punchy you like your cocktails you may want to increase or decrease the amount of cachaça (anywhere between 45 and 60ml).
history:
One of the best-known drinks in Brazil, Rabo de Galo originated in the mid-1950s after a Cinzano factory opened in São Paulo, leading to rosso (sweet) vermouth being mixed with the local cachaça.

'Rabo-de-galo' literally translates from Brazilian Portuguese as rooster tail or 'cock tail'. In Brazil, a cocktail is called 'coquetel'. Originally served as a shot in dive bars, which in Brazil are called pés-sujos (literally translates as 'dirty feet'), the proportions and the drink's ingredients vary greatly from bar to bar and region to region, but are generally accepted as being 2/3 cachaça and 1/3 sweet vermouth.

In São Paulo, it is now commonplace for the vermouth to be substituted with Cynar, or (as above) both sweet vermouth and Cynar to be mixed with the cachaça. As the Rabo-de-Galo has transitioned from pés-sujos to cocktail bars, so it has moved from being served straight-up in large tall shot glasses to tumblers and on-the-rocks, as above.

name: El Viajero
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
THROW all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of dust side of glass with coconut dust and garnish with lime zest twist.
review: Rum, coconut water and Pedro Ximénez sherry are unusual bedfellows in a cocktail but work well together and are toned by lime juice and chocolate bitters.
history: Originally based on Bacardi Carta Blanca rum, this was the Hungarian Bacardí Legacy 2016 winning cocktail, created by Norbert Schök at the Four Seasons Gresham Palace in Kollázs, Budapest.

name: Curtain Call
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE basil (just to bruise) in base of shaker.
Add next 4 ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: Garnish with skewered olives.
review: Rum, lemon, bitter liqueur and basil combine brilliantly with sparkling wine adding biscuity complexity, crispness and enlivening effervescence.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Arash Ghassemi at Green Door & Schwarze Traube, Berlin, and originally made with Bacardi Carta Blanca rum. This was the German Bacardí Legacy 2016 winning cocktail.

name: Anything Else
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: Zesty lemon, bitter liqueur and tangy IPA makes for a zippy, refreshing drink. A characterful IPA and nailing exactly the right amount of honey are key to this drink's success.
history: Originally made with Bacardi Carta Blanca, this was the Belarus Bacardí Legacy 2016 winning cocktail and was created by Lizaveta Molyavka at News Café in Minsk.

name: Champagne Cocktail
instructions: Select a FLUTE GLASS.
COAT sugar cube with bitters and drop into chilled glass.
POUR chilled cognac over soaked cube.
TOP with sparkling wine.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review:
Starts bone dry and becomes slightly sweeter as you reach the dissolving cube at the bottom, depending on how briskly you drink of course.

Back in the 1940s, David Embury, an American attorney and amateur bartender, wrote the most succinct instruction on how to make a Classic Champagne Cocktail in his The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks: "This drink should be served in a pre-chilled saucer champagne glass. Place a medium-sized loaf of sugar in the glass and saturate it with Angostura bitters - about 2 dashes. Fill with thoroughly chilled champagne. Add a twist of lemon or orange peel, or both.."

Embury also added his opinion of the drink, "From every point of view, other than cost, this cocktail is a decidedly inferior drink, and no true champagne lover would ever commit the sacrilege of polluting a real vintage champagne by dunking even plain sugar - much less bitters - in it. So if you must... serve this incongruous mess just for the sake of 'putting on the dog,' then, in the name of all that a true lover of the grape holds sacred, use a cheap domestic champagne or even an artificially carbonated white wine."

Tips on how to make
Despite Embury's protestations, "this incongruous mess" of a drink remains, some 70 years later, one of the most popular champagne cocktails and one of the most enduring classics. So it can't be that bad! Like most cocktails, it's all about the quality of ingredients used and how you use them. Follows eight tips for making a perfect Champagne Cocktail:

1. Use a well-chilled, clean glass pre-chilled in a freezer. A flute gives a better drinking experience, but a saucer looks more decadent.

2. As cocktail historian David Wondrich says, "Don't use loose sugar or try to crush the cube - the whole point isn't so much to sweeten the drink as to create bubbles, which the cube will do as it slowly dissolves." I prefer a rustic looking brown sugar cube.

3. Place a small napkin (bevnap) over the glass and place the cube on top of the napkin. Dash the bitters over the cube to soak the surface thoroughly. The napkin handily absorbs excess bitters and then works, with some dexterous finger work, to act as a stylish chute to direct the soaked cube into the glass.

4. Use good quality cognac/brandy and store it in a refrigerator or even freezer prior to use. Consider decanting some into a small bottle for such occasions. Pour a measure of the chilled cognac into the glass over the sugar cube. Room-temperature cognac acts as a heat bomb to wreck this cocktail.

5. "Some prefer an ice cube in theirs, which will (to state the bleeding obvious) prolong the chill at the cost of a certain dilution," again, to quote Wondrich. Ice is unnecessary if the sparkling wine, brandy and glass are well chilled.

6. The dry biscuity and citrus flavour of a good brut champagne/sparkling wine is integral to this cocktail. Tip the glass at a slight angle and pour the chilled wine down the inside of the glass before straightening the glass to finish the pour to create as little foam as possible. The cube will continuously generate bubbles, so a careful pour will help retain CO2 in the drink and add to the drinking experience.

7. As Embury says, "Add a twist of lemon or orange peel, or both." Discard twist(s) after expressing citrus oils.

8. Enjoy with friends. This cocktail is not suited to solo consumption and lends itself to a crowd. Depending on the size of your glasses, expect 6 - 8 servings per bottle of sparkling wine, or stretch to 10 if you're a tightwad.
history:
One of the oldest cocktails, dating back to at least the mid-1800s. The Champagne Cocktail consists of a sugar cube doused in aromatic bitters, dropped into the base of a flute, over which a small measure of brandy is poured before the glass is topped up with champagne - hence the name. Although now often made with other brut sparkling wines, it remains one of the most popular 'champagne' cocktails.

The origins of the Champagne Cocktail are lost in the mists of bartending history, with Wondrich saying it "dates from the Iron Age of American mixology - that final prehistoric period between the invention of the cocktail, whenever that was, and 1862 when the first cocktail book was published."

The first written mention of the Champagne Cocktail appears in the 'Panama in 1855. An Account of the Panama Rail-road, of the cities of Panama and Aspinwall with sketches of life and characters on the Isthmus by Robert Tomes'. Published 1855 in New York by Harper & Brothers. On page 61, Tomes writes, "I profess the belief that drinking Champagne cock-tails[sic] before breakfast, and smoking forty cigars daily, to be an immoderate enjoyment of the good things of this world." On the following page, he handily goes on to describe in some detail how a Champagne Cocktail is made:

name: Med Mex Cocktail
ingredients: Top up with Aegean Tonic Water
instructions: POUR ingredients into ice-filed glass and lightly stir.
history: Created in 2015 by Dimitris Dafopoulos, Athens, Greece.

name: Pink Americano
ingredients: Top up with Pink grapefruit soda
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Lightly stir.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
history: Created in 2015 by George Tsirikos, Athens, Greece.

name: Picasso Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of spray Angostura Bitters through stencil or drip a few drops on surface foam..
review:
We were struck by the photograph that accompanied the recipe sent to us by this cocktail's creator. The photo was of the cocktail at Burj Al Arab in Dubai, where it is served with a tiny wooden artist's colour palate alongside a micro easel holding a print of Picasso's Portrait of The Absinthe Drinker.

The cocktail itself is an artistic citrus-led riff on a Pisco Sour, but the original presentation amounts to true cocktail artistry.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2016 by Sean Halse at Gold On 27, Burj Al Arab, Dubai. Sean says, "The drink was designed to show the artistic side of the modern bartender." (His original recipe called for 40ml Lapostelle Pisco and 10ml 1:1 sugar syrup.)

name: Champs-Elysees
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A great after-dinner cocktail for lovers of brandy and Chartreuse. The sweet elements in this sour are cranked up but balanced by spiritous alcohol. Achieving perfect dilution in this classic is crucial to its deliciousness.
history: A riff on the Sidecar, The Champs-Élysées is named after the touristy Parisian avenue. The first known recipe for this cocktail appears in Nina Toye and Arthur H. Adair's 1925 Drinks-Long and Short.

name: Harvest Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Boozy with notes of spiced apple brandy. A great after-dinner sipper.

name: Daisy de Santiago
instructions: Select and pre-chill a WINE GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
DRIZZLE a spoon of liqueur over cocktail.
garnish: Garnish with berries and mint sprigs bouquet dusted with icing sugar (or lime wheel).
review: Chartreuse adds subtle aromatics to this refreshing herbal Daiquiri/Daisy.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Charles H. Baker, Jr.'s 1939 The Gentleman's Companion volume II – An Exotic Drinking Book.

name: Summer Americano
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
STIR first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring tonic water.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
review: Aperitif-style, tall and refreshing.
history: Created in May 2015 by Ignacio "Nacho" Vázquez, the Spanish Martini Brand Ambassador. Naturally Nacho originally used Martini Bitter.

name: 18th Century
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with (dehydrated) lime wheel floated on surface.
review: Sweet 'n' sour with the unmistakable funky twang of Batavia arrack.
history: A riff on the classic 20th Century Cocktail, created in 2008 by Phil Ward at Death & Co., New York City, USA.

name: The Stag
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with skewered crystallised ginger.
review: Boozy and stirred down. Cognac fortifies and combines harmoniously with the bittersweet herbal and spicy notes of the liqueur. Rich lavender enhances and amplifies these flavours with ginger bitters adding extra zingy spice and toning the blend. Best appreciated as an after-dinner digestif.
history: Created by yours truly in October 2016 at The Cabinet Room, London, after being inspired by the Élysée Treaty cocktail and appreciating how well Jägermeister mixes with brandy.

name: The Reindeer
ingredients: Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: MUDDLE ginger in base of shaker
Add next three ingredients (all but champagne), SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with champagne
<strong>Spicy sugar syrup</strong>
To make your own festive spice syrup: Stir half a cup of hot water and half a cup of sugar in a pan until sugar is dissolved
Add 1 cracked cinnamon stick, ½ a split vanilla pod and 1 teaspoon of cracked Szechuan peppers
Cover for 15 minutes then strain and cool liquid
If using this syrup in the recipe below then use 12.5ml.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2016 by Ketel One Brand Ambassador, Dave Beatty.

name: Knickerbocker
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass 2/3rd filled with cubed ice.
TOP up glass with crushed ice.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: Fruity and refreshing – a party drink with rum and raspberry flavours predominating. At its best when made with homemade raspberry syrup.
history:
The Knickerbocker was created sometime in the mid-19th century, and I have been one of those who previously went with the assumption that it was created at New York City's grand Knickerbocker Hotel. However, the luxurious Knickerbocker Hotel with its Beaux-Arts architecture was not built until 1906, while this drink first appears in print in 1862.

What is a knickerbocker?: The name 'Knickerbockers' originally referred to the style of pants, rolled up just below the knee, that Dutch settlers wore. (This is where the word 'knickers' comes from.)

In 1809, author Washington Irving wrote the satiric A History of New York under the pseudonym Diedrich Knickerbocker. The book became known as Knickerbocker's History of New York and the word 'knickerbocker' came to signify a New Yorker who could trace their ancestry to the original Dutch settlers. The term 'knickerbocker' became linked to anything from New York, including Jacob Ruppert's Knickerbocker Beer and the 1938 Broadway musical Knickerbocker Holiday.

So, the name suggests the Knickerbocker Cocktail was first made in New York, but by whom remains unknown. However, thanks to printed recipes, we do know how the drink evolved.

Cocktail's timeline: The Knickerbocker cocktail first appears in Jerry Thomas' 1862 The Bartender's Guide.

name: Perfume Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into shaker and STIR to incorporate honey.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf floated on the cocktail or pegged/stuck with spot of honey to the rim.
history: The Argentinean Bacardí Legacy 2015 winning cocktail created by Ezequiel Rodríguez at Victoria Brown Bar in Buenos Aires.

name: Empire of Dreams
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: A Pineapple Daiquiri with apricot and almond shaken with coffee beans. With good ice and a brisk shake, you'll be surprised how much the coffee beans are broken up, so also impacting this fruity cocktail. (Be sure to have a strainer in your sink when you dump the spoils from your tin.)
history: Australian Bacardí Legacy 2014 winning cocktail created, originally using Bacardi Carta Blanca, by Fred Siggins at The Black Pearl in Melbourne, Australia.

name: Nineteen Twenty Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with redcurrant bunch laid across rim.
review: Bite-sized and delicately flavoured – the combination of redcurrant syrup and kirsch is almost rose water-like, diluted by dry vermouth with a splash of gin adding botanical complexity.
history: The Nineteen-Twenty Cocktail first appears in Harry McElhone's 1922 Harry's ABC of Mixing Cocktails and also notably in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Dutch Artist's Special
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of redcurrant bunch laid across rim.
review: Bready genever is aromatised with sherry and kirsch while red currant syrup provides the main flavour with its richness balanced by lemon juice.
history:
A riff on the classic Artist's Special cocktail, which is based on Irish Whiskey. The only change is to use Dutch genever instead of whisky – hence the name. 

Created in January 2016 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Slow Negroni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: As the name suggests, this is a classic Negroni with sloe gin introduced as a fourth ingredient. Its flavour is exactly as it says on the tin – a sloe berry flavoured Negroni.

name: Gin Pahit
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: The balance of bitters to sugar is key to the success of this cocktail. Use a Japanese dasher bitters bottle and measure your 2:1 sugar syrup judiciously and you'll have made something special. Go easy while spraying the lemon zest over the drink – too much lemon oil will wreck its fine balance.
history: The name of this vintage variation on a Pink Gin cocktail is pronounced 'Gin Fight' in the Malay language ("pahit" means bitter).

name: Oaxacan Negroni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Mezcal lends a characterful smoky note to this complex riff on a classic Negroni.
history: Recipe adapted from one created by William Aporih at Añejo Tribeca. William’s original recipe called for ¾ Espolon Reposado mezcal, 1 oz Botanist Gin, ¾ oz Campari, ¾ oz Barolo Chinato and a splash of Cola Nut Extract.

name: Suze you, Sir
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: We do like a pun and appropriately the original recipe for this cocktail specifies Suze gentian liqueur. The flavour of gentian combines brilliantly with tequila while dry vermouth adds herbal complexity.
history: Created Rufus Grantham at Demon, Wise & Partners, London, England. Rufus' original recipe called for 2 Ocho blanco tequila, ½ Dolin dry vermouth, ½ Suze and 4 drops Bittermens hopped grapefruit bitters. The agave syrup was our own addition.

name: Maximiliano
instructions: STIR all ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass (preferably over a chunk of block ice.
review: The subtly smoky notes of mezcal combine brilliantly with the aromatic bittersweetness of gentian liqueur in this fabulously complex White Negroni-like aperitivo. (If using  dry vermouth in place of bianco vermouth, you'll need to add 2.5ml of rich sugar syrup.)
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2015 by Julio Xoxocotla at Ward III in New York City.

name: Tara Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: Imagine a delicately clove influenced Daiquiri but with the bready influence of poitín, then you are some way towards imagining the taste of this refreshing and invigorating cocktail.
history: Adapted from a drink created by Tommy Cummins and discovered in January 2017 at The Sun Tavern, Bethnal Green, London, England.

name: Yellow Cactus Flower
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Jordan Smith, describes his creation as "bright, herbaceous, subtly smoky, and refreshingly bittersweet." We concur!
history:
Named after the 1929 Georgia O'Keefe painting due to both the shades of yellow, white and green, and for the inclusion of a southwestern ingredient - mezcal.

Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Jordan Smith, Head Bartender at Le Coucou French restaurant in Soho, New York City.

name: Yellow Negroni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into chilled glass.
Fill glass with ice and briefly stir.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: As the name suggests, this pisco-based, five equal parts, vivid yellow, bittersweet aperitif was inspired by the classic Negroni, along with three of the world's most iconic yellow liqueurs: limoncello, gentian and Chartreuse Jaune.
history: Created 21st January 2017 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Cherry Blossom
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of skewered Luxardo Maraschino Cherry with cherry blossom when in season.
review: Kirsh eau-de-vie and grappa give depth of flavour to vodka as a base in this cherry blossom and maraschino sour.
history: Created by yours truly as a refresh of an old recipe. The refresh became a remake.

name: Kula Negroni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with strawberry.
review: If you haven't been organised or committed to infusing strawberries in advance, then muddle a couple of small ripe strawberries in the base of the stirring glass before adding other ingredients and, after stirring with ice, fine strain into ice-filled glass.
history: Adapted from a cocktail created in 2017 by Julie Reiner at Clover Club in Manhattan, New York City.

name: Milanese G&T
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice wheel.
review: Depending on how you view this, it's a traditional Gin & Tonic with a splash of bitter liqueur adding colour and herbal fruity complexity. Or a G&T take on the classic Negroni cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2016 at Campari's then-London office in The Shard.

name: Cadáver Reviver
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: The combination of tequila and triple sec make this riff on the classic Corpse Reviver remarkably Margarita-like, only with added complexity and subtlety due to the aromatised wine and faintest hint of absinthe.
history: Created in February 2017 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Mexican Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Considering tequila is replacing vodka in this French Martini riff, you'd think that when it came to tequila, a blanco tequila would be best suited. However, having tried this and then reposado tequila, unexpectedly I found myself turning towards and favouring añejo as being best suited to this cocktail's tropical and berry fruitiness.
history: Created in February 2017 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Last Palabra
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: Tequila replaces gin in this riff on the classic Last Word, with the amount of maraschino slightly reduced to accommodate the introduction of lavender syrup, which in turn is also balanced by a dash of lavender bitters.
history:
Created in February 2017 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.
Last Word history

name: Cherry Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry &/or lemon zest twist.
review: Rich cherry is balanced by botanically complex dry gin and citrus freshness, and further dried and deepened by vermouth.
history: Created in 2005 by yours truly in London, England. Originally based on citrus vodka, I amended this cocktail to be gin-based in May 2020 after the creation of my vodka-based Cherry Fruitini.

name: Dama Blanca
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: This tequila (in place of gin) based riff on a White Lady is basically a straight-up Margarita made with lemon rather than lime, softened and smoothed by egg white. This is a delightful drink. Go easy spraying the zest, as this can overly dominate the drink. It also works best with light agave syrup, as the darker versions affect its virgin-like hue and can add unwanted caramel notes.
history: Created in February 2017 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Negroni Bianco
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Lightly stir.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: This white Negroni is not quite as dry and bitter as a traditional red Negroni and has additional pleasing creamy honeysuckle notes.
history: The creation of this cocktail was inspired by Wayne Collins' White Negroni and made possible by the launch of Luxardo Bitter Bianco liqueur in 2016.

name: Amber Negroni (D's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Lightly stir.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: As the name suggests, this riff on a classic Negroni is amber in colour with honeyed notes and is not quite as bitter as the classic red version.
history: Created in February 2017 by yours truly after the launch of Luxardo's Bitter Bianco liqueur.

name: Tuxedo Affumicato
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist and a mist of Liquore Strega from an atomizer over drink.
review: Mezcal lends a lightly smoky note to this riff on the classic Tuxedo. This boozy Martini-esque cocktail makes for a great aperitif.
history:
Created in 2016 by Riccardo Aletta (AKA 'Ricky D Wing') at Holy Birds in London, England.
See: Tuxedo cocktail history

name: Ginger Last Word
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE ginger with gin in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: As the name suggests this is simply a Last Word with a hint of ginger. Simple can be beautiful.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2016 by Riccardo Aletta (AKA 'Ricky D Wing') at Holy Birds in London, England. Ricky calls this cocktail a "Vegan Last Word," but we thought 'Ginger' better encapsulated what was in the tin.
Last Word history

name: Creme Egg Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with chocolate grated over cocktail and skewered Creme Egg.
review: As befits the name, this chocolate and vanilla vodka-based Easter Egg of a cocktail has both cream and egg – the later in the form of Dutch advocaat.

name: Jäger Negroni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A lightly spicy Negroni perfectly suited to the after-dinner "digestive" moment. If it doesn't aid your digestion it will aid your sleep.
history: This cocktail emerged in London in 2016, originally with equal parts Sacred Gin, Sacred Sweet Vermouth and Jägermeister (no Italian red bitter) aged for one week in medium toasted oak barrel.

name: Duchess
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Aniseed and liquorice notes from absinthe pervade this vermouth-based cocktail, even though I have dramatically reduced the amount used from the original recipe which calls for equal parts of each ingredient. The Duchess is a cocktail for absinthe lovers.
history: Adapted from a recipe in the 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book which specifies one-third of each: absinthe, French vermouth and dry vermouth with the instruction to "stir well and strain into cocktail glass." The recipe for the Duchess Cocktail in the 1931 The Old Waldorf-Astoria Bar Book calls for the same trio of equal parts ingredients but with the addition of a dash of orange bitters and the indication that the drink should be served frappé. Interestingly, the instruction to serve "frappé" is omitted from the 1935 third edition of The Old Waldorf-Astoria Bar Book.

name: Smoking Gun Vesper
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist and small strip cured smoked pork on a skewer..
review: Delicately smoky and almost meaty – be sure to serve with cured meat. A great aperitif cocktail.
history:
Adapted from a drink created in 2016 by Dale DeGroff, King Cocktail, New York City, USA.

This recipe is courtesy of gazregan.com. Dale's original recipe calls for: 1½ oz Chase English Oak Smoked Vodka, ½ oz Tanqueray No. TEN Gin, ¼ oz La Quintinye Vermouth Royal Dry Vermouth and 1 dash Bitter Truth's Jerry Thomas Own Decanter Bitters. Dale's preferred garnish is a small strip cured smoked pork on a skewer.

More information on the story behind Bond's Vesper can be found on our Vesper cocktail page page.

name: Celery Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of celery stick.
review: This aptly named drink is beautifully balanced with delicate celery flavours.
history: A cocktail named the Celery Sour first appears in Tom-Bullock's 1917 book, The Ideal Bartender.

name: Celery Gimlet No.2
instructions: MUDDLE celery with salt in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass.
review: Sweet 'n' sour, salt 'n' vinegar served with dry celery gin crunch – a cocktail to awaken your senses.
history: A riff on the classic Gimlet, this recipe is adapted from Naren Young's Celery Gimlet and inspired by salt and vinegar flavours.

name: Espresso Martini (simple 3 equal parts recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with 3 coffee beans floated on the surface to represent health, wealth and happiness.
review: Balanced, tasty and enlivening, this cocktail is incredibly quick and easy to make using just three ingredients that should be every day essentials in every domestic kitchen.
history: This simplified version of Dick Bradsell's Espresso Martini (or Vodka Espresso) is preferred to the original recipe by some due to its simple equal parts formula and omission of sugar syrup. (If using a dry coffee liqueur, you'll need to add 5ml to 7.5ml sugar syrup, preferably demerara syrup.)

name: Angels' Advocate
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest over cocktail and then use as a garnish to form a cross shape on the cocktail.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist (cut to be used to form a cross).
review: Advocaat, gin, and lemon juice harmoniously combine in this pale yellow cocktail. Vanilla syrup adds balance, and cardamom enlivens the blend.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Tess Posthumus in Amsterdam, The Netherlands.

name: Chiclet Daiquiri
instructions: BLEND all ingredients with 6oz scoop of crushed ice and serve in a chilled glass.
review: A wonderfully refreshing drink on a summer's day with surprisingly subtle flavours.
history: Often found on Cuban bar menus, this was created at La Floridita, Havana.

name: Black Mesa
ingredients: 15 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail, then use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Pungent, funky and boozy with harmonious rum-dominated bitter sweetness.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Damon Boelte at Grand Army, Brooklyn, New York, USA. Damon's original recipe called for El Dorado 12 year old Demerara rum and Smith & Cross Jamaican rum.

name: Dutch Coupe
instructions: STIR all ingredient with ice and strain into chilled glass.
review: Reminiscent of a genever Manhattan served on the dry side with notes of carciofo amaro.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created by Tess Posthumus, Amsterdam, The Netherlands. Originally based on Bols Barrel Aged genever.

Tess says, "I created this cocktail in honour of the Dutch Queen Beatrix abdicating the throne. She willingly resigned and passed the crown to her son, King Willem Alexander, but as the drink also comes in a coupe glass I thought the name 'Dutch Coupe' would fit perfectly. It's a slightly bitter drink with orange notes and genever based.

"I used Genever as it's the spirit of the Netherlands. Try to use an aged old genever or corenwyn style genever, as the drink needs the malty backbone to support the other ingredients. I've included orange because our royal family is called 'van Oranje', the House of Orange. Back in the days the Dutch would drink oranjebitter at royal celebrations, which is a bitter orange liqueur. So this celebration cocktail needed to have some bitter notes as well. Inspired by Ada Coleman's Hanky Panky, another great woman in history, I think this drink is fit for a Queen. The only thing missing was Queen Beatrix' feathery hat, a signature of her. So, try to replicate this by garnishing the Dutch Coupe with a pretty 'feathery' orange zest."

name: Chihuahua Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with grapefruit slice wheel.
review: Tequila and grapefruit juice pepped up with a splash of agave nectar and dash of bitters. A tart and delicious tequila sour to pucker the palate.
history: Named after both the Mexican region of the same name and the dog breed, the Chihuahua is a Greyhound with tequila replacing gin.

name: The Artist's Muse
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of atomise absinthe over surface of cocktail.
review: Bittersweet and fruity, with complex aromatics from the pisco and strong flavours of gentian liqueur.
history: Created in 2016 by Maggie Eckl at The Dead Rabbit, New York City, USA. The Artist's Muse is named due to its originally being made with Suze gentian, a brand identified with artists and the arts - a bottle of Suze is depicted in a painting by Pablo Picasso.

name: The Ribbon
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Dry sherry and bitter orange with underlying ripe grape sweetness, The Ribbon makes for a perfect aperitif cocktail.
history:
The Ribbon was created in 2017 at Swift in Soho, London, England, and was originally made with 40% alc./vol. Solerno blood orange liqueur and Cocchi Storico Vermouth di Torino rosso vermouth. My previous interpretation of their recipe comprised:
20ml (2/3oz) Fino sherry
20ml (2/3oz) Cocchi Torino vermouth
10ml (1/3oz) Solerno
10ml Campari liqueur
Due to the demise of the availability of Solerno liqueur in the UK, in August 2022, at Swift Bar in Shoreditch, London, I discovered they had switched to Grand Marner so I also adapted my recipe.

name: The Last Time (AKA Stop Fiddling With My Drink)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of square of white chocolate balanced on rim.
review: Richly flavoured and yet dry – rum and subtle desiccated coconut with monastic herbal sweetness dried by aromatic fino sherry and vanilla bitters.
history: Created in April 2017 by Paloma, my better half, at the Cabinet Room in London, England. Named after she instructed yours truly to stop trying to make the drink she'd just created better.

name: She's Like
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: An unlikely combination of big rich flavours, but when served ice-cold this is either a very interesting Mediterranean aperitivo or a palate-cleansing digestivo.
history: Created in 2016 by Ilias Marinakis at 42 Bar, Athens, Greece.

name: Via Roma
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: This aperitivo cocktail will appeal to lovers of Italian amari.
history: Created in 2017 by Mattia Conti at London Cocktail Club, Shoreditch, London, England. Originally named Bartender's Elixir.

name: Pass the Dutchie
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review:
Like a bottle of port, please be sure to pass this cocktail to the left, or at least drink using your left hand. You'll be rewarded with a spirituous bready vinous zesty orange flavoured cocktail that suits late-night imbibing.

Challenging right-handed drinkers to maintain drinking only using their left hand throughout the night makes for a great drinking game. Next round is on the first person to impulsively use the wrong hand.
history:
Created November 2016 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England and originally served on-the-rocks. Updated to a straight-up serve in September 2019.

 As for the name, Google "Pass the Dutchie" (with a 't') by Musical Youth. Incidentally, a "dutchie" is a cast iron cooking pot (Dutch oven) and the song lyrics are, "Pass the dutchie 'pon de left 'and side...how does it feel when ya got no food?"

name: For Sake's Sake
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Float (dehydrated) apple slice for garnish.
review: Being dyslexic, I'm bewildered as to how to spell dyslexic while also amused by sake (the beverage brewed from rice) being spelt the same way as sake (as in for f**k's sake!). Hopefully, you'll find this aromatic, subtly orange-influenced sake and rum-based Martini-like cocktail both delightful to drink and easy to spell.
history: Created in April 2017 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Old Hickory (Britten's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: Heavy doses of Creole-style bitters and orange bitters work with dry vermouth to balance this drink's rich sweet vermouth base. A great aperitivo.
history:
Adapted from a 2017 recipe by Maxwell Britten at Maison Premiere, Brooklyn, NYC, USA. Britten's drink is riff from the Old Hickory cocktail in Stanley Clisby Arthur's 1937 Famous New Orleans Drinks and How to Mix 'Em.

'Old Hickory' was the nickname of Andrew Jackson, the seventh President of the USA (1829 to 1837) and founder of the Democratic Party. Before becoming president, Jackson was a general in the U.S. army who, despite orders to the contrary, in 1812 led his soldiers on a long march from New Orleans to Nashville with a toughness of discipline and resolve that drove his men to start calling him Hickory, a name that stuck and later became Old Hickory. To quote Famous Old New Orleans Drinks, "When General Andrew Jackson was in New Orleans helping pirate Jean Lafitte win the Battle of New Orleans in the winter of 1814-15, this was his favourite tipple."

name: Blueberry Caipirinha
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
CUT lime in half (from pole to pole) and then slice one of the halves into eight 3-4mm thick half-moon shaped segments and drop into the base of a robust rocks glass.
Add 3 bar spoons caster sugar (or ½oz / 15ml sugar syrup 2:1) and MUDDLE.
Add blueberries and MUDDLE some more.
When sure fruit juice is fully extracted and the sugar dissolved, transfer the muddled fruit and sugar into serving glass (preferably a 30cl / 10oz highball glass).
POUR cachaça (stored in the freezer) into the rocks glass (used to muddle) and swirl to rinse glass before pouring cachaça into serving glass. STIR to combine all ingredients.
Fill glass with ice. (If using large ice cubes, crack (rather than crush) four to five large ice cubes using an ICE TAPPER and drop into serving glass.)
STIR before topping with more ice and serve with a stirrer (but no straws).
STIR before topping with more ice.
garnish: Garnish with stirrer (but no straws).
history: For information on the history and other variations of the Caipirinha, please see our Caipirinha cocktail page.

name: Strawberry and Basil Caipirinha
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
CUT lime in half (from pole to pole) and then slice one of the halves into eight 3-4mm thick half-moon shaped segments and drop into the base of a robust rocks glass.
Add 3 bar spoons powdered sugar [or 15ml (½oz) sugar syrup 2:1] and MUDDLE.
Add strawberries and basil, and MUDDLE some more.
When sure fruit juice is fully extracted and the sugar dissolved, transfer the muddled fruit and sugar into serving glass (preferably a 30cl / 10oz highball glass).
POUR cachaça (stored in the freezer) into the rocks glass (used to muddle) and swirl to rinse glass before pouring cachaça into serving glass. STIR to combine all ingredients.
Fill glass with ice. (If using large ice cubes, crack (rather than crush) four to five large ice cubes using an ICE TAPPER and drop into serving glass.)
STIR before topping with more ice.
garnish: Garnish with stirrer and serve without a straw.
review: Succulent ripe strawberries are delicious in a Caipirinha but so much better with basil.
history: See Caipirinha history and other variations.

name: Bamboo Cocktail (Stuart's 1904 recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Balanced and complex, heavier than Bamboo recipes with dry vermouth.
history:
Adapted from a recipe in Thomas Stuart's 1904 Stuart's Fancy Drinks and How to mix them.
Bamboo Cocktail history.

name: Bamboo Cocktail (with Palo Cortado Sherry)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Dry, vinous, and best enjoyed as an aperitif.
history: Bamboo Cocktail history.

name: Kiwi Caipirinha
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
CUT lime in half (from pole to pole) and then slice one of the halves into eight 3-4mm thick half-moon shaped segments and drop into the base of a robust rocks glass.
Add 3 bar spoons of caster sugar (or ½oz/15ml sugar syrup 2:1) and MUDDLE.
Add kiwi slices and MUDDLE some more.
