When sure fruit juice is fully extracted and the sugar dissolved, transfer the muddled fruit and sugar into serving glass (preferably a 30cl / 10oz highball glass).
POUR cachaça (stored in the freezer) into the rocks glass (used to muddle) and swirl to rinse glass before pouring cachaça into serving glass.
STIR to ensure all ingredients are well mixed.
Fill glass with ice. (If using large ice cubes, crack (rather than crush) four to five large ice cubes using an ICE TAPPER and drop into serving glass.)
STIR before topping with more ice.
garnish: Garnish with stirrer (but no straws).
review: Fruity and refreshing, perfect for a summer afternoon.
history: Caipirinha cocktail history.

name: Chimayo
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with red apple slice on rim.
review: I used to make this cocktail with reposado tequila, but it's cleaner and better with blanco tequila. Either way, cassis and apple juice mellow tequila.
history: Named after El Potrero de Chimayo in northern New Mexico, USA.

name: Bamboo Cocktail (Boothby's 1908 recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist (discarded) and Fragata Green Olive - preferably pimiento-stuffed ('pimola' olive).
review: This bone dry cocktail makes for a great apéritif, but you may find the Bamboo 'Perfect' version of this cocktail easier going.
history:
Adapted from a recipe in William T. Boothby's 1908 World Drinks and How to Mix Them.
Bamboo Cocktail history.

name: Let's Go Bananas
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE banana slices with rum in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with a dried banana chip
review: Fresh and enlivening. This banana cocktail is a delight and makes for a great and most unusual aperitivo. Be sure your bananas are ripe/overripe - you'll be surprised how much flavour just three slices contribute, not to mention how much they clog your strainers.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created for Bacardí Legacy 2017 by Nicolas Munoz at Bespoke Cocktail Bar in Paris, France. The original recipe: 3 slices ripe banana, 60ml Bacardí Carta Blanca rum, 15ml Noilly Prat Original Dry, 20ml lime juice, 7.5ml sugar syrup, and 1 spoon pastis.

Nicolas says, "With this cocktail, I wished to celebrate the return of 'fun' in establishments offering quality cocktails. What better than bananas to symbolize this - served in a coupette, a glass historically associated not only with parties and fun, but also with quality and class. This cocktail wonderfully complements the notes of the Bacardí Carta Blanca rum. Cheers and... let's go bananas!"

name: Electric Avenue
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR first 4 ingredients into chilled glass.
Two-thirds FILL glass with crushed ice.
CHURN (stir) with bar spoon.
Fill glass with more crushed ice and briefly CHURN.
FLOAT port.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: Very fruity with pineapple and citrusy rum sipped through port wine. A refreshing, easy-drinking crowd-pleaser.
history: Created for Bacardí Legacy 2017 by Conor Myers at The Exchequer in Dublin, Ireland.

name: Elixir Tropical
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: There's a load of orgeat (almond syrup) in this Daiquiri-style cocktail, but it's only just on the sweet side of balanced with that almond richness contributing to its tropical character.
history: Created for Bacardí Legacy 2017 by Maurizio la Spina at Bartender's Italia, Naples, Italy.

name: Showgirl Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with edible flowers.
review: Wonderfully floral – flavours of rose and raspberry dominate with dry vermouth adding balance and complexity.
history:
Created in 2017 at the Beaufort Bar, The Savoy Hotel, London, to celebrate Marilyn Monroe and her stay at the hotel in 1956. The American actress held a press conference at the Hotel where she stayed while in England to film The Prince and the Showgirl with English actor and director Laurence Olivier.

The Beaufort Bar's original recipe calls for an infusion of rosebuds, rose petals and hibiscus flowers and uses Dom Perignon Champagne and a "homemade Aperol Cordial".

name: Pomegranate Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime wedge and pomegranate seeds.
review: Freshly pressed pomegranate juice is essential to the success of this deliciously fruity but dry Daiquiri. Sadly, packaged juice doesn't produce a cocktail of comparable quality.
history: Created in May 2017 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Courtside
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: MUDDLE berries in base of shaker
Add next five ingredients (all but soda), SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda.
review: Easy drinking, fruity, refreshing and lightly sweet. Bring on the summer's afternoon.

name: Watermelon Caipirinha
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.

Cut a ripe lime in half (from pole to pole) and remove the core from each half by cutting a V-shaped groove. (You'll need one-half of the lime per cocktail.) CHOP into wedges and each wedge chopped in half before dropping the resulting 6 or 8 lime chunks into the base of shaker.
Add 3 bar spoons caster sugar (or ½oz / 15ml sugar syrup 2:1) and MUDDLE.
Add watermelon and MUDDLE some more.
When sure fruit juice is fully extracted and the sugar dissolved, TRANSFER the muddled fruit and sugar into serving glass (preferably a 30cl / 10oz hi-ball glass).
POUR cachaça (stored in the freezer) into the rocks glass (used to muddle) and swirl to rinse glass before pouring cachaça into serving glass.
STIR to ensure all ingredients are well mixed.
Fill glass with ice. (If using large ice cubes, crack (rather than crush) four to five large ice cubes using an ICE TAPPER and drop into serving glass.)
Briefly STIR before topping with more ice.
garnish: Garnish with stirrer (but no straws).
review: The melon flavours are very subtle in this delicious Caipirinha.

name: Melon Caipirinha
instructions: <strong>1.</strong> Cut lime in half (from pole to pole) and then slice one of the halves into eight 3-4mm thick half-moon shaped segments and drop into the base of a robust rocks glass
<strong>2.</strong> Add 3 bar spoons caster sugar (or ½oz / 15ml sugar syrup 2:1) and MUDDLE
<strong>3.</strong> Add melon and MUDDLE some more
<strong>4.</strong> When sure fruit juice is fully extracted and the sugar dissolved, transfer the muddled fruit and sugar into serving glass (preferably a 30cl / 10oz hi-ball glass)
<strong>5.</strong> POUR cachaça (stored in the freezer) into the rocks glass (used to muddle) and swirl to rinse glass before pouring cachaça into serving glass
STIR to ensure all ingredients are well mixed
<strong>6.</strong> Crack (rather than crush) four to five large ice cubes using an <a href="/bar-equipment-and-barware/250/ice-tapper">Ice Tapper</a> and drop into serving glass
STIR before topping with more ice and serve with a stirrer (but no straws).
review: Exactly as the name suggests, a fruity, melon flavoured Caipirinha.
history: For information on the history and other variations of the Caipirinha, please see our Caipirinha cocktail page.

name: Melon and Apple Caipirinha
instructions: <strong>1.</strong> Cut lime in half (from pole to pole) and then slice one of the halves into eight 3-4mm thick half-moon shaped segments and drop into the base of a robust rocks glass
<strong>2.</strong> Add 3 bar spoons caster sugar (or ½oz / 15ml sugar syrup 2:1) and MUDDLE
<strong>3.</strong> Add melon and MUDDLE some more
<strong>4.</strong> When sure fruit juice is fully extracted and the sugar dissolved, transfer the muddled fruit and sugar into serving glass (preferably a 30cl / 10oz hi-ball glass)
<strong>5.</strong> POUR cachaça (stored in the freezer) into the rocks glass (used to muddle) and swirl to rinse glass before pouring cachaça into serving glass
Add apple juice and STIR to ensure all ingredients are well mixed
<strong>6.</strong> Crack (rather than crush) four to five large ice cubes using an <a href="/bar-equipment-and-barware/250/ice-tapper">Ice Tapper</a> and drop into serving glass
STIR before topping with more ice and serve with a stirrer (but no straws).
review: Melon and lime dominate this deliciously fruity Caipirinha.
history: For information on the history and other variations of the Caipirinha, please see our Caipirinha cocktail page.

name: Pineapple Caipirinha
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
CUT lime in half (from pole to pole) and then slice one of the halves into eight 3-4mm thick half-moon shaped segments and drop into the base of a robust rocks glass.
Add 3 bar spoons caster sugar (or ½oz / 15ml sugar syrup 2:1) and MUDDLE.
Add pineapple and MUDDLE some more.
When sure fruit juice is fully extracted and the sugar dissolved, TRANSFER the muddled fruit and sugar into serving glass (preferably a 30cl / 10oz hi-ball glass).
POUR cachaça (stored in the freezer) into the rocks glass (used to muddle) and swirl to rinse glass before pouring cachaça into serving glass.
STIR to ensure all ingredients are well mixed.
FILL glass with small cubes or cracked ice. (If using large ice cubes, crack (rather than crush) four to five large ice cubes using an ICE TAPPER and drop into serving glass.)
Briefly STIR before topping with more ice.
garnish: Garnish with stirrer and serve without a straw.
review: Natural acids from the pineapple make this tarter in flavour than some other fruit Caipirinhas, those with a sweet tooth may want to add an extra ½ spoon of sugar.
history: For information on the history and other variations of the Caipirinha, please see our Caipirinha cocktail page.

name: Brandy Fruit Cup (D's Cup No.3)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into glass.
Half fill glass with ice.
Add a citrus slice and a couple of mint leaves from garnish
Fill to brim with ice.
garnish: Finish with rest of garnish.
review: Cognac works well in this fruit cup, rounding the other ingredients to present as a more harmonious blend when compared to the same recipe made with gin. However, it's more of a winter's cup, without the freshening edge expected of a summer's fruit cup.
history: Created in June 2017 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England. For more recipes and history see Fruit Cups and Pimm's.

name: Vodka Fruit Cup (D's Cup No.6)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into glass.
Half fill glass with ice.
Add a citrus slice and a couple of mint leaves from garnish
Fill to brim with ice.
garnish: Finish with rest of garnish.
review: Vodka's clean neutrality allows the fruity flavours in this summery cocktail to shine. Of the original line-up of seven different Pimm's spirit bases, only No.1 (gin-based) still serves but No.6, which had a vodka base, clung onto life longer than the other five.
history: Created in June 2017 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England. For more recipes and history see Fruit Cups and Pimm's.

name: Genever Fruit Cup (D's Cup No.12)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into glass.
Half fill glass with ice.
Add a citrus slice and a couple of mint leaves from garnish
Fill to brim with ice.
garnish: Finish with rest of garnish.
review: Given the use of genever and orange curaçao, this cocktail should be named "Dutch Fruit Cup". Genever's sweet maltiness adds a bready character to this fruit cup.
history: Created in June 2017 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England. For more recipes and history see Fruit Cups and Pimm's.

name: Pineapple Martini (1990s recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge on rim.
review: This cocktail is deliciously simple, fruity, and ridiculously easy to drink. The quality and freshness of your pineapple juice are key to this cocktail's success, so muddling fresh pineapple instead of using juice also works well.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 1997 by Ben Reed at the Met Bar in London, England. Ben has appointed, or should I say anointed, the 26th June as International Pineapple Martini Day.

name: Rhubarb Negroni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Rabarbaro amaro influences this Negroni, making it a tad drier and punchier than standard three ingredient Negronis.
history: Created in 2009 by Paolo Tonellotto at The Hide Bar, London, England.

name: Paisley Spritz
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Lightly stir.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
review: The sweet honeyed spice notes of Scottish liqueur combine harmoniously with the gentle acidity of the wine, lengthened and freshened with ginger ale.
history:
Created in October 2013 by Simon D at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

Our Spritz page contains information on the history of, and other recipes for, the Spritz family of cocktails.

name: Le Sang Jaune (The Yellow Blood)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
Express lemon and orange zest twists over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon and orange zest twists.
review: Spirituous apple and rich elderflower with herbal dry vermouth and gentian complexity, finished with the merest hint of anise courtesy of a dash of absinthe.
history: Adapted from a cocktail created in 2017 by Mauricio Barbosa at Tuju in São Paulo, Brazil. Mauricio's original recipe called for 50ml Calvados, 20ml Noilly Prat, 15ml St-Germain, 5ml homemade gentian liqueur and 5 drops absinthe bitters.

name: Czechmate
instructions: SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with pilsner.
review: The herbal notes of Becherovka are noted for combining harmoniously with beer, fittingly in this recipe a pilsner (Becherovka is Czech, as are the origins of pilsner). This combo is fortified with vodka and freshened with lemon juice to produce a long refreshing cocktail which makes a great alternative to merely drinking beer.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Jim Meehan in 2015 for a seminar he was presenting in Prague.

name: Shadow Boxer
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: As the name suggests this is a punchy cocktail – spirituous and bittersweet with a long bitter finish. Far from being a crowd-pleaser.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2016 by Ivy Mix at Leyenda, New York City, USA. The original recipe calls for Dolin Dry Vermouth, Giffard Pamplemousse and Blume Apricot Eau de Vie.

name: French 75 (Judge Jr's 1927 recipe)
instructions: STIR powdered sugar with lemon juice in base of shaker until sugar dissolves
Add gin, SHAKE with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with champagne and lightly stir.
review: Judge Jr's original 1927 recipe calls for "2 jiggers Gordon water; 1 part lemon juice; a spoonful of powdered sugar; cracked ice. Fill up the rest of a tall glass with champagne!" I've loosely interpreted this recipe to the one above (with the 3 spoons of powdered sugar being flat 5ml measuring spoons). The result is dangerously quaffable gin spiked champagne with a zesty lemon bite. The powered sugar adds an attractive sherbet note, but if you prefer to use sugar syrup then substitute with ½oz/15ml (2:1).
history:
Adapted from a recipe in the 1927 book Here's How by Judge Jr..
See: French 75 history.

name: Lemon Sherbet Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lemon sorbet balls.
review: This Margarita does indeed have a sherbet flavour with a lemony citrus bite.

name: Brazuca Remedy
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SOUR OR MARTINI/COUPETTE GLASS.
MUDDLE basil in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with a basil leaf boat floated on surface.
review: Bittersweet and complex with underlying cachaça character. Perfect as an aperitivo.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Joao Paulo Balzani at Aqua Spirit in Hong Kong, China.

name: Renaissance
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Spirituous and bittersweet with a cleansing citrusy bite and underlying cognac complexity, this cocktail is suited to both aperitif and after-dinner occasions.

name: Red Earl
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE raspberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with raspberry floated in centre of cocktail.
review: This vodka-laced, rich (but not overly sweet) raspberry cocktail is a crowd-pleaser.

name: French 125 (French '75' with cognac) Jones' 1977 recipe
instructions: STIR powdered sugar with lemon juice in base of shaker until sugar dissolves
Add cognac, SHAKE with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with champagne and lightly stir.
review: Jones' original 1977 built in the glass recipe, which consists: 2oz/60ml cognac, 1oz/30ml lemon juice and 2 spoons sugar, topped with champagne, is a powerful drink. Hence, we hope Jones would approve of our tweaking his recipe. The result still has plenty of oomph behind it, but with the extra dilution of shaking, is a tad more harmonious.
history:
A cognac-based version of the gin-based French 75 cocktail, which was named after the French 75mm field gun used by the French army during WWI. This cognac version is appropriately named after another gun, the tank mounted Soviet 2A46 (also called D-81T) 125mm/L48 smoothbore cannon. This recipe is adapted from a recipe in Stanley M. Jones' 1977 book Jones Complete Barguide, modern French 125 recipes tend to be served straight-up in a flute glass.

French 75 history and variations

name: Old Friend
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review:
Tart, dry and lightly bitter, but with just enough underlying sweetness to stroke the palate and make another sip so very desirable – a complex, bittersweet taste sensation. Brilliant as an aperitif on a hot summer's evening.

Jim's original recipe calls for 7.5ml (¼oz) elderflower liqueur, but in October 2020, due to popular demand (see comments below), I amended the above recipe to have twice the volume of elderflower liqueur. Then, in June 2022, also in the pursuit of balance and pleasing the tastes of as many as possible, I nudged this back to  10ml (1/3oz). Given variations in citrus, I believe this is my final amendment.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Jim Meehan in 2012 for the cocktail menu at Chef's Club in Aspen, Colorado.

name: Pineapple Daiquiri (simple but tasty recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Light rum provides vibrancy while aged rum adds rich complexity. Pineapple juice adds tropical fruitiness and replaces the dilution necessary to open and amplify a classic Daiquiri.
history: Recipe created in July 2017 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Fin de Siècle (End of Century) Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Dry, spirituous and zesty, this is a cocktail that lends itself equally to apéritif or after-dinner occasions.
history: In his 1935 The Old Waldorf Astoria Bar Book A.S. Crockett describes the Fin de Siècle as a very different drink to the above, a "Tom Collins with grenadine and raspberry syrup." He adds, "Name dates it back to 1899 or 1900, when the term was much used, but much mispronounced." In the "Baptismal" chapter he says, "The Fin de Siècle came toward the end of the century, when the expression became current in magazines and newspapers, and when lots of Americans were taking their first steps in French. What they said when they meant to order such a cocktail is another matter."

name: Beginning of the End
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
EXPRESS a flamed orange zest twist into empty chilled glass to line the inside with smoky oils.
STIR other ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into pre-prepared glass.
EXPRESS flamed lemon zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist (flamed).
review: Burnt orange oils add smoky notes, complexity and balance to this bittersweet drink. A perfect after-dinner cocktail.
history: Created in 2008 by Paul Mathew at The Hide, London, originally for the Boutique Bar Show "Free Spirit" cocktail competition. Based on the classic Fin de Siècle, but using rum in place of gin. Paul's original recipe called for: 45ml Diplomatico Reserva Exclusiva, 15ml Antica Formula, 10ml Amer Picon and Palo Cortado Viejo sherry.

name: Slow Comfortable Screw Against a Cold Hard Wall
instructions: STIR first four ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
Then FLOAT Galliano and overproof rum.
review: Sloe gin is the 'Slow', Sothern Comfort is the 'Comfortable', vodka and orange juice are the 'Screw' (as in Screwdriver), ice is the 'Cold', overproof rum the 'Hard' and Galliano the 'Wall' (as in Harvey Wallbanger). The result is a much better drink than the sleazy name might suggest, at least it is when freshly squeezed orange juice is used.
history: Origins and evolution of the "Slow Screw" family of cocktails.

name: Slow Comfortable Screw Against The Wall With A Bang
instructions: STIR first five ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
Finish with a FLOAT of Galliano.
review: One of the better Sloe Comfortable Screw derivations - Overproof rum provides the "Bang". Boozy fruity and balanced rather than sweet – what's not to like?
history: Origins and evolution of the "Slow Screw" family of cocktails.

name: Slow Comfortable Mexican Screw Against the Wall
instructions: STIR first four ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
FLOAT sloe gin on surface so it bleeds into drink
Finish with Galliano FLOAT.
review: Tequila adds a Mexican touch to a Slow Comfortable Screw. Great for a lazy summer afternoon or a barbecue.
history: Origins and evolution of the "Slow Screw" family of cocktails.

name: Slow Comfortable Screw Between the Sheets
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: The rum and cognac combo from Between the Sheets served as part of the Slow Comfortable genre. As with so many of the Slow Comfortable family of cocktails, when made with freshly squeezed orange juice, this is a tasty, fruity and balanced long drink.

name: Gin Tonica
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COPA GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Lightly stir.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist, lime wedge and 3 cardamom pods.
review: Something of a Spanish phenomenon, where the G&T (which in England has been traditionally served in a tall slim Collins glass) tends to be served in a huge goldfish bowl of a glass – big enough to house garnishes ranging from cardamom pods and juniper berries to fruit peels and slices of ginger.

name: Eclipse (by Leo Robitschek)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
FLOAT mezcal.
garnish: Serve naked, without garnish.
review: Beautifully balanced bitter-sweetness with tequila and mezcal sitting harmoniously with cherry and aperitivo liqueurs, with lemon freshness. A perfect aperitivo cocktail.
history:
The colour of the moon during an eclipse inspired the name of this rusty-orange-red cocktail.

In Jim Meehan & Chris Gall's 2011 The PDT Cocktail Book, the creation of the Eclipse is credited to "Leo Robitschek, New York, Winter 2009."

name: Negrino
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
STIR.
garnish: Garnish with pink grapefruit wedge/zest twist and scorched rosemary sprig.
review: A riff on the classic Negroni from Vietnam, which brilliantly set the richness of sloe gin against bone dry fino sherry while harnessing the herbal bitterness of gentian. Hit the right dilution, and by the third sip, when your palate has adjusted, you'll not be able to stop sipping this bitter aperitif cocktail.
history: Created in 2017 by Trinh Quan Huy-Philip, a "Travelling Mixologist in Vietnam.

name: Pirate Queen
instructions: MUDDLE strawberry in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass over a large cube or chunk of block ice.
review: Lightly spicy with an attractive underlying bready fruitiness from the fresh strawberry and genever.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2014 by Jillian Vose for the 3rd volume of The Dead Rabbit menu, New York City, USA.

name: Fade To Black Cocktail
instructions: MUDDLE blackberries in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE (without ice) and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: A Rum Sour blackened with blackberries and charcoal powder. The black charcoal which makes this cocktail black does little for the taste and given the potential issues with charcoal this tasty cocktail is perhaps best appreciated without its addition (as illustrated photographed here).
history: A cocktail which was widely made to celebrate the total solar eclipse which worked its way across the USA on 21 August 2017 – the first for 38 years.

name: Dr. Rieux
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain into chilled and rimmed glass.
review: Rounded, citrusy and delicately spicy with an attractive underlying funk from Swedish punch.
history: Adapted from a recipe discovered at Vaucluse, New York City, USA. Originally made with Facundo NEO rum.

name: Macca Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered tangerine/ mandarin/clementine.
review: Orange juice adds fruity dilution to what could also be called a Breakfast Margarita.
history: Sir Paul McCartney (yup, of Beatles fame) likes his Margarita's made with a splash of freshly squeezed orange juice (preferably clementine juice). Hence, this recipe is named in his honour.

name: 'Two Guns' Miller
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of smouldering (smoking) cinnamon stick.
review: Sweet vermouth rounds while pink grapefruit liqueur freshens reposado tequila with gentle but enlivening cinnamon spice and faint bitter chocolate.
history:
Adapted from a recipe discovered in 2015 at Pouring Ribbons, New York City, USA.

This cocktail is named after Harry "Two Guns" Miller, who in the 1920s set up a zoo and the Apache Death Cave tourist attraction in the Arizona town of Canyon Lodge on Route 66. He renamed the town Two Guns and took on the Chief Crazy Thunder persona. However, all did not go to plan. Miller fell out with the couple who owned the land he was renting and on 3rd March 1926 shot the husband during a heated dispute. He was acquitted at the trial but shortly after was mauled by a mountain lion, not once but twice - on two separate occasions. Finally in 1929 fire gutted the attraction.

name: The Road Not Taken
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of chamomile flower pegged to rim or lemon zest twist.
review: Lightly spiced and bittersweet, a good option for an aperitivo.
history:
Created in 2017 by Joe Macbeth at 400 Rabbits, Nottingham, England. Joe says of his drink, "A nod to Americana and the Robert Frost poem from which it derives its name. A bittersweet dedication to all those who wistfully regret bygone decisions and ponder a life where the grass is greener."

Joe's original recipe calls for: 40 ml Few Spiced American Gin, 25 ml Suze, 25 ml Rinquinquin and 2 dash Chamomile bitters.

name: The Italian Job
ingredients: 22.5 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
22.5 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with dehydrated orange wheel.
review: Bittersweet and boozy with a dry finish. Striking the right degree of dilution is key to the success of this cocktail – stir a little longer than usual and if using ice straight from the freezer perhaps consider adding a dash of chilled water.
history: Created in 2017 by Joseph Akhavan at Mabel, Paris, France. Joseph's original recipe calls for Smith & Cross Jamaican rum.

name: Old Acquaintance
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: Dry, bitter, very refreshing and mouth-puckering with grapefruit and gin bittered and coloured by red Italian bitters.
history: Adapted from a recipe by Henrik Buus Larsen, which in turn is a riff on the Old Friend cocktail.

name: The Dandy (by Iain Meldrum)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Rosemary sprig.
review: Dry and slightly on the bitter side of bittersweet, single malt scotch, gentian liqueur and dry vermouth sit well together in this appealing aperitif-style cocktail.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Iain Meldrum at Michael Neave Kitchen & Whisky Bar in Edinburgh, Scotland. Iain's original recipe called for 25ml single malt scotch (sweet & fruity), 25ml Suze, 25ml Dolin Blanc and 3 drops 5% saline solution.

Iain says, "The Boulevardier cocktail got its name from Parisian urbanites and fashion-followers. This pale, Scotch-based version takes its name from the British (and particularly Scottish Border) word for a man-about-town. It's also a nod to that other famous Scottish product, the Dandy comic."

name: Suze-Ann Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A bittersweet, gentian-influenced riff on a classic White Lady.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Dom Saunders and Simone Nelli at Cinnamon Club in London, England. (I took the liberty of adding egg white to their recipe.)

name: Sunny Disposition Highball
instructions: STIR first 4 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with bitter lemon tonic.
review: A bittersweet lemony aperitif cocktail which is also great on a sunny afternoon - indeed anytime you find yourself with a Sunny Disposition.
history:
Adapted from a recipe sent to us by Christine Lavall, France, who in turn credits "an elderly friend who worked in a bar for many years in France."

Information on Highball cocktails, their history and tips on the perfect serve can be found on our page.

name: Swiss Watch
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and fine strain into ice-filled sugar rimmed glass.
review: As Peter Konkoly, this cocktail's creator says, it is reminiscent of a Last Word. However, we prefer this tasty drink served on-the-rocks as an aperitif alternative to a Negroni, rather than straight-up as originally specified by Peter.
history: Created in 2017 by Peter Konkoly at The Arts Club, London, England, who describes this as "An alpine variation of The Last Word cocktail, using ingredients found in the Alps (Chartreuse), Switzerland (Kirschwasser) and nearby."

name: Swiss Account
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: A vodka charged bittersweet aperitif with herbal notes laced with grainy vodka.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Griffin Cox at Lot Six, Halifax, Canada.

Griffin says, "Hailed as a venerable French bitter, the story of Suze veritably began to the west in Switzerland before it was brought across the border by Fernand Moureaux in 1885. The Swiss Account pays homage to that beginning and shows how Suze finds its home alongside other French spirits just as it found its home in French palates over a century ago."

Griffin's original recipe calls for 45ml Grey Goose Vodka, 15ml Suze, 5ml Bénédictine and 2 dash Bittermens Hopped Grapefruit Bitters.

name: Sbagliato With Tonic
instructions: POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass and lightly stir.
review: Lightly bittersweet and superbly refreshing, a tasty aperitif cocktail.
history: This adaptation of a classic cocktail recipe is to a Tony Conigliaro 2017 formulation.

name: It's Hartnell Damn It!
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: An equal parts riff on a Doctor No.1 using white wine acidity in place of lime juice. Tangy and slightly sharp in flavour with more than a hint of Swedish punch funkiness.
history: Created by yours truly as a thank you to Doctor Salt, who was left in a "pother of Whovian pedantry" due to my misspelling of another good Doctor's name on our Cocktail of the Day for 23rd November 2017. The actor who portrayed the first Dr Who is William Hartnell (not Hartness), who incidentally also played Sgt Grimshaw in the first-ever Carry-On film, Carry on Sergeant.

name: Marguerite No.2 (modern)
instructions: SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice and strain into an ice-filled glass
TOP with splash of soda.
review: Tequila laced with rich maraschino balancing sour grapefruit and dry sherry – a very refreshing aperitif.

name: Keyser Suze
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Chris Grotvedt at The Thief, Oslo, Norway. Chris’ original spec call for 45ml Suze, 45ml Carpano dry vermouth and 3 dash orange bitters. The name is a play on words from the character in the movie The Usual Suspects.

name: Time for Suze
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
review: A wonderfully delicate and dry aperitif. Genever provides a bready spirituous base over which flavours of gentian, fennel and absinthe are layered.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Emily Muscutt at Toto, Newcastle, England.

name: Glass and Bottle
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Float cucumber slice for garnish.
review: Sour, dry and fabulously complex with bittersweet herbal gentian notes over a gin base.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by J Lynn at Lyons at Ace Hotel New Orleans, New Orleans, USA, who "wanted to create something more palatable for those new to gentian." The name comes from the 1912 Picasso collage featuring a bottle of Suze.

name: Word by Word
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf.
review: A bittersweet aperitif-style riff on the classic Last Word cocktail.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Vitaliy Severinov at El Copitas, Saint-Petersburg, Russia. (Vitaliy's original recipe calls for 15ml Chartreuse Jaune.)
See: Last Word history

name: Youth and Treachery
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: This spirituous bittersweet aperitif is reminiscent of a White Negroni, but based on tequila rather than gin.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Jason Simplot at Rumba, Seattle, USA. Jason's original spec called for equal parts El Tesoro Platinum, Suze and Carpano Bianco.

Jason says, "Essentially a white Rosita, created for a guest who wanted a tequila cocktail on the bitter side. Slightly spicy, kinda sweet and gentian-heavy. I thought I named this after a 'Modern Life Is War' song, one of my favorite punk bands, but upon a little research, they never titled a song with the aforementioned phrase. Which is too bad. It's fitting for not only their casse-toi brand of aggressive punk but also for this slightly aggressive and feisty cocktail."

name: Suzette au Bal (Suzette at the Ball)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pear slice.
review: Dry and lightly bitter with a faint mezcal smokiness and delicate rich pear fruit.
history: Created in 2017 by Alexandre Girard at La Queue du Coq in Annecy, France, who told us, "Suzette is the name of French song. It's the story of a guy who falls in love with Suzette; a smart, smooth, incredible young woman. The scene takes place in a local dance in the middle of summer."

name: Tezonita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A dry and fairly bitter aperitif-style cocktail combining tequila's herbaceous notes and the herbal flavours of gentian.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Daniel Kaizen at Calexico Adliya, Adliya, Bahrain for the Olmeca Tahona Society Cocktail Competition 2016. Daniel's original recipe called for 40ml Olmeca Altos Blanco, 25ml Suze and 25ml Belsazar white vermouth.

Daniel says, "It's a delicate drink made as an ode to the Tezontle, the volcanic rock that makes up the Tahona wheel used to crush agave in the traditional method of making tequila. The juxtaposition of being such a light, delicate drink bringing out the bittersweet flavours of the tequila while being quite a punchy agave faithful drink."

name: Jersey's Best Dancers
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review:
Three equal-part ingredients, the holy grail of cocktail recipes, makes for a dry spirituous apéritif cocktail. This cocktail is very similar to a Maximiliano.

Incidentally, Jersey's Best Dancers is the third studio album by the New Jersey band Lifetime (released June 1997).
history:
Adapted from a 2017 recipe by Charlie Martin at Eclipse Restaurant, Saint Louis, USA, who says, "I wanted a version of the Negroni that used mezcal as the base spirit and Suze as the bittering agent. Whipped this drink up and now it's on our menu! Rarely you nail it on the first try but this one is perfect!"

Charlie's original recipe calls for equal parts: Suze, Vida Mezcal and Carpano Dry vermouth.

name: Fernand x Nando
instructions: STIR all ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass (preferably over block ice).
review: A bittersweet apéritif combining gentian herbal complexity with attractive coffee, cachaça and calvados spirituous notes.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Nando Imamura, Hong Kong.

Nando says, "Fernand Moureaux, the genius that believed in create something different than the casual, versus Nando Imamura, bartender, Brazilian-Japanese and married with a French Woman creating flavours around the world. I created this cocktail using French and Brazilian products to celebrate the birth of my 1st baby. I used Suze infused with Brazilian Coffee beans, Cachaça Leblon aged in a French oak for three years and Calvados Cristian Droin VSOP and Angostura Bitters to balance it out."

Namdo's original recipe calls for 30ml Suze infused with Brazilian coffee beans, 20ml Leblon cachaça, 10ml Calvados Cristian Droin VSOP and 2 dashes Angostura Aromatic Bitters.

name: Castle Cobbler
ingredients: 30 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice and strain into glass filled with pebble ice
Float Peychaud's on drink.
review: A fruity bittersweet apéritif with "orchard fruit, citrus and bittersweet gentian sipped through a sarsaparilla-like layer of Peychaud's Bitters."
history:
Created in 2017 by Taoufike Zrafi at Taology, Montreal, Canada, who says, "I was basically trying to work on a low proof cobbler style cocktail that really puts the flavor of Suze on the forefront of the flavor profiles. I went towards using only southern French ingredients and whilst tasting it behind the bar a guest asked me to try it, as soon as he did, he ordered one right away and demanded for it to be on the menu for next time he's going to come in. Enjoy this drink while you're castle hopping in your next trip to southern France!"

For information on the fascinating history of Cobblers please see our page.

name: The Duchess
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Gin's light botanical spice combines with rich orange and complex vinous notes from the combination of wine and dry vermouth.
history: Created in February 2018 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Mexican Duchess
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Spirituous and lightly vinous with subtle agave notes, bready genever and spicy gin all refreshed by zesty orange curaçao. Five equal parts is a faff to measure for individual cocktails but a dream to batch.
history: A riff on Seth Brammer's excellent Farmer's Tan created in February 2018 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Dutch Orange Cup
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into chilled glass half filled with ice.
Add an orange slice and a couple of mint leaves from the garnish, then FILL to the brim with more ice.
garnish: Garnish with fruit and mint/borage.
review: Easy drinking, fruity, summery and gin laced with caramel vermouth freshened by zesty orange.
history: Created in February 2018 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Mai Dutch Tai
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
Crown with crushed ice.
review: Wonderfully fruity with underlying bready genever replacing the almond notes from the orgeat syrup almond notes of a classic Trader Vic Mai Tai.
history: Created in February 2018 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: No Borders
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of fresh bay leaf.
review: A tasty bittersweet Negroni-style aperitif.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Emily Couty at Sketch, London, England.

name: Wild and Dirty Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI (SMALL) GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of kalamata olive and rosemary sprig.
review: Pine (birch) notes mix well with the vermouth in this 2:1 Martini with lemon bitters adding citrus freshness the olive and rosemary sprig garnish adding to the cocktail's aroma and complexity.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Dean Railton at Vice & Virtue, Leeds, England. Deans original recipe called for 50ml Freya Birch Spirit, 25ml Noilly Prat vermouth and 1 dash lemon bitters.

name: Pan Galactic Gargle Blaster
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI (LARGE 10OZ) GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Prepare garnish of dust with lots of freshly grounded black pepper.
review: Orange, basil and red berries laced with vodka makes for a well-balanced, easy-drinking fruity cocktail - arguably too easy for a drink titled Pan Galactic Gargle Blaster which is said to be like "having your brains smashed in by a slice of lemon wrapped round a large gold brick." Remember, Beeblebrox advises, "never drink more than two Pan Galactic Gargle Blasters unless you are a thirty ton mega elephant with bronchial pneumonia."
history:
Based on the Basil Grande, this recipe for the legendary Pan Galactic Gargle Blaster was created circa 2011 by Christos Chouseas at his Bar 42 in Athens, Greece – a bar where the work of Douglas Adams influenced many of the drink names.

In Adams' thought-provoking and amusing book, The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy, the number 42 is the "answer to the ultimate question of life, the universe, and everything", while the Pan Galactic Gargle Blaster is considered by the Guide to be the "Best Drink in Existence."

According to the guide, the original Pan Galactic Gargle Blaster was invented by ex-President of the Universe Zaphod Beeblebrox. "After two of those babies, the dullest, most by-the-book Vogon will be up on the bar in stilettos, yodeling mountain shanties and swearing he's the king of the Gray Binding Fiefdoms of Saxaquine."

name: Del Famoso (The Famous)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Serve naked, without garnish.
review: The herbal spicy peppermint and liquorice notes of Fernet Branca dominate this digestif-style tequila laced cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in June 2017 by Nathan Hinze at 99 Hanover Street, Edinburgh, Scotland. The cocktail's name is inspired by the wording on the Fernet Branca label.

name: Vanadis
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Dry and spirituous, yet (with the right dilution) complex and subtle – even delicate. An interesting aperitif alternative to a Dry Martini or a Negroni.
history: Created in 2017 by Thom Solberg at Black Rock, London, England.

name: La Alianza
instructions: Select and pre-chill a DOUBLE OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Prepare garnish of wide grapefruit zest twist.
review: Mezcal sings in this dry aperitif-style cocktail with delicate vinous notes and light gentian bitterness.

name: Napoléon Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: As the name suggests, this tasty Margarita benefits from a slug of rich mandarin orange liqueur.
history: A variant of the popular Margarita cocktail. A detailed history, along with other variants can be found on our page.

name: The Queen's Steeple
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Amaro pleasantly dominates this bittersweet, delicately orangey aperitif cocktail.
history: Created in 2017 by Loris Carotenuto at The Dorchester Hotel in London, England.

name: First Emperor
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
review: Grassy spirituous agricole rhum is mellowed by fruity pineapple, sweet herbal liqueur and vinous herbal vermouth.
history:
Adapted from a drink discovered in March 2018 at Merchant House of Bow Lane, London, England. (The original recipe: 30ml Agricole rhum, 60ml Lillet Blanc, 15ml Yellow Chartreuse and 10ml pineapple syrup, served in a small stemmed wine glass with a single pebble-shaped slither of ice.)

The drink is named after Napoleon Bonaparte (1769-1821) and the following accompanies the drink on the Merchant House's menu, "After rising to the rank of General at the young age of 24, Napoleon later arranged a coup, placing himself as the first Emperor of France. His skill in command led to numerous years of French dominance in Europe and further afield, and his reforms continue to influence our society today."

name: Sage Sazerac
instructions: Rinse chilled glass Pastis and set aside
MUDDLE sage in base of stirring glass
Add other ingredients, STIR with ice and fine strain into the pre-rinsed glass over fresh ice (preferably a chunk of block ice).
review: Subtle notes of pine, pastis and sage combine in this pre-dinner sipper of a cocktail.
history:
Adapted from a drink created in 2017 by Jason Knowles at Hixter Bankside, London, England. His original recipe uses Tarquin's Cornish Pastis and sage infused sugar syrup.

The history of the Sazerac cocktail can be found on our Sazerac cocktail page.

name: Cold Comfort
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime wedge or slice wheel.
review: Take at the first sign of a cold, and then retreat under your bed covers. Repeat dose regularly while symptoms persist. Warning - do not consume with other forms of medication.
history: Discovered in Jamaica in 2001.

name: Forbidden Fruit
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 7 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring ginger beer.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice wheel.
review: Given the name, don't expect a sweet 'fruity' cocktail, instead, this is dry, spicy and complex with zesty citrus over an appley spirituous base with enlivening ginger spice.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2012 by Aisha Sharpe in New York City. The original recipe was based on bonded applejack rather than calvados and was more or less:
- 45ml (1½oz) Bottled-in-bond applejack 
- 30ml (1oz) Pimm's No. 1
- 15ml (½oz) Lemon juice
- 7.5ml (¼oz) Sugar syrup (1:1)
- 2 dashes Angostura Aromatic Bitters
- 2 dashes Peychaud's bitters
- 45ml (1½oz) Ginger beer

name: Banana Calling
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with dried banana chip pegged to rim, or fresh banana slice on the rim.
review: This short, sharp cocktail is best described as a Banana & Fino Sherry Gin Sour. The fino and banana notes are subtle, so subtle that it is not obviously a banana cocktail, although the name should serve as a clue.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in August 2018 by Cameron Attfield at Dandelyan in London, England, this is a riff on the popular London Calling cocktail created by Chris Jepson in 2002.

name: Death Star
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Garnish with star anise floated on surface.
review: This equal-parts stirred cocktail is either a spirit-forward aperitivo or a bittersweet digestif come nightcap. Whatever classification it falls into, it's tasty.

name: Purpurina Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of dehydrated jalapeño slice.
review: With a whole measure of watermelon syrup, you may expect this cocktail to be on the sweet side. Instead, it is beautifully balanced with delicate Jalapeño heat – at least it is if you go easy with the amount of Jalapeño you infuse in your pisco.
history: Created by David Osborne at Bellota tapas bar, San Francisco, USA. Incidentally, purpurina is Spanish for glitter / tinsel.

name: Four Poster Red
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Prepare garnish of Luxardo Maraschino Cherry or strawberry.
review:
This deep cherry red Negroni-like aperitivo is equally tasty shaken and served straight-up – indeed that's how it was originally made, like a Shakerato. If served shaken, then a white head should cover the drink's surface, or it's not been shaken hard enough. The cocktail should then be consumed before the head fades.

Either shaken or stirred, sweet vermouth/port predominates with bitter liqueur providing balance.
history: Created March 2018 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Ernst Happel
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with apricot wedge or dried apricot half on rim.
review: Gin-laced, tangy, herbal, fruity and citrusy.
history: Ernst Franz Hermann Happel was a famous Austrian football player and coach, regarded by many as one of the most successful managers of all time.

name: Lo Stregone
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist and mint sprig.
review: Red bitter soda (originally Crodino) and Strega make a very tasty and very Italian aperitivo. Go easy on the aromatic bitters.
history: Adapted from a recipe by Matteo Schianchi.

name: Corpse Reviver No.0824
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lemon zest (not expressed) and spray spritz of absinthe over cocktail.
review: A suitably Italian riff on the classic Corpse Reviver No. 2 with Strega liqueur.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Matteo Rebuffo.

name: Acapulco No.2
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon slice (wheel).
review: This Margarita-like drink demonstrates what a great combo tequila and Strega are.

name: Witches' Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Aniseed, sweet almond and delicate spice subtly influence this tasty Daiquiri.

name: Witches' Grappa
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: As the name suggests, grappa and Strega predominate in this citrusy cocktail.

name: Polish Pear Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pear wedge on rim.
review: Pear fruitiness with suitably Martini boozy aromatic vodka.
history: A riff on Dick Bradsell's brilliantly simple Polish Martini by yours truly in May 2018.

name: Egg Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of float 3 small dashes Angostura bitters on surface of drink.
review: A well-balanced sour with brandy and orange zestiness shaken with a whole egg. Try it. This combo is tastier than it sounds.
history: A vintage cocktail of unknown origin.

name: Velvet Revolver
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR strawberry jam and brandy in base of shaker until jam is dissolved.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with strawberry on rim and mint sprigs bouquet.
review: As Robb, the creator of this cocktail, says, it is "short strong and fruity, pulls out all the deeper notes from the cognac."
history: Created by Robb Collins when he was Group Head Bartender at London Cocktail Club in London, England. Developed for Cocktails in The City summer 2017.

name: Rusty Peach Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A dark, delicately bittersweet and light in alcohol aperitif cocktail.
history: A peachy riff on the classic Grassoide Ferro Cocktail in Elveio Grassi's 1936 book Mille Misture by yours truly.

name: Tuxedo Cocktail No.2 (Savoy and Duffy's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: A Fifty-Fifty Martini with added flavour and aroma thanks to the addition of a trio of bitters, liquor and liqueur.
history:
Adapted from recipes in The Savoy Cocktail Book (1930) by Harry Craddock, The Official Mixer's Manual (1934 & 1940) by Patrick Gavin Duffy.
Also see: Tuxedo cocktail history

name: Sorrentino Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Garnish with dehydrated orange wheel and thyme sprig.
review: Bittersweet with pleasing rich lemon zestiness from the limoncello.
history: A well-established and popular cocktail in southern Italy (Mezzogiorno), the home of limoncello.

name: Remind Me Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A delicious stirred down, grappa laced aperitif or summertime digestif.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2018 by Andre Tarantino at Collage Cocktail Bar, Barcelona, Spain, who says "this cocktail was inspired by the song Remind Me by Patrice Rushen." His preference is Grappa Poli as it "gives a very sharp feeling to the cocktail."

name: Oh Snap!
instructions: MUDDLE peas in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: The light green colour comes from the sugar snap peas and their delicate flavour sits well in this floral Aviation-style cocktail.
history: Created in 2018 by Gareth "G" Franklin, Luxardo global brand ambassador, this cocktail was originally called Howard Hughes, but given the recipe includes sugar snap peas, G thought "Oh! Snap!" more appropriate.

name: Cherry Springer
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: We do like a pun, especially a tasty pun. Sweet and sour cherry fortified with gin notes and subtle freshening crème de menthe and delicate chocolate.
history: We're indebted to Gareth "G" Franklin of Luxardo fame for bringing this brilliantly named recipe, adapted from a drink originally created in 2018 by Filip Palkiewicz at Mercy Brown, Kraków, Poland, to our attention. Naturally, G uses Luxardo's excellent Sangue Morlacco cherry liqueur when he makes this drink.

name: Champagne Piña Colada
instructions: BLEND first 6 ingredients with 4oz scoop of crushed ice (including 15ml/ ½oz champagne) and pour into chilled glass which has been pre-charged with 45ml/ 1½oz of chilled champagne.
review: Deliciously creamy pineapple and coconut enlivened by champagne with rum notes shining through.
history:
Adapted from Chris Moore's award-winning signature cocktail which has been served at Coupette, Bethnal Green, London since the French-influenced neighbourhood bar opened in 2017. The recipe's proportions have been tweaked since the bar opened but the ingredients have remained constant:

20ml Bacardi Heritage
10ml Trois Rivières Blanc
40ml Fresh Pineapple Juice
35ml House-made acidic pineapple cordial
3 scoops Coconut Sorbet
60ml Moet & Chandon Brut Imperial

Coconut Sorbet is not easily available in the UK, hence our recipe to make your own. For ease, we've also swapped Coupette's house-made acidic pineapple cordial for pineapple liqueur and rebalanced proportions accordingly. At Coupette, they make their cordial by soaking leftover pineapple skins in sugar syrup overnight and mixing with citric acid. 

See Piña Colada history

name: Accoutrement
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with your chosen accoutrement.
review: Chris originally garnished this Calvados Sidecar/Applecart-like cocktail with brandied cherries, but Strega's excellent chocolates seemed a far more fitting accoutrement for our photograph.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2008 by Chris Hannah at Arnaud's French 75 Bar, New Orleans, USA.

name: Champagne Julep
instructions: SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice
Add champagne and more to shaker, then throw twice before straining into julep tin filled with crushed ice
Serve with a metal straw.
review: Lower in strength than most old-school julep cocktails, champagne adds a zing if not a sparkle.

name: Twelve Miles Out Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Stir or shake (as originally stipulated in The Savoy Cocktail Book) this cocktail is still boozy. However, it's also wonderfully aromatic with all three ingredients combining harmoniously.
history:
The Twelve Miles Out is one of several Prohibition-era cocktails whose name references the distance from a shore that a nation's territorial waters. During the 18th century this was established as three nautical miles (5.6 km), the distance a cannonball could be shot from the shore to repel incursions. Hence, during Prohibition, US jurisdiction was limited to three miles offshore so allowing the legal supply of alcohol to parties on board boats just outside territorial waters.

As the number of offshore parties grew, the US negotiated agreements with countries such as the UK and France to recognize the right of the American authorities to enforce Prohibition and anti-smuggling laws to an extended 12-mile limit. Predictably, the parties moved further off-shore. On 29th December 1988, President Reagan extended the territorial waters of the United States to 12 miles from 3 miles.

The notoriety of the three-mile and then 12-mile limit to the reach of the Feds during Prohibition led to the Three Miller/Three Mile Limit, the Twelve Miles Out, and the Twelve Mile Limit cocktails.

Recipe adapted from Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book where this cocktail is shaken rather than stirred.

name: Lost Plane
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Amaro's distinctive flavours shine through alongside the dark rum in this aperitivo cocktail.
history: Created by Emily Gosling of Goslings Black Seal Rum, this is a riff on Sam Ross' contemporary classic, Paper Plane.

name: Black Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: More deep golden amber than black in appearance, this Daquiri takes its name from the black rum (well, dark brown rum) it's made with. Pungent rum notes sit well with zingy citrus.

name: Bloody Mary (no added sugar and low-calorie)
instructions: ROLL (rotate) all ingredients with ice in shaker (rather than shake) and strain into ice-filled glass
Use ice freshly from freezer to fill glass (not ice directly from ice-machine) to avoid unwanted dilution from melting ice
review:
With vitamin loaded tomato and lemon juices, this sugar-free, low-calorie Mary is almost a healthy option.

Be sure to use a tomato juice with no added sugar and be aware that adding Worcestershire sauce will also contribute a very small amount of sugar.
history:
Adapted from the classic serve by yours truly (Simon D) in January 2019.

Also see Bloody Mary cocktails - how to make, origins and history

name: Daiquiri (no added sugar and low-calorie)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Light and delicate, and not overly citrusy so allowing the rum to shine. And it's sugar-free! At least it is if you choose your rum carefully.
history:
Adapted from the classic serve by yours truly (Simon D) in January 2019.

You may also be interested in:
Daiquiri cocktail – its history and the story behind its creation
Daiquiri cocktail – up, on-the-rocks or frozen
Daiquiri cocktail – bar calls and variations
Daiquiri cocktail – best recipes and how to make
Daiquiri cocktail – D's Daiquiri Bitters

name: Espresso Martini (no sugar and low-calorie)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with 3 coffee beans floated in a petal formation in the centre of the cocktail's foamy head.
review: Slightly thinner in mouthfeel than a regular sugar loaded Espresso Martini but equally as tasty.
history:
Recipe adapted from Dick Bradsell's contemporary classic by yours truly (Simon D) in January 2019.

Also see the origin and story behind the Cosmopolitan cocktail.

name: Gimlet (no added sugar and low-calorie)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of express lime zest or two into glass and discard before pouring cocktail. Then place lime wedge on rim..
review: Fresh lime juice replaces cordial in this vitamin C-loaded, almost healthy gin-laced sugar-free version of a classic Gimlet.
history: Created by yours truly in January 2019.

name: Gin Basil Smash (no added sugar and low-calorie)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
MUDDLE basil sprigs (no need to pluck leaves from stem) in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with basil leaf or sprig.
review: Herbal and refreshing. Basil, gin and lemon sit harmoniously, balanced by sugar-free sweetener.
history: Adapted from Joerg Meyer's original Gin Basil Smash by yours truly in January 2019.

name: Gin Sling (no added sugar and low-calorie)
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda water.
review: Light and refreshing with lemon freshening gin while a splash of sugar-free sweetener provides balance.
history:
Adapted from the classic serve by yours truly (Simon D) in January 2019.

You may also be interested in the story behind the Singapore Sling.

name: Long Island Iced Tea (no added sugar* and low-calorie)
instructions: SHAKE first 8 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with cola and briefly stir.
review:
* BEWARE: Although free of cane sugar and beet sugar, this cocktail contains small amounts of fructose sugars from the agave nectar used in the Agave Sec orange liqueur as well as natural sugars in the lime and lemon juice.

As the cola falls and bleeds its way through the otherwise clear drink (it takes noticeably long to fall than full sugar cola), it does end up looking remarkably like week black tea and indeed the taste isn't so far removed from spirit laced weak tea with citrus fruit.
history:
Adapted from the classic serve by yours truly (Simon D) in January 2019.

For the origins of the Long Island Iced Tea see 1980s cocktails.

name: Margarita (no added sugar* and low-calorie)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of salt rim (optional) and lime wedge.
review:
* BEWARE: Although free of cane sugar and beet sugar, this cocktail contains small amounts of fructose sugars from the agave nectar used in the Agave Sec orange liqueur.

Tasty, zingy, low-calorie and sugar-free. What more can you ask of a Margarita?
history:
Adapted from the classic serve by yours truly (Simon D) in January 2019.

Also see  Margarita Cocktails - best recipes and history.

name: Mojito (no added sugar and low-calorie)
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE mint (just to bruise) in base of glass
Add rum, lime juice and sugar free sweetener
Half fill glass with crushed ice and CHURN (stir) with bar spoon
Fill glass with more crushed ice and CHURN some more
TOP with soda, stir and serve with environmentally friendly straws.
history:
Adapted from the classic serve by yours truly (Simon D) in January 2019.

For more recipes, its origins and history, see the Mojito Cocktail.

name: Tom Collins (no added sugar and low-calorie)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda and briefly stir.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange slice and cherry.
review: If opting to make this Tom Collins with an Old Tom gin (as it should be), beware which brand you choose as many Old Tom gins contain sugar.
history:
Adapted from the classic serve by yours truly (Simon D) in January 2019.

Also see Collins Cocktails - recipes & history.

name: Vodka Sour (no added sugar and low-calorie)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
review: As with most sours, adding a couple of dashes aromatic bitters greatly contributes to the cocktail's flavour but this also adds trace amounts of sugar.
history:
Adapted from the classic serve by yours truly (Simon D) in January 2019.

Also see Sours - history, recipes and glassware.

name: Vanilla Espresso Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of float 3 coffee beans (and if feeling extravagant, also half, or even a full vanilla bean).
review: If you're the kind to opt for a splash of flavoured syrup at the coffee shop, then this variation on Dick Bradsell's original Espresso Martini is for you. And with the range of flavoured vodkas and sugar syrups available, why stop at vanilla?
history: See the story behind the Espresso Martini.

name: Dutch Mule
instructions: SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with ginger beer.
review: Ginger spice invigorates this long refreshing cocktail with a splash of genever adding to the Dutch influence.
history:
A signature cocktail for Ketel One Dutch vodka.

A detailed history of the Moscow Mule cocktail, of which this drink is a variant, can be found on our page.

name: First Post
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Pale green in colour with a refreshing bitter/sour sweet profile.
history:
This cocktail was published in an article called The Golden Cocktail Mile for the release of the 2013 World's End film comic science fiction film. It is named after the first pub on the bar crawl that Simon Peg and co. embark on in the film.

Created in 2013 by Alastair Walker, then at The Everleigh in Melbourne, Australia.

name: Suzepect Swizzle
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
Squeeze grapes to yield 20ml juice and SWIZZLE this with next 3 ingredients in glass half filled with crushed ice.
Fill glass with more crushed ice.
FLOAT amaro.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: A bittersweet refreshing drink for a summer's afternoon or an aperitif with grape and peach fruitiness and complexity courtesy of gentian and fresh mint.
history: Created by Linus Morgan at the Tap Room in Kungsholmen, Stockholm, Sweden. Who says, "The name is a wordplay on Suze and suspect, it's a swizzle with a nice bitter hit and a nice peach and mint finish.

name: Reno
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
THROW all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Garnish with lemon wedge.
review: The distinctive flavours of amaro and gentian dominate this bittersweet but mellow aperitif.
history:
Created in 2018 by Giorgio Ferrarese at The Holy Birds restaurant in London, England.

Giorgio says "The story which inspired this cocktail took place during WWII on 21st April 1945, when the allied forces planned to retake Bologna. Polish, English, American and French troops helped Italian partisans to capture the city in a very unusual battlefield, a river called Reno. I used ingredients that remind me of the forces involved in the battle. Amaro Montenegro symbolises the city. Cocchi Americano to celebrate the partisan's movement born in Asti. Italicus represents braveness. And finally, Suze, this sophisticated liqueur de gentian, used to remember the aid received by the allies. Without this strong bitter touch, my cocktail wouldn't be the same, and the battle wouldn't have been won!"

name: Pavoni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with blue cheese stuffed olive.
review: Sweet and sour with delicate bitterness and a complex cherry flavour, this cocktail is designed to pair with cheese.
history: Created 2018 by Odd Strandbakken at Himkok, Oslo, Norway, this was the winning cocktail in the final of the Heering Classics competition. For the competition, Odd garnished this drink with olives stuffed with Kraftkar cheese, a spicy Norwegian blue cheese judged the Supreme Champion in 2016 World Cheese Awards. And as he says, this cocktail "perfectly pairs with cheese."

name: Fairbanks No.2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Original Savoy and Vermeire recipes for this cocktail specify 2 dashes of crème de noyeaux. I've interpreted this as being a generous 5ml / 1/6oz measure to add a delicate pink hue and a fruity almond note to this Dry Martini-style cocktail.
history:
Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book where he notes, "We often wondered what Doug did it on. Now we know we are going to try and do it ourselves" in reference to the charisma of Douglas Fairbanks Sr., this drinks namesake.

However, the earlier, Robert Vermeire's 1922 Cocktails: How to Mix Them says, "This drink is named after Senator Fairbank, a personal friend of the late President Roosevelt, of America." Vermeire's recipe also differs from the Savoy in that it specifies equal parts gin and vermouth, and if you'd like to try this version, I suggest 45ml / 1.5oz of each.

name: Old Etonian
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: This Wet Martini-style cocktail is basically, a Fairbanks No.2 but with Lillet in place of dry vermouth.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Lily
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Pinky red, gin laced delicate red fruit and almond nuttiness with light white wine and lemon acidity.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book. The original recipe calls for Kina Lillet in place of Lillet Blanc.

name: Corleone
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE grapes in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered grape.
review: Aromatic, floral and surprisingly dry with pleasing grape fruitiness and botanical complexity.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Ryan Magarian at Oven & Shaker, Portland, Oregon. Apparently, Ryan named this cocktail due to his being inspired by the Sicilian countryside.

name: Eastern Standard (Soho House Tonic)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE cucumber in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
