garnish: Float cucumber slice for garnish.
review: Fresh and refreshing with gin-laced mint, cucumber and lime.
history: One of the Soho House groups' House Tonic cocktails, the Eastern Standard is based on the Eastside, a drink which in turn was inspired by the Southside.

name: Dunderstruck
ingredients: 30 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with strawberry slice on rim.
review: Fruity in every sense of the word – not to mention estery with namesake dundery rum notes taking centre stage. (Flavours are somewhat reminiscent of a Reggae Rum Punch.)
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Matthew Roedel at The Public House, Bay City, Michigan, USA.

name: Aviazione
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: An Italian influenced riff on the classic Aviation with fragrant grappa in place of the usual gin base and floral crème de violette very slightly dialled up to match the aromatics of the grappa.
history: Created February 2019 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: The Persuader
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge on rim.
review:
Named due to this drink's friendly approachability and The Persuaders!, a 1970s British TV series starring Tony Curtis and Roger Moore made brilliant by John Barry's theme music. 

This cocktail harnesses rum's affinity to pineapple juice and is Daiquiri-like but with white wine's acidity replacing that of lime juice. Chinotto liqueur adds delicate chocolaty citrus complexity with a touch of rich vanilla liqueur adding balancing sweetness.
history: Created February 2019 by yours truly, this is a riff on the Palermo, a cocktail I came across in 2001 and the first cocktail I encountered that replaced the acidity of citrus fruit with white wine.

name: Cool Coronation
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
STIR first 5 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring soda.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: This elongated Coronation is enjoyable as a light and aromatic aperitivo.

name: Dynasty Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass pre-rimmed with grapefruit salt
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: Inspired by Hong Kong, the Dynasty Margarita appropriately uses traditional Chinese ingredients of lychee and ginger and combines these with elderflower liqueur to make a distinctive floral Margarita.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Jay Khan in Hong Kong, China for Patrón Tequila's Margarita of the Year competition. This was the winning cocktail! 

Jay's original recipe uses homemade lychee & ginger syrup, Patrón Silver Tequila and Patron Citronge. To make the homemade lychee & ginger syrup, blend fresh peeled and de-stoned lychees until smooth. Double-strain this juice and combine with equal parts of freshly pressed ginger juice. Add an equal amount of granulated sugar to the combined weight of lychee and ginger juice and blend until the sugar is completely dissolved.

name: Wild Rose Margarita
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass.
review: Bittersweet floral rose and faint gin botanical notes underpin this balanced riff on a classic straight-up Margarita.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Makina Labrecque in Calgary, Canada. Originally made with Patrón Silver tequila and Patrón Citrónge Orange (as the triple sec), this cocktail was the 2019 Patrón Margarita of the Year.

name: Coronation
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon (or orange) zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with skewered olive.
review: Dry and wonderfully aromatic with faint notes of almond from the fino sherry and delicately fruity maraschino. Delicious.
history:
Created for, or soon after, the Coronation of England's King Edward VII, who became King on 22 January 1901, upon the death of his mother, Queen Victoria. His Coronation was set for 26 June 1902, but just two days before, an emergency operation required for an attack of appendicitis forced the investiture to be postponed until 9 August 1902.

The Coronation Cocktail first appears in Charles S. Mahoney's 1905 The Hoffman House Bartender's Guide, where it is credited to Joseph Rose of Murry Bros.' Café in Newark, New Jersey, USA. The date of its creation is not given, but the cocktail won second prize in the 1903 Police Gazette Bartenders' Contest.

name: Grapefruit and Rose Seltz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a LARGE WINE GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit wedge.
review:
Very refreshing and light. Perfect for a summer's afternoon. Our own favourite of the three initial Ketel One botanical serves.

Also, consider serving as a Spritz: Pour 90ml/3oz Prosecco, 45ml/1.5oz Botanical Grapefruit & Rose and 60ml/2oz soda water into an ice-filled glass and briefly stir.
history: One of three signature serves launched in the summer of 2018 to accompany the launch of Ketel One Botanicals.

name: Bijou (by Jamie Gordon)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: This bittersweet digestivo is one of those drinks that becomes easier and gets better as dilution, from the melting ice, opens and softens.
history: Adapted from a 2018 recipe by Jamie Gordon, San Francisco, USA.

name: Eggnog
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: This vintage cocktail is literally a thickened alcoholic milkshake flavoured with cognac and rum. Yum!
history: Eggnog is a vintage Christmas libation, and George Washington, America's first President, famously made his own. It is traditionally made by whisking the egg yolks with sugar; separately stirring the milk, cream, rum and bourbon; and finally combining these two mixtures along with beaten egg white. This is best made in batches and is a faff. Our quick and easy single-serve version delivers on flavour, and if you seek a thicker consistency, then opt for cream in place of milk.

name: Coconut Rum Punch
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with a sprinkle of roasted dehydrated coconut flakes.
review: Coconut and rum with a splash of lime are a tried and tested flavour matches. In this case with the added punch of funky overproof Jamaican rum.

name: Crystal Clear
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with star anise floated on surface.
review: Subtly citrusy and piney with delicate caraway. Clearly refined.
history: Created in March 2019 by yours truly (Simon D) at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Vodka Silver Bullet
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Perhaps best described as a Kümmel Martini with kümmel liqueur's strong caraway flavours fortified with vodka, freshened and made more complex by the addition of grapefruit bitters.
history: A classic with unknown origins.

name: Weekly Special
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Not far removed from an Aviation. Depending on your grapefruit juice, this particular "Special" may be very slightly on the sweet side, but it's fantastically aromatic. If you find it too sweet, then slightly up the grapefruit juice.
history: Adapted from a recipe first published in William James "Billy" Tarling's 1937 Café Royal Bar Book.

name: Cosmopolitan Cocktail (D's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and float as garnish.
review: The Cosmopolitan was originally made with citrus vodka, but it's great with unflavoured vodka or a combination of the two vodkas - my preference. For this recipe, as long as your combined vodka totals 37.5ml (1¼oz) or a splash more, you should end up with a tasty, balanced cocktail. I've previously added orange bitters, but the combination of triple sec liqueur and the bitter oil from the garnish is orangy enough. Like so many cocktails, two or three drops of saline solution help meld all harmoniously.
history: The origins of the Cosmo seem to originate in the gay bars of 1970s San Francisco, but this contemporary classic cocktail was perfected and popularised in 1990s New York City. Along the way, several bartenders played their part in its success, but perhaps TV's Sex and the City was the most influential. For the full story, see Cosmopolitan cocktail history.

name: Picante de la Casa (Spicy Margarita)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
MUDDLE chilli and coriander/cilantro in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with chilli pepper slices. (Depending on strength, perhaps warn drinker not to eat.)
review: a Tommy's Margarita with coriander/cilantro and chilli spice. The "Spicy Margarita" that led the trend.
history: Created by Chris Ojeda at Soho House, West Hollywood, USA, this "House Tonic" is the best-selling cocktail across the Soho Houses worldwide and has also become a popular contemporary classic served in other bars.

name: The Herbalist
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with celery ribbon around inside of glass.
review: Kummel's herbal caraway flavours and botanical gin notes form the backbone of this citrusy celery cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created circa 2018 by Jiri Majerik, Bar Manager at Blixen, London, England.

name: Once Upon A Time
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
THROW all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A short and lightly bitter aperitif-style cocktail with an attractive golden, almost sunny appearance.
history: Adapted from a 2019 recipe by Mattia Lombardi, Amsterdam, this cocktail was inspired by the Negroni and the Brandy Alexander, and a mistake he made during his first bar shift while making them. Mattia says, "The white wine vinegar is a dedication to my colleague and friend Cécile Fayn, as it's her favourite ingredient."

name: Improved Bronx
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of tangerine/mandarin wedge on rim.
review: Delicate, fragrant fruity notes from freshly squeezed tangerine/mandarin juice freshen this version of the classic Bronx. My recipe for both versions of the Bronx are the same, it's just the use of tangerine/mandarin/clementine orange juice that denotes this "Improved".
history: See: Bronx cocktail history

name: Cosmopolitan Cocktail (Dale DeGroff's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail, then use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: This is the definitive Cosmo recipe from the man most associated with the drink's development and popularity.
history: In his 2002 The Craft of the Cocktail, Dale DeGroff explains that while he did not invent the Cosmopolitan, "What I did do was popularize a definitive recipe that became widely accepted as the standard." He also gives the above recipe but with just ½oz (15ml) Cointreau, a measure which he increased to ¾oz (22.5ml) for the 2020 Revised Edition of his book. This could be as much due to a reduction of sugar in Cointreau to conform with fashion since Dale created his recipe at Manhattan's Rainbow Rooms in the mid-1990s as his revisiting and polishing an old recipe.

name: Cosmopolitan Delight
instructions: SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with red wine.
review: No relation to the modern Cosmopolitan, this is a mellow, balanced blend of citrus, brandy and red wine.
history: Adapted from Dale DeGroff's 2002 book, The Craft of the Cocktail. He, in turn, credits the original recipe to Charlie Paul's 1902 book American and Other Iced Drinks.

name: Cowboy Hoof Martini
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients (including mint) with ice and syuper fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Lightly sweetened gin shaken with fresh aromatic mint.
history: Created in the early 1990s by Dick Bradsell at Detroit in London, England.

name: Cox's Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of cox's apple ring (in memory of Jennings Cox).
review: Vanilla and pineapple bring out the sweetness of the rum against a citrus background.
history: One of two cocktails with which I won Havana Club's The Best Daiquiri in London Competition back in 2002. It is named after Jennings Cox, the American mining engineer credited with first creating the Daiquiri.

name: Tequila Manhattan
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express orange zest twist over cocktail and discard. Garnish with maraschino cherry.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: A Sweet Manhattan but with the distinctive herbal agave notes of tequila.
history: Manhattan cocktail history

name: Yellow Daisy
instructions: SHAKE (or stir) all ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: This is the kind of cocktail that many bartenders will want to stir rather than shake. However, it is classically shaken. Herbal, zesty and spirituous. Absinthe plays a key role but does not overwhelm.
history: Despite its name, the Yellow Daisy is not a member of the daisy family of cocktails. Best described as a sweetened and aromatised Martini, It was a popular drink in the early 20th century and featured in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book accompanied by a notation saying the drink was the favourite of, and perhaps invented by, Richard William "Deadwood Dick" Clark. He was a famous larger-than-life wild west character of the day who mixed with the likes of Buffalo Bill and Calamity Jane.

name: Isla de Piño
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with grapefruit wedge on rim.
review: There's an interesting play between rum, vermouth and grapefruit which, with the right grapefruit, can be delicious.  (Juice ripe pink or red grapefruit.)
history: This classic Cuban cocktail takes its name from the eponymous island off the Cuban coast which is famed (at least it is in Cuba) for the quality of the grapefruit grown there.

name: Clarified Vodka Milk Punch
instructions: <strong>Milk Punch Base pre-batch</strong> (scale-up recipe according to requirement)
<strong>1.</strong> POUR first 7 ingredients (with peels from the zests lemons and orange) into a sealable container and leave to infuse for 12 hours in a refrigerator
<strong>2. Hot milk method</strong> HEAT milk until just starting to simmer and pour (while still simmering) the vodka tea and juice mix into the milk while stirring
As soon as the milk curdles, stop stirring
Chill for at least 2-3 hours and up to 24 hours
<strong>OR 2. Cold milk method</strong> POUR vodka tea and juice mix into cold milk while stirring
As soon as the milk curdles, stop stirring
Chill for at least 2-3 hours and up to 24 hours
<strong>3.</strong> Fine STRAIN mixture through cheesecloth or Superbag into a large jar or another container
Ideally, this should be in a refrigerator (I removed a shelf in ours) with the cheesecloth/bag hung over the jar
<strong>4.</strong> Repeat step three, without removing curds from the filter or move to a finer strainer (e
. from a 400-micron bag to a 100-micron bag) or coffee filter
<strong>5.</strong> Stored in the refrigerator, this will keep for a week (and potentially months), but be sure to sterilise containers, etc
<strong>Cocktail itself</strong>
STIR 50ml pre-prepared Milk Punch Base with 50ml vodka and 7.5ml sugar syrup and strain into an ice-filled glass (preferably over a chunk of block ice)
review: This cocktail requires starting preparation at least a day before you want to drink it, but with its velvety texture and delicate citrus flavour, it's worth the faff. This recipe works with Lactose-free milk, but it must be whole-fat milk.
history: Popular in eighteenth-century England, the earliest known clarified brandy milk punch recipe, by housewife Mary Rockett, dates back to 1711. The milk punch became so highly regarded that in 1838, Queen Victoria awarded a Royal Warrant to Nathaniel Whisson & Co. as "Purveyors of Milk Punch to Her Majesty" for their bottled version.

name: Sidecar
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review:
Cognac shines over freshening lemon and orange zestiness. Hopefully, you'll find this recipe perfectly balanced, but those with a sweet tooth may prefer to serve in a glass with a sugar rim - as has become traditional for a Sidecar.

The proportions of this cocktail are debated as much as the Sidecar's origin (see below). The earliest published recipe (Robert Vermeire's 1922 Cocktails: How to Mix Them and Harry McElhone's 1922 ABC of Cocktails) follows an equal parts formula (1 x brandy, 1 x triple sec and 1 x lemon juice) and, perhaps due to ease rather than balance, remains popular.

In his 1948 Fine Art of Mixing Drinks, David A. Embury writes of the 'equal parts' Sidecar, "This is the most perfect example of a magnificent drink gone wrong". He argues, "Essentially the Side Car is nothing but a Daiquiri with brandy in the place of rum and Cointreau in the place of sugar syrup or orgeat." Embury then gives his 8:2:1 Daiquiri formula (2 brandy, ½ triple sec and ¼ lemon juice).

In his 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book, Harry Craddock calls for 2 brandy, 1 triple sec and 1 lemon juice.

Some refer to MacElhone and Vermiere's equal parts recipe as belonging to "the French school," while the Savoy's 2:1:1 formula is said to be from the "English school." 

My previous recipe of 6:3:2 recipe (45ml brandy, 22.5ml triple sec and 15ml lemon juice) [replaced 15 Dec 25] takes the middle ground between The Savoy and the 'equal parts' camp, but the Sidecar is better when the triple sec's sweetness is bolstered by a spoon of sugar syrup and the proportion of liqueur lowered and the lemon juice raised. As per the recipe above. Or, even better, add Pineau des Charentes in place of sugar syrup and follow my Sidecar recipe.
history:
In his 1948 Fine Art of Mixing Drinks, David A. Embury says of the Sidecar's origin: "It was invented by a friend of mine at a bar in Paris during World War I and was named after the motorcycle sidecar in which the good captain customarily was driven to and from the little bistro where the drink was born and christened."

Embury doesn't name the bar, but it's commonly assumed that he meant Harry's New York Bar and that its owner, Harry MacElhone, created the cocktail. However, in his own 1922 ABC of Cocktails, Harry credits the cocktail to Pat MacGarry, "the Popular bar-tender at Buck's Club, London." However, in later ABC of Cocktails editions, he appears to take credit for the cocktail himself. The fact that MacElhone worked alongside Malachi "Pat" MacGarry at Buck's Club when it first opened just a few years earlier in June 1919 may have some bearing on this.

MacGarry being the originator of the Sidecar is further reinforced by the first printed recipe appearing in Robert Vermeire's Cocktails How To Mix Them, published in May 1922, just a few months before Harry MacElhone's ABC of Cocktails.

name: Strawberry Caipirinha
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
CUT lime in half (from pole to pole) and then slice one of the halves into eight 3-4mm thick half-moon shaped segments and drop into the base of a robust rocks glass.
Add 3 bar spoons powdered caster sugar (or ½oz / 15ml sugar syrup 2:1) and MUDDLE.
Add strawberries and MUDDLE some more.
When sure fruit juice is fully extracted and the sugar dissolved, TRANSFER the muddled fruit and sugar into serving glass (preferably a 30cl / 10oz hi-ball glass).
POUR cachaça (stored in the freezer) into the rocks glass (used to muddle) and swirl to rinse glass before pouring cachaça into serving glass.
STIR to ensure all ingredients are well mixed.
FILL glass with small cubes or cracked ice. (If using large ice cubes, crack (rather than crush) four to five large ice cubes using an ICE TAPPER and drop into serving glass.)
Briefly STIR before topping with more ice.
garnish: Garnish with stirrer (but no straws).
review: Easy drinking, fruity and refreshing with cachaça's distinctive flavour.
history: For information on the history and other variations of the Caipirinha, please see our Caipirinha cocktail page.

name: Alabaster
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of small lime wedge on rim.
review: Aloe liqueur sits brilliantly with clove-led falernum in this refined rum sour.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2023 by Jake Powell, bar manager at Death & Co. in Denver, USA. Originally made with Probitas rum.

name: Double Black
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with cocoa powder.
review: Dark bitter chocolate and rich espresso with delicate spice.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 Wilson Hernandez at El Del Frente in Havana, Cuba.

name: Little Cuba
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Chocolate and raisin flavours with delicate herbal spice add complexity to this Rum Negroni.
history: Created in 2019 by Lisa Kempen, Head Bartender at Deadshot in Auckland, New Zealand.

name: Spiced Rum Negroni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Mellow and slightly sweet with flavours of caramel, chocolate and rich orange.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Rogerio Igarashi at Trench Bar in Tokyo, Japan.

name: Aigua de València
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COPA GLASS.
Slowly, POUR sparkling wine into ice-filled glass.
STIR other ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass over sparkling wine.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: This boozier Spanish take on a Mimosa is authentically made with the juice of oranges from the Valencia region. Perfect for a lazy hot afternoon, preferably served with some Spanish sun.
history:
Aigua de València (Valencian Water) is often made and served as a punch in pitchers and is a popular drink in the tapas bars of the eponymous Spanish city.

Aigua de València was first made in 1959 by Constante Gil at Café Madrid in Valencia and the story goes that a group from the Basque Country had become regulars at the bar with their usual order being "Agua de Bilbao", meaning Water of Bilbao and referring to cava. (The majority of cava is produced in Catalonia, but there is also some production in the Basque Country.) Challenged to devise an alternative for the group, Gil created "Agua de Valencia" by adding local orange juice and then fortifying the mixture with gin and vodka. This proved a hit with the group and became a staple at the bar before finding broader appeal in other Valencian bars during the 1970s.

name: Smoky Orchard Highball
instructions: STIR first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass glass charged with some of the smoky tonic water, then TOP with more smoky tonic.
review: Savoury, delicately fruity and refreshing with delicate ripe pear, dry gin and celery with a hint of smoke.
history: Created in April 2019 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Verdita (makes 12 shots)
instructions: BLEND all 5 ingredients with stalks but without ice (no need to chop ingredients if your blender is powerful enough) and fine strain into suitable storage container from which to pour into individual shot glass serves
Refrigerate, SHAKE before use and use within 3 days.
review: Verde is Spanish for green and Verdita is best described as being a green herbal Sangrita with pineapple adding a hint of tropical fruit and sweetness. Leave the seeds in the Jalapeño if you want spicy and remove if you don't.
history: A traditional accompaniment to tequila in Mexico where it is sipped alternatively.

name: Rum Negroni No. 2
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: I started with a classic three equal parts Negroni, simply subbing rum for gin, but the rum was masked by the bitter liqueur so I boosted rum notes with a splash of cachaça.
history: Inspired by Rogerio Igarashi's Spiced Rum Negroni, recipe by yours truly in August 2019 at The Clocktower, Rye, England.

name: Mrs Hendrickx Lemonade Highball
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass, while also pouring soda water.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice wheel.
review: Bready genever fortifies and rounds this mandarin orange influence 'real' lemonade.
history: Created in 2019 and served at Boilerman Bars in Hamburg and Munich, this highball honours Myriam Hendrickx, Master Distiller of Rutte.

name: Gin Spider Highball
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A Pink Gin & Ginger would also be a fitting name for this tasty alternative to a Gin & Tonic.
history: As part of his long section on Highballs, in his 1948 The Fine Art Of Mixing Drinks David Embury notes, "Gin and ginger ale, with a dash of Angostura, is sometimes called a Gin Spider."

name: Darkside
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: This gin, Barolo Chinato and Peychaud's bitters trio is remarkably reminiscent of a Negroni.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Adam Bernbach at Bar Pilar in Washington, D.C.. Adam's original recipe calls for a boozy 75ml of gin and 22.5ml Barolo chinato.

name: Ad Astra
instructions: STIR all ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass
(Ideally, use strained lemon juice
Otherwise, fine strain cocktail when pouring.)
review: Ad Astra is a Latin phrase meaning "to the stars", an apt name for this spirituous and complex riff on a classic Aviation.
history:
Created by Ben Simpson of Smoke & Oakum Manufactory Ltd, Wellington, New Zealand and the name behind Gunpowder Rum. Ben shared his "spin on the old Aviation cocktail" with us in May 2019 saying, "Somewhere along the line I got it into my head that the Aviation was, at heart, a martini variant (I think I even found some gin/maraschino type cocktails to back my argument). For this reason, the lemon juice is vastly reduced, and the drink is stirred.

"To this idea, I added the suggestion that the washed-out purple colour of crème de violette in the finished drink references aviation fuel (or 'Av-Gas' as they used to call it when I did my basic flight training). Then I kept making stuff up as I prepared the drink: the spinning of the ice within the mixing glass is similar to the action of the rotary engine that dominated the early years of flight, with the mixing spoon being the propeller shaft. Finally, the drop of castor oil on top harks back to the use of this in rotary engines to keep the whole hot, spinning mass from seizing. Unfortunately, the result of this was a slipstream saturated in castor oil, including both the plane and the pilot (hence the image of the leather trenchcoat-wearing pilot wiping his goggles with his silk scarf)."

name: Kingston
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Caraway (kummel) and pimento spice (allspice dram) shine in this aged rum sour.
history: The Kingston is a classic cocktail from the 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book but this recipe is by Frank Caiafa, author of the 2016 The Waldorf Astoria Bar Book, who says, "I've added the touch of lemon juice for a bit of zip, but you could use a couple of dashes of Acid Phosphate or try it without, as they did at The Old Bar [at The Waldorf Astoria]." Frank prefers to use Appleton Reserve for his rum and Combier Kummel.

name: Improved Dunlop Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: Rich and fruity tawny port sits beautifully with aged rhum agricole in this spirit-forward late-night sipper.
history: A 2018 riff on the classic Dunlop Cocktail from The Savoy Cocktail Book by Lulu Martinez of LunaDrinks Inc., USA.

name: Barracuda
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: There's a touch of Tiki to this pineapple rum sour that's made distinctive by peppermint and herbal flavours.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in the late 1950s by Italian bartender Benito Cuppari while working on the Cristoforo Colombo cruise liner. Benito perfected his tropical cocktail in 1965 soon after moving to Bar Lido, one of seven bars onboard the new SS Michelangelo cruise liner and re-named it after the Barracuda beach club in Portofino which was managed by a great friend of his and during the 50s and 60s was one of Europe's most famous nightclubs. Benito and his cocktail won the Long Drink category at the 1966 Italian AIBES National Cocktail Competition held in Saint Vincent so also helping the cocktail's notoriety.

Initially served in half a pineapple shell, after marketing support from Galliano, this was upgraded to a souvenir ceramic pineapple and quickly became the ship's signature cocktail. Galliano's promotional pineapple mug was imprinted with the Barracuda recipe, also using two other products from the same brand portfolio.

name: Harvest Moon (1930s recipe)
instructions: Select a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review:
Described as being an "applejack sour with orgeat," we've adapted the recipe to use commercially available orgeat. If using a more artisan orgeat then you may want to use as much as 30ml/1oz orgeat and forgo the sugar and water.

There is no orange in the cocktail but it's surprising how much the orange zest twist influences this drink and adds complexity.
history: We have Robert Hess and his The Cocktail Spirit podcast to thank for the Harvest Moon and drawing our attention to its feature in a list of the Top 10 Most Popular Cocktails in the December 1934 edition of Esquire Magazine.

instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Mezcal and cognac fuel this stirred and strong nightcap sipper.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2014 by Ezra Star at Drink in Boston, USA, this Martinez riff is named after the year the half-decade long The Mexican Revolution (Mexican Civil War) started. This had a transformative effect on both Mexican culture and government.

name: Queen Mother
ingredients: 25 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
5 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lemon and orange zest twists over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare lemon and orange zest for garnish.
review: Pot still Jamaican rum adds delicate but distinctive funky notes to this lightly sweet fruity gin-laced cocktail.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2014 by Jake Burger at The Portobello Star, London, England. Naturally, Jake's original recipe calls for Portobello Road Gin.

Elizabeth Angela Marguerite Bowes-Lyon (1900–2002), better known as Queen Elizabeth The Queen Mother, was the wife of King George VI and the mother of Queen Elizabeth II and Princess Margaret, Countess of Snowdon. During WWII her spirit gave moral support to her subjects while in her later years she was viewed as matriarch to the Royal Family, enjoying public popularity throughout. She remained publicly active until just before her death at 101 years old, perhaps helped by her reputed love of gin and Dubonnet Red.

name: Absolute Gangster
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A mere four drops of Abbotts Bitters play a crucial role in this delicately sweetened and complex vodka-based cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2016 by Ricardo Dynan, global brand ambassador for Absolut vodka. Naturally, the name is a reference to this cocktail originally being based on Absolut vodka.

name: Tropicalia
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of bouquet of Gypsophila (Baby's Breath) and cocktail umbrella.
review: Delicately spiced with cacao and coffee sitting atop a basket of fruit (overripe banana, pineapple and lime).
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Sufian Mahmoud, Sugar Sand (Hotel Indigo Seminyak), Bali, Indonesia.

name: Sweet Tears of Mine
instructions: Select and pre-chill a DOUBLE OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of caramel popcorn.
review: The smell of the caramel popcorn garnish as you sip completes this lightly sour caramel, cacao and coffee cocktail.
history: Adapted from a 2019 recipe created by Shavinraj Gopinath at The Iron Fairies, Bangkok, Thailand.

name: Apple Business
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Garnish with skewered apple slice fan.
review: Rich honey balances zesty lime in this flavoursome gin and apple sour.
history: Adapted from a 2017 recipe by Theo Lieberman, New York City.

name: Spanish Espresso Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with 3 coffee beans floated in a petal formation in the centre of the cocktail's foamy head.
review: This riff on Dick Brasell's classic Espresso Martini introduces rich vanilla and faint and delicate herbal notes.
history: A cocktail promoted by Licor 43 Liqueur and added to D in 2019.

name: Bitter Giuseppe
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SQUEEZE the oils from a lemon zest into stirring glass and drop zest in.
Add other ingredients and REGAL STIR with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist (in addition to twist used in the recipe).
review: It's bitter Giuseppe, best you enjoy this as an aperitivo.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2007 by Stephen Cole at The Violet Hour in Chicago, Illinois.

name: Ce Soir (This Evening)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Brandy-based, bittersweet and herbal. A good after-dinner digestif.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2010 by Nicole Lebedevitch at The Hawthorne, Boston, USA.

name: Combustible Edison
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
WARM brandy in a metal ladle and ignite before pouring (from a height) as a flaming stream into the glass already containing other ingredients. PLEASE EXERCISE CAUTION!
EXPRESS flamed lemon zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist (flamed).
review: The variation, the non-combusted merely "Edison" or "The Edisonian", which calls for all the ingredients (inc. brandy) to be stirred together with ice, is a bitter drink but lacks the theatre. Opt for showmanship or discernment – your call. Whichever, this bittersweet cocktail is suited to the aperitivo moment.
history: Adapted from a recipe by Robert "Brother Cleve" Toomey, a regarded bartender, DJ and musician. Brother Cleve was a member of the band Combustible Edison and this cocktail was created in the 1990s by Michael "The Millionaire" Cudahy, the band's leader. The drink appeared on the back cover of the band's first CD, I, Swinger.

name: El Patron
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of black olive.
review: Chocolate and coffee flavours from the spiced rum are central to this spirit-forward aperitivo/digestivo.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Jorge A. Conde at Smoke & Mirrors, Singapore. Originally made with Cocchi Storico Vermouth di Torino rosso vermouth.

name: La Familia (by Ian Burrell)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Tommy's Margarita meets a Daiquiri with spice, chocolate and coffee. Tasty!
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Ian Burrell, London, England.

name: Devil's Margarita
instructions: SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
Carefully FLOAT wine.
review: Due to a lack of orange liqueur, this is not strictly a Margarita. However, the red wine float fits the name and sipping the underlying 'margarita' through this layer makes for a devilishly good drink.

name: Bicicletta Spritz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Basically the same as a Veneziano Spritz but with equal proportions wine to Italian red bitter liqueur, we like to further distinguish the Bicicletta from the spritz by serving it with less volume in a tall slim highball glass rather than the larger glassware associated with spritz. Much more appropriate for the ride home.
history: Bicicletta (Italian for bicycle) is another name for Veneziano Spritz and is one of many examples of regional differences across Italy in the name of what is essentially the same drink. The name is said to have originated due to old men wobbling home on their bikes after an evening of Biciclettas with their friends.

name: Glee Club Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with skewered maraschino cherry.
review: Raspberry notes hit your palate first, followed by Chartreuse influenced sherry. Fruity yet spirit-forward and complex.
history:
The Glee Club Cocktail first appeared in Tim Daly's 1903 Daly's Bartenders' Encyclopedia and comprised "2 dashes of orange bitters, 1 dash of yellow Chartreuse, 1 dash of maraschino, 1 wine glass of raspberry wine."

This recipe, adapted from one by Jack McGarry at New York's Dead Rabbit, uses framboise eau-de-vie in place of raspberry wine.

name: Lemon Drop Crusta
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass with pre-crusted rim.
garnish: Due to attractive pre-crusted rim, no further garnish is required.
review: Zesty lemon with a sugar-crusted rim. This tasty drink's name aptly describes it.
history: This riff on a vodka sour, inspired by the hard candy sweet, may have earlier origins but rose from the popularity of the Lemon Drop Martini during the 1990s, a time when pretty much every cocktail came in a v-shaped glass and was called a "Martini". Then it was usually garnished with a lemon zest twist, and was sometimes also served in a glass with a sugar crusted rim, in which case it is a bona fide Crusta (although it was never a true Martini). This version by yours truly.

name: Persepolis
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of turkish delight slice.
review: With its strong rose flavour, the Persepolis is somewhat reminiscent of a Turkish Delight so is very appropriately garnished.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2018 by Sergio Leanza at Funkidory, Peckham, London, England. Sergio describes his drink as being "a Middle Eastern version of a Daiquiri, dedicated to Marjan Satrapi (novelist/illustrator)." Sergio's original recipe calls for 40ml Puerto Rican style rum, 10ml Strega, 15ml rose syrup and 15ml lime juice. He uses a homemade 1:1 rose syrup: 5g rose petals, 500ml water and 500gr caster sugar.

Incidentally, Pārsapura was the capital of the Achaemenid Empire (ca. 550–330 BCE), situated 60 km northeast of the modern-day Iranian city of Shiraz.

name: Count Mast
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: This three equal part cocktail makes for a perfect aperitivo.
history: A signature cocktail promoted by Jägermeister.

name: Nui Nui
instructions: BLEND all ingredients with 6oz scoop of crushed ice
Place garnish in glass so hanging over the edge
Pour cocktail into glass and top with more crushed ice.
review: Cinnamon and pimento spiced golden rum with cleansing brightening citrus. Balanced, cooling and delicious.
history: Adapted from a 1930s recipe by Don Beach at his Don The Beachcomber, Restaurant.

name: Saturn
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SLING GLASS.
DRY BLEND (without ice) first 5 ingredients (all but Xanthan gum) on a slow setting and then gradually add Xanthan while continuing to blend.
Add 7oz scoop of crushed ice and BLEND some more.
POUR into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pre-prepared skewered cherry planet encircled by a lemon zest ring rested across the rim.
review: A Tiki-influenced frozen gin sour with passion fruit and almond.
history:
Created by José "Popo" Valencia Galsini, who emigrated from the Philippines to become a well-known bartender in California. He came up with the Saturn in 1969, while working at Palms In The Jungle restaurant in Anaheim, and it was his winning cocktail in that year's International Bartender's Association World Championship.

Originally named X-15, after the North American Aviation rocket spaceplane, Popo renamed his creation after the third of the experimental planes crashed on 15th November 1967. The pilot who died that day, Major Michael J. Adams, was posthumously awarded astronaut wings as his flight, prior to the in-flight breakup, exceeded the altitude that NASA recognises as the edge of space.

The Saturn is known to modern bartenders thanks to Jeff "Beachbum" Berry, who copied the recipe from a commemorative glass he found in a Californian thrift shop. After researching the cocktail and its creator, he published the Saturn in his 2010 book Beachbum Berry Remixed.

name: La Cola Nostra
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass pre-charged with prosecco.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: We do like a pun and this one is a particularly tasty aperitivo.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2018 by Don Lee, New York City.

name: Black Espresso
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
review: This riff on a classic Espresso Martini has great depth of flavour with cacao and currant flavours.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2019 by Keisuke KSK Yamamoto at Jeremiah Tokyo in Tokyo, Japan.

name: Vanessa Polk
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered Luxardo Maraschino Cherries.
review: Cherry red, spirit-forward and dangerously quaffable as a late-night tipple.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2025 by J. E. Clapham of Clapham Cocktails and named after the "rather impressive heiress" in P.G. Wodehouse's A Pelican at Blandings. The original recipe calls for Chairman's Reserve Rum.

name: Rum Dum Sour
ingredients: 15 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE (without ice) and fine strain into ice-filled glass
FLOAT pot still rum.
review: A light rum sour sipped through a float of pungent pot still rum.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 1971 by Wilfred Sands while bartending at the Lyford Cay Club in New Providence, Bahamas. Wilfred apparently made the drink at the request of a member who sought a short rum cocktail that was not overly sweet.

name: Bucket List
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Rosemary sprig.
review: Floral, honeyed, herbal and vodka-laced.

name: Mezcal Jackson
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Prepare garnish of smoked almonds or a single flake of coconut.
review: Smoky almond and delicate coconut combine in this elegant after-dinner sipper.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Sean Lisik.

name: Life is Beautiful
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: This dry herbal aperitif cocktail is enriched with the floral tones of elderflower liqueur.
history:
Adapted from a 2019 recipe created by Andy Keir at Bruach, Dundee, Scotland. Andy's original recipe calls for Star of Bombay Gin.

"This drink was inspired by my La Classica experience, the places we visited, and drinks shared with friends.  The name is taken from an Italian movie of the same name, which was filmed in Arezzo, of course the starting point for La Classica 2019.  The name reflects the feeling we all shared that 1st morning.

This cocktail is essentially a reverse Martinez, chosen to showcase Martini Ambrato.  I've paired it with Star of Bombay as the higher ABV and intensity of flavour ensure the presence of gin isn't lost.  I discounted using an Old Tom gin here as there is enough sweetness from the other ingredients, and it also keeps the cocktail 'on brand'.  The addition of elderflower helps to balance the cocktail and adds a floral finishing note."

name: Bon Vivant's Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of mist with spray of Islay single malt (& optional Cigarillo across the rim).
review: Ideally, this Martini should be accompanied by a cigar which compliments the tobacco liqueur.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2018 by Tom Sipos in Austria. This cocktail was the winning Elit Art of Martini 2018 Global Final in Bilbao, Spain.

name: Clockwork Orange No.2
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail, then use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Easy drinking, neither sweet or sour, this cocktail with its subtle coffee notes suits breakfast, teatime or aperitivo but was originally created as a dessert cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Taylor Hall at Tandem Bar, Newtown, Sydney, Australia. Taylor was inspired to create this dessert cocktail after eating a Jaffa Cake.

name: Piña Clara
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
THROW all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: This delicious and light long drink harnesses the classic combination of coconut, rum and pineapple.
history: Adapted from a cocktail discovered in October 2019 at Soho House, 76 Dean Street, London, England.

name: Ginger Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of candied ginger on a stick.
review: A warming medium-dry Vodka Martini with enlivening ginger spice for a cold evening.
history: Created in October 2019 by yours truly at the Clocktower, Rye, England.

name: Hunter's Tea
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR first 3 ingredients until maple syrup dissolves.
Add bittersweet liqueur and STIR with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Liquorice, chocolate, black tea and citrus. Equally as good enjoyed as an aperitif or digestive.
history: Adapted from a signature cocktail promoted by Jägermeister.

name: Grapefruit Drop Crusta
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COPITA SHERRY GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into pre-prepared glass.
garnish: Garnish with grapefruit peel around inside of rim.
review: Easy-drinking vodka-laced refreshingly zesty grapefruit.
history: Created in October 2019 by yours truly (Simon D) at the Clocktower, Rye, Sussex, England.

name: Burnt Fuselage
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lemon (or orange) zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon (or orange) zest twist.
review: Cognac and Grand Marnier are well-suited bedfellows in this spirit-forward "stiff steadier". Dry vermouth combines with these surprisingly well. Adding balance and herbal complexity.
history:
The Burnt Fuselage first appears in the "Cocktails Around Town" section of Harry MacElhone's 1927 Barflies and Cocktails where he lists cocktails of regulars ("Men About Town") at his Harry's New York Bar.

To quote MacElhone, "Chuck Kerwood takes to the air so frequently that he likes a stiff steadier when he comes down to earth. The famous flying man calls his concoction the 'Burnt Fuselage'. And believe me, 1/3 Grand Marnier, 1/3 Cognac, and 1/3 French vermouth, and your own fuselage will be warm, to say the least." From Philadelphia, USA Colonel C.W. (Chuck) Kerwood, was President of the International League of Aviators and was apparently known as the "wild man of aviation."

name: Landing Gear
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A riff on the classic Burnt Fuselage. Spiritous and richly fruity with orange and cherry combining harmoniously with brandy.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by J. E. Clapham of Clapham Cocktails.

name: Swedish Paloma
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring soda.
garnish: Garnish with grapefruit wedge.
review: Lighter and zestier than a Mexican Paloma.

name: Dunkel and Stürmig
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with beer and ginger beer.
Briefly STIR.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Dunkel & Stürmig is German for Dark & Stormy and indeed this ia a tasty coffee and cacao spiced rum riff on the classic Dark 'N' Stormy.
history: Created by Dirk Hany at Bar am Wasser in Zurich, Switzerland.

name: Bird of Paradise Gin Fizz
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker

DRY SHAKE (without ice) and slowly pour 2/3rds of the contents of the shaker from a higher than normal height into a chilled empty glass (no ice in glass) while simultaneously pouring soda with your other hand
The cocktail and soda should form a single stream of liquid falling into the glass
As when pouring a Guinness, to achieve a perfect head on a fizz, the cocktail should be left to settle for at least a minute before topping off with what remains in the shaker
Ideally, during the settling period, the glass should be placed in a glass froster/fridge/freezer
As the final contents of the shaker are poured into the glass so the head should rise like a souffle
review: A Gin Fizz with pleasing rich raspberry notes, discovered by Charles H. Baker at the Strangers Club in Panama.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Charles H. Baker's 1939 The Gentleman's Companion, a book of travel memoirs and recipes.

name: Absinthe Spider Highball
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Absinthe and ginger ale combine well in this refreshing highball.

name: Three of Strong
instructions: Select and pre-chill a TIKI MUG OR COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Prepare garnish of cinnamon stick and bamboo leaf (optional).
review: Cacao and coffee flavours from the rum shine in this fruity and delicately spicy Tiki influenced cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Dinos Constantinides at Lost + Found, Nicosia, Cyprus. Dinos original recipe calls for "Grandpa's Lemonade" made using a Thermomix (which we simplified by instead using with lemon juice and sugar syrup).

name: Patchanka
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with dehydrated orange wheel slice.
review: Lightly bitter, chocolate and coffee with zesty orange. Perfect as an aperitivo.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Ilaria Bello at Talea Bar, Milan, Italy. (Ilaria's original recipe specifies Oscar.697 Rosso vermouth.)

name: White Sting
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf.
review: This riff on a classic Stinger brings in floral rose notes which sit alongside delicately freshening mint.
history: Created in 2020 by Salvatore "The Maestro" Calabrese at the Donovan Bar, London, England.

name: White Grasshopper
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grated white chocolate.
review: Creamy and indulgent but not sickly sweet, with delicate chocolate, rose, mint and zesty orange.
history: Created in 2020 by Salvatore Calabrese at the Donovan Bar in London, England.

name: Cuba Libre
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
MUDDLE lime wedges in base of glass.
Add other ingredients.
Fill glass with ice and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Basically, a rum and coke with a squeeze of lime, but Cuba Libre has much more of a ring about it. Zesty lime and dashes of bitters add layers of complexity, balancing the sweetness of the cola.
history: In his 1939 The Gentleman's Companion, Charles H. Baker Jr. calls for the lime to be muddled to extract the oils in the skin:

name: White Spider
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with green olive.
review: A cleansing Martini-style cocktail with sophisticated citrus, rose and mint notes.
history: A White Spider is another name for a Vodka Stinger (vodka and white crème de menthe) and in 2020 Salvatore Calabrese, at London's Donovan Bar, adapted this vodka-based classic cocktail to use his Acqua Bianca, an alternative white crème de menthe liqueur.

name: Ciao Bella
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf.
review: Use a great quality white crème de cacao alongside Aqua Bianca and you'll have a surprisingly well-balanced complex and delicately chocolaty freshening after-dinner cocktail.
history: Created in 2020 by Salvatore "The Maestro" Calabrese at the Donovan Bar, London, England.

name: White Water Ride
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Funky rum and coconut water enveloped in zesty orange, rose and faint minty freshness.
history: Created in February 2020 by yours truly at the Clocktower, Sussex, England.

name: Sweetheart
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Serve accompanied by a bunch of flowers.
review:
A floral riff on a classic Cosmopolitan.  The quality of rose liqueur used makes or breaks this romantically themed cocktail.

Photographed in a glass from the Gibson Bar, London.

name: Florence Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Floral and aromatic grappa shines in this citrusy fresh cocktail.

name: Grappa Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist (discarded) and lemon slice.
review: Omit the lemon garnish and instead float a few dashes of bitters and this tasty, aromatic cocktail is a ringer for a Pisco Sour.

name: Jet Pilot
ingredients: 30 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill a DOUBLE OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
DRY BLEND (without ice) first 9 ingredients (all but Xanthan gum) on a slow setting and then gradually add Xanthan while continuing to blend.
Add 7oz scoop of crushed ice and BLEND some more.
POUR into chilled glass.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: A lightly spicy blend of three characterful rums with citrus mellowed by blending with crushed ice.
history: Created circa 1958 by socialite, Steven Crane at his Polynesian-themed Luau Restaurant in Hollywood, USA. The Jet Piolet is a riff of Donn Beach's 1941 The Test Pilot cocktail. The two recipes are very similar but the Jet Pilot benefits from the addition of grapefruit juice and cinnamon syrup.

name: Dry Martini (4:1 ratio)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lemon zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish &/or adorn with skewered olive.
garnish: Prepare garnish of green olive &/or lemon zest twist.
review: A stirred four-to-one Martini is indeed a delicious thing. But is a five-to-one Martini even more delicious? Try both, and perhaps also a three-to-one. What a wonderful way to lose an evening.

name: Jean Harlow
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest spiral twist.
review: Reminiscent of a Martinez but a tad lighter.
history: Adapted from a recipe in the 1933 book Hollywood Cocktails published by the wonderfully named, Buzza-Cardozo, a Hollywood-based company that specialised in illustrated cards and books.

name: Anurka
instructions: STIR first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass
TOP with cider.
review: Bittersweet with cider harmoniously combining with Italian red bitter liqueur and rosso vermouth.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2016 by Eddie Rudzinskas of Cocktails For You fame. After participating in Martini Ciclismo in 2019 Eddie told us, "This was my winning drink that led me to participate in the Martini Grand Prix in 2016 which introduced me to the Martini Family. Without this, I would've never got on the bike in the first place!"

name: Crazy Crossing
ingredients: 22.5 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Bittersweet with herbal botanical complexity.
history: Adapted from a recipe in W. J. Tarling's 1937 Café Royal Cocktail Book where the invention of this cocktail is credited to "F. G. Hunt". We've used a mandarin liqueur, but the original recipe calls for Van Der Hum tangerine liqueur. The original recipe is also shaken rather than stirred. (Our thanks to Jason Clapham of Oxford, England for bringing this recipe to our attention and suggesting the use of Mandarine Napoléon.)

name: The Broady
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: An agave-influenced riff on a Blood & Sand. Cherry and orange laced with tequila and a smoky hint of mezcal.
history: Created in February 2020 by yours truly for Ian Broad, a lover of cherries known by all at The Rose & Crown in Beckley, East Sussex, as "Broady". After sipping, Ian remarked, "It reminds me of going down a dark alley. The kind of place you shouldn't venture but you can't help going down."

name: Early Spring
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda.
garnish: Garnish with dill sprigs and edible flower.
review: Rhubarb flavours shine in this tall floral lavender sour.
history: A cocktail promoted in 2021 by Absolut to support their Absolut Juice: Rhubarb Edition.

name: Perennial
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist (discarded) and orange wheel slice.
review: Gently bitter rhubarb - a very tasty aperitivo.

name: Rhubarb and Tonic Highball
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of rhubarb ribbon &/or lemon wheel slice.
review: Surprisingly tasty and very refreshing. Forget rhubarb & custard.

name: Grapefruit Drop
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of sugar rim (optional) and grapefruit zest twist.
review: The grapefruit lovers Cosmopolitan. Refreshing and enlivening zesty citrus.

name: Golden Fortune
instructions: STIR marmalade with vodka in base of shaker to dissolve marmalade
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: A golden kumquat-flavoured sour with bittersweet orange marmalade.
history: Adapted from a recipe, originally based on Kavka Vodka, by Karol Terejlis for Fortunella liqueur.

name: Cucumber Martini
instructions: (Ideally, cut skin from cucumber log before chopping)
MUDDLE cucumber in base of shaker, add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Cucumber has never tasted so good, helped by being vodka-laced and infused with notes of bison grass. The touch of sugar in this cocktail helps amplify flavours rather than sweetening the cocktail.
history: There are many different Cucumber Martini recipes; this is mine - much improved in July 2023 thanks to a comment by Marco, a fellow Discerning Drinker (below).

name: Italian Chocolate Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with 3 coffee beans floated in a petal formation in the centre of the cocktail's foamy head.
review: There may be some chocolate in your coffee liqueur and perhaps cocoa beans play a role in the amaro, but like the Coffee Cocktail (which has no coffee) this delicious riff on an Espresso Martini is chocolaty without really featuring the headline chocolate in the recipe.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Nicholas Boden at Tavernonna Italian Kitchen, Kansas City, USA.

name: Tsipouro Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Falernum adds delicate clove spice to this delicious sour which as the name suggests beautifully harnesses the complex flavour of tsipouro.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Thanos Prunarus at Baba Au Rum in Athens, Greece.

name: Ramos Greek Fizz
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE (without ice) and strain into chilled (empty) glass, while at the same time pouring soda water with your other hand so both liquids form a single stream to slowly fill the glass.
review: This clever riff on a classic Ramos Gin Fizz harnesses a trio of Greek specialties: tsipouro, mastiha and yoghurt.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Aristotelis Makris.

name: Greek Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: This delicately sharp sour is indeed redolent of a rum-based Daiquiri and is a great showcase for Tsipouro's vinous flavours.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Kostas Ignatiadis at Schuman's Bar in Munich, Germany.

name: Spiced Alexander
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: As the name suggests, this riff on a Brandy Alexander has a delicately spicy finish that cuts through the creaminess.

name: Sweet Liberty's Sherry Cobbler
instructions: Select and pre-chill a WINE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Prepare garnish of fresh raspberries and mint sprig bouquet.
review: Fino sherry's minerality sits beautifully with subtle pineapple and elderflower, freshened by zesty grapefruit.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by John Lermayer at Sweet Liberty Drinks & Supply Co. in Miami, USA.

name: Fair Harvard
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: This wonderfully aromatic cocktail; benefits from dilution so consider adding a splash of chilled water or serving on-the-rocks.
history: A recipe created in 2016 by drinks writer Robert Simonson to serve at a reception at Yale School of Architecture. The speaker was from Harvard so a classic dark liquor based Harvard Cocktail seemed appropriate but the venue was an all-white room, so, in case of spillage, Robert created this clear riff on the classic.

name: Fourteenth Century
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A riff on the classic 20th Century based on Black Tears spiced rum and so named due to the Black Death reaping its havoc in the 14th Century.

name: Fumata Bianca
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
STIR first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda and briefly stir.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: Dry, lightly bitter and smoky – a thirst-quenching aperitivo.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2016 by Timothy Miner in New York City.

name: Cosmonaut
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients in base of shaker to dissolve jam.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered raspberry.
review: It's rich in both colour and flavour. Don't scrimp on the quality of raspberry jam; use three flat barspoons (15-20ml). Sasha's original recipe specifies Bonne Maman Raspberry Conserve.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created by Sasha Petraske at his Milk & Honey bar in New York City, USA.

In Georgette Moger-Petraske's 2016 Sasha Petraske Regarding Cocktails, Michael Madrusan, who worked alongside Sasha at Milk & Honey, says of the Cosmonaut, "Sasha named it as a jab towards the popularity of the Cosmopolitan, which this drink, Emphatically, is not."

name: Strawberry Margarita (frozen)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
DRY BLEND (without ice) first 5 ingredients (all but Xanthan gum) on a slow setting and then gradually add Xanthan while continuing to blend.
Add two-thirds full 7oz scoop of crushed ice and BLEND some more.
POUR into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with strawberry on rim.
review: Slightly sweet but yummy with an interplay between tequila, rich strawberry and tart fresh lime.

name: Herbaceous Border
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of celery stick stirrer.
review: Genepi and celery add herbal and vegetal flavours (in place of triple sec) to this Margarita riff.
history: Created by yours truly on 11th April 2020 during the Covid-19 lockdown.

name: Honey Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Strictly speaking, as this cocktail doesn't contain an orange liqueur it is not a Margarita but should properly be termed a "Honey Tequila Sour". We decided Honey Margarita had more of a ring to it. Very tasty it is too.

name: The Field Marshal
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of split grapefruit coin zest on rim.
review: Sauvignon Blanc wine, celery, pineapple and rum all sit harmoniously in this cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Ed Belshaw at World Service Nottingham, England, this cocktail earned him a place in the UK final of Bacardi Legacy 2019 (held in February 2020).

name: Pear and Elderflower Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pear slice on rim (& pegged elderflower bouquet when in season).
review: With its blend of gin and vodka this fruity floral Martini is entering Vesper territory.
history: Created in February 2020 by yours truly.

name: Pedaling Diplomat
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist (discarded) and chilled Fragata Green Olive (inside glass).
review: Two different dry vermouths and a jasmine liqueur combine to produce an aromatic aperitivo.
history: Adapted from a recipe by Iván Villegas, created after being inspired by Martini Ciclismo 2020 La Classica. Iván says, "As we crossed the villages and cruised the small roads, I was sometimes struck by aromas of pines, flowers and fig trees that triggered my fond memories of long-missed Mediterranean family holidays; so I let this bond with my love for vermouth and then thought of the "Diplomat" classic cocktail since diplomats are always around people from many countries as we were during La Classica."

name: Maria Soto Robbins
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist (discarded) and float 3 coffee beans.
review: We've added a splash of water to 'open' up this subtly coffee and cacao riff on a classic Daiquiri.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2020 by Ian Burrell.

name: Adios Amigos
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review:
A trio of base spirits blend harmoniously in a cocktail of which  Trader Vic himself said, "You know that adios means good-bye. You drink two or three of these, and it's adios, believe me, it's adios."

His original recipe calls for "light Puerto Rican rum" and I originally interpreted that as a charcoal-filtered light white rum. However, I've since found this cocktail works best with a light rum with a little more age and character.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Victor Bergeron's 1972 Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide.

name: Aku Aku
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
BLEND all ingredients with a 4oz scoop of crushed ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass (help through sieve with a bar spoon).
garnish: Garnish with skewered pineapple wedge, cherry and mint sprigs bouquet.
review: This Tiki classic looks a little like frozen stagnant pond water but tastes minty fresh and rather good.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Victor Bergeron's 1972 Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide (revised edition).

name: Alaska (Straub's 1914 recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Made with an Old Tom gin with amplified botanicals and minimal sugar (some are way too sweet), this is a spirituous and, if you hit the right dilution, tasty and complex cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Jacques Straub's 1914 book Drinks. The proportions are exactly as per his recipe, but Straub calls for his Alaska to be shaken rather than stirred. [Hence, I've added a splash of water to very subtly increase dilution.]

name: Almond Old Fashioned (tequila based)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: One to please fans of both tequila and the Old Fashioned cocktail genre with amaretto adding the headline hint of almond.
history:
Created in 2005 by Mark Prat at Maze, London, England.
See: Old-Fashioned cocktail history

name: Angel's Share No.1
instructions: STIR heaped spoon of orange marmalade with cognac in base of shaker until marmalade dissolves
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Tangy citrus fruit and cognac smoothed with a hint of vanilla.
history: Created in 2005 by Milo Rodriguez, London.

name: Angel's Share No.2
instructions: Select a SNIFTER/BRANDY BALLOON GLASS.
POUR liqueur into pre-warmed glass then tilting and rotating to coat the inside with the liqueur.
DISCARD excess liqueur.
Carefully IGNITE liqueur inside the glass and allow to burn for a few seconds before extinguishing flame by placing a saucer over the glass.
Add other ingredients.
garnish: Serve without garnish but tell drinker to beware of hot glass rim.
review: A fabulous cocktail, especially when VEP Chartreuse, family reserve cognac and a 20-year-old tawny port are used, as per the original Starlight Room recipe.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2005 by Jacques Bezuidenhout at Harry Denton's Starlight Room in San Francisco, California.

name: Ante
ingredients: 20 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Medium-dry, spiritous aromatised apple with delicate grape/berry fruit and faint orange.
history: The Ante notably features in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.
