review: Rich flavoursome honey replaces sugar syrup in this natural Daiquiri. Try experimenting with different kinds of honey. We favour orange blossom honey.
history: Recipe by yours truly.

name: Honeymoon
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review:
This equal parts apple brandy and Bénédictine recipe broadly follows Ensslin's 1916 (the first published) recipe and is not only faithful to the original but the best I've tried.

Most contemporary recipes call for:
60ml (2oz) apple brandy
15ml (½oz) Bénédictine
15ml (½oz) Orange curaçao 
15ml (½oz) Lemon juice

My own previous recipe called for:
45ml (1½oz) apple brandy
22.5ml (¾oz) Bénédictine
7.5ml (¼oz) Orange curaçao
15ml (½oz) lemon juice
15ml (½oz) egg white
history: This pre-Prohibition cocktail was first published in Hugo R. Ensslin's 1916 Recipes for Mixed Drinks (1st Edition) and was popularised during the 1920s at the Brown Derby restaurant in Los Angeles, USA.

name: Hornitos Lau
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
CHURN and add more crushed ice so the cocktail fills the glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: Jaspar recommends making a batch in their glasses and refreezing them with the straws already in the glass, like the old colonels and their julep freezers in Kentucky.
history: Created in 2005 by Jaspar Eyears at Bar Tiki in Mexico City, Mexico.

name: Apricot Cosmo
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR apricot preserve with vodka until dissolved.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with apricot wedge or dried apricot half on rim.
review: The apricot preserve adds a flavoursome tang to the contemporary classic.
history: A riff on the classic Cosmopolitan cocktail created in 2004 at Aura Kitchen & Bar in London, England.

name: Hunk Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of Luxardo Maraschino Cherry dropped into glass.
review: Pineapple and vanilla combine harmoniously. American readers may notice more than a passing resemblance to a Key Lime Pie served without the Graham Cracker rim.
history: The Neo-Martini/Altern'atini Carrie Bradshaw and friends discovered in the summer of 2003. In this series, the Sex and the City stars dropped Cosmopolitans in favour of Hunks - no change there then.

name: Hurricane
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HURRICANE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: A strong, tangy, refreshing drink packed with fruit and laced with rum.
history: Named after the shape of a hurricane lamp and served in the tall, shapely glass of the same name. Thought to have originated in 1939 at The Hurricane Bar in New York City, but made famous at Pat O'Brien's in New Orleans. Some old cocktail books list a much earlier Hurricane made with cognac, absinthe and vodka.

name: Ice 'T' Knee
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Honeyed palate topped off with tannin and jasmine.
history: Created in 2004 by yours truly (Simon D), London, England

name: Iced Sake Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with cucumber slices floated on surface.
review: Icewine adds interesting and wonderfully subtle honeyed notes to this Sake Martini.
history: Created in 2004 by yours truly, London, England.

name: Abacaxi Ricaço
instructions: Select and pre-chill a PINEAPPLE SHELL (FROZEN) GLASS.
Remove the hard core from the pineapple flesh and discard; roughly chop the remaining flesh, add other ingredients and BLEND with one 12oz scoop of crushed ice.
POUR into the pineapple shell. (The flesh of one pineapple blended with other ingredients will fill two shells.)
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: Looks and tastes great but is a load of hassle to make.
history: Adapted from David Embury's 1948 The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks. Pronounced 'Ah-bah-Kah-shee Rich-kah-So', the Portuguese name of this Brazilian drink literally translates as Extra Delicious Pineapple.

name: Absolutely Fabulous
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
SHAKE first 2 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: Garnish with strawberry on rim.
review: Easy to quaff – Patsy would love it.
history: Created in 1999 at Monte's Club, London, England, and named after the Absolutely Fabulous television series.

name: Icey Apple Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Delicate with subtle flavours.
history: Created in 2004 by Simon D and updated in October 2013.

name: Iguana Wana
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Orange juice and peach schnapps, laced with vodka.

name: Illicit Affair
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Fruity, easy drinking.

name: Illusion
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with watermelon wedge.
review: This medium-sweet, lime green drink is one for a summer's day by the pool.

name: Imperial Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered olive.
review: A Fifty Fifty Martini with a dash of aromatic bitters and subtly fruity maraschino.
history: Adapted from a recipe published in Hugo R. Ensslin's 1917 Recipes for Mixed Drinks (Second Edition). The Imperial Cocktail also notably features in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Indian Rose
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with rose petal floated on cocktail.
review: Subtle rose hue and flavour.
history: Adapted from a drink discovered in 2005 at Mie N Yu, Washington D.C..

name: Inga from Sweden
instructions: MUDDLE strawberries in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass.
review: Inga must like a touch of bitter Italian with her fruit.

name: Ink No. 2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Muscle shell blue in colour. Vodka charged cranberry and orange in flavour.
history: Discovered in 2005 at Halo in Atlanta, Georgia, USA.

name: Insomniac
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with 3 coffee beans arranged in a petal formation in the centre of the cocktail's foamy head.
review: Wonderfully balanced, creamy and caffeine-laced.

name: International Incident
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: Rich and creamy.

name: Intimate Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Sweet apricot dried and balanced by vermouth and bitters. Surprisingly complex and pleasant.
history:
This cocktail featured in our first D book, published in 2001, and has been in every edition since. The recipe is from my own hand-written note for an "Intimate" cocktail in my copy of Mark Torre's 1984 The Bartender's Cherry, 2nd Edition.

I made many such notes in this, my first cocktail book, over the course of 1991, the year after I bought my liquor store and started developing my interest in cocktails. Sadly, I didn't record where I came across the "Intimate" it, just the recipe. However, this dates the cocktail's origins to before 1991.

These notes, along with recipes from the book, served as the basis for the database that drives this website. As with many of the other recipes, I've tweaked this one much over the decades.

name: Irish Alexander
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Dust with cacao powder or sprinkle crumbled Cadbury's Flake over cocktail.
garnish: Prepare garnish of cacao powder or Cadbury's Flake bar..
review: Rich, thick and creamy.
history: An Irish whiskey cream liqueur variation on the Brandy Alexander.

name: Apricot Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: This light scarlet classic cocktail combines gin, apricot and lemon juice. The aromatic bitters and lemon zest twist are essential to its balance.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Stanley M. Jones' 1977 Jones' Complete Barguide.

name: Irish Espresso Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with 3 coffee beans floated in a petal formation in the centre of the cocktail's foamy head.
review: Richly flavoured with a pleasantly bitter finish.
history: Story behind the Espresso Martini.

name: April Shower
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: This mustard coloured, medium dry, cognac-based drink harnesses the uniquely herbal edge of Bénédictine.

name: The Italian Job No. 2
ingredients: Top up with Shiraz red wine
instructions: SHAKE first three ingredients with ice and strain into glass filled with crushed ice
TOP with wine and serve with straws.
review: Mix layers with straw prior to drinking for vanillaed, almond, fruity wine.
history: Discovered in 2002 at Rapscallion, London, England.

name: Italian Sun
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Tastes rather like a bon bon (a round, sugar coated, lemon flavoured sweet).
history: Created in 2002 by Dan Spink at Browns, St Martin's Lane, London, England.

name: Jack-in-the-Box
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Smooth 'n' easy pineapple laced with apple brandy. If you like this, then you may also like French Martinis

name: Jackie O's Rose
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge on rim.
review: In its simplest form, this is a Daiquiri with added triple sec and rose water - or a Margarita with rum. Whatever, it's a good balance of sweet and sour.
history:
I don't know the origin of this cocktail, but in March 2021, I revisited the recipe to arrive at the above. Our/the original recipe:
60ml (2oz) Light white rum
15ml (½oz) Triple sec
30ml (1oz) Lime juice
15ml (½oz) Sugar syrup
2.5ml (½ spoon) Rosewater

name: Jade Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf floated on the cocktail or pegged/stuck with spot of honey to the rim.
review: A Daiquiri with a splash of fresh breath-enhancing orange and mint liqueurs.

name: Jade Garden
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
review: Not too dry, nor too sweet, but tasty, balanced and refreshing.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2004 by Michael Butt and Giles Looker of Soulshakers, England.

name: Jaded Lady
instructions: SHAKE first four ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
Carefully POUR blue curaçao through centre of drink (it should sink to the bottom).
review: Distinctly Dutch in style, this thick, creamy drink delivers orange, vanilla and more than a hint of gin.
history: Created by yours truly in 1996.

name: Jalisco No. 2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE grapes in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with grapes.
review: It's amazing how well grapes combine with tequila.
history: Created in 2003 by Shelim Islam at GE Club, London, England. Pronounced 'Hal-is-co', this cocktail takes its name from the Mexican state that is home to the town of Tequila and the spirit of the same name.

name: Jamaican Mule
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COPPER MUG OR COLLINS GLASS.
POUR first 3 ingredients into mug/glass.
Briefly STIR.
Fill glass two-thirds full with cubed ice.
TOP with ginger beer and fill glass with a crown of crushed ice.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: A spicy rum lime sour, lengthened and invigorated with ginger beer.
history: Moscow Mule history

name: Jamaican Sunset
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice wheel.
review: Overproof rum adds flavour and oomph to cranberry and orange juices.

name: Ja-Mora
ingredients: Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: SHAKE first four ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with champagne.
review: Ja-mora of this fruity champagne cocktail you drink, ja-mora you'll like it.
history: Created by Jamie Terrell and Andres Masso in 1998. Named after 'mora', the Spanish for blackberry. The 'j' and 'a' stand for the names of its two creators.

name: Japanese Slipper
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry dropped into cocktail.
review: Sweet melon, orange liqueur and lemon. A bit of a sweetie, as its lurid green colour might suggest.
history: Created in 1984 by Jean-Paul Bourguignon at Mietta's Restaurant in Melbourne, Australia.

name: Jasmine
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A slightly sour bittersweet riff on the Pegu Club. The original is just on the tart side of balanced without our addition of the optional spoon of sugar syrup.
history: Created in the mid-1990s by Paul Harrington at Townhouse Bar & Grill in Emeryville, California and named after his college friend, Matt Jasmin, who challenged Paul to "Make me something you've never made before." Inspired by the classic Pegu Club, this cocktail is the result of that challenge. The Jasmine was promoted by its inclusion in his 1998 book Cocktail: The Drinks Bible for the 21st Century.

name: Jean Marc
ingredients: Top up with Sparkling apple juice
instructions: MUDDLE mint and ginger in base of shaker
Add next two ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with Appletiser, stir and serve with straws.
review: Chartreuse combines well with apple, ginger and mint in this summertime drink.
history: Created in 2003 by yours truly after judging a Chartreuse cocktail competition in London and realising which flavours best combine with Chartreuse. Named after my friend, the then President Directeur Général of Chartreuse.

name: Jelly Belly Beany
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with Jelly Beans.
review: It's a sweetie but you're going to enjoy chewing on it.
history: Created in 2002 at Hush, London, England.

name: Genever Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with skewered maraschino cherry.
review: Bready genever notes enhance this sour.

name: Jerez
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
review: Sherry depth and stoned fruit flavours.
history: This drink heralds from one of the noble houses in Spain - well that's what the sherry PR told us, anyway, we've changed the recipe slightly.

name: Jockey Club
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Reddy-pink coloured with a sweet and sour, almondy, amaretto-like flavour fortified with gin botanicals.
history:
Adapted from a recipe which first appears in Harry McElhone's 1922 Harry's ABC of Mixing Cocktails and established as being the default recipe thanks to its being repeated by Harry Craddock in his influential 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

However, a "Jockey Club cocktail" is first mentioned in the Friday 1st September 1882 edition of The Greenville Journal as being an alternative name for a Manhattan. Quite different and unrelated cocktails named "Jockey Club" also appear in Edward Spenser's 1903 The Flowing Bowl (equal parts gin and vermouth with gum syrup, Angostura, and raspberry syrup) and Tim Daly's 1903 Daly's Bartenders Encyclopedia (equal parts Jamaican rum and vermouth with dashes of Kümmel , gum syrup and Angostura bitters).

name: John Collins
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring soda.
garnish: Garnish with skewered orange slice and maraschino cherry (sail).
review: A refreshing balance of sour lemon and sugar, laced with London dry gin and lengthened with soda. Some know this cocktail as a Tom Collins, understandably so, but a Tom Collins is based on old tom gin, while a John Collins is based on London dry gin. I borrow the wise words of David A. Embury (below) to address this confusion on our Tom Collins page.
history:
It would appear that the Collins morphed from the Gin Punch, a popular drink of the day and its creation and name are attributed to John (or possibly Jim) Collins,  head waiter at Limmer's Hotel on Conduit Street in London during the late 1800s. The 'coffee house' of Limmer's Hotel was a true dive bar, popular with sporting types during the 19th century and famous, according to the 1860s memoirs of Captain Gronow (a Victorian writer of four observational tomes) for its Gin Punch as early as 1814.

John Collins is immortalised in an 1892 limerick written by Frank and Charles Sheridan about John Collins:

name: Arnaud (Parisian Nights)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with blackberry.
review: This flavoursome, three equal parts cocktail has a lightly sweet palate followed by a dry finish. An interesting balance of blackcurrant, vermouth and gin. Some might describe it as "alcoholic Ribena and I've tried upping the gin and reducing the cassis to make drier e.g. 40ml Gin, 30ml Dry vermouth and 20ml Crème de cassis. However, this is best served with equal parts as a dessert Martini at its rich and indulgent best.
history: A classic cocktail named after the pre-war French pianist, singer and stage actress Yvonne Arnaud (1890-1958). A concert pianist as a child she went on to act in musical comedies before switching to non-musical comedy and drama around 1920. She then took dramatic cinematic roles during the 1930s and 40s, continuing to act into the 1950s.

name: Jolt'ini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with 3 coffee beans floated in a petal formation in the centre of the cocktail's foamy head.
review: A vanilla-flavoured Espresso Martini.
history: Discovered in 2005 at Degrees in Washington DC, USA.

name: Jose Collins
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring soda.
garnish: Garnish with skewered orange slice wheel and maraschino cherry (sail).
review: The classic long balance of sweet and sour with tequila adding Mexican spirit.
history: See the history of the Collins cocktail on our Tom Collins page.

name: Journalist
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Like some journalists I've met, this cocktail is classically bitter and sour. Hence, I balanced with an optional splash of sugar syrup, which I've increased from 25ml to 3.75ml. (Before selling on this recipe, I've also tried introducing bianco vermouth with three equal parts (10ml each) vermouths, and various proportions of old tom gin and dry gin.)
history: Adapted from the following recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Jubilant
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: A well-balanced aromatic, herbal and fruity sour.
history: Adapted from a recipe attributed to "J. Perosino" in W. J. Tarling's 1937 Café Royal Cocktail Book - Coronation Edition.

name: Jules Delight
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE strawberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with strawberry.
review: Sweet fortified strawberries with a cleansing balsamic vinegar bite.
history: Created in 2005 by Julien "Papa Jules" Gualdoni at Trailer Happiness, London, England.

name: Jupiter Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Bone dry and aromatic, this drink's colour is the grey hue of an overcast sky.
history:
A classic which is thought to have originated sometime in the 1920s. 

Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Kamikaze
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SHOT GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A bite-sized vodka take on the Margarita.

name: Asian Ginger Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE ginger in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with ginger slice on rim.
review: Lightly spiced with ginger, distinctly oriental in character.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2004 by Chris Langan of Barnomadics.

name: Kee-Wee Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
Cut kiwi fruit in half, scoop out flesh into base of shaker and MUDDLE.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish wit kiwi slice in rim.
review: The citrus hints in the kiwi combine brilliantly with those in the gin and fresh lemon juice.
history: My version of this ubiquitous cocktail.

name: Acapulco
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge.
review: Fruity and tropical in style and laced with tequila and rum.

name: Asian Mary
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
MUDDLE ginger with vodka in base of shaker.
Squeeze wasabi paste onto bar spoon and STIR with vodka and ginger until dissolved.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lemongrass.
review: An aptly named Bloody Mary with plenty of Asian spice.
history: Based on the classic Bloody Mary, of which more information can be found on our Bloody Mary cocktail page.

name: Key Lime
instructions: BLEND all ingredients without ice and serve.
review: Tangy, smooth and rich! Alcoholic ice-cream for grown-ups.

name: Key Lime Pie #1
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) cream and vanilla liqueur in a pre-chilled shaker to mix and aerate.
Use to rim glass and set the remainder rest aside.
SHAKE last 3 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled, rimmed glass.
Carefully FLOAT pre-prepared cream and liqueur mix over the cocktail's surface.
garnish: RIM glass crunched-up Graham Cracker or digestive biscuits. Wipe outside edge of glass rim with cream mix (from the float) and dip into crunched-up biscuits.
review: This rich cocktail is great when served as a dessert alternative.
history: Created by Michael Waterhouse, owner of Dylan Prime in New York City, USA.

name: Key Lime Pie #2
instructions: Shake all ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled, rimmed glass
To make the pie rim, wipe outside edge of rim with cream mix and dip into crunched up Graham Cracker or digestive biscuits.
review: Beautiful balance of pineapple, vanilla, sweet and sour.

name: Key Lime Pie #3
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled, rimmed glass.
review:
My favourite rendition of this dessert-in-a-glass cocktail.
To make the pie rim - wipe with cream mix and dip into crushed Graham Crackers or digestive biscuits.
history: Recipe adapted from one by Claire Smith in 2005, London, England.

name: Kir Royale
instructions: Select a FLUTE GLASS.
POUR cassis into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: Garnish with berry.
review: Champagne replaces Bourgogne Aligoté white wine in this 'Royal' rendition of Mayor Canon Kir's classic apéritif. Easy to make, easy to drink.
history:
A sparkling version of the Kir Apéritif which is thought to have first been mixed in 1904 at Café George in Dijon, France. It is named after Canon Félix Kir who promoted the mixture while Mayor of Dijon. 
Kir and Kir Royale history

name: Kiwi Bellini
instructions: Cut kiwi in half, scoop out flesh into base of shaker and MUDDLE
Add next three ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with prosecco.
review: Lemon fresh kiwi, fortified with vodka and charged with prosecco.
history: Adapted from a drink discovered at Zuma, London, England, in 2004.

name: Kiwi Collins
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: Cut kiwi fruit in half, scoop out flesh into base of shaker and MUDDLE
Add next three ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda water.
review: A fruity adaptation of a Vodka Collins.
history: For the full story behind the origins of the Collins and its many variations see our Collins page.

name: Kiwi Crush
instructions: Cut kiwi fruit in half, scoop out flesh into base of shaker and MUDDLE
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Spirit-laced kiwi, citrus and almond.
history: Adapted from a recipe by Claire Smith in 2005, London, England.

name: Knickerbocker Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Aromatic vermouth dominates but gin botanicals put up a spirited defence in this flavoursome 'medium' Martini.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Knickerbocker Special
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered Luxardo Maraschino Cherry and pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Slightly sweet and very fruity with raspberry and pineapple to the fore. Made with freshly prepared homemade raspberry syrup (1½:1) this is particularly delicious.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Knockout Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf floated on the cocktail or pegged/stuck with spot of honey to the rim.
review: A Wet Martini with hints of aniseed and mint. Stir well as it benefits from dilution.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry MacElhone's 1922 Harry's ABC of Mixing Cocktails and his 1923 Harry of Ciro's ABC of Mixing Cocktails.

name: Koi Yellow
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of rose petal.
review: Sherbet raspberry Martini with a sweet and citrus sour finish.
history: The signature drink at Koi Restaurant, Los Angeles, USA.

name: Kool Hand Luke
ingredients: 45 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill a ROCKS GLASS.
MUDDLE lime in base of glass to release juices.
POUR other ingredients into glass.
Two-thirds fill glass with crushed ice and CHURN with barspoon.
ADD more crushed ice to fill glass.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: Looks like a Caipirinha and has a similar balance of sweet, sour and spirit. The bitters bring out the spice in the rum, which is every bit as pungent as cachaça.

name: Koolaid
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Tangy liquid marzipan with hints of melon, cranberry and orange juice.
history: A drink with unknown origins that emerged and morphed during the 1990s.

name: Krakow Tea
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE mint in base of shaker (just to bruise)
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass.
review: Refreshing and floral with a dry, citrus finish.
history: Created in 2002 by Domhnall Carlin at Apartment, Belfast, Northern Ireland.

name: Kurrant Affair
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered lemon slice and blackberry (sail).
review: Berry and lime-flavoured vodkas sit with apple juice to create a surprisingly tasty, long, refreshing cocktail.
history: Created in the mid-1990s, this cocktail is named after and was originally made using Absolut Kurrant Vodka.

name: Lavender Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Infusing lavender in liquor tends to make it bitter but the Parfait Amour adds sweetness as well as flavour and colour.
history: Created in 2006 by yours truly.

name: Leap Year Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: To follow this cocktail's original proportions use 60ml (2oz) dry gin and omit the Old Tom, but you'll be missing out. Either way, despite the rich liqueur, this cocktail is on the bone dry/sour side.
history: Created 29th February 1928 by Harry Craddock for the Leap Year celebrations at The American Bar in London's Savoy Hotel. The cocktail features in his 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book with the notation, "It is said to have been responsible for more proposals than any other cocktail that has ever been mixed."

name: Leave It To Me No.1
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Gin, apricot, vermouth and lemon create an old-fashioned but well-balanced drink.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Lemon Butter Cookie
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: An appropriate name for a most unusually flavoured cocktail modelled on the Polish Martini.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2002 by Mark 'Q-Ball' Linnie and Martin Oliver at the Mixing Tin bar in Leeds, England.

name: Lemon Chiffon Pie
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
BLEND all ingredients with crushed ice.
Serve with straws.
garnish: Garnish with grated lemon zest dusted over surface.
review: Creamy and tangy - like a lemon pie. Consume in place of a dessert.

name: Lemon Drop Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Sherbety lemon - well-balanced and refreshing, but not really a Martini.
history: An alternatini which emerged and become popular during the 1990s. Like the Lemon Drop shot, it is believed to have morphed a Lemon Drop cocktail created in the 1970s by Norman Jay Hobday at his Henry Africa's bar in San Francisco, California.

name: Lemon Meringue Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Slightly creamy consistency, this tangy lemon drink is indeed reminiscent of the eponymous dessert.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2000 by Ben Reed, London, England.

name: Lemon Meringue Pie'tini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN strain into chilled and rimmed glass.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) cream and liqueur to mix and whip.
FLOAT cream and liqueur mix by pouring over back of spoon.
garnish: Apply pie rim by wiping the outside edge of glass rim with 50:50 mix of cream and sugar syrup, then dip into crunched up Graham Cracker or digestive biscuits.
review: Rich and syrupy base sipped through a vanilla cream topping.
history: Created by Michael Waterhouse at Dylan Prime in Manhattan, New York City.

name: Lemon Sorbet
instructions: Heat water in pan and add sugar
Gently simmer and stir until sugar dissolves, add lemon juice and the grated rind of 5 lemons and continue to simmer and stir for a few minutes
Take off the heat and allow to cool
Fine strain into a shallow container and stir in liqueur and orange bitters
Beat egg whites and fold into the mix
Place in freezer and store for up to 3-4 days before use.
review: Our favourite recipe for this dessert and occasional cocktail ingredient.

name: Atomic Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Cognac supercharged with vodka, rounded with sherry and invigorated by champagne. Handle with care.
history: Created in the early 1950s in Las Vegas, USA, then known as the "Atomic City" due to its proximity to the Nevada Test Site (NTS), just 65 miles north of the city. Between 1951 and 1992, NTS was America's leading nuclear weapons test site, with mushroom clouds easily seen up to 100 miles away, leading to increased tourism to Las Vegas. Throughout the 1950s into the early 1960s, the Las Vegas Chamber of Commerce capitalized on the nuclear tests and the spectacle of nuclear clouds and flashes. Casinos hosted "dawn parties" and created atomic-themed cocktails, including this one.

name: Lemony
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: With London dry gin, as originally, this is a good cocktail, but it's better with the extra character and richness of an old tom gin. As the name implies, lemon leads with pleasing botanical and herbal notes from the gin and monastic liqueur.
history:
Adapted from a cocktail called "The Lemony Snicket Cocktail" created in 2003 by Ted "Dr. Cocktail" Haigh, who named his creation after Mr. Lemony Snicket, author of the popular children's books A Series of Unfortunate Events, after Ted was commissioned as graphic designer for the Lemony Snicket's A Series of Unfortunate Events movie. Ted's original boozier recipe calls for:
75ml (2½oz) Gin
15ml (½oz) Lemon juice
15ml (½oz) Limoncello liqueur
15ml (½oz) Yellow Chartreuse

name: Leninade
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 6 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring soda.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice wheel.
review: Orange undertones add citrus depth to the lemon explosion.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created by Dick Bradsell at Fred's bar in London in the late 1980s. His original recipe (1½ shots citrus vodka, ¼ shot triple sec, 1 shot lemon juice, ¼ shot sugar syrup and 3 dashes orange bitters) was served short and straight-up with sugar syrup rather than grenadine.

I added grenadine and made the Leninade a long cocktail in July 2013 due to comments from Discerning Drinkers saying the name was suggestive of a long red vodka-based drink.

name: Limeade
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A superbly refreshing alternative to lemonade. Great on a summer's afternoon.

name: Limey Cosmo
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge on rim.
review: As the name suggests, a more lime-forward flavoured Cosmopolitan while remaining balanced rather than overly sour.
history:
Created in the early 2000s by yours truly.
See: Cosmopolitan cocktail history

name: Limey Mule
instructions: SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with ginger ale, lightly stir and serve with straws.
review: Made with plain vodka this drink is a Moscow Mule. This variant uses lime flavoured vodka.
history: A detailed history of the Moscow Mule cocktail, of which this drink is a variant, can be found on our page.

name: Limoncello Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: If you like the liqueur, you'll love the cocktail.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2005 by Francesco at Mix, New York City, USA.

name: Livingstone
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: The classic gin and vermouth Dry Martini made not so dry and a tad more approachable by a splash of sugar.
history: This vintage cocktail is named after Doctor Livingstone, the famous African missionary.

name: London Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Chilled, diluted and very subtly sweetened gin invigorated by a hint of absinthe and zesty orange.
history: Adapted from a recipe in the 1922 book Cocktails: How to Mix Them by Robert Vermeire who worked at London's Embassy Club. The title page confirms this, saying "By Robert of the Embassy Club" and the book was published in London by Hebert Jenkins Limited.

name: Long Island Lemonade
instructions: SHAKE first seven ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with lemonade
Serve with a stirrer.
review: Surprising dry with sharp zesty citrus, laced with 4 different white spirits and triple sec liqueur, lengthened with lemonade.

name: Long Flight of Stairs
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: A seriously tasty, strong, long drink. The name is a reversal of the London rhyming slang 'apples and pears' (stairs).
history: Created in 2005 by yours truly in homage to the G.E. Club's Stairs cocktail.

name: Long Island Iced Tea
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 8 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring cola.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
review: A cooling combination of four different white spirits, triple sec, lemon and lime, crowned with a splash of cola.
history:
This infamous cocktail reached the height of its popularity in the early 1980s and by 1985 The New York Times reported, "A potent alcoholic drink called Long Island Iced Tea is sweeping the Island's bars as well as watering spots across the country." 

Of the many stories surrounding its origin, perhaps the most credible attributes its creation to the 1970s, perhaps 1972, by Robert "Rosebud" Butt at Oak Beach Inn in Babylon, New York. This area of New York State is known as Long Island, and the cocktail resembles a glass of iced tea, so disguising its alcoholic content – something that has many claiming its true origins go back to Prohibition.

name: Long Island Spiced Tea
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 7 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring cola.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A contemporary spicy twist on the American classic Long Island Iced Tea.

name: Loud Speaker Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A gin influenced Sidecar.
history:
Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

Under his recipe: 1/8 lemon juice, 1/8 Cointreau, 3/8 dry gin and 3/8 brandy, Craddock states, "This it is that gives to Radio Announcers their peculiar enunciation. Three of them will produce oscillation, and after five it is possible to reach the osculation stage."

name: Loved Up
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with seasonal berries.
review: A berry and orange, loved-up, fruity riff on a Margarita.
history: Adapted from a cocktail discovered in 2002 at the Merc Bar, New York City, where the original name was listed as simply 'Love'.

name: Acapulco Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge on rim.
review: A smooth, yet citrus-rich Daiquiri.

name: Aunt Emily
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist or apricot wedge on rim.
review: Aunt Emily was onto something - equal parts gin, calvados, apricot brandy and orange juice combine well.
history: This vintage cocktail is notable due to its appearing in Charles H. Baker's 1939 An Exotic Drinking Book or Around The World With Jigger, Beaker and Flask. Baker says, "a creation of Sloppy Joe's, in Havana, years before his spot got to be a sort of halfway house for every itinerant American on the Island. The cocktail appears on Sloppy Joe's menus after 1932.

name: Lux Daiquiri
instructions: BLEND all ingredients with half a 7oz scoop of crushed ice and serve in chilled glass.
review: A classic frozen Daiquiri heavily laced with maraschino cherry.
history: Created by yours truly. This was one of the two cocktails with which I won a Havana Club Daiquiri competition in 2002. It is named after Girolamo Luxardo, creator of Luxardo Maraschino liqueur. Lux is also Latin for light.

name: Luxury Mojito
ingredients: Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: MUDDLE mint in glass with sugar and lime juice
Fill glass with crushed ice, add rum, Angostura and champagne, then stir.
review: A Mojito made with aged rum and topped with champagne instead of soda water: more complex than the original.
history: Mojito cocktail recipes and history.

name: Lychee and Rose Petal Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of float edible rose petal.
review: Light pink in colour and subtle in flavour.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2002 by the legendary bartender Dick Bradsell for Opium in London, England.

name: Lychee and Sake Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lychee.
review: Spirit-forward but mellowed by sake and lychee.
history: Created in 2004 by yours truly.

name: Juicy Lychee Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with a skewered lychee.
review: An easy-drinking, crowd-pleasing, fruity Lychee Martini.
history: Adapted from a recipe thought to have been first made in 2001 at Clay, a Korean restaurant in New York City, USA.

name: M.G.F.
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Short and sharp.
history: Discovered in 2003 at Claridge's Bar, London, England.

name: Macka
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
review: A long fruity drink for parched palates.
history: Famously, the only cocktail, indeed the only drink, served since 1928 at the Damour Colette bar in Lamballe-Armor, Brittany, France. Each customer is only allowed two.

name: Mae West cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with melon wedge on rim.
review: A rose-coloured, semi-sweet concoction with a cherry, chocolate flavour.

name: Mai Tai (Beaumont-Gantt's)
ingredients: 45 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE mint in base of shaker (just to bruise).
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
history:
It is claimed that Ernest Raymond Beaumont-Gantt first served this cocktail in 1933 at his Don The Beachcomber's bar in Hollywood, California. This is eleven years earlier than Victor "Trader Vic" Bergeron's Mai Tai origin story.

In his 2014 Potions of the Caribbean, Jeff Berry recounts that Don "told Edward Brownlee [a Tiki-carver and friend of Don] that Vic had derived the Mai Tai's flavour profile from a punch that Vic had particularly liked at the Beachcomber's: "The Q.B. Cooler was the basis for a cocktail he took with him, and he called it the Mai Tai." Fred Fung, who worked as Vic's executive assistant for 41 years, disagrees. "Vic did copy Don The Beachcomber's look. He did go down and copy. But the Mai Tai, he did concoct that, and Carrie Guild did name it."

name: Maiden's Blush
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Pale pink (depending on your grenadine), gin-laced rich fruit and subtle lemon sourness.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Maiden's Prayer
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Fresh, zesty orange with a pleasing touch of gin.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Magic Bus
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Tequila-laced cranberry and orange. Our original recipe called for equal parts of all four ingredients but, this particular bus is a tad racier when fueled with a touch more tequila.

name: Malcolm Lowry
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: The Daiquiri meets the Margarita in this suitably 'funky' and punchy cocktail.
history: Created by drinks author David Broom. Named after Malcolm Lowry's 1947 novel Under the Volcano which explores a man's battle with alcoholism in Mexico.

name: Mandarine Sidecar
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of optionally, moisten rim with orange juice and rim with sugar. Lemon zest twist expressed and discarded..
review: Sweet and sour brandy and mandarin orange.
history: See Sidecar cocktail history.

name: Mandarito
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE mint (just to bruise) in base of glass
Add next four ingredients, half fill glass with crushed ice and CHURN (stir)
Fill glass to brim with more crushed ice and churn some more
TOP with soda, stir and serve with straws.
review: A vodka Mojito with mandarine accents.

name: Mango Collins
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: SHAKE first three ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda, stir and serve with straws.
review: Lemon juice and gin combine with mango in this refreshing tall drink.
history: For the full story behind the origins of the Collins and its many variations see our Collins page.

name: Mango Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Tropical yet potent and refreshing.

name: Mango Margarita (served 'straight up')
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: The character of the tequila is not overwhelmed by the fruit.

name: Mango Margarita (frozen)
instructions: BLEND all ingredients with 6oz scoop of crushed ice
Serve with straws.
review: Mango first and Margarita second.

name: Mango Vodka Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered mango strip.
review: Full-on mango fruit. A "Martini" in so much that it's made with vodka and vermouth.
history: Adapted from a 1990s recipe which comprised 60ml (2oz) citrus vodka and 50ml (12⁄3oz) mango purée.

name: Mango Punch
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Prepare garnish of mango slice.
review: A distinctly tropical cocktail flavoured with mango.
history: Created in 2004 by yours truly (Simon D).

name: Marama Rum Punch
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE mint (just to bruise)
Add next five ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with lemonade, lightly stir and serve with straws.
review: A tangy, well-balanced punch.

name: Marguerite
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange (or lemon) zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange (or lemon) zest twist.
review: An equal parts (Fifty-Fifty) Dry Martini with a hint of orange due to the use of orange curaçao, orange bitters and an orange zest twist.
history:
Not to be confused with the much later, tequila-based Margarita, the Marguerite is a gin-based forerunner to the modern-day Dry Martini.

The earliest known Marguerite Cocktail recipe appears in the 1898 book Cocktails... How To Make Them where it tellingly precedes recipes for "Martini Cocktail – No. 1" and "Martini Cocktail – No. 2".

name: Marmalade Cocktail
instructions: STIR 3 flat barspoons of marmalade (15-20ml) with other ingredients in base of shaker to dissolve the marmalade
SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: As Harry Craddock says of his own drink, "By its bitter-sweet taste this cocktail is especially suited to be a luncheon aperitif."
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Marmarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of marmite (yeast extract) rim.
review: A Margarita with a Marmite rim. After all, yeast extract is slightly salty.
history: Created in 2005 by Simon (Ginger) Warneford at Blanch House, Brighton, England.

name: 'Martini' Thyme
instructions: MUDDLE thyme in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: A wonderfully fresh herbal Martini with the distinctive taste of Chartreuse. You'll either love it or hate it.
history: A combination of two very similar drinks, that both originally called for thyme infused gin. The first I discovered at The Lobby Bar (One Aldwych, London) and the other came from Tony Conigliaro at Isola, London, England.

name: Mary Rose
instructions: MUDDLE rosemary in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Gin botanicals with Chartreause's complex herbal notes bolstered by rosemary. Spirituous and lightly sweet. Best enjoyed after dinner.
history: Adapted (We've reduced the volume of each ingredient by 25% from the original recipe) from a recipe created in 1999 by Philip Jeffrey at the Great Eastern Hotel, London, England. Named after King Henry VIII's warship, sunk during an engagement with the French fleet in 1545 and now on display in Portsmouth.

name: Matador No.1
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim speared with a maraschino cherry.
review: A Margarita lengthened with pineapple juice. The lime and tequila work wonders with the pineapple.

name: Mauresque
instructions: POUR absinthe and almond syrup into glass
Ideally, serve iced water separately in a small jug (known in France as a 'broc') so the customer can dilute to their own taste
Lastly, add ice to fill the glass.
review: Long, refreshing aniseed, liquorice and almond.
history: Pronounced 'Mor-Esk', this classic drink is very popular in the South of France, where it is now commonly made with pastis in place of absinthe. It was originally created by French soldiers serving in the Bataillon d'Afrique during the Algerian campaign of the 1830s and 40s, and was alternatively known as Bureau Arabe after the military department which dealt with local affairs and was said to act like 'an iron fist in a velvet glove'.

name: Mayan
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim &/or float 3 coffee beans.
review: Tequila, coffee and pineapple combine harmoniously in this medium-dry cocktail.

name: Mayfair
instructions: MUDDLE clove in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Clove adds enlivening spice to this orange and apricot, gin-laced cocktail.
history:
In his 1922 How To Mix Them Robert Vermeire includes the following recipe for the Mayfair Cocktail and crucially also claims that this cocktail is his own creation.

Mayfair Cocktail
Fill the shaker half full of broken ice and add:
  ¼ gill of Dry Gin.
  ¼ gill of plain Orange Juice.
  3 or 4 dashes of Apricot Syrup flavoured with a little Cloves Syrup.
Shake well and strain into a cocktail glass. This cocktail possesses a delicious flavour. I invented it at the Embassy Club in London, 1921. Mayfair is the aristocratic quarter of London, called so because during the reign of Charles II (seventeenth century) they used to hold a yearly fair during the months of May.

This cocktail also appears in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book:

"Mayfair Cocktail
1 Dash Clove Syrup.
¼ Apricot brandy.
¼ Orange juice.
½ Gin.
Shake well and strain into cocktail glass.

name: Aviator No. 1
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
POUR cassis into centre of cocktail (it should sink).
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: An Aviation with egg white and a splash of cassis in the bottom, but is perhaps better and more balanced when the cassis is shaken with the other ingredients.

name: Melon Collie Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Crumble a piece of Cadbury's Flake over the cocktail.
garnish: Prepare garnish of Cadbury's Flake.
review: Something of a holiday disco drink, but tasty all the same.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2003 by Simon King at MJU, Millenniun Hotel, London, England.

name: Melon Daiquiri #1 (served 'up')
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
CUT melon into 8 segments and deseed.
Cut cubes of flesh from skin of one segment and MUDDLE in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with melon wedge on rim or skewered melon balls.
review: A classic Daiquiri with a touch of melon fruitiness.

name: Melon Daiquiri (served frozen)
instructions: Cut melon into 8 segments and deseed
Cut cubes of flesh from skin of one segment and place in blender
Add other ingredients and BLEND with half scoop of crushed ice
Serve with straws.
review: A cooling, fruity Daiquiri.

name: Melon Margarita No. 1
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
CUT melon into 8 segments and deseed.
Cut cubes of flesh from skin of one segment and MUDDLE in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with melon wedge or skewered balls.
review: Looks like stagnant pond water but tastes fantastic.

name: Awol
instructions: Select a SHOT GLASS.
LAYER in chilled glass by carefully pouring ingredients in the order listed.
Then FLAME drink and allow to burn for no more than ten seconds before extinguishing flame and consuming.
Take extreme CARE and BEWARE of hot glass rim.
garnish: Serve naked, without garnish.
review: A strong but surprisingly palatable shot.
history: Created in 1993 by Lane Zellman at Louis XVI Restaurant, St. Louis Hotel, New Orleans, USA.

name: Melon Martini (fresh fruit)
instructions: Cut melon into eight segments and deseed
Cut cubes of flesh from skin of one segment and MUDDLE in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Probably the most popular of all the fresh fruit Martinis.

name: Meloncholy Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Fruity and creamy. The flavours in this smooth, lime-green cocktail combine surprisingly well.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2002 by Daniel O'Brien at Ocean Bar in Edinburgh, Scotland.

name: Merry Widow(er)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A Fifty-Fifty Martini with delicate herbal and aniseed notes.
history:
The Merry Widow first appears in Hugo R. Ensslin's 1917 Recipes for Mixed Drinks (2nd Edition) (It may have also appeared in his 1916 first edition.) The cocktail also appears in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book and Albert Stevens Crockett's 1935 The Old Waldorf-Astoria Bar Book.

This cocktail takes its name from The Merry Widow Broadway production which opened on 21st October 1907 at the New Amsterdam Theatre. The operetta was premiered in Vienna in 1905, and played all around the world, leading to a ballet version and at least five film versions.

name: Mesa Fresca
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wheel.
review: Sweet and sour tequila and grapefruit.
history: Adapted from a recipe discovered in 2005 at Mesa Grill in New York City, USA.

name: Metropolitan (1993)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail, then use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of coin-sized orange zest twist (flamed).
review: A Cosmopolitan with more than a hint of berry fruit.
history: Created in 1993 by Chuck Coggins at Marion's Continental Restaurant & Lounge, New York City, using the recently launched Absolut Kurant Vodka. Incidentally, Marion's opened in 1950 by fashion model Marion Nagy, who came to the States after seeking asylum while swimming for Hungary in the Paris Peace Games after WWII.

name: Merry-Go-Round Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered olive or lemon zest twist.
review: Stir this 'perfect' Martini around and then get merry.
history: Long lost classic variation on the Dry Martini.

name: Ace
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: Pleasant, creamy, sweetened gin.

name: Azure Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of apple slice .
review: A tangy cocktail that's reminiscent of a cinnamon-laced apple pie. Shame it's not blue.
history: Ben Reed created this cocktail in 1998 at the Met Bar in London, England. It was originally named the Azure Martini and was made with muddled fresh apple.

name: Mexican
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Delicately sweetened pineapple and tequila. Yumm! If you like this then you'll love a Mexican Martini.

name: Mexican 55
ingredients: Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with champagne.
review: Suitably hard, yet surprisingly refreshing and sophisticated.
history:
An adaptation of the classic French 75 created in 1988 at La Perla, Paris, France. The name is said to come from Fidel Castro's statement, "Bullets come by vintages, like wine, especially Latin American bullets. Mexican '55 is a good year, '52 not so good."

More information on the history and variants of the French 75 can be found on our page.

name: Mexican Manhattan
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: You've tried this with bourbon and rye, now surprise yourself with aged tequila.
history: Manhattan cocktail history

name: Mexican Surfer
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Frothy topped, easy to make, and all too easy to drink. Looking at the recipe you might expect this cocktail to be overly sweet. it's not.

name: Miami Beach
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: Fruity and frothy topped. Like Baywatch!

name: Miami Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf.
review: The merest hint of mint in a refreshing Daiquiri with a dry finish.

name: Michelada
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of coarse sea salt rim - salt the rim of a chilled beer mug by rubbing it with a lime wedge and dipping rim in the coarse salt. Save the lime wedge to squeeze over and drop into finished drink..
review: Savoury, sweet n sour beer with enlivening chilli heat. On a hot day, if a beer is not refreshing enough, then a Michelada may hit the spot.
history:
Loosely translates as "my cold beer" (mi means 'my', chela is Mexican slang for pint/beer) and ada from helada is cold. This drink, which is popular in Mexico, is thought to have originated there sometime in the 1940s. 

There are many variations with Micheladas served with and without ice. It is usually served with light Mexican lager, although we prefer a darker beer. Even the spicy ingredients vary with Worcestershire sauce often omitted, but the hot pepper sauce remains a constant. In Mexico City itself, a Michelada almost always refers to something much simpler than this: 1 shot fresh lime juice in a glass with a salted rim, into which a light lager is poured.

name: Milk Punch
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: The cream, vanilla and sugar tame the cognac and rum.

name: Millionaire No.1
ingredients: 22.5 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime or apricot slice/wedge on rim.
review: High proof pot still rum funk, along with a decent quality apricot brandy and sloe gin is key to the success of this full-flavoured red cocktail.
history:
Recipe adapted from Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book. Craddock also lists a "Millionaire Cocktail (No.2)" as follows:
"1 Dash Anisette
The white of 1 Egg
1/3 Absinthe
2/3 Dry gin
Shake well and strain into cocktail glass."

In his 1948 The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks, David Embury's says "At some bars a drink served under the name of "Millionaire" which consists of lime juice, slow gin, and apricot brandy, with a few dashes of Jamaica rum. Since the sloe gin, which is a liqueur, predominates in this drink, I do not regard it as a true cocktail." Embury is more complimentary of what he calls a "Millionaire Royal", proclaiming it to be "a very satisfactory drink."

name: Mimosa
instructions: Select and pre-chill a GOBLET GLASS.
POUR first half of the sparkling wine and orange juice into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
TOP with the rest of the sparkling wine.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice wheel.
review: In his 1948 book The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks, David A. Embury writes of the Mimosa, "Just another freak champagne mixture. It is not half bad and the ladies usually like it. Use a good quality domestic champagne, medium dry." Indeed, when made with freshly squeezed orange juice mixed with a quality dry/brut sparkling wine, this is a great breakfast cocktail, but it's better served without ice as a Buck's Fizz.
history:
Named after the Mimosa tropical flowering shrub, Acacia dealbata - perhaps because of its trembling leaves, rather like the gentle fizz of this cocktail. The Mimosa is thought to have been created by Frank Meier at the Ritz Hotel in Paris, where he was head bartender from when the hotel's bar opened in 1921 until 1947.

The cocktail made its print debut in Frank Meier's 1936 book, The Artistry of Mixing Drinks titled "Mimosa or Champagne Orange" with the instruction, "In large wineglass, a piece of ice, the juice of one-half Orange; fill with Champagne stir and serve."

The fact that Meier also calls the Mimosa a "Champagne Orange" is key to appreciating the earliest known reference to this cocktail being served at the Ritz. This was unearthed by cocktail historian Philip Greene, in his 2018 book A Drinkable Feast: A Cocktail Companion to 1920s Paris. Greene found two references to the drink within entries for 25 August and 29 August 1923 in poet and publisher Harry Crosby's diaries, which were reprinted in the 1977 book, Shadows of the Sun: The Diaries of Harry Crosby, edited by Edward B. Germain.

name: Mint Collins
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE (just to bruise) mint in base of shaker
Add next 3 ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with soda and briefly stir.
review: A minty John Collins – just as the name suggests.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Victor Bergeron's 1972 Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide (Revised Edition).

name: Mint Fizz
instructions: SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda and briefly stir.
review: Long, refreshing citrus and mint fizz.

name: B-52 Shot
instructions: Select a SHOT GLASS.
Refrigerate ingredients.
LAYER in chilled glass by carefully pouring ingredients in the order listed.
review: Probably the best-known and most popular shot-style cocktail.
history:
A bartender named Peter Fich is most often credited for creating this cocktail in 1977 while working at the Banff Springs Hotel in Alberta, Canada.

The 1977 date of origin fits when cocktail books of the era are checked. For example, the B-52 doesn't make it into Jones' Complete Barguide, published that very year as "The most comprehensive and up to date collection of drink recipes (4,000)." Ten years on, the B-52, B-53, and B-54 all make it into The Bartender's Cherry published 1987.

Apparently, Fich tended to name his cocktail creations after his favourite bands, their songs, or albums - one such band being The B-52s. The band, which formed in 1976, was named after the beehive hairstyle that adorned vocalist Cindy Wilson, the shape of which resembled the nosecone of the B-52 bomber. The Boeing B-52 Stratofortress, a U.S. high-altitude bomber, was built between 1952 and 1962 and entered service as a long-range nuclear bomber. So this layered shot is named after a band, named after a hairstyle, named after a bomber. Hence, the roundabout truth is that this shot takes its name from the B-52 bomber.

name: Mint Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf.
review: An after-dinner palate cleanser.
history: Created in 2005 by yours truly, London, England.

name: Mitch Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: One of London's contemporary classics. Far from a proper Martini, this is fruity and sweet, but not overly so.
history: Created in 1997 by Giovanni Burdi at Match EC1, London, England.

name: Mojito de Casa
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE mint (just to bruise) in base of glass
Add tequila, lime juice and sugar
Half fill glass with crushed ice and CHURN (stir) with bar spoon
Fill glass with more crushed ice and churn some more
TOP with soda, stir and serve.
review: A tequila based Mojito.
history:
Created at Mercadito, New York City, USA.

For more recipes, its origins and history, see the Mojito Cocktail.

name: Momisette
instructions: POUR pastis and almond syrup into glass
Serve with bottle of sparkling water so the customer can dilute to their own taste
Lastly, add ice to fill glass.
review: Complex balance of anise, almond and liquorice.
history: A traditional French drink, the name of which literally translates as 'tiny mummy'.

name: Momo Special
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE mint (just to bruise) in base of shaker
Add next 3 ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda, lightly stir and serve with straws.
review: Enrich the minty flavour by macerating the mint in the vodka some hours before making.
history: Created in 1998 by Simon Mainoo at Momo, London, England.

name: Monarch Martini
instructions: MUDDLE mint (just to bruise) in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Wonderfully floral and minty - worthy of a right royal drinker.
history: Created in 2003 by Douglas Ankrah at Townhouse bar in Knighsbridge, London, England.

name: Monkey Gland
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: I've added the merest touch of sugar to the classic recipe and a dash more absinthe to make this old-school cocktail pop. However, approach with caution, unlike the experiments of Serge Voronoff, nothing about this drink is designed to enhance virility.
history:
Created in the late 1920s by Harry MacElhone at his Harry's New York Bar in Paris, France. The Monkey Gland takes its name from the work of Dr Serge Voronoff who, convinced that testosterone was vital to a long and healthy life, transplanted monkey testicles onto elderly Frenchmen. 

The "Monkey's Gland Cocktail" first appears in MacElhone's 1922 Harry's ABC of Mixing Cocktails (which, sadly, I don't have access to). In his 1923 edition, he specifies the recipe as "1 dash Absinthe, 1 teaspoonful of Grenadine, ½ Orange Juice, ½ Gordon Gin. Shake well, and strain into a cocktail glass. (Invented by the Author and deriving its name from Voronoff's experiments in rejuvenation.)."

name: Monkey Gland No.2
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: A somewhat off-putting name for a very palatable cocktail with zesty orange, gin botanicals, and delicate herbal notes combining well.

name: Montego Bay
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: The name suggests Jamaica, but the recipe stipulates Martinique agricole rum.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Victor Bergeron's 1972 Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide where it is accompanied by Trader Vic's "TV" logo, indicating it is a signature cocktail.

name: Monza
instructions: Cut passion fruit in half and scoop the flesh into shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass.
review: Often made with up to four times as much bitter liqueur but in such high doses this dominates the other ingredients. Hence in December 2020, thanks to a comment below, we made our recipe more restrained to allow the passion fruit to shine while delivering a relatively dry cocktail.
history: A classic cocktail promoted by Campari and named after the Italian Grand Prix circuit.

name: Morning Glory
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Sophisticated and complex - one for sipping.
history: My interpretation of a classic.

name: Moscow Mule
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COPPER MUG OR COLLINS GLASS.
POUR first 3 ingredients into copper mug/glass and stir.
Two-thirds fill vessel with cubed ice.
TOP with ginger beer and crown with crushed ice.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: "The vodka drink with a kick" is simply vodka and ginger beer with a squeeze of lime served over ice. This drink's all-important enlivening spice is provided by the ginger beer, so pick your brand wisely.
history:
Part of the Buck family of cocktails, the Moscow Mule was created in 1941 - 1946 (the date and place of creation are disputed).

John G. Martin had recently (in 1939) acquired the rights to Smirnoff vodka for Heublein, a minor Connecticut-based liquor and food distributor. Meanwhile, Jack Morgan, a friend of his and owner of Hollywood's famous British pub, the Cock'n'Bull Saloon on Sunset Strip, was trying to launch his own brand of ginger beer, but sales were not going well.

Legend has it that the two men met at New York City's Chatham Bar and hit on the idea of mixing Martin's vodka with Morgan's ginger beer and adding a squeeze of lime (perhaps inspired by the Cuba Libre) to create the Moscow Mule.

Others, most notably Eric Felton in a 2007 article in the Wall Street Journal, say the drink was invented back in Hollywood at the Cock 'n' Bull by Wes Price, its head bartender. Price is said to have created the drink in an attempt to clear an overstock of ginger beer from the pub's basement. He served the new drink to actor Broderick Crawford, and the drink caught on from there. 

Wes Price, apparently resigned from the Cock 'n' Bull in 1953 and is quoted as saying "I wasn't truly appreciated. I never got an extra cent for my invention".
