What is sure is that the combination of vodka and ginger beer helped both Morgan and Martin shift their products, but the cocktail's success is greatly due to its being served in a copper mug specially engraved with a kicking mule. This initiative was driven by a girlfriend of Morgan's who'd recently inherited a copper factory that made the previously poorly selling copper mugs. The success of the Moscow Mule was most fortuitous for all three friends.

By 1947, when Edwin H. Land invented the Polaroid Land Camera, the Moscow Mule was already established on the drinks menus of numerous bars. Martin bought himself one of the instant cameras and went from bar to bar, photographing bartenders holding a bottle of Smirnoff Vodka in one hand and a copper mule mug in the other. He gave one photograph to the bartender and used a second to show the next neighbouring bar what they were missing out on. The Moscow Mule and the use of the Polaroid Camera was a stroke of marketing genius.

name: Motox
instructions: MUDDLE ginger and coriander in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Each sip is fruity, lemon fresh and followed by a hot ginger hit.
history: Adapted from a drink discovered in 2005 at Mo Bar, Landmark Mandarin Oriental Hotel, Hong Kong.

name: Moonshine Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: A wet Martini with balanced hints of maraschino and absinthe.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Mrs Robinson #2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: A short Harvey Wallbanger.
history: Adapted from a recipe discovered in 2006 on Kyle Branch's Cocktail Hotel blog.

name: Mudslide
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HURRICANE GLASS.
BLEND all ingredients with 7oz scoop of crushed ice.
Pour into glass.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: A simply scrumptious dessert cocktail with whiskey cream and coffee.
history:
Legend has it that the Mudslide was created at the Wreck Bar & Grill in Rum Point Club on Grand Cayman Island, although the bar's own website says, "It may have been born elsewhere in the world but we have claimed it as our own!  We are the home of the mudslide!" As for the story behind the legend, the folk at the Wreck say:

"Our legend of the Rum Point Mudslide comes from the 1970s when guests came to the bar and asked the bartender for a white Russian.  The bartender asked the ingredients; well it is simple vodka, Kahlua and cream. Well the bartender replied, I have Vodka and Kahlua but I don't have any cream but how about some of this Bailey's Irish cream.  And thus the mudslide was born at the Wreck Bar at Rum Point Club...

"There have been many variations created over the years and rumor has it that the original trials of this illusive libation contained Vanilla Hagan Daz ice cream.  The mudslide recipe was tweaked and the recipe at Rum Point has stayed as it is since the 1980's and served as is it now with Absolut Vodka, Kahlua and Bailey's or Molly's Irish cream a and ice.  No fillers, no ice cream nor chocolate, and garnished with cinnamon, a cherry and a shot of Kahlua down the straw!"

name: Mujer Verde
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: The name means 'Green Lady'... and she packs a Chartreuse punch.
history: Discovered in 2006 at Absinthe, San Francisco, where 'D Mexican' resurrected this drink from his hometown of Guadalajara.

name: Mule's Hind Leg
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR (or shake) all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with apricot wedge or dried apricot half on rim.
review: Gin and apple brandy enriched with apricot and Bénédictine liqueurs along with maple syrup.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Mulled Wine
instructions: POUR ingredients into pre-warmed glass and stir.
review: Warming, soothing and potent. Better if several servings are made at the same time and the ingredients mixed together and warmed in a saucepan.

name: B and B
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Honeyed and spiced brandy. Traditionally mixed in equal parts proportions but I prefer slightly drier with a touch more brandy than  Bénédictine. The ice will add dilution with time but some, depending on the ice being used, may want to add 10ml (1/3oz) or more chilled water prior to mixing.
history: Bénédictine and various types of brandy have long been mixed together and combo first enters cocktail books in the early 1900s as a pousse-café layered cocktail with the brandy floated over the liqueur.

name: Nantucket
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Essentially a Seabreeze with rum in place of vodka.
history: Popularised by the USA Cheers Bar chain, this is named after the beautiful island off Cape Cod.

name: Napoleon
ingredients: 15 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Rust-coloured, spirituous and bittersweet with assertive fernet herbal notes. Good as an aperitivo but brilliant as a digestivo.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Navigator
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: This fruity, grapefruit-led drink is pleasantly bitter and sour.
history: Created in 2005 by Jamie Terrell, London, England.

name: Negus (Hot)
instructions: Select and pre-heat a TODDY GLASS.
Place bar spoon in pre-warmed glass.
POUR all ingredients into glass.
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Dust with grated nutmeg (and optionally garnish with cinnamon stick).
review: A warming classic winter's cocktail with rich ruby port and lemon citrusy freshness.
history:
Popular during the 18th and 19th centuries, this warm, mulled-wine-like cocktail takes its name from its creator, Colonel Francis Negus (1670-1732), an English army officer who served as Member of Parliament for Ipswich, England, from 1717 to 1732. He is said to have created his namesake beverage during a wine-fuelled heated political discussion, seeking to dilute the wine, and perhaps the debate.  

Charles Dickens mentions the Negus in his 1843 A Christmas Carol and Isabella Mary Beeton's 1861 Mrs. Beeton's Book of Household Management includes a Negus recipe with the useful advice, "As this beverage is more usually drink at children's parties than at any other, the wine need not be very old or expensive for the purpose. A new fruity wine answering very well for it." And, should you be planning such a party, she says to "Allow 1 pint of wine, with the other ingredients in proportion, for a party of 9 or 10 children." Forget the clown and the bouncy castle!

name: Night and Day
instructions: POUR ingredients into chilled glass.
review: Aromatic, bittersweet zesty orange enlivened by prosecco.

name: Nine-20-Seven
ingredients: Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: POUR ingredients into chilled glass and lightly stir.
review: Champagne with a hint of vanilla.
history: Created in 2002 by Damien Caldwell at Home Bar, London, England. Damian was lost for a name until a customer asked for the time.

name: Northern Lights
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of star anise floated on surface.
review: Wonderfully refreshing: apple and anise served up on a grassy vodka base.
history: Created in 2003 by Stuart 'Holiday' Hudson at MJU Bar, Millennium Hotel, London, England.

name: North Pole
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Shake or whip cream to aerate then carefully pour to FLOAT on the surface of the cocktail. (It should sit between the cocktail and the foaming head.)
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare coin-sized lemon zest twist for garnish.
review: A Savoy Aviation mellowed by egg white and cream.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Victor Bergeron's 1972 Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide.

name: Nome
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf.
review: This dyslexic Gnome is dry and interesting.
history:
A variation on the Alaska cocktail, the Nome is mentioned in the Alaska cocktail's entry in David Embury's 1948 The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks.

Like the Alaska, the Nome is thought to take its name from its golden colour referencing the gold rush. The Alaska Gold Rush started in the Klondike region of the Yukon in Canada but many prospectors left the Klondike for an area around Nome in west Alaska following the discovery of gold there in 1899.

name: Ole Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Cognac and orange with rich vanilla and delicate spice.

name: Opal Café
instructions: SHAKE first two ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
FLOAT thin layer of cream over drink.
review: A great liquorice and coffee drink to sip or shoot.

name: Opera
ingredients: 20 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: I've combined classic recipes from both Jacques Straub (1914) and Harry Craddock (1930) in this gin-laced, vinous, late-night sipper with delicate mandarin and maraschino richness.
history: The Opera cocktail first appeared in Jacques Straub's 1914 book Drinks. However, the version of the Opera mostly seen today comes from Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Orang-A-Tang
instructions: SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
FLOAT layer of rum over cocktail.
review: As the name suggests, orange predominates in this long, tangy, tropical cooler.

name: Orange Bloom Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Fruity, zesty, delicately herbal orange laced with gin. Modern bartending convention is to stir rather than shake this "Martini" but it's better shaken, as stipulated by Harry Cradock.
history: Adapted from the following recipe in Harry Cradock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Orange Blossom
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Gin-laced orange juice delicately enriched with a splash of grenadine. A regal shake (with a swath of orange peel) adds a balancing zestiness to this cocktail.
history:
This Prohibition-era cocktail first appears in Robert Vermeire's 1932 Cocktails: How to Mix Them where it is credited to "Malloy of Pittsburg." Vermeire was born in Belgium but learnt his craft in Germany before bartending in London, most notably at The Embassy Club, where he served many Americans escaping Prohibition. This also drove Americans to travel to Havana, Cuba where this cocktail appeared in Sloppy Joe's Bar Season 1932-33 combined menu and recipe book. It was obviously popular in Cuba as, the following year, the Orange Blossom appeared on the menu of another famous Cuban cocktail bar, Bar La Florida Cocktails (later renamed El Floridita).

In his 1935 Old Waldorf Astoria Bar Book, A.S. Crockett mentions to things to note regarding the Orange Blossom, firstly that it also went by the name Adirondack, and that another version was made with equal parts old tom gin, orange juice and rosso vermouth. Indeed, this Bronx-like vermouth version appears in numerous cocktail books through to and including Stanley M. Jones' 1977 Jones' Complete Barguide.

In his 1947 book The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks, David A. Embury says of the Orange Blossom, "Another Spawn of the Prohibition toad that I have already denounced is the Orange Blossom, consisting of equal parts of gin and orange juice. If, however, this drink is treated as a Sour-type cocktail and made accordingly, this is not too bad. In doing this it must be borne in mind that orange juice is sweeter and less pungent than either lime or lemon juice. Hence, the quantity of sugar must be cut down and that of the citrus juice increased." The recipe he gives calls for "1/2 part Sugar Syrup, 4 parts Orange Juice, and 8 parts Gin. He goes on to say, "The zest and flavour of the drink may be improved by adding 2 of 3 dashes of lime juice for each drink."

name: Orange Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A sophisticated, complex balance of orange and gin.
history: Adapted from the Orange Cocktail and Orange Martini Cocktail in in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Orchard Breeze
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of apple slice .
review: A refreshing combination of white wine, apple, lime and elderflower laced with vodka.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2002 by Wayne Collins for Maxxium UK.

name: Oriental Grape Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE grapes in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with grapes.
review: Sake adds some oriental intrigue to what would otherwise be a plain old grape cocktail.
history: Created in 2004 by yours truly (Simon D).

name: Oriental Tartini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Optionally, garnish with skewered lychee.
review: A sour, tart, fruity cocktail with more than a hint of lychee.
history: Created in 2004 by yours truly.

name: Osmo
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A sake based Cosmopolitan.

name: Pall Mall Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A classic Martini served 'perfect' with the merest hint of mint.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Bajan Mojito
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
CUT passion fruit in half and scoop flesh into glass.
Add mint and gently MUDDLE (just to bruise mint).
ADD rum, lime juice and sugar syrup.
Two-thirds fill glass with crushed ice and CHURN to mix.
Fill glass with more crushed ice and briefly CHURN.
DRIZZLE passion fruit liqueur.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: A laid-back, fruity, slightly sweet Mojito.
history:
Adapted from a recipe by Wayne Collins, London, England. 
See: Mojito cocktail history

name: Pancho Villa
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge.
review: Dilution is key to this cocktail so either shake with crushed ice (as per the original recipe) or do as I do and add chilled water. Even then, to quote Victor Bergeron, "This'll tuck you away neatly - and pick you up and throw you right to the floor".
history:
Named for Francisco Villaruel Guilledo (1901-1925), a Filipino professional boxer known as Pancho Villa. He was only 154cm (5 feet and 1 inch) tall and weighed 51kg (114 pounds) at his heaviest, and was the first Asian to win the World Flyweight Championship in 1923. Never knocked out in his entire career, he is regarded as one of the greatest Flyweight boxers of all time. He died suddenly, aged 23, from complications following a tooth extraction.

The Pancho Villa cocktail first appears in Charles H. Baker, Jr.'s 1939 The Gentleman's Companion volume II – An Exotic Drinking Book. Our recipe adapted from Victor Bergeron's 1947 Bartender's Guide by Trader Vic, a recipe repeated in his 1972 Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide.

name: Paradise
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with apricot wedge or dried apricot half on rim.
review: This gin-based cocktail elegantly highlights the quality of your apricot liqueur. Off-dry rather than sweet.
history: The Paradise Cocktail first appears in Harry McElhone's 1922 Harry's ABC of Mixing Cocktails as an equal parts dry gin, apricot brandy, and orange juice. It then reappears with a better formulation in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Bajan Passion
instructions: Cut passion fruit in half and scoop flesh into shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: A Daiquiri laced with generous fruit and a touch of vanilla.
history: Created in 2004 by Wayne Collins, London, England.

name: Parisian Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Rich cassis tempered by gin and dry vermouth.
history: A drink created in the 1920s to promote crème de cassis. Recipe adapted from Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Parisian Spring Punch
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
POUR sparkling wine into shaker.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist..
review: Dry apple and upmarket cider. Perfect for parties.
history: Dick Bradsell's riff on his own Russian Spring Punch.

name: Parma Negroni
instructions: SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with tonic water, briefly stir and serve.
review: A long citrusy riff that takes the classic from an early evening cocktail to perhaps one to enjoy on a sunny afternoon.
history:
Discovered in 2005 at Club 97, Hong Kong, China.

The history and other recipes for the Negroni cocktail can be found on our Negroni cocktail page.

name: Passion Fruit Collins
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
