Wash and cut a fresh passion fruit in half. SCOOP out the seeds and flesh of both halves into your shaker.
Add next 3 ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice wheel.
review: This fruity adaptation of the classic Collins may be a tad sharp for some: if so, add a dash more sugar.
history: For the full story behind the origins of the Collins and its many variations see our Collins page.

name: Passion Fruit Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SCOOP out the seeds and flesh from 3 of the passion fruit halves into your shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Float the remaining passion fruit half for garnish.
review: The rum character comes through in this tropically fruity cocktail.

name: Passion Fruit Martini (simple)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
Wash and cut 2 fresh passion fruits in half.
SCOOP out the seeds and flesh from 3 of the passion fruit halves into your shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Float the remaining passion fruit half for garnish.
review: Very fruity, slightly sweet, easy drinking and vodka-laced.

name: Passion Fruit Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
Cut passion fruit in half and scoop out flesh into shaker.
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with passion fruit half floated on surface.
review: Full-on passion fruit with gin and citrus.
history: Created in 2004 by yours truly.

name: Passion Fruit Rum Punch
instructions: Cut passion fruit in half and scoop out flesh into shaker
Add next four ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain into glass filled with crushed ice
CHURN (stir) and then top with sparkling wine.
review: Rum and fruit combine in this punchy tropical-style drink.
history: Created in 2004 by yours truly.

name: Planteur
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Handle with extreme care.

name: Balalaika
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Richly flavoured with orange and lemon.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Stanley M. Jones' 1977 Jones' Complete Barguide.

name: Peach Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of peach wedge on rim.
review: A classic Daiquiri with fragrant peach liqueur replacing sugar.

name: Pear and Elderflower Fruitini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pear slice on rim.
review: Pear and elderflower fortified with vodka.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2001 by Angelo Vieira at St. Martins, London, England.

name: Pear and Vanilla Rickey
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with lemonade and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge on rim.
review: Vanilla and pear create a creamy vanilla mouthful cut by lime juice.

name: Pear-Shaped Spritz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a WINE GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into chilled glass.
FILL glass with ice.
garnish: Garnish with pear wedge on rim.
review: A classic white wine and soda spritz, enriched with a splash of pear and cognac liqueur.
history: Created by yours truly in September 2024.

name: Achilles Heel
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of apple slice.
review: If you like French Martinis you'll love this semi-sweet Tatanka.
history: Created in 2005 at Koba, Brighton, England.

name: Pedro Collins
instructions: SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda, lightly stir and serve with straws.
review: This rum-based Tom Collins is basically a long Daiquiri with soda.
history: See the history of the Collins cocktail on our Tom Collins page.

name: Pendennis Club Special
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: Tangy, subtle, sweet, and sour. Depending on your palate and apricot liqueur you may want to add 2.5ml sugar syrup.
history:
Eponymously named after the Pendennis Club in Louisville, Kentucky, the first known reference to this cocktail appears as the Pendennis Club Cocktail in the "Seductive American Cocktails" appendix in William "Cocktail Bill" Boothby's 1908 The World's Drinks and How to Mix Them book with just three ingredients: gin, apricot brandy, and dry vermouth.

By the time it resurfaces in Charles H. Baker Jr.'s 1939 The Gentleman's Companion as "The  Pendennis Club's Famous Special" it morphed into a four-ingredient cocktail served over a split fresh kumquat. Modern-day recipes, such as the above, are nearer to Baker's 1939 Pendennis Club Cocktail than Cocktail Bill's 1908 Pendennis Club.

name: Pepper and Vanilla
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with strip of yellow bell pepper.
review: Vanilla and pepper seem to complement each other in a sweet and sour kind of way.
history: Created in 2002 by yours truly and originally somewhat dodgily named Pepper & Vanilla'tini. Incidentally, Pieprz i wanilia (which translates as Pepper and Vanilla) was a popular Polish television programme broadcast by TVP. This travel documentary series was broadcast for over 20 years with 300 episodes.

name: Peppered Mary
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
ROLL (rotate shaker over and over, rather than shake) all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with yellow bell pepper and celery stalk.
review: Lightly spicy, sweet red bell pepper adds to this Bloody Mary.

name: Perfect Alibi
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
MUDDLE ginger in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of mint leaf .
review: A very unusual and pleasant mix of flavours.
history: Created in 2001 by Douglas Ankrah for Akbar, London, England.

name: Perfect John
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A straight up Harvey Wallbanger with triple sec.

name: Perry Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: Garnish with pear wedge on rim.
review: Pear with a hint of sparkle.
history: Created in 2002 by yours truly.

name: Peter Pan Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Smoother, lighter and easier than most classic cocktails - perhaps a little too much so.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book. The original recipe called for equal parts, including the bitters - surely a mistake.

name: Pharmaceutical Stimulant
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MEDICAL CUP OR OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of coffee beans.
review: A real wake-up call and the drink that led to many Espresso Martinis.
history: Essentially an Espresso Martini served on the rocks, legendary bartender Dick Bradsell repurposed his coffee 'Martini' when given a list of 20 cocktail names by Damien Hirst for the menu at his new Pharmacy restaurant in Notting Hill where Damien Hirst artwork hung and Dick was bar manager (the site on Notting Hill Gate is now a Waitrose store).

name: Pichuncho Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Grape combines with fortified wine in a sublime after-dinner sipper.
history: Based on the traditional Chilean drink: pisco and vermouth.

name: Pierre Collins
instructions: SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda, lightly stir and serve with straws.
review: A Tom Collins made with cognac. Cognac's character shines through.
history: A classic Collins variation.

name: Pilgrim Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: Whether you serve this hot or cold, it's a delicately spiced drink to warm the cockles.

name: Pimm's Cocktail
instructions: SHAKE first four ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with champagne.
review: Luxuriate in this quintessentially English tipple.
history: See our Fruit Cups and Pimm's page for more information.

name: Pimm's Cup (or Classic Pimm's)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR Pimm's into glass half filled with ice.
ADD fruit and fill glass with more ice.
TOP with equal parts lemonade and ginger ale (or just use lemonade, or ginger ale).
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: You've not properly experienced an English summer until you've drunk one of these whilst sheltering from the rain.
history: See our Fruit Cups and Pimm's page for more information.

name: Piña Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge.
review: Rich pineapple but not too sweet.

name: Pineapple and Ginger Cocktail
instructions: MUDDLE ginger in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Smooth, rich pineapple flavour with hints of vodka and ginger.

name: Pineapple Fizz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring soda.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple or lime wedge on rim.
review: A Pineapple Daiquiri lengthened with soda. You'll be surprised just how tasty and refreshing this drink is.

name: Pink Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: The quality of the pomegranate syrup will make or break this delicate Daiquiri.
history: A classic from the 1930s that's more formally known as the Daiquiri No. 5.

name: Pink Hound
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A flavoursome balance of sweet and sour.

name: Pink Lemonade
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
review: A tall, pink, tangy alcohol-free cocktail.
history: Discovered in 2004 in New York City.

name: Pino Pepe
instructions: Select and pre-chill a PINEAPPLE SHELL (FROZEN) GLASS.
DRY BLEND (without ice) first 7 ingredients (all but Xanthan gum) on a slow setting and then gradually add Xanthan while continuing to blend.
Add 7oz scoop of crushed ice and BLEND some more.
POUR into chilled glass.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: To quote Trader Vic, "Lethal but smooth - pineapple at its best".
history: Adapted from a recipe in Victor Bergeron's 1972 Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide (Revised Edition).

name: Pisco Collins
instructions: SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda, lightly stir and serve with straws.
review: Perhaps the most aromatic and flavoursome of the Collins family.
history: See the history of the Collins cocktail on our Tom Collins page.

name: Pisco Punch (Lannes' recipe)
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: SHAKE first four ingredients with ice and strain into glass filled with crushed ice
TOP with soda.
review: Pisco's character shines through the sweetened pineapple fruit in this long, refreshing classic.
history:
This recipe is adapted from John Lannes' recipe, owner of the famous Bank Exchange when it closed in 1920.

Our Pisco Punch cocktail page gives a more detailed history of this drink and explains the different recipes.

name: Pisco Punch (Prosser's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE grapes in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with grapes.
review: Grape juice and aromatic grape-based spirit paired with pineapple and the merest hint of absinthe.
history:
Jack Koeppler, the bartender at the Buena Vista Café in San Francisco who's also famous for being the first bartender in America to serve Irish Coffee, was given this recipe by the son of its creator, a fellow San Franciscan by the name of Mr Prosser.

Adapted from Mr Prosser's original recipe which is said to have comprised: 2oz white grape juice, 2 oz pisco, 1 spoon pineapple juice and 1 spoon absinthe.

Our Pisco Punch cocktail page gives a more detailed history of this drink and explains the different recipes.

name: Pisco Naranja
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Aromatic brandy and orange juice pepped and sweetened with a slug of orange liqueur.
history: Based on the traditional Chilean combination of pisco and orange juice.

name: Piscola
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR pisco and bitters into ice-filled glass.
TOP with cola and briefly stir.
Squeeze lime wedge and drop into cocktail.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime wedge.
review: 'Brandy' and cola with a hint of Angostura bitters. Try and see why the Chileans enjoy it.
history: A popular long drink in its native Chile where the 8th of February has been celebrated as Piscola Day since 2003.

name: Platinum Blonde (creamy)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: This after-dinner sipper maybe creamy but it's balanced rather than sweet with aged rum nuanced by cognac and orange liqueur. Creamy yet sophisticated.

name: Playa del Mar
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge.
review: Fruity with a hint of tequila.
history: This cocktail was created in 1997 by Wayne Collins at Navajo Joe, London, England. A riff on a classic Sex on the Beach, the name translates as Beach of the Sea.

name: Bamboo (with triple sec)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: For sophisticated dry palates - a touch of triple sec takes the dry edge off of fino sherry and dry vermouth.
history: Bamboo Cocktail history

name: Plum Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
CUT plum into quarters, remove stone and peel.
MUDDLE plum in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge on rim.
review: Depending on the ripeness of the plums, you may need to adjust the quantity of sugar.

name: Plum Sour
instructions: MUDDLE plum in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass.
review: Soft, ripe plums are key to this fruity sour.

name: Pogo Stick
instructions: BLEND all ingredients with 6oz scoop crushed ice
Serve with straws.
review: To quote Trader Vic, "A refreshing blend of gin with pineapple and grapefruit juice... a real romper".
history: Adapted from a recipe in the 1947-72 Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide by Victor Bergeron.

name: Ponce de Leon
ingredients: Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: SHAKE first four ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with champagne.
review: A well-balanced classic champagne cocktail.
history: A long lost classic.

name: Poncha
instructions: STIR honey with aguardiente in base of shaker to dissolve honey
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain into ice-filled glass.
review: This citrus refresher is reputedly an excellent cold remedy.
history: My adaptation of a traditional drink from the island of Madeira.

name: Pontberry Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of skewered blackberries.
review: A light, fruity, easy drinking cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Dick Bradsell in the late 90s for the opening of Agent Provocateur in Pont Street in London, England.

name: Port Wine Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Port and brandy served straight-up and dressed up.
history:
A classic from the early 1900s.

A recipe for a "Port Wine Cocktail" appears in Frank Newman's 1900 French book American-Bar Recettes des Boissons Anglaises & Américaines.

name: President
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Delicately fruity sweet and sour.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: El Presidente (sans grenadine)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express orange zest twist over cocktail and discard. Garnish with maraschino cherry.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: Bone dry, light and delicate. The sweetness and colour contributed by the maraschino cherry garnish are essential to this drink's success.

name: Prickly Pear Mule
instructions: SHAKE first five ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with ginger beer.
review: Subtle pear with ginger spice. Fill the glass with ice and go easy on the ginger beer which can predominate and overpower the pear.
history:
Created in 2002 by yours truly.
See:  Moscow Mule cocktail history

name: Banana Boomer
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with dried banana chip pegged to rim, or fresh banana slice on the rim.
review: Fortified bubble gum for the young at heart.

name: Prince Charlie
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Cognac and honey with sweet and sourness in harmony.
history: A long-lost classic.

name: Prince of Wales No. 3
instructions: Select a FLUTE GLASS.
RUB a sugar cube with a lemon zest (or on the skin of an unwaxed lemon) and then coat cube with a dash of bitters. Drop cube into chilled glass.
POUR chilled brandy and liqueur over soaked cube.
TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: Serve naked, without garnish.
review: Less well-known but more interesting than the similarly styled modern Champagne Cocktail.
history: See Champagne Cocktail history.

name: Princess Marina
ingredients: 15 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Delicate yet loaded with alcohol and flavour.
history: Created in the late 1920s/early 1930s and named after the Princess Marina, the late mother of The Duke of Kent, Prince Michael of Kent and Princess Alexandra.

name: Princess Mary
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with cocoa powder.
review: Slightly sweet, very creamy - drink after dinner.
history:
Created in London during 1922 by Scottish bartender Harry MacElhone to celebrate H.R.H. Princess Mary's marriage to Lord Lascelles. The year after, Harry MacElhone purchased his eponymously named bar in Paris.

The original recipe featured equal parts of all three ingredients and was first published in Robert Vermeire's 1922 Cocktails – How to Mix Them accompanied by a note to its creation, "Harry, of Ciro's Club, introduced this cocktail in honour of Princess Mary's wedding to Lord Lascelles, February, 1922." Harry repeats this recipe and origin a year later in his own book, Harry of Ciro's A B C of Mixing Cocktails.

name: Princess Mary's Pride
ingredients: 25 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: STIR (or shake) all ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass.
review: Apple brandy to the fore, followed by aromatised wine.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Harry Craddock on 28th February 1922 to celebrate the wedding of H.R.H. Princess Mary, and published in his 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book where he calls for this cocktail to be shaken rather than stirred.

name: Princeton
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review:
Kappeler's 1895 recipe calls for the Old Tom gin and orange bitters to be stirred with ice and strained into a glass, with port poured down the side of the glass so it settles to form a red layer beneath the orange bitters tinted gin.

I've added a spoon (5ml) of sugar syrup to Kappeler's recipe to slightly sweeten the gin (omit if using a sweet old tom) and a splash of water to add some much-needed dilution. I also prefer to abandon the original layering and stir all the ingredients together.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's influential 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Princeton Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: The Dry Martini meets the Gimlet. They should meet more often.

name: Pruneaux
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of prunes.
review: Sherried prunes further fortified by gin.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Banana Colada
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HURRICANE GLASS.
BLEND all ingredients with 1.5 x 7oz scoops of crushed ice.
POUR into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with banana chunk on rim.
review: Don't skimp, use a whole banana per drink for real flavour.
history: See our main Piña Colada page for the story behind the classic and for links to numerous other variations.

name: P.S. I Love You
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
Crumble a piece of Cadbury's Flake over the cocktail.
garnish: Prepare garnish of Cadbury's Flake.
review: P.S. You'll love this creamy, slightly sweet dessert cocktail.

name: Pulp Fiction
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass while also pouring lemon-lime soda.
garnish: Garnish with apple wedge or slice.
review: Originally made with apple pulp, this drink has a zingy apple taste.
history: Discovered in 2001 at Teatro Club in London, England.

name: Purple Cosmo
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: If shaken this becomes more of a grey cosmo. The flavour and colour make for an interesting twist.
history: For more recipes, origins and history behind the Cosmopolitan cocktail, see our Cosmopolitan page.

name: Purple Pear Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pear wedge on rim.
review: Depending on your choice crème de violete, both in hue and flavour, this floral cocktail suits its name.
history: Created in 2002 by yours truly.

name: Banana Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
DRY BLEND (without ice) first 5 ingredients on slow setting and then gradually add Xanthan gum while continuing to blend.
Add 7oz scoop of crushed ice and BLEND some more.
POUR into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with dried banana chip or fresh banana slice on the rim.
review: Good banana flavour without being overly sweet. Indeed, this Banana Daiquiri is more sophisticated than the uninitiated might expect.
history: Legendary Tiki bartender Harry K. Yee is credited with being the first to add banana to a Daiquiri at Henry Kaiser's Hawaiian Village Hotel in Waikiki, Oahu, Hawaii.

name: Quarter Deck
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: With flavours of prune, toffee, and maple syrup this is a complex after-dinner cocktail.
history: Long lost classic.

name: Quarterback
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: This white, creamy drink has a flavoursome bite.

name: Queen's
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Gin-based with vermouth's bittersweet wine notes and pineapple tropical fruitiness. This fruity Martini takes its name from a three-equal parts Perfect Martini being called the Queen. This version not only has pineapple but traditionally 1 part dry vermouth and 1 part rosso vermouth to 2 parts gin. I prefer with a tad less dry vermouth.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Quince Sour
instructions: STIR quince jam with vodka in base of shaker to dissolve jam
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass.
review: The sweet quince both flavours and balances the sour.

name: Randy Sangaree
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Named after the London rhyming slang for port and brandy, its base ingredients. The love interest comes courtesy of orange and vanilla.
history: Created in 2003 by yours truly, London, England.

name: Raspberry Caipirinha
instructions: <strong>1.</strong> Cut lime in half (from pole to pole) and then slice one of the halves into eight 3-4mm thick half-moon shaped segments and drop into the base of a robust rocks glass
<strong>2.</strong> Add 3 bar spoons caster sugar (or ½oz / 15ml sugar syrup 2:1) and MUDDLE
<strong>3.</strong> Add raspberries and MUDDLE some more
<strong>4.</strong> When sure fruit juice is fully extracted and the sugar dissolved, transfer the muddled fruit and sugar into serving glass (preferably a 30cl / 10oz hi-ball glass)
<strong>5.</strong> POUR cachaça (stored in the freezer) into the rocks glass (used to muddle) and swirl to rinse glass before pouring cachaça into serving glass
STIR to ensure all ingredients are well mixed
<strong>6.</strong> Crack (rather than crush) four to five large ice cubes and drop into serving glass
STIR before topping with more ice and serve with a stirrer (but no straws)
review: A fruity twist on the popular Caipirinha.
history: For information on the history and other variations of the Caipirinha, please see our Caipirinha cocktail page.

name: Raspberry Collins
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
MUDDLE raspberries in base of shaker.
Add next 5 ingredients (all but soda) and SHAKE with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with skewered lemon slice wheel and raspberry (sail).
review: A popular modern-day adaptation of the classic Collins with a whopping 45ml (1½oz) of lemon juice, yet is far from being overly sour. (I've tried reducing the lemon and rebalancing with less sugar but the result is a lesser cocktail.)
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 1999 by Cairbry Hill, London, England.

name: Raspberry Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE raspberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Just as it says - a raspberry flavoured Margarita.

name: Raspberry Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE raspberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered raspberry.
review: Vodka-laced rich berry fruit with dashes of zesty citrus.

name: Raspberry 'Martini' (by Dick Bradsell)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE raspberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with raspberries.
review: Rich raspberry flavour laced with gin.
history: Created in 1997 by Dick Bradsell, London, England and originally named Raspberry Martini.

name: Raspberry Mocha'tini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with raspberries.
review: Sweet chocolate and raspberry tempered by dry coffee and vodka.
history: Discovered in 2002 at Lot 61, New York City, USA.

name: Raspberry Mule
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
MUDDLE raspberries in base of shaker.
Add next 3 ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with ginger beer and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge and serve with a straw.
review: The fruity alternative to a Moscow Mule.

name: Bananas and Cream
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
BLEND ingredients with 6oz scoop of crushed ice.
Pour into glass.
Serve with straws.
garnish: Prepare garnish of banana chunk .
review: Banana and cream frappé with hints of almond. One for a summer afternoon.

name: Ray's Hard Lemonade
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE (just to bruise) mint in base of shaker.
Add next 4 ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda and briefly stir.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: Alcoholic lemonade with mint? A vodka mojito? However you describe it, it works.
history: Discovered in 2004 at Spring Street Natural Restaurant, New York City, USA.

name: Banoffee Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE banana in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with cocoa powder.
review: Thick and rich, one for after the cheese course.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2002 by Barrie 'Snood' Wilson, Zinc Bar & Grill, Edinburgh, Scotland.

name: Real Lemonade
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 2 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring grapefruit soda.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice wheel.
review: The classic English summertime refresher.

name: Red Angel 'Martini'
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A subtly flavoured cocktail with a dry, almost tannic edge.
history: Created in 2001 by Tony Conigliaro at Isola, Knightsbridge, London, England.

name: Red Rover
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Carpet-scarring red with the body of red wine but the palate of a cocktail.

name: Reef Juice
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: The Reef takes no prisoners! Tangy, fruity, and dangerously moreish.
history: Charles Tobias, the original proprietor of Pusser's Rum, created this cocktail at the Beach Bar in Fort Lauderdale, Florida. It was a favourite of a friend who crashed his boat on a reef.

name: Barbara
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: Creamy and delicately flavoured. The nutmeg garnish plays as important a role as the cacao liqueur.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Stanley M. Jones' 1977 Jones' Complete Barguide.

name: Resolute
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Simple but tasty. All three flavours are harmonious.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Rhett Butler
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A simple and well-balanced classic cocktail.

name: Rhubarb and Custard Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: As sharp, sweet, creamy and flavoursome as the dessert it imitates.
history: Created in 2002 by yours truly. The great British dessert of rhubarb and custard inspired a cult children's TV cartoon in the 1970s featuring a naughty pink cat called Custard and a green dog named Rhubarb. Like many British men, Rhubarb spent a lot of time in his garden shed.

name: Rhubarb and Honey Bellini
instructions: SHAKE rhubarb syrup and honey liqueur with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with Prosecco and gently stir.
review: The implausible combination works surprisingly well.
history: A simplified adaptation of a drink created in 2003 by Tony Conigliaro at London's Shumi.

name: Ribalaigua Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Float mint leaf.
review: This unusual Daiquiri leads with sweet maraschino and finishes with sour grapefruit. I've tried with a more flavoursome 'gold' rum, but this is a cocktail that shines with a light white rum.
history: Named for Constantino Ribalaigua, who introduced Hemingway to the Daiquiri at El Floridita, Havana, Cuba.

name: Gin Rickey
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FIZZ OR HIGHBALL (8OZ TO 10OZ) GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda.
garnish: Garnish with long string or ribbon of lime peel.
review: Clean, sharp and refreshing. Please don't serve this in a Collins glass, it should be "a short drink served long" in a 10oz Highball or Fizz glass.
history:
Rickeys are a family of cocktails believed to have been created by bartender George A. Williamson in 1880 at Shoemaker's Bar in Washington DC and named after Colonel Joseph Kyle Rickey, a Democratic lobbyist who it is said Williamson witnessed squeeze lime into his whisky before topping his drink with soda. Coincidentally or not, Colonel Rickey purchased the bar in 1883 and became a major importer of limes into the US.

The Gin Rickey cocktail first appeared in print in Harry Johnson's 1900 Bartenders Manual, where he calls for "1 wine glass of Tom or Holland gin if required."

name: Riviera Breeze
instructions: POUR all ingredients into chilled glass, then fill glass with ice.
review: A ginger and aniseed-fresh summertime cooler.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2003 by Rool Buckley at Café Lebowitz in New York City, USA.

name: The Roadrunner
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Citrus and tequila with a hint of maple and vanilla, smoothed with egg white.
history: Discovered in 2005 at The Cuckoo Club, London, England.

name: Robin Hood
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of apple wedge.
review: American readers might consider this an Apple Martini based on rum.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2002 by Tony Conigliaro at The Lonsdale in Notting Hill, London, England.

name: Roc-A-Coe
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Aromatic and balanced.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Roffignac
instructions: SHAKE first 2 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda, lightly stir and serve with straws.
review: This bright red, fruity drink is simple but tasty.
history: This classic cocktail is named after Count Louis Philippe Joseph de Roffignac, Mayor of New Orleans 1820-1828. Roffignac notably introduced streetlights to New Orleans and instigated the laying cobblestones on the roads of the French Quarter.

name: Roman Punch
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass two-thirds filled with crushed ice.
Briefly CHURN (stir).
Fill glass with more crushed ice.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: Spirited and refreshing with herbal notes.

name: La Rosa Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A fruity yet dry, crimson-coloured Margarita

name: Rose Petalini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of float rose petal.
review: Peychaud's bitters give this fragrant cocktail a delicate pink hue.
history: Adapted from a recipe discovered in 2005 at Rain in Amsterdam, The Netherlands.

name: Rossini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
MUDDLE strawberries in base of shaker.
Add liqueur and lemon juice, SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with strawberry slice on rim.
review: Strawberries seem to complement prosecco even better than white peaches so perhaps order this in preference to a Bellini.
history: Named for the 19th-century Italian opera composer, Gioachino Antonio Rossini. This is one of the most popular Bellini variants in Venice.

name: Rosy Cocktail
ingredients: 22.5 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: An aptly named drink with hints of spice, citrus peel, honey and mulled wine.

name: Roulette
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Balanced apple and spice.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Royal Cosmopolitan
ingredients: Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: SHAKE first four ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with champagne.
review: The classic Cosmopolitan with a layer of fizz on top adding a biscuity complexity. Sex And The City meets Ab Fab.
history:
Created in 2003 by Wayne Collins, London, England. 

For more recipes, origins and history behind the Cosmopolitan cocktail, see our Cosmopolitan page.

name: Ruby 'Martini'
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon slice on rim.
review: A sour, citrus-led variation on the Cosmopolitan.
history: Several appearances in episodes of the U.S. hit TV series, Sex And The City, helped this drink become fashionable in 2002, particularly in New York City.  It is thought to have originated at the Wave restaurant in Chicago's W Hotel.

name: Rude Ginger Cosmopolitan
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE ginger in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: To quote Halo's cocktail list, the bar where we discovered this drink, "Looks like a cosmo, goes like a Mexican!"
history:
Created in 2003 by Jeremy Adderley at Halo, Edinburgh, Scotland.

Also see the the story behind the Cosmopolitan cocktail's origin.

name: Rum Runner
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HURRICANE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: Fruity and rounded with a touch of rum funk, rich banana and blackberry.
history: The Holiday Isle Tiki Bar in Islamorada, Florida Keys, USA, claims to be the "home of the Run Runner." Originally named The Hapi Hula Hut, the bar opened in 1969 and was renamed in 1971. As the Holiday Isle's own website says, "Legend has it [so evidence is perhaps sketchy] over 40 years ago the Tiki Bar manager John Elber (Tiki John) had to get rid of an excess of rum and other liqueurs before the arrival of new inventory. Tiki John was challenged to concoct a cocktail that would help use the surplus inventory. So he mixed banana liquor, Meyers rum, brandy and grenadine and named it the Rum Runner" after the liquor smugglers who used the Florida Keys as their base in the early 1900s.

name: Russian
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of coin-sized orange zest twist.
review: Gin and vodka with a sweet hint of chocolate.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Russian Bride
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with cocoa powder.
review: A little on the sweet side for some but vanilla, coffee and chocolate smoothed with cream is a tasty combination.
history: Created in 2002 by Miranda Dickson, A.K.A. the Vodka Princess, for the UK's Revolution bar chain, where some 500,000 were sold each year.

name: Sage Margarita
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE (just to bruise) sage in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Exactly as promised - a sage flavoured Margarita.

name: Sage Cocktail
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE (just to bruise) sage in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Delicate sage and a hint of apple, dried with vermouth and fortified with vodka.

name: St Kitts
instructions: SHAKE first three ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with ginger ale, lightly stir and serve with straws.
review: Rust coloured and refreshing.

name: Barnum Was Right
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A gin apricot sour balanced and favoured with generous dashes of aromatic bitters.
history:
Named for Barnum Was Right, a 1929 American comedy film released with sounds and also as a silent version for theatres not wired for sound. Only the silent version serves.

Adapted from a recipe in Crosby Gaige's 1941 Crosby Gaige's Cocktail Guide & Ladies' Companion.

name: Sake'Politan
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A Cosmo with more than a hint of sake.

name: Salty Dog
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FIZZ OR HIGHBALL (8OZ TO 10OZ) GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with grapefruit slice wheel.
review:
Whether enjoyed with breakfast or as an evening aperitivo, this is a superbly cleansing and refreshing cocktail.

Our previous recipe called for 50ml vodka, 15ml maraschino and 50ml grapefruit juice. I like maraschino, but, as many pointed out, it's not traditionally an ingredient in this vintage cocktail.
history: A cocktail from the late 1950s/early 1960s, its first appearance in a recipe book is in Ted Saucier's 1962 Bottom's Up: New and Revised Edition.

name: Salty Lychee Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered lychee.
review: Pale pink in colour and subtle in flavour with gin and the delicately salty tang of manzanilla sherry and drying rich rose and lychee.
history: I created this drink in 2002 after trying Dick Bradsell's Lychee & Rose Petal Martini.

name: San Francisco
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Long, fruity, slightly sweet and vodka-laced.
history: Unknown, but almost certainly not originally from the city of San Francisco in California. It certainly originated before 1992, as that's when I first encountered this cocktail.

name: Sandstorm
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: A long, fruity cocktail featuring well-balanced sweet and sourness.
history: Created in 2003 by James Cunningham at Zinc in Glasgow, Scotland, and is named for its cloudy yellow colour.

name: Bartender's Martini
ingredients: 22.5 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: This aromatic Martini with a florish has rich orange notes followed by a dry fino sherry finish.

name: Le Sang
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of chopped fruit.
review: The tannin in the wine balances the fruit and sweetness nicely.
history: French for 'blood', this cocktail is a twist on the classic Spanish Sangria, which also means 'blood'.

name: Sangria Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Brandy-based and fruit laced - just like its namesake.
history: Created in 2003 by Angelo Vieira at The Light Bar, St. Martins Hotel, London, England.

name: Santiago Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: This Daiquiri is particularly delicate in its balance between sweet and sour.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Satsuma Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Tastes like its namesake - hard to believe the recipe comprises almost half apple juice.
history: Adapted from a drink discovered in 2002 at the Fifth Floor Bar, Harvey Nichol's, London, England.

name: Savannah
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Gin and orange with a hint of chocolate - smoothed with egg white.
history: A pre-Prohibition cocktail that first appears in Albert Stevens Crockett's 1931 book Old Waldorf Bar Days.

name: Savoy Special No.1
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: An aromatised 2:1 shaken Dry Martini with a tiny dose of grenadine, which turns this the merest rust-pink, while just a dash of absinthe adds wormwood complexity.
history: Adapted from Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Scorpion
instructions: Select and pre-chill a DOUBLE OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
DRY BLEND (without ice) first 5 ingredients on slow setting and then gradually add Xanthan gum while continuing to blend.
Add half full 7oz scoop of crushed ice and BLEND some more.
POUR into chilled glass.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: A fruity cocktail with a sting in its tail.
history: Created by Victor Bergeron circa 1950s, this recipe is adapted from his 1972 Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide (Revised Edition).

name: Basil and Honey Daiquiri
instructions: STIR honey and lime juice in base of shaker until honey dissolves
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Basil adds dry vegetal notes to a Canchànchara (honey) Daiquiri.
history: Created in 2005 by yours truly in London, England.

name: Screwdriver (D's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
Briefly STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review:
The temperature at which this cocktail is served, and the freshness of the orange juice are crucial to its success, but it's perhaps better made into a Harvey Wallbanger.

My own preference is to make Screwdrivers with a whopping 75ml of vodka, 90ml orange juice, 7.5ml rich sugar syrup, and 3 dashes of orange bitters, served in a Collins glass. I like the grainy bite the generous measure of vodka adds, but it's a punchy cocktail, so I have cut the ingredients back by a third and used a smaller Highball glass for sobriety, while retaining the ratios.
history:
Legend has it that this cocktail first appeared in the late 1940s in Iran, where parched American oil engineers working in the desert supposedly added orange juice to their vodka and stirred it with the nearest thing to hand, usually a screwdriver in their work clothes pocket.

This is partly supported by the following reference to the drink in the 24th October 1949 issue of Time Magazine, "In the dimly lighted bar of the sleek Park Hotel, Turkish intelligence agents mingle with American engineers and Balkan refugees, drinking the latest Yankee concoction of vodka and orange juice, called a 'screwdriver'."

name: Seabreeze
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Many don't bother to shake this simple drink, instead simply "building" by pouring ingredients into ice-filled glass and then briefly stirring to mix.
history: Marketing by Absolut Vodka and Ocean Spray helped drive this cocktail's popularity throughout the 1990s but the Seabreeze may have originated in the 1980s or even 1960s along with the Harpoon.

name: 75 Cocktail (Vermeire's 1922 recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Spirituous, dry and fruity, the botanicals in the gin add depth of flavour to the other fruity ingredients.
history: Adapted from a recipe by Henry Tépé of Henry's Bar in Paris, France, originally published in Robert Vermeire's 1922 book Cocktails - How to Mix Them.

name: Basil Beauty
instructions: Cut passion fruit in half and scoop flesh into shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Pineapple and passion fruit laced with citrus vodka and infused with hints of lime, basil and coconut.
history: Created in 1999 by Wayne Collins, London, England

name: Showbiz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered blackberries.
review: Sweet cassis soured with grapefruit and fortified with vodka.

name: Silver Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Dry and aromatic with a hint of maraschino richness.
history:
Adapted from the Silver Cocktail in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book:
"SILVER COCKTAIL.
2 Dashes Maraschino.
2 Dashes Orange Bitters.
½ French Vermouth.
½ Dry Gin.
Shake well and strain into cocktail glass."

name: Singapore Sling (using Old Tom gin)
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: SHAKE first six ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda and lightly stir.
review: Slightly on the dry and sour side, this is decidedly more complex than most Singapore Sling recipes.
history: For more Singapore Sling recipes and the cocktails full story, see our Singapore Sling page.

name: Sleepy Hollow
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE mint in base of shaker (just to bruise).
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: Hints of lemon and mint with gin and apricot fruit. Very refreshing.
history: An adaptation of a drink created in the early 1930s and named after Washington Irving's novel and its enchanted valley with ghosts, goblins and headless horseman.

name: Sloppy Joe
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A well-balanced combination of sweet and sour.
history:
One of two vintage cocktails that variously appear under the names of Sloppy Joe or Sloppy Joe's. They either originate from or are named after Sloppy Joe's, a famous bar in Havana, Cuba during the 1930s. Ernest Hemingway was a regular and Sloppy Joe's inspired the fictitious Freddy's Bar in his novel To Have and Have Not.
Adapted from a recipe in Stanley M. Jones' 1977 Jones' Complete Barguide.

name: Smooth and Creamy
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: Creamy and moreish.

name: Sorrel Rum Punch
instructions: Select a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Serve with straws.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: This drink harnesses the flavour of sorrelade and combines it with the traditional strength and bitter sweetness of rum punch. Jamaica in a glass.
history: A classic Jamaican punch using the classic proportions of 'one of sour, two of sweet, three of strong and four of weak.'

name: So So Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of float (dehydrated) apple slice.
review: This beautifully balanced, delicately herbal cocktail is so much more than just so-so.
history: The "So So Cocktail" appears in Harry MacElhone's's 1923 Harry of Ciro's ABC Of Mixing Cocktails  where he credits this drink to "Mr P. Soso, the popular manager of Kit-Kat Club, London."

name: South Beach
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: An unusual bittersweet combination with a strong orange and almond flavour.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 1992 by Dale DeGroff in New York City, USA.

name: South China Breeze
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Orange and grapefruit with an oriental influence by way of lychee.

name: Screaming Banana Banshee
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HURRICANE GLASS.
DRY BLEND (without ice) first 5 ingredients (all but Xanthan gum) on a slow setting and then gradually add Xanthan while continuing to blend.
Add 7oz scoop of crushed ice and BLEND some more.
POUR into chilled glass.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: An alcoholic banana and chocolate milkshake - not too sweet.
history: Without the vodka, this is a plain Banana Banshee.

name: Sensation
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE mint in base of shaker (just to bruise).
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: Fresh, fragrant and balanced.
history: The Sensation notably appeared in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Sidecar Named Desire
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Take a classic Sidecar and add some love interest - apples!
history: See our Sidecar cocktail page for the full story behind the origins of the Sidecar, its history and variations.

name: Singapore Sling (Baker's Formula)
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SLING GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring soda.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: Lacks the citrus of other Singapore Slings but the strength of the gin and dilution cuts and so balances the sweetness of the liqueurs.
history: Singapore Sling history and other versions

name: Sir Charles Punch
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Cognac-laced citrusy wine flavours make this somewhat reminiscent of boozy, chilled mulled wine.
history: Adapted from the following recipe in the 1949 Esquire's Handbook For Hosts book.

name: Sleeping Bison-Tini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pear slice.
review: A light cocktail featuring a melange of subtle flavours.

name: Sling (Generic Name)
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SLING GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda or ginger ale.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice wheel.
review: Sugar balances citrus juice sourness, the spirit fortifies, and the carbonate lengthens.
history:
The word 'Sling' comes from the German 'schlingen', meaning 'to swallow', and Slings based on a spirit mixed with sugar and water were popularly drunk in the late 1800s.

Slings are similar to Toddies and like Toddies can be served hot. (Toddies, however, are never served cold.) The main difference between a Toddy and a Sling is that Slings are not flavoured by the addition of spices. Also, Toddies tend to be made with plain water, while Slings are charged with water, soda water or ginger ale.

The earliest known definition of 'cocktail' describes it as a bittered sling.

Also see:Cocktail Categories & Families

name: Smartini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of smarties.
review: Citrus with a crispy chocolate edge. A sweetie.

name: Snood Murdekin
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SHOT GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Carefully FLOAT cream. (Lightly whip or shake sealed container before pouring cream to aerate.)
garnish: Serve naked, without garnish.
review: Moreish combination of coffee and raspberries topped with cream.
history: Created in the late 90s by Dick Bradsell at Detroit bar in Covent Garden, London, England for Karin Wiklund. Named for the sad, flute-playing Moomin Troll character from the Moomin books by Tove Jansson.

name: Snowball
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR lemonade and lime juice into ice-filled glass.
Add the advocaat (which will mostly sink though the other ingredients).
Briefly STIR to integrate while retaining the lemonade's carbonation.
garnish: Garnish with lime zest twist or lime slice.
review: Fresh lime juice and lemonade freshens and invigorates creamy advocaat. Try our more luxurious and better Champagne Snowball.
history: Thought to have originated in Britain in the late 1940s or early 1950s, reaching its peak of popularity in the 1970s.

name: Southern Mint Cobbler
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE mint (just to bruise) in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into glass filled with crushed ice
Serve with straws.
review: Very fruity and easy to drink.
history: For information on the fascinating history of Cobblers please see our page.

name: Southern Tea
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with apricot wedge or dried apricot half.
review: Sweet fruity flavours balanced by tannic bitterness in the tea.
history: Created in 2002 by yours truly in London, England.

name: South Side Royale
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE (just to bruise) mint in base of shaker.
Add next 4 ingredients (all but sparkling wine) and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf.
review: A White Lady with fresh mint and sparkling wine.
history: The sparkling wine version (hence "Royale") of the classic South Side Fizz.

name: Spencer Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: To quote Harry Craddock, "Very mellifluous: has a fine and rapid action: for morning work."
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Spiced Pear
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pear wedge on rim.
review: Just as it says on the tin - spiced pear.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2002 by James Stuart-Gammie in Edinburgh, Scotland.

name: Spiked Apple Cider (Hot)
instructions: MUDDLE cloves in base of shaker
Add cognac and apple juice
SHAKE without ice and fine strain into glass
WARM in microwave then FLOAT double cream over drink.
review: Warming and lightly spiced under a creamy head.
history: Adapted from a drink discovered in 2006 at Double Seven, New York City, USA.

name: Sputnik #2
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Orange, cognac and rum meld well. Fruity and refreshing.
history: A cocktail served in underground clubs all over the former Eastern Bloc. It was originally made with cheap Cuban rum, Georgian brandy and tinned orange juice.

name: Star
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: Apple notes from calvados combine harmoniously with vinous vermouth with creole-style bitters, this Sweet Manhattan-like cocktail's anchoring ingredient.
history:
Said to have been created in the 1870s by a bartender at the legendary Manhattan Club, which once stood at the north corner of 34th Street and 5th Avenue, New York City.

Our recipe is adapted from George J. Kappeler's 1895 Modern American Drinks – How to Mix and Serve All Kinds of Cups and Drinks.

name: Steep Flight
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with apple or pear wedge.
review: "Apples and pears" is the cockney rhyming slang for stairs, hence the flavours in this flight.
history: Created in 2005 by yours truly. Incredibly, this cocktail was awarded a Gold in the Long Drink category at the Drinks International Bartender's Challenge on 31st May 2006.

name: Stone and Gravel
instructions: POUR all ingredients into glass filled with crushed ice and briefly churn (stir).
review: Simple, boozy and surprisingly good.
history: A popular drink in Jamaica.

name: Stork Club
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Gin and orange with a souring splash of lime juice.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Lucius Beebe's 1946 The Stork Club Bar Book.

name: Strawberry Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE strawberries (4 to 7 depending on size) in the base of the shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
garnish: Garnish with strawberry (or half if large) on rim with mint sprig tip leaves.
review:
Makes strawberries and cream appear very dull.

NOTE: Strawberry pulp is notorious for blocking strainers and sink wastes. Hence, double straining is essential when making this cocktail, as is the use of a quality Hawthorne strainer (I use one with an extra spring and a wide-gauge fine strainer with a large capacity). The Hawthorne strainer should retain most of the fruit pulp, along with the ice in the shaker. Pouring through an additional fine strainer ensures the clarity of the cocktail. Even with the best strainers, the pulp will start to restrict the flow towards the end of the pour and moving your shaker to help the liquid find a new escape route helps. Finally, use a bar spoon to help move the pulp in the fine strainer to allow the last of the cocktail through the strainer. Also, don't be tempted to make two cocktails in one shaker by doubling quantities, as this will compound pulp issues. The cocktail is worth the faff!
history:
A popular cocktail in Cuba where it is known as a Daiquiri de Fresa.
See: Daiquiri cocktail history

name: Strawberry Éclair
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SHOT GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
review: Far from sophisticated but appropriately named.
history: This drink heralds from Australia where it was a popular shot in the 1990s.

name: Strawberry Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE strawberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass. (You may find the strawberry pulp clogs your fine strainer, so have a spoon handy to clear the mesh to enable the liquid run through.)
garnish: Garnish with strawberry on rim.
review: Fresh strawberries combine well with tequila in this fruity margarita. (The strawberries makes this cocktail viscous, so sugar syrup is preferable to agave syrup, and blanco tequila freshens more than reposado tequila.)

name: Strawberry Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE strawberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of strawberry on rim.
review: Rich strawberries fortified with vodka and a hint of pepper spice.

name: Strawberry and Balsamic Mojito

name: Three Miller
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A seriously strong drink, in flavour and in alcohol.
history:
The Three Miller is one of several Prohibition-era cocktails whose name references the distance from a shore that a nation's territorial waters. During the 18th century this was established as three nautical miles (5.6 km), the distance a cannonball could be shot from the shore to repel incursions. Hence, during Prohibition, US jurisdiction was limited to three miles offshore so allowing the legal supply of alcohol to parties on board boats just outside territorial waters.

As the number of offshore parties grew, the US negotiated agreements with countries such as the UK and France to recognize the right of the American authorities to enforce Prohibition and anti-smuggling laws to an extended 12-mile limit. Predictably, the parties moved further off-shore. On 29th December 1988, President Reagan extended the territorial waters of the United States to 12 miles from 3 miles.

The notoriety of the three-mile and then 12-mile limit to the reach of the Feds during Prohibition led to the Three Miller/Three Mile Limit, the Twelve Miles Out, and the Twelve Mile Limit cocktails.

This cocktail appears in in Harry MacElhone's 1922 Harry's ABC of Mixing Cocktails and his 1927 Barflies & Cocktails as the "Three Mile Limit Cocktail" and then in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book as the "Three Miller Cocktail."

Life Savers are an American brand of ring-shaped hard and soft confectionery created in 1912 by Clarence Crane, a candy maker in Ohio.

history: Created in 2003 by yours truly and originally named the Triangular Martini after the triangular Toblerone chocolate bar, which incidentally was invented in 1908 by the Swiss chocolate maker Theodor Tobler. The chocolate bar's name is a blend of Tobler with Torrone, the Italian word for honey-almond nougat, one of its main ingredients.

Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice.

garnish: Crumble graham cracker or plain digestive biscuit over cocktail.
review: Surprisingly, the biscuity top continues to float as you sip the vanilla cream layer right down to the point when you hit the raspberry base.

name: Vanderbilt
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with skewered maraschino cherry.
review:
Tangy, rich cherry and hints of vanilla fortified with brandy.

My previous specs for this cocktail were to serve in a coupe glass and called for a generous 67.5ml (2¼oz) cognac, 22.5ml (¾oz) cherry brandy, 5ml (1 bar spoon) sugar syrup, and aromatic bitters. In July 2022, in a quest for some semblance of sobriety, I reduced the volume of this boozy late-night treat back by a third to better suit a Nick & Nora glass.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Robert Vermeire's 1922 Cocktails: How to Mix Them which gives the origin of this cocktail as being at "the Kursaal" in Ostend, Belgium, where, as the name would suggest, it was made for a member of the wealthy Vanderbilt family. However, Colonel Cornelius Vanderbilt, the American railroad tycoon cited by Vermeire died in 1877 so this cocktail is more likely to have been made for his great-grandson, Alfred Gwynne Vanderbilt (1877–1915) who died as a passenger on the RMS Lusitania ocean liner when it was sunk by a German U-boat torpedo during WWI.

name: Vodka Collins
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.

history:
The exact origins of this drink are unknown, but its earliest appearance in a cocktail recipe book is in Ted Saucier's 1962 Bottoms Up - New and Revised Edition, which credits its creation to "Ted Majeski, United Press International."

Edward Majeski (1919-1988) was executive editor of the news picture service at United Press International and, according to his obituary in The New York Times, "boasted of never having taken a news picture in his career. But he was celebrated for a keen eye in recognizing a good picture." Apparently, he was also a keen amateur cocktail maker.
