TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: Prepare garnish of fresh strawberry.
review: A crowd-pleaser - looks great and its fruity, balanced flavour will offend few.

name: Warsaw Cooler
instructions: STIR honey with vodka in base of shaker until honey dissolves
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain into ice-filled glass.
review: Orange, honey, apple and spice laced with Polish Bison grass vodka.
history: Created in 2002 by Morgan Watson of Apartment, Belfast, Northern Ireland.

name: Wasabi Martini
instructions: Squeeze a pea-sized quantity of wasabi paste onto a barspoon and STIR with vodka until wasabi dissolves
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: This "Martini" would be more accurately named a Wasabi Vodka Sour, but besides the now-dated name, this child of the early noughties is balanced with attractive spicy heat and a zesty finish.
history:
Created in 2004 by Philippe Guidi at Morton's, London, England.
Martini cocktail and its evolution

name: Watermelon and Basil Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
Cut watermelon into 16 segments, chop the flesh from one segment into cubes and MUDDLE in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with watermelon wedge on rim.
review: Refreshing watermelon with interesting herbal hints from the basil and gin.

name: Watermelon and Basil Smash
instructions: Cut watermelon into 16 segments, chop the flesh from one segment into cubes and MUDDLE in base of skater
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with ginger ale.
review: Sweet and sour, long and refreshing with subtle hints of basil, ginger and tequila amongst the fruit.

name: Watermelon Cosmopolitan
instructions: Cut watermelon into 16 segments, chop the flesh from one segment into cubes and MUDDLE in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: It looks a tad pinkier than a standard Cosmo and tastes just as the name suggests.
history: Created in 2003 by Eric Fossard at Cecconi's, London, England. One of many variations on the Cosmopolitan to emerge in the late 1990s and early 2000s.

name: Webster Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: Balanced, or even a tad on the sour side rather than sweet. Indeed, your choice of apricot liqueur will impact the need for added sugar. Adjust accordingly in the pursuit of deliciousness.
history: Adapted form a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: What The Hell
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime wedge .
review: Gin and dry apricot. On the 4th of September 2022, I reduced the volume of my previous recipe by 25% and while doing so I omitted sugar syrup from the recipe to be more in keeping with classic versions of this cocktail which call for lemon juice. (I prefer lime.) My previous recipe: 60ml (2oz) gin, 30 (1oz) apricot brandy, 22.5ml (¾oz) dry vermouth, 7.5ml (¼oz) lime juice, 5ml (1 spoon) sugar syrup.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Stanley M. Jones' 1977 Jones' Complete Barguide.

name: White Cosmo
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Just what it says on the tin.
history:
Emerged during 2002 in New York City.

For more recipes, origins and history behind the Cosmopolitan cocktail, see our Cosmopolitan page.

name: White Elephant
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of dust with freshly grated nutmeg (or chocolate powder).
review: Smooth and creamy with a hint of chocolate.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Stanley M. Jones' 1977 Jones' Complete Barguide.

name: White Lion (David Embury's recipe)
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients, including the shell of half a lime, with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Please don't judge this recipe until you have experienced this fruity Daiquiri made homemade "grenadine" (1 part freshly pressed pomegranate juice and 2 parts caster sugar). Then you'll end up with a delicious cocktail with an off-white hue. With commercial grenadine syrup, this cocktail can have a jammy flavour and is likely to be bright cherry red - hardly a 'White' Lion. That said, some prefer cherry red and jammy!
history:
Adapted from a recipe purloined from David Embury's seminal 1948 book, The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks. Embury had a dry palate so we've added a spoon of sugar syrup to his recipe, which, depending on your palate, you may want to reduce or omit this.

Embury does the While Lion an injustice by saying,  "This, of course, is simply a White Lady without the egg white and with lime juice in place of lemon". That might sum up his version but the original White Lion in Jerry Thomas' 1862 The Bar-tenders Guide is so much more.

name: White Russian Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR first 2 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
Carefully FLOAT cream. (Lightly whip or shake sealed container before pouring cream to aerate.)
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review:
One of the best-known cocktails of the modern era, the White Russian, consists of vodka and coffee liqueur served on the rocks, sipped over a silky layer of cream or milk.

A White Russian can be as simple as equal measures vodka, coffee liqueur and fresh cream poured over ice. However, it looks better if you stir at least the first two ingredients and then layer cream on the surface of the drink (as above). Or, some prefer to shake all three ingredients to make a properly integrated cocktail.
history:
Named due to its appearance and vodka base. Smirnoff and Stolichnaya were the main vodka brands at the time, so vodka was associated with Russia.

The White Russian is obviously related to the Black Russian whose origins are thought to date from 1949. However, who first came up with this creamy interpretation and when is unknown.

Mention of the White Russian cocktail in print first appeared in an advertisement for a now long-defunct coffee liqueur called 'Coffee Southern', which ran in the Boston Globe on 21st March 1965, then in California's Oakland Tribune on 21 November 1965. Along with the White Russian, the "Try delicious Coffee Sothern" advert also gives the recipes for 'Coffee and Cointreau', 'Southern Grasshopper' and 'Java Sundae' using the liqueur.

The White Russian strutted its way through the 1970s disco era before its popularity waned in the 80s, only to be rescued and made more popular than ever after being the libation of choice of The Dude in the cult 1998 movie The Big Lebowski. Played by Jeff Bridges, throughout this comedy caper film, Jeffrey "The Dude" Lebowski has little interest or passion for anything other than bowling and drinking White Russians, which he calls a "Caucasian". Over the duration of the film, The Dude consumes seven of the creamy cocktails.

name: White Stinger
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
review: A liquid After Eight. Some will prefer to stir, rather than shake this cocktail but it's better to "shake it to wake it."

name: Wilton Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of cinnamon dusted apple slice.
review: Refined cinnamon and apple.
history: A 2003 adaptation of the signature cocktail at The Blue Bar, The Berkeley Hotel, London, England.

name: Wimbledon Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE strawberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of strawberry in rim crowned with tip of mint sprig..
review: Takes some getting through the fine strainer, but when you do it's simply strawberries and cream with the faintest hint of alcohol. Wimbledon is all about strawberries & cream.
history: Created in London sometime during the 1990s when every cocktail served in a V-shaped glass was called a Martini.

name: The Windsor Rose
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of rose petal.
review: An orange vodka and rosewater Cosmo.
history: Adapted from a drink discovered in 2005 at The Polo Club Lounge, New Orleans, USA.

name: Windy Miller
instructions: SHAKE first three ingredients with ice and strain into glass filled with crushed ice
TOP with lemonade.
review: British readers over 40 may remember the children's TV series Trumpton, Chigley and Camberwick Green. If you do, then sing between sips, "Pugh, Pugh, Barney McGrew, Cuthbert, Dibble and Grubb."
history: Discovered in 2000 at Teatro Club in London, England.

name: Winter Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Reminiscent of an Apple Cart (a Calvados Sidecar), this is simple, balanced and tastes great.

name: Woo Woo
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Fruity cranberry laced with vodka and sweet peach liqueur. A quaffable cocktail from the 1980s.
history: A Fuzzy Navel with cranberry instead of orange juice.

name: Yacht Club
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Rum-laced and slightly sweet with hints of apricot fruit.
history: Adapted from a recipe purloined from David Embury's classic book, The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks.

name: Yellow Parrot
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: The aniseed of the absinthe combines well with the other ingredients but dilution is key. A bit of a sweety but a strong old bird.
history: Some say this was created in 1935 by Albert Coleman at The Stork Club, New York City, but the cocktail first appeared in in Robert Vermeire's 1922 Cocktails: How to Mix Them and also notably in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Zakuski Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE cucumber in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of cucumber peel.
review: Named after the Russian snack.
history: Created in 2002 by Alex Kammerling, London, England.

name: Zaza
ingredients: 60 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: SHAKE (or stir) all ingredients with ice and strain fine into chilled glass.
review: Zaza is a diminutive of Isabela. However, other than the simplicity of its ingredients, there is nothing diminutive about this classic cocktail.
history: It is named after a French play that was a hit around the verge of the 20th century and was followed by opera and film versions, the Zaza Cocktail appears in Hon. William "Cocktail Bill" Boothby's 1908 The World's Drinks And How To Mix Them.

name: Zelda Cocktail
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE mint (just to bruise) in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Bison grass vodka combines brilliantly with mint and almond.
history: Created in May 2002 by Phillip Jeffrey at the GE Club, London, England. He made it for a friend called Zelda - the name really wouldn't have worked if she'd been called Tracy.

name: Alabazam (long)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 6 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring soda.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
review: This long, fruity cocktail packs a cognac-charged punch, but as the ice melts, it mellows and becomes refreshing.
history: The first known reference to a cocktail of this name appears in Leo-Engel's 1878 American Other Drinks.

name: Apricot Rickey
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FIZZ OR HIGHBALL (8OZ TO 10OZ) GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda.
garnish: Prepare garnish of length of lime peel.
review: Light, fruity and refreshing, if a little on the sweet side.

name: Arnold Palmer
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring soda water.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice wheel.
review: A real lemon iced tea. Balanced and refreshing.
history: A popular drink throughout the United States. Named after and said to be a favourite of the legendary golfer.

name: Boxcar
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of sugar rim and orange zest twist (expressed and then discarded).
review: A White Lady in a sugar-rimmed glass with the addition of a dash of grenadine and substituting lime juice for lemon.

name: Citrus Rum Cooler
instructions: SHAKE first three ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with lemonade
Lightly stir and serve with straws.
review: Light, fruity, easy and very refreshing.

name: Embassy Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: The Daiquiri and Sidecar's lovechild. Some add aromatic bitters, but it's better without, as this allows the rum and brandy interplay to shine. However, like a Daiquiri, the Embassy opens with a touch of dilution, so, depending on your ice, consider adding a splash of water.
history:
Adapted from a recipe in Buzza-Cardozo of Hollywood's 1933 book Hollywood Cocktails: Over 200 Excellent Recipes, this cocktail is thought to have been created at the Prohibition-era Embassy Club in Hollywood, a speakeasy opened in 1928 by Adolph "Eddie" Brandstatter (1886-19400). One of Hollywood's leading restaurateurs, he owned Café Montemartre at 6757 Hollywood Boulevard and his Embassy Club was a private venue exclusive to fee-paying Hollywood stars and glitterati. To quote the Los Angeles Times, " He had the "Brandstatter touch": one part dapper European suavity - he brought a sheen of elegance to the rough-edged Hollywood of the 1920s - and one part straight-up charisma. Wherever Eddie was, that was the party."

However, some believe the Embassy Cocktail was created at the Embassy Club in London rather than any of the numerous American clubs with the same name. Robert Vermeire, author of the 1922 Cocktails: How to Mix Them worked at London's Embassy Club, indeed the title page says "By Robert of the Embassy Club", but his book (published in London) doesn't include a cocktail named Embassy.

The "Embassy Cocktail" does appear in William T. "Cocktail Bill" Boothby's 1934 The World Drinks and how to mix them, but without attribution. Tellingly this book was published in San Francisco, California – the same state where I'm sure this cocktail emerged after the 1928 opening of Hollywood's Embassy Club.

name: Fireman's Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered orange wheel and maraschino cherry sail.
review: Smooth and balanced with great rum character. Lime fresh and fruity sweet.
history: Circa 1930s, USA

name: Fix (Generic Name)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of seasonal fruit.
review: Match the juice and spirit and this formula works every time.
history: A Fix is a classic style of drink that consists of a spirit, lemon juice, and some kind of sweet fruit served short.

name: Froth Blower Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Salmon-pink and very frothy but surprisingly complex and tasty.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Gloria
instructions: SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with sparkling wine.
review: A tequila sour topped with champagne or a tequila French 75.

name: Hot Toddy (brandy and lemon)
instructions: Select and pre-heat a TODDY GLASS.
POUR first 3 ingredients in warmed glass and stir.
TOP with boiling water while spoon still in glass and briefly stir.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of coin-sized lemon zest twist.
review: Warms the cockles with brandy and a good dose of citrus.
history:
The first published reference to a Toddy was in England in 1741.

A Brandy Toddy features in Jerry Thomas' Bar-tenders Guide, published in 1862.

name: Houla Houla Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Orange generously laced with gin. Our previous version of this recipe called for just 20ml (⅔oz) orange juice, but I've come to realise this cocktail is better with a touch more juice.
history: Adapted from Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book but was first published in Robert Vermeire's 1922 Cocktails How to Mix Them and in other books spelt Hoola-Hoola or even Hula Hula. For the full story see Hula Hula, Houla Houla or Hoola-Hoola.

name: Huapala
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon wedge &/or lemon zest twist.
review: In his book, Vic prefaces this cocktail with the comment, "Nice, easy drink". It's basically a lemon Daiquiri with gin and grenadine.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Victor Bergeron's 1972 Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide where this recipe is accompanied by the "TV" logo indicating that this recipe is a Trader Vic original.

name: Iced Tea Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Tannic and bittersweet. A refreshing after-dinner cocktail.
history: Created in 2006 by yours truly, London, England.

name: London Cosmopolitan
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A Cosmopolitan but made with London dry gin in place of vodka.
history:
A subtle twist on the classic Cosmopolitan created in 2008 by yours truly at The Cabinet Room in London, England.
Cosmopolitan cocktail history.

name: Oceanbreeze
instructions: POUR cranberry juice into ice-filled glass
SHAKE other ingredients with ice and carefully strain into glass to LAYER over the cranberry juice
Serve with straws.
review: Refreshingly juicy.
history: Created in 2007 by yours truly (Simon D) for Ocean Spray.

name: Picador
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: The name might be more masculine, but it is still a classic Margarita. This 2:1:1 recipe is a touch on the sour side for most so you may want to add a 5ml (1 bar spoon) of agave or sugar syrup.
history: Yes, you're right! This drink is exactly the same as a classically proportioned Margarita. It was first published in William J. "Billy" Tarling's 1937 Café Royal Bar Book, 16 years before the first written reference to a Margarita.

name: El Presidente Menocal Special
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of mint sprig and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: What hot Cuban summers are made for.
history: Created by Constantino (Constante) Ribalaigua Vert at El Floridita in Havana, Cuba. This recipe is adapted from a 1937 Bar Florida (later renamed Floridita) menu. The name refers to Mario García Menocal, who was president of Cuba from 1912 to 1920.

name: Shark's Tooth No.1 (Trader Vic's)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SLING GLASS.
SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring soda water.
garnish: Garnish with spent lime shell.
review: Pinky red in colour and heavily influenced by flavoursome overproof rum.
history: Adapted from Victor Bergeron's Trader Vic's 1972 Bartender's Guide (revised edition) where he writes, "One of the first drinks we ever made".

name: Silver Bronx
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A Bronx made 'silver' by the addition of egg white.
history: See: Bronx cocktail history

name: Afterburner
instructions: Select and pre-heat a SNIFTER/BRANDY BALLOON GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into glass.
SWIRL glass to mix ingredients.
Tip glass to near horizontal, and CAREFULLY flambé.
EXTINGUISH flame by covering flaming tankard with base of the other tankard.
review: A surprisingly smooth and moreish peppermint-laced drink.

name: Southside
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE mint in base of shaker (just to bruise).
Add next 4 ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with chilled soda from a bottle/can or just the merest squirt from a chilled soda siphon.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf.
review: Gin and mint with a splash of lime. If you like a Mojito and you like gin, then you'll also like the Southside.
history: The cocktail (straight-up) version of the vintage South Side Fizz.

name: Strawberry Blonde
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
MUDDLE strawberries in base of shaker.
Add next 3 ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with strawberry on rim.
review: This long, refreshing orange-red drink is basically a vodka-laced strawberry-flavoured real lemonade.
history: Created in 2008 by yours truly at The Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: The Surfer
instructions: POUR lemonade into ice-filled glass to two-thirds full
FLOAT cognac over lemonade
Serve with straws and instruct drinker to stir ingredients before drinking.
review: Simple but tasty and very refreshing. The use of good ice and a quality lemonade is essential to this drink's success.
history: Created in 2008 by yours truly. The original Cognac Surfer is made by floating cognac on mineral water.

name: Tom Arnold
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
review: Traditional lemonade laced with vodka and lengthened with tea to make a light and refreshing drink.
history: This is one of a series of tea-based drinks that were originally named after golfers. It takes its name from the actor and comedian who starred in National Lampoon's Golf Punk.

name: Alberto Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Dry, complex and aromatic. A Fifty-Fifty Martini with a generous dose of fino sherry and a splash of triple sec.
history: First published in William James "Billy" Tarling's 1937 Café Royal Bar Book where the invention of this cocktail is credited to A. J. Smith.

name: Amalia
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple foam.
review: Sip a gooseberry and mint influenced lemon Daiquiri though a foam topping.
history: Created in 2008 by Sam Dean, Mobar,  Nottingham England.

name: Amber Room
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with skewered maraschino cherry.
review: Serious and packed with bold flavours. Fellow Chartreuse fans will approve.
history: This vintage cocktail originated from a layered or 'pousse-café' style drink called a Bijou. The original drink was so named after the French word meaning 'jewel' due to its trio of ingredients being coloured after the three most precious jewels: diamond (gin), ruby (sweet vermouth) and emerald (Green Chartreuse). Stir rather than layer and the colours combine to make this an aptly named amber coloured drink.

name: Apple, Cucumber and Elderflower cup
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE cucumber in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of cucumber slices.
review: Apple and Zubrówka is a magic combination, as is apple and elderflower. Here these flavoursome combinations are enhanced and freshened by cucumber.
history: Discovered in 2007 at Public Restaurant, New York City, USA.

name: Argentina Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A Wet Martini softened by liqueur and given a hint of citrus and spice.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Stanley M. Jones' 1977 Jones' Complete Barguide.

name: Argha Noah
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Delicately spiced, honeyed and sherried aged rum.
history: Created in January 2009 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Army Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf.
review: This rosé-coloured Martini is harder and more spirituous than it looks - perhaps why the name?
history: See Martini cocktail and its evolution

name: Astor
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Citrus and the tang of Swedish punch add a distinctive flavour to gin in this tasty vintage cocktail.
history:
Recipe adapted from Albert Stevens Crockett's 1931 The Old Waldorf-Astoria Bar Book where the recipe is accompanied by a notation as to who or what this cocktail is named after, citing "John Jacob" or "William Waldorf", both members of the Astor family, a wealthy American family whose fortune originated in the fur trade and became centred on New York City real estate investments.

John Jacob Astor IV (1864-1912) was the real estate magnate who built New York's Astoria Hotel, "the world's most luxurious hotel" and died as the richest passenger when the RMS Titanic sunk.

William Waldorf "Willy" Astor, 1st Viscount Astor (1848-1919) was John Jacob's cousin and rival who built the original Waldorf Hotel next door to his cousin's hotel. The combined complex became known as the Waldorf-Astoria Hotel.

name: Astoria
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: An Old Tom-based super wet (two-thirds vermouth to one-third gin) Martini with a slug of orange bitters and served with a twist.
history:
Recipe adapted from Albert Stevens Crockett's 1931 The Old Waldorf-Astoria Bar Book where the recipe is accompanied by the following notation, "After the big annex to the Old Waldorf, which at its opening, in 1897, became the main part of the establishment."

William Waldorf Astor built the original Waldorf Hotel, which opened in 1893, next door to his aunt's home, on the site of his father's mansion and today's Empire State Building. John Astor persuaded his aunt to move uptown and then built the Astor Hotel. The two hotels were connected and the combined Waldorf-Astoria became the largest hotel in the world at the time.

name: Balabushka
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of apple slice .
review: This vodka-based cocktail has flavours of apple, lemon, orange and almond.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2001 by Julien Escot at Langdon Hall Hotel in Cambridge, Ontario, Canada. Named in homage to George Balabushka, the legendary billiards cue maker.

name: Balm Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered olives.
review: Orange-flavoured dry sherry with subtle pimento spice.
history:
Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book:
"BALM COCKTAIL. (6 people)
½ Glass Orange Juice.
½ Glass Cointreau.
3 Glasses Sherry.
1 Dash Orange Bitters.
2 Dashes Pimento Dram Liqueur.
Fill up the shaker with cracked ice, shake and serve with an olive."

name: Bandera
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SHOT GLASS.
POUR tequila to fill first shot glass (caballitos).
POUR lime juice to fill second shot glass.
To make the sangrita to fill the third glass, SHAKE rest of ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into third shot glass.
garnish: Instruct the drinker to sip from each of the three glasses alternatively.
review: In Mexico the quality of the homemade Sangrita can make or break a bar. The Sangrita in this trio is spicy and slightly sweet.
history:
A popular and classic way of serving tequila in Mexico. Bandera is Spanish for the flag, and the Bandera de México is, of course, green, white and red, hence the three glasses: lime juice (green), Blanco tequila (clear or "white") and sangrita (red).

The Bandera is also drunk to celebrate Mexican Independence Day with the green lime juice representing the independence movement and the red sangrita chaser representing the blood of the national heroes. Appropriately, sangrita means 'little blood' in Spanish, and Sangrita is served with tequila in practically every bar in Mexico.

name: Batida de Abacaxi
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: This easy crowd pleaser is creamy, fruity and to taste - only vaguely alcoholic.
history:
The Batida (meaning 'shake') is a traditional Brazilian drink and 'Abacaxi' means pineapple in Portuguese, the official language of Brazil. 

Batida is a broad term for a drink usually containing fresh fruit, sugar and/or sweetened condensed milk (leite condensado). They are often blended with crushed ice or shaken and served over crushed ice.

name: Beachcomber's Daiquiri
instructions: BLEND all ingredients with one 6oz scoop crushed ice and serve with straws.
review: Basically a frozen rum margarita.
history: Created by Ernest Raymond Beaumont-Gantt, A.K.A. Don The Beachcomber at his bar in Hollywood, California, USA.

name: Rum Barrel (by Donn Beach)
ingredients: 60 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill a RUM BARREL MUG OR PINT GLASS.
BLEND all ingredients with 7oz scoop of crushed ice.
Serve with straws.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge, Luxardo Maraschino Cherry and mint sprig.
review: A foamy head hides a tasty (and be warned) rum laced, tangy fruity drink.
history: Adapted from a drink created by Ernest Raymond Beaumont-Gantt, A.K.A. Don The Beachcomber at his bar in Hollywood, California, USA.

name: Bearskin Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered olives.
review: A caraway-influenced Vodkatini.
history: Our Martini cocktail page details its evolution and other common variants.

name: The Beauty Beneath
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Rum-forward with zesty orange and bittersweet herbal notes.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2008 by Jeffrey Morgenthaler at Bel Ami Lounge in Eugene, Oregon and featured on his jeffreymorgenthaler.com. Jeff's original recipe calls for Appleton Estate Signature Blend rum and Vergano 'Americano' chinato.

name: Berry Smash
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Garnish with seasonal berries.
review: Red berries with a splash of apple juice and honey served over crushed ice.

name: Beverly Hills Hotel Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.

garnish: Rosemary sprig.
review: Zingy grapefruit sourness balances rich elderflower in this gin-laced cocktail which you might consider enjoying with your breakfast. That's how they rock in Beverly Hills!
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2007 by Philip Spee at The Beverly Hills Hotel, California, USA.

name: Bitches Brew
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SOUR OR MARTINI/COUPETTE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: This flavoursome flip-style drink comes served with a hint of Caribbean spice.
history: Created in 2008 by Daniel Eun at PDT in New York City, USA.

name: Bitter Lady
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: We have dramatically cut the bitter liqueur and lemon in Mickael's original recipe to make a much lighter drink.
history: Adapted from a drink created by Mickael Perron from Bar Now On.

name: Black Strap
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: This drink benefits from the dilution, which comes with a lengthy stir.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2007 by Neyah White, San Francisco, USA.

name: Bloodhound #1
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
MUDDLE raspberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of skewered raspberries.
review: Looks like fruity disco drink fodder but is actually surprising dry and strong.
history: Unknown but in his 1922 Mixing Cocktails, Harry MacElhone credits the Duke of Manchester,  William Angus Drogo Montagu (1877-1947). The Bloodhound first appears, in a simple form, in William Boothby's 1907 World Drinks,  the, in Tom Bullock's 1917 The Ideal Bartender, and perhaps most notably in in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Blue Eyed Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A blue-rinsed Vodka Martini with a hint of piney gin.

name: Blue Hawaii
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HURRICANE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: Aloha!
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 1957 by Harry K. Yee at Henry Kaiser's Hawaiian Village Hotel (latterly the Hilton) in Waikiki, Oahu, Hawaii. The drink, created at the behest of Bols to aid sales of their blue curaçao, is named after the hit song from the 1937 Bing Crosby film, Waikiki Wedding, and not after what is generously described as a "musical-comedy" Elvis Presley 1961 film of the same name. That said, the Elvis film probably contributed to this cocktail's popularity.

name: Bohemian Mule
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring ginger beer.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Long and refreshing whilst also packing a full shot of absinthe - perhaps the best spirit to hold its own when drowned with fiery ginger beer, a combo used to good effect in this mule with lime juice.
history:
Created in 1990s by Giovanni Burdi, London, England.
Moscow Mule cocktail history

name: Bouquet

EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Rose delicately flavours gin with a sweet and sour balance provided by honey and lemon.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2005 by Alex Pacumbo at Cocoon, London, England.

name: Bronze Adonis
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.

Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.

review: Light and refreshing but with citrus complexity.

name: Byculla

FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Rich cherry liqueurs fortified by gin and bittered with a hint of orange.

name: Cloysters
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: There's a suitable subtle monastic influence to this complex, gin-based, most learned of cocktails.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2007 by John Deragon at PDT bar in New York City, USA. Perhaps named after the Pokémon "Cloyster", this is a gin-heavy riff on the earlier (circa 1970) Cloister cocktail.

name: Clubland

review: Go easy on the bitters, and this is a complex and rewarding Martini-style cocktail.
history: In William James "Billy" Tarling's 1937 Café Royal Bar Book, the creation of this drink is credited to one A. Mackintosh. Originally made with a brand of port called Clubland, hence this drink's name.

name: El Coco

garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: Rum and coconut water with the merest hint of lime.
history: Created in 2008 by Richard Hunt at Mahiki in London, England and named after the coconut palm outside the original Bacardi distillery in Santiago de Cuba.

name: Corpse Reviver No.1 (Gilberg's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.

history: In his book The Joy of Mixology, Gary Regan recommends a version of the Corpse Reviver with 2 oz applejack, ¾ oz cognac and ¾ oz sweet vermouth, which he writes was created in 2001 by Steve Gilberg, publisher of happyhours.com. This may make a cocktail strong enough to awaken the dead, but those wishing to remain compos mentis may prefer this equal parts version I created after being inspired by Steve Gilberg's Corpse Reviver.

name: Cuban Heal

review: Honey-ed and initially sweet with herbal spirity rum notes adding depth.

name: Daiquiri No. 5 (Pink Daiquiri)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.

garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A Daiquiri sweetened with pomegranate syrup and a splash of maraschino. (Classically blended rather than shaken.
history: Created by Constantino "Constante" Ribalaïgua Vert, who owned and bartended at Havana's famous El Floridita from 1918 to 1952. The Daiquiri No. 5, also known as the Pink Daiquiri due to its hue, appears in the 1939 edition of his bar's Cocktails Bar La Florida cocktail menu book.

name: Daiquiri Noir
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE mint in base of shaker (just to bruise).
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with a mint leaf or float the tip of a mint sprig.
review: Honey herbal whiskey liqueur adds complexity to this minty, fresh, aged rum Daiquiri.
history: Discovered in 2008 at Hugos Bar Pizza in Sydney, Australia.

name: Darlington
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Aquamarine blue, this delicately fruity gin-based cocktail is dried by a splash of vermouth and soured by lemon juice.
history: In William James "Billy" Tarling's 1937 Café Royal Cocktail Book (Coronation Edition), the invention of this cocktail is credited to J. B. O'Brien. Made to the proportions specified in Tarling's book, the above recipe would only have 5ml (1/6oz) sugar syrup.

name: Death In The Gulf Stream
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain into glass filled with crushed ice
CHURN (stir) and add more ice until glass is filled.
review: Remembering that Hemingway was a diabetic so had a very sour tooth, we believe this drink needs more than 'one teaspoon' of sugar. Thus we've added half a shot of sugar syrup to the original recipe. It also benefits from dilution, so be sure to shake well and churn in the glass.
history: According to Charles H. Baker's 1946 The Gentleman's Companion, this libation was a favourite of Ernest Hemingway.

name: Dimi-tini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: Like Dimi - this drink's easy to like.
history: Adapted from a recipe by Dimitri Lezinska, London, England.

name: Filthy Dirty Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of  Lemon zest twist and skewered Fragata Green Olives.
review:
The transition from a mere Dirty Martini to a Filthy Dirty Martini isn't so much about more olive brine but the addition of lime juice. This introduces citrus freshness (beyond the lemon zest twist) and sourness. Using Old Tom rather than London Dry Gin helps offset lime's sourness while bolstering botanical notes to stand up to the 'dirty' ingredients.

Unusually for a cocktail with citrus juice, I recommend you stir this cocktail, but if you prefer your Martinis shaken, then you'll need to dramatically cut back on the vermouth, olive brine and lime juice as shaking amplifies their flavours while masking the gin.
history: A variation of a dry martini. Detailed information can be found on our Dry Martini page.

name: Doheny Pisco Punch
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE (just to bruise) mint in base of shaker
Add next five ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with ginger beer
review: Vincenzo's twist on the San Franciscan classic.
history:
Adapted from a drink created in 2007 by Vincenzo Marianello at Doheny in Los Angeles, USA.
Pisco Punch cocktail history

name: Don Daisy

history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2007 by Heiko Tagawa, Germany.

name: Dubonnet Cocktail No.1
ingredients: 45 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh

FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Simple yet complex. Dry and aromatic.
history: A classic cocktail that was popular in Britain during the 1920s and appeared in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Dutch Master

garnish: Garnish with grapes.
review: It's a vodka and tonic but not like you know it.

name: East Village Athletic Club

history: The Last Word, a vintage cocktail classic, was first documented in Ted Saucier's Bottoms Up in 1951 where its creation was attributed to the Detroit Athletic Club. In 2008, the folk at New York's PDT bar tinkered with the recipe to create this drink named after their own hood.

MUDDLE raspberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.

garnish: Prepare garnish of sugar rim and raspberry.
review: Refreshing, fruity, easy drinking.
history:
Created by Ryan Magarian, Seattle, USA.

See our Sidecar cocktail page for the full story behind the origins of the Sidecar, its history and variations.

name: Elixirita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
review: Basically, a Tommy's Margarita with a splash of cognac served straight-up.
history: Created in 2007 by H. Joseph Ehrmann, this is the signature Margarita at his bar Elixir in San Francisco, USA. He dedicated it to the Boston College Eagles.

name: Especie
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
