review: Rum-laced and rust coloured with hints of clove and jasmine. A herbal aperitivo.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2008 by Juraj Ivan at Coq D'Argent in London, England.

name: Eureka Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A punchy, full-flavoured, veritable basket of fruit.
history: Recipe adapted from a 1935 Bar Florida (later renamed Floridita) menu, Havana, Cuba. "Sherry Brandy" in the menu's recipe is presumed to be a typo for cherry brandy.

name: Fizz á la Violette
ingredients: Top up with Soda from siphon
instructions: 1/ Flash BLEND first 6 ingredients without ice (to emulsify mix)
Then pour contents of blender into shaker and SHAKE with ice
Strain into chilled glass (no ice in glass) and TOP with soda from siphon
Alternatively: 2/ SHAKE first 6 ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE (without ice) and strain into chilled glass (no ice)
TOP with soda water from siphon.
review: A delicate floral cocktail that is so creamy and smooth that it's almost fluffy.
history: An adaptation of the Ramos Fizz. In his 1939 The Gentleman's Companion, Charles H. Baker Jr. credits this drinks creation to Ahmed Soliman a manufacturer and seller of Perfume Essences in the Khan el Kalili Bazaar, Cairo.

name: Flame of Love Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
RINSE chilled glass with sherry (pour sherry into glass, swirl to coat inside and discard excess).
SQUEEZE the oils from a flamed orange zest so the burnt oils coat the inside of the sherry-coated glass.
SHAKE vodka with ice.
FINE STRAIN into the sherry and orange oil coated glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist (flamed).
review: Bone dry but fresh and most definitely citrusy.
history:
Created at Chasen's, a legendary Hollywood restaurant that opened in 1936 and was a haunt of movie stars and even royalty until its eventual demise in 1995. The memorabilia that decorated the restaurant was held in storage and in 1997 Maud and Dave Chasen's grandson opened another Chasen's on Beverly Hills' Cañon Drive which sadly failed due to a lack of patrons.

During the original Chasen's heyday, its star-studded clientele enjoyed drinks created by its noted bartender Pepe Ruiz. Of these, the Flame of Love is his most famous creation, partly due to its originally being made for Dean Martin. The legend of this drink is further embellished by Dean dragging his old pal Frank Sinatra to Chasen's to try the drink. The story goes that Frank was so impressed that he ordered one for everyone in the place.
Martini cocktail history

name: French Connection
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: The apricot and almond notes in amaretto combine perfectly with brandy in this simple cocktail.
history: Named after the 1971 American crime thriller film, The French Connection, this brandy-based cocktail is a sibling of the better-known Scotch whisky-based Godfather. Both cocktails, along with the similar vodka-based Godmother, appear in Brian F. Rea's 1976 Brian's Booze Guide.

name: Full Monte
ingredients: Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: SHAKE first eight ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with champagne, lightly stir and serve with straws.
review: Champagne replaces cola and maraschino liqueur replaces triple sec in this sophisticated adaptation of a Long Island Iced Tea.
history: Created by Audrey Saunders at Pegu Club, New York City, USA.

name: Gin Daisy (Jerry Thomas style)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with splash of soda.
garnish: Garnish with skewered berries.
review: Lightly bready genever combines wonderfully with orange liqueur in this balanced classic.
history:
The origins of the Daisy are lost in time, but the first known written reference to the cocktail is in an 1866 novel Gay Life in New York! Or, Fast Men and Grass Widows by Henry Llewellyn Williams. [ref: David Wondrich, Imbibe!, 2007]. However, the author only names the Daisy as one of several drinks one character suggests in preference to ordering a lemonade. "Wouldn't you rather have a concentrated zephyr, in a daisy, or an iced dew drop?"

No description of what a Daisy is, or recipe, has been unearthed before Jerry Thomas' 1876 The Bartenders Guide or How To Mix Drinks: The Bon-Vivants Companion. A second, quite different Daisy recipe then appears in the 2nd (1888) edition of Harry Johnson's Bartenders Manual for a Whiskey Daisy and a Brandy Daisy (no Gin Daisy). Tellingly, both bartenders omit Daisies (be that brandy, whiskey, gin or rum-based) from their first editions.

name: Gin Daisy (modern and tall)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 7 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring soda.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: Fruity, long and refreshing. Gin notes are surprisingly subdued.
history: Adapted from a September 2025 recipe by Al of Al's Cocktail Club. The original calls for homemade 1:1 strength pomegranate juice syrup (grenadine) and acid-adjusted orange juice.

name: Gin Punch No. 2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with seasonal berries.
review: Easy on the palate yet incredibly complex. Linseed oil notes of genever shine through.
history: Adapted from Hebert Asbury's 1928 reprint of Jerry Thomas' 1862 How to Mix Drinks, or The Bon Vivant's Companion.

name: Ginger Snap
instructions: SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice and strain into an ice-filled glass
TOP with ginger ale.
review: This balanced Gin Collins is sweetened by ginger and orange liqueurs.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2007 by Gary "gaz" Regan, New York, USA.

name: Golden Reign
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A lightly creamy, after dinner drink.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2002 by Peter Dorelli at The American Bar at The Savoy Hotel, London, England to celebrate the Queen's golden jubilee.

name: Good Hope Plantation Rum Punch
ingredients: 20 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill a DOUBLE OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with skewered lime slice wheel and maraschino cherry (sail) with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: A classic rum and citrus-laced, generously flavoured, single-serve punch.
history: Originally made at the Good Hope Hotel in Falmouth, Jamaica. The Good Hope is an 18th Century country house set in a 2,000-acre plantation high in the lush landscape of Cockpit Country near Montego Bay.

name: Grand Designs
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE rosemary in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Rosemary sprig.
review: Easy drinking and slightly sweet, dried and made altogether grander by the rosemary.
history: Created in 2008 for Grand Designs Live exhibition by yours truly.

name: Grand Slam
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of strawberry on rim.
review: This after-dinner libation is slightly sweet but wonderfully aromatic.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Grape Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE grapes in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered grapes.
review: Green Chartreuse adds extra complexity to what would otherwise be simply vodka-laced grape juice.

name: Guards
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist (expressed and discarded) and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: Old Guard but this Sweet Martini, which benefits from a splash of orange curaçao, deserves a place on modern cocktail lists.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Stanley M. Jones' 1977 Jones' Complete Barguide.

name: Habanero
instructions: MUDDLE ginger and chilli in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine-strain into chilled glass.
review: A Daiquiri-style drink with honey, ginger and chilli.
history: Created in 2008 by Giuliano Morandin at The Bar at The Dorchester Hotel, Hotel, London, England.

name: Hibiscus Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with hibiscus flower.
review: Hibiscus combines wonderfully with tequila in this flavoursome Margarita.

name: Hoffman House
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A shaken 5:1 Martini with orange bitters.
history:
Considered the 'house' cocktail at Manhattan's Hoffman House in the 1880s, although the 1905 book by its head bartender Charles S. Mahoney, The Hoffman House Bartender's Guide, doesn't include a "Hoffman House Cocktail", raising doubts about the veracity of the 'house cocktail' claim. Particularly as the book does have a "Hoffman House Recipes" section listing a "Hoffman Highball", "Hoffman Highball", Hoffman House Cooler", "Hoffman Morning Bracer", Hoffman House Fizz", "Hoffman House Old Fashioned Whiskey Cocktail" and a "Hoffman House Punch".

Evidence of the 'house' status is cited by barrypopik.com and others from a "Appetizing Drinks" column in 18 September 1892, New York Herald (pg. 14, col. 6)

name: Hula Hula, Houla Houla or Hoola-Hoola
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with Ppneapple wedge on rim speared with a cherry.
review: Gin and juice - orange with a splash of orange liqueur.
history: First published, spelt Houla-Houla, in Robert Vermeire's 1922 Cocktails How to Mix Them with the notation, "This drink originates from Hawaii".

name: Indochine
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of fresh ginger slice.
review: Slightly sweet and very frothy. Easy drinking with a ginger kick.
history: Adapted from a drink promoted at the end of 2007 by the owners of Canton Ginger Liqueur.

name: Jägerita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: This Margarita may look a little like liquid mud but is surprisingly tasty with liquorice-forward herbal notes alongside bright citrus.
history: Origin: Created by David Cordoba and made in September 2008 for yours truly and others to try at my Cabinet Room in London. Jeffrey Morgenthaler, who was among the influential bartenders present that night, took the Jägerita back to Portland, Oregon, where he wrote about that night and David's whacky Margarita on JeffreyMorgenthaler.com. Then, in 2014, he included it on the opening menu of his bar, Pépé Le Moko. Thanks to Morgenthaler's influence, the Jägerita has gone on to become a contemporary classic.

name: Judgement Day
instructions: SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Charles was pretty sour about his experience when he created this very aromatic sour.
history: On 16th May 2008, superstar bartender Charles Vexenat was unjustly jailed in New Orleans during Tales of the Cocktail after his friend Dre dropped a bottle in the Old Absinthe House. Fortunately for Charles he was saved a second day in the slammer by Melanie Asher, owner of Macchu Pisco who bailed them out. This cocktail, created at PDT, New York City, is Charles's tribute to Melanie.

name: Juniport Fizz
ingredients: Top up with Soda from siphon
instructions: SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice and strain into empty chilled glass
TOP with soda from a siphon
review: This straightforward fizz benefits from the unusual combination of gin and port.
history: Created in 2007 by Julian de Feral at Milk & Honey, London, England.

name: Kanu-No
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: The rum's sherry notes are heightened by the addition of port and sherry.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2007 by Mr Ueno at Star Bar, Ginza, Tokyo. Originally served straight-up.

name: The Lady Wears Red
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
FLOAT champagne foam made by macerating orange zests in champagne overnight, adding gelatin and charging with N2O siphon.
review: This 'Lady' is high maintenance but she delivers a complex reward.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2008 by Ben Carlotto at The Voodoo Rooms, Edinburgh, Scotland. The original recipe calls for homemade dry orange syrup made by macerating orange zests in a blend of different sugars with Lillet Blanc and fino sherry. It also calls for homemade citrus bitters, a complex mixture of kaffir lime leaves, various citrus elements, and spices like cardamom and cassia.

name: Lekker Lekker
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of apple slice.
review: Most unusual with apple, hazelnut and lemon freshness.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Tom Lawman at Snafu in Aberdeen. Lekker Lekker (pronounced 'Laker Laker') is Afrikaans for 'very nice', which is what Tom's South African friend exclaimed when he tasted this drink.

name: Lemon Lime and Bitters
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR lime and lemonade into ice-filled glass and briefly stir.
DASH bitters onto surface.
garnish: Serve with a straw so drinker can stir in the bitters.
review: The dose of alcoholic bitters is so tiny compared to the overall volume of this cocktail that this mocktail is probably less alcoholic than most ripe bananas. But equally as tasty.
history: Very popular in its homeland, Australia.

name: Lemon Caipirovska
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
MUDDLE lemon in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with 7oz scoop crushed ice.
POUR into glass without straining.
Serve with straws.
garnish: No garnish to prepare.
review: A lemon-tastic Caipirovska.
history: Created in 2002 by Tony Conigliaro at Isola, London, England.

name: The Leavenworth
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: This great cocktail is dramatically influenced by the style of rum used. Francesco's original recipe calls for English Harbour rum.
history: Created in 2008 by Francesco Lafranconi of Southern Wine & Spirits, USA.

name: The Libertine
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with ginger beer and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Huge flavours delicately combine in this surprisingly approachable drink.
history: Created in 2008 by Joe Parrilli at Bacar, San Francisco, USA.

name: London Scramble
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
DRIZZLE créme de mûre over cocktail. (It will slowly bleed through the cocktail as it sinks.)
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Garnish with skewered blueberries.
review: A tequila Bramble of which I'm sure Dick Bradsell (the legendary bartender behind the original Bramble) would be most approving.
history: Created in 2007 by Dre Masso and Henry Besant, who, back then, were the Worldwide Cocktail Club.

name: The Long Shot
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice and strain ice-filled glass
TOP with soda and briefly stir.
review: A simple variation on William Schmidt's Alabazam.
history: Created in 2007 by Julian de Feral at Milk & Honey, London, England.

name: Lonsdale
instructions: STIR honey syrup with gin until honey dissolves
TEAR basil leaves and add to shaker with all other ingredients
SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Not dry, not sweet, just balanced, long and refreshing.
history: Created by Alexandra Fiot at The Lonsdale, London, England.

name: Love Unit
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE pepper in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with basil leaf and bell pepper slice.
review: Delicate, complex and balanced with enough fruit and veg to make your five-a-day.
history: Created by Ryan Magarian, Seattle, USA.

name: Maçã Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of apple slice and mint sprig.
review: Subtle combination of cachaça, fresh lime, apple juice and elderflower.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2007 by Jamie Terrell, New York, USA while he was working for Sagatiba. Maçã means apple in Portuguese.

name: Maison Charles Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of frost the rim of the glass with powdered sugar and finely chopped mint. Moisten the outside rim of a glass with lime juice and roll in powdered sugar and mint..
review: Reminiscent of concentrated Mojito.
history: Pronounced 'May-Sawn Sharl', this recipe is adapted from David A. Embury's 1948 The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks.

name: Mañana Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of apricot slice.
review: Salmon pink in colour, the Mañana has a subtle and delicate rum laced apricot flavour.
history: Mañana, literally meaning 'tomorrow' in Spanish but more usually used to mean some indefinite time in the future. This recipe is adapted from David A. Embury's 1948 The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks.

name: Manchester Special Rum Punch
ingredients: 50 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill a DOUBLE OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Garnish with grapefruit slice wheel.
review: Fruity punch.
history: Originally made at the Manchester Hotel, Mandeville, Jamaica. The Manchester Hotel lies in a lush tropical setting in the centre of Jamaica high in the cool mountains of Manchester.

name: Margaret Duffy
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Spiced and sweetened cognac.
history: Vintage cocktail of unknown origin.

name: Market Daiquirí
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pear wedge on rim.
review: A classic Daiquiri influenced by the addition of pear, mint and almonds.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2008 by Dez O'Connell, London, England

name: Martinez (with London Dry gin)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review:
This Martinez is aromatic, complex and dry. Our previous recipe included dry vermouth to add balance and complexity:
60mml (20z) Drygin
15ml (½oz) Rosso (sweet vermouth
7.5ml (¼) Dry vermouth
5ml (1 barspoon) Maraschino liqueur
1 dah Aromatic bitters
history:
London dry has been the dominant gin style for over 100 years, so by extension was the gin most frequently used to make a Martinez.
See Martinez cocktail history

name: Martinez (Orange)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Stir well as dilution helps to tame this old-school classic in which bitter orange predominates.
history: See Martinez cocktail history

name: Mayflower Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Fragrant balance of fruits and flowers.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2002 by Wayne Collins in London, England. Wayne was representing Plymouth Gin at the time, so used this in his cocktail. Plymouth Gin, which takes its name from and is made in the port city of Plymouth in Devon, in turn influenced the cocktail's name.

The first permanent English settlers to establish a Colony in New England (America) were pilgrims who, in 1620, set sail from Plymouth on two ships, the Mayflower and the Speedwell. The Speedwell proved unseaworthy, so had to be abandoned, but after an arduous two-month voyage, the Mayflower anchored off the coast of Cape Cod in November 1620. The passengers arrived at what they named Plymouth Harbor on 16th December 1620 and settled there.

name: Le Minuit
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Absinthe based but incredibly subtle. Absinthe combines wonderfully with wine and apple.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2001 by Tony Conigliaro at Isola, Knightsbridge, London, England.

name: Moondream
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with peach wedge on rim.
review: Reminiscent of a delicately peach-flavoured Dry Martini.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2008 by Thomas Waugh at Death & Co. in Manhattan, New York City.

name: Mosquito
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: A short pisco-based Mojito.
history: Adapted from a 2008 recipe created by Hans Hilburg at El Pisquerito in Cuzco, Peru.

name: Naranja
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Rum based twist on the classic Old-Fashioned.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2008 by Chris Edwardes at the Hanbury Club in Brighton, England.

name: Negroni Sbagliato
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice wheel.
review: This lighter-styled Negroni has some bitterness but lacks the gin punch and character of the original Negroni.
history:
Pronounced 'spal-yacht-oh' or perhaps more correctly 'sbal .. ee .. ah .. toe' [thanks, MIke], which in Italian means "bungled" or "mistaken", this drink was created in the late 1980s by Mirko Stocchetti at his Bar Basso in Milan when making a Negroni he apparently mistakenly reached for a bottle of spumante instead of gin. Unlikely as the mistake might sound, it proved inspired and Negroni Sbagliato cocktails are still served at Bar Basso today with spumante (not prosecco). Sadly, Mirko passed away on the 10th of November 2016, but his family continue to run Bar Basso as a living museum of Milanese bar culture.

If you visit Bar Basso (and you should), ask for your Sbagliato to be served in one of the enormous stemmed chalice glasses. Your cocktail may appear daunting but a giant rectangular ice cube will occupy much of the large glass. Mirko designed this chalice himself back in the 1980s and licensed the design to glassmakers WMF, hence you'll also see the same glasses used in many of Milan's other older bars.

What has long been a cocktail known amongst bartenders became a social media sensation in 2022 thanks to a TikTok video posted by HBO to promote their House of the Dragon series with actor Olivia Cooke asking co-star Emma D'Arcy, "What's your drink of choice?" The response, "A Negroni Sbagliato...with prosecco in it."

Being pedantic, a Negroni Sbagliato is properly made with spumante and not prosecco. This is confused by spumante being the Italian term for sparkling wine and prosecco of course being a distinct style of sparkling wine. However, spumante is also a style of sparkling wine, the most famous spumante being Asti Spumante from Italy's Piemonte region. Spumante tends to be sweeter than Prosecco and lower in alcohol strength, 6% to 10.5% alc./vol versus 9% to 11% alc./vol.

Negroni cocktail history

name: Negroski
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
STIR.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: A Negroni where vodka is substituted for gin.

name: Northern Sun
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
MUDDLE ginger in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pear wedge.
review: Subtle and easy. Hard to believe this drink contains two measures of rum.
history: Recipe adapted 2008 from LCBO Magazine and comes courtesy of Sean Murray of Aurora, Ontario, Canada.

name: Noshino Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of cucumber slices.
review: If good quality sake and shochu are used then this can be a great drink. However, it lacks the alcoholic bite I associate with a true Martini, so I much prefer this drink when vodka is used in place of shochu.
history: Adapted from a drink discovered in 2007 at Shochu Lounge, London, England.

name: Nuez Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of walnut.
review: An aged rum Daiquiri with nutty notes.
history: Named after the Spanish for walnut.

name: Oceans 21
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail, then use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Apple, cranberry and orange harmoniously presented.

name: Oooh Ginger
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered crystallised ginger.
review: Subtle ginger spice flavours this dry tequila martini-esque cocktail.
history: Created in March 2008 by Dick Bradsell and yours truly at The Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Orange Spur
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with star anise floated on surface.
review: Stir well - this bittersweet drink benefits from dilution.
history: Created in 2008 by Don Lee at PDT, New York City, USA.

name: Original Sin
instructions: POUR absinthe into ice-filled glass and top with water
Leave the mixture to stand in the glass
Separately, STIR all ingredients with ice
Finally discard contents of absinthe-coated glass and fine strain contents of shaker into absinthe washed glass
review: Sake and vodka with a delicate hint of honey.
history: Created in 2009 by Spike Marchant and yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Pablo Alvarez de Cañas Special
instructions: Select a COUPE GLASS.
REGAL SHAKE all ingredients (including lemon zest).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered fruit.
review: Sherry is most prevalent on the palate, but all the other ingredients, including the lemon zest, also contribute to this subtle, balanced and altogether a most unusual cocktail.
history: Recipe adapted from 1937 Bar Florida (later renamed Floridita) menu, Havana, Cuba.

name: Papa Bear
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Delicately spiced and honeyed cognac.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2008 by Tim Homewood at Dirty Martini Bar in London, England. Named after an old family friend who passed away. He was known to all of his children as 'daddy bear' so Papa Bear seemed appropriate.

name: Patrick Gavin Duffy's Punch
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of mint sprigs bouquet.
review: A full three ounces of alcohol per serve means this sure packs a tasty punch.
history:
Adapted from the following recipe in Patrick Gavin Duffy's 1934 The Official Mixer's Manual:

"Patrick Gavin Duffy's Punch
Juice of 1 rich Orange
1 Drink Brandy
½ Teaspoon Powdered sugar
½ Glass Benedictine
Shake in ice and pour all into tall glass, decorate with Mint and Fruit in season.
Drink through straw."

name: Perfect Lady
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with pear wedge on rim.
review: This White Lady riff uses peach liqueur in place of triple sec to make a lighter, fruitier elaboration.
history:
Created in 1936 by Sidney Cox, a bartender at the Grosvenor House, London, for the British Empire Cocktail Competition, where it took the 1st Prize. That same year, a constitutional crisis in the British Empire was caused by King-Emperor Edward VIII's proposal to marry Wallis Simpson, a twice-divorced American socialite. Religious, legal, political, and moral objections were raised because Mrs Simpson was twice divorced, and the marriage was opposed by the King's governments in the United Kingdom and the autonomous Dominions of the British Commonwealth. Despite the opposition, Edward declared his love for Mrs Simpson, and his refusal to give her up led to his abdication in December 1936.

He was succeeded by his brother Albert, who took the regal name George VI. Disgraced due to renouncing the throne, Edward was given the title His Royal Highness, the Duke of Windsor. He married Mrs Simpson the following year, and they remained married until his death 35 years later. She was obviously his 'Perfect Lady'.

name: Pineapple Fix
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge.
review: Rum and pineapple are a match made in heaven, here with lemon adding citrus freshness.

name: Port Flip
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: Old-school and something of a meal in a glass. Well, more of a tasty snack!
history: The "Port Wine Flip" first appears in Jerry Thomas' 1887 Bartender's Guide: How to Mix Drinks, or The Bon Vivant's Companion.

name: Pre Siesta
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Orange liqueurs and bitters flavour this tequila-based, salmon-pink bittersweet cocktail that is perhaps better-served on-the-rocks.
history: Created in 2007 by Rich Hunt at Mahiki in London, England.

name: Quince Mustard Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime wedge and cracked black pepper.
review: A wonderfully quince influenced Margarita.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Ryan Magarian in Seattle, USA.

name: The Quingenti
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Cognac with apple notes and a touch of herbal complexity by way of dry vermouth.
history: Created in 2008 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England, originally to celebrate the Future 500 initiative - quingenti is Latin for five-hundred.

name: RAC Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express orange zest twist over cocktail and discard. Garnish with maraschino cherry.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: A one-to-one Perfect Martini with extra grenadine sweetness and orange bitters adding complexity.
history:
The house cocktail at the Royal Automobile Club in London's Pall Mall. King Edward VII awarded this private members' club its royal title in 1907.

Adapted from a recipe in the 1933 The Green Cocktail Book written by the mysterious" Jimmy".

name: Richmond Gimlet
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf.
review: A properly grown-up Gimlet bearing a striking resemblance to a Southside.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2008 by Jeffrey Morgenthaler at Bel Ami Lounge, Oregon, USA.

name: Santiago al Anochecer
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.

name: South Side Fizz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FIZZ OR HIGHBALL (8OZ TO 10OZ) GLASS.
SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass. (No ice in the glass.)
TOP with soda and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: A minty Collins.
history:
Bar legend says the Southside (or South Side) was created during Prohibition, either at a New York City speakeasy called Jack & Charlie's, or at Manhattan's Stork Club, or by Chicago's Southside mobsters to make their bootleg liquor more palatable, while on the other side of town hoodlums enjoyed the Northside (gin and ginger ale).

The truth is more mundane and the Southside evolved from the Mint Julep at Snedecor's Tavern, Long Island in the 1890s when the style of drink known as a Fizz was at the height of its popularity. The club later became the Southside Sportsman's Club. This cocktail is also closely associated with New York's Twenty-One Club. 

The South Side Fizz features in Hugo Ensslin's 1916 Recipes for Mixed Drinks but over the decades has morphed into the South Side Rickey served over cracked ice and more recently to the Southside served straight-up without soda.

review: Unusually combining gin and cognac, lengthened and spiced with ginger beer, this vintage classic was originally designed as a hangover cure.
history: Created in 1942 by Joe Scialom at the Shepheard's Hotel, Cairo, Egypt.

The origins of this Daiquiri, with rosso vermouth and crème de cacao, are unknown.

Early origins of this database saw this cocktail titled "Daiquiri No.2 (El Floridita style)" but is quite different to the Daiquiri No. 2 created by Constantino "Constante" Ribalaïgua Vert, who owned Havana's famous El Floridita from 1918 to 1952. Hence, in June 2025, I retitled this cocktail Chocolate Presidente after the classic El Presidente, the best-known rum-based cocktail with vermouth, lime and grenadine.

See: Daiquiri cocktail history

name: Grasshopper
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf or tip of a mint sprig floated on the surface.
review: It's hard not to like this creamy slightly sweet minty after-dinner treat.
history:
The Grasshopper is the signature cocktail at Tujague's, the second oldest restaurant in New Orleans, which was opened in 1856 by Guillaume Tujague. Guillaume's sister took over the running of the restaurant when he died in 1912 but a few years later, after her husband's death, she sold Tujague's to Jean-Dominic Castet and his business partner, Philip Guichet, Sr..

Legend has it, in 1919 (or some say during Prohibition in the 1920s), that Philibert Guichet won second prize in a prestigious New York cocktail competition for his Grasshopper cocktail. Sadly, there is no documentary evidence to support this win or his creation of the cocktail.

A recipe for a drink called the Grasshopper, consisting of equal parts crème de menthe layered over crème de cacao appears in William "Cocktail Bill" Boothby's 1908 World's Drinks and How to Mix Them where its creation is attributed to "Harry O'Brien, late of the Palace Hotel, San Francisco."  

At some point (perhaps even by Philibert Guichet at Tujague's) cream is added as a third ingredient to the 1908 two-ingredient layered Grasshopper to make the shaken cocktail we recognise today.

Several newspapers from the 1950s point to the modern-day Grasshopper's creation being in the late 1940s in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, USA, including a piece in the Sunday 19th February 1950 Monroe Morning World (Monroe, Louisiana, USA).

SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: An elderflower-flavoured Sidecar.
history:
Created in 2006 by yours truly.
Sidecar cocktail history

name: Pina Colada Cocktail (Cuban Style)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS OR PINEAPPLE SHELL GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: This Colada has no coconut, but it is smooth, balanced and rather tasty.
history: Basically a pineapple-flavoured Daiquiri. This recipe is based on a piece in the December 1922 edition of Travel Magazine, the first known written reference to a Piña Colada, and is what we now refer to as a 'Cuban Style Piña Colada' - one without coconut.

name: Pink Squirrel
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg and finish with a line of creole bitters across the surface.
review: Light pink, sweet and creamy with subtle notes of chocolate, toasted almond and pomegranate.
history:
The Pink Squirrel was created in 1941 by Bryant Sharp at Bryant's Cocktail Lounge in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, USA. Apparently, the original recipe called for ice cream rather than cream and indeed the Pink Squirrel may have started life as a milkshake spiked with spirits prior to Repeal. Considering it unnecessary, in the 1960s, Bryant's second owner, Pat Malmberg removed the crème de cacao from the original recipe and this is how Bryant's make their Pink Squirrels to this day.

We're indebted to Michael Lazar (AKA Mr. Manhattan) and his Stirred Not Shaken for researching the origin of this cocktail, and there's more detail on Bryant's and the Pink Squirrel on his blog.

name: Pomme et Sureau
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: POUR first 2 ingredients into ice-filled glass, TOP with soda and lightly stir.
review: Light, long and refreshing apple and elderflower.
history: Created in 2006 by yours truly. The name is French for 'apple and elderflower'.

name: Rizzo
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of apple slice.
review: The tangy, sharp grapefruit reveals hints of apple spirit smoothed by grenadine.
history: Created in 2006 by Gregor de Gruyther at Ronnie Scott's in London, England, and named for Betty Rizzo, the leader of the Pink Ladies in the film Grease.

name: Scorched Earth
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Dry, very aromatic and bordering on bitter. Interesting but not to everybody's taste. The lemon zest twist makes this cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2006 by Nicholas Hearin at Restaurant Eugene in Atlanta, USA, and first published by Gary "gaz" Regan.

name: Screaming Orgasm
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HURRICANE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Dust with cocoa powder.
review: As fattening as it is alcoholic, this indugent, creamy dessert in a glass cocktail is served with generous innuendo.
history: One of the best-known cocktails from the 1980s.

name: Serendipity (by Colin Field)
instructions: SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with sparkling wine.
review: Spirity, minty apple invigorated by a splash of champagne.
history: Our adaptation of Colin Field's signature drink. Collin first served this drink on 31 December 1994 at Bar Hemingway Bar in the Paris Ritz Hotel for Jean-Louis Constanza. Upon tasting it, Jean-Louis exclaimed, "Serendipity!"

name: Sidecarriage
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Hints of cider and elderflower come through in this calvados-based Sidecar.
history:
Created in 2006 by yours truly in London, England.
Sidecar history

name: Strawberry and Balsamic Fruitini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE strawberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with strawberry.
review: Balsamic adds extra bite to vodka-laced strawberries.
history: Adapted from a recipe created circa 2001 at Che Bar, St James's Street, London, and originally named Strawberry & Balsamic Martini. This cocktail was popular in London bars during 2002, then made with sugar syrup (in place of strawberry syrup).

name: Valentino
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A variation on the Negroni. More gin and less liqueur and vermouth make for an unusual bittersweet Martini.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in February 1999 by Gary "gaz" Regan and published in his 2001 book, The Joy of Mixology.

name: Wah-Wah
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Bittersweet and complex with hints of elderflower and grapefruit.
history: Created in 2007 at Range in San Francisco, USA.

name: Wash House
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE basil (just to bruise) in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with basil leaf boat floated on cocktail.
review: Delicately herbal - simple but refreshing.
history: Adapted from a recipe by Neyah White at Nopa in San Francisco, California. The building that houses Nopa was once a laundry, hence the name.

name: Waters of Chaos
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime zest coin.
review: Robust flavours of overproof rum and Chartreuse combine with Dutch genever in this Last Word-like cocktail.
history: Created by yours truly and named after the words of the track Argha Noah by Nightmares on Wax.

name: BBC (by Marian Beke)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with apple fan or apple slice.
review: Based on the classic B & B cocktail with rum replacing brandy and Becherovka in place of Bénédictine.
history: Created in 2009 by Marian Beke at the Artesian Bar, London, England. The Artesian Bar sits in the Langham Hotel opposite the BBC's Broadcasting House. Indeed, the BBC moved into the Langham after the Broadcasting House was bombed during the Second World War ... they stayed almost 20 years.

name: Berlin Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SOUR OR MARTINI/COUPETTE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered lemon slice wheel and maraschino cherry (sail).
review: The sweet powerful flavours of kummel and maraschino liqueurs are tamed and harnessed in this sour.
history: Created in 2009 by Thorsten Pannek at Hilton, Frankfurt, Germany.

name: The Bird is the Word No. 1
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: To quote Fraser Campbell, the drinks creator, "influenced primarily by "The Last Word" with hints of "Yellow Parrot ...and a tequila base."
history:
Adapted from a drink created in May 2010 by Fraser Campbell at Tony Starrs Kitten Club, Melbourne, Australia.
Last Word history

name: Black Tie
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with physalis (cape gooseberry) on rim.
review: Depending on the sweetness of your molasses, you may not need to add sugar syrup.
history: Adapted from a drink created by Mark Lynch at Green 19 in Dublin, Ireland.

name: Breakfast Gimlet
instructions: STIR marmalade with gin in base of shaker to dissolve marmalade
Add lemon juice and lime cordial, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Orange marmalade justifies the 'breakfast' prefix and adds complexity to this gimlet.
history: A mashup of the classic Gimlet and Salvatore Calabrese's Breakfast Martini. Created by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Fibber McGee
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: Delicately flavoured, bittersweet grapefruit and sweet vermouth fortified with gin.
history: Adapted from a recipe in David A. Embury's 1948 The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks.

name: A. J.
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with cinnamon powder.
review: Amazingly simple and beautifully balanced.
history: The initials in the name stand for 'applejack', the American style of apple brandy this drink was originally based upon.

name: Hot Tommy
instructions: Place spoon in glass
POUR first 3 ingredients into glass
TOP up with boiling water and STIR until agave nectar dissolves.
review: A Mexican twist on the original winter pick-me-up.
history: Created in December 2009 by Alan Brown of Menzel's Bar, who says of this drink, "Over the busy festive period, feeling full of flu and cold I decided to make a hot toddy to soothe my symptoms. Having no honey to hand I tried using agave nectar instead. This led me to the idea of using tequila and lime instead of lemon (to complement the tequila) with the agave and boiling water. The name for the drink is a Hot Tommy, a nod to the Tommy's Margarita, which makes use of both tequila and agave."

name: Limey Gimlet
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge on rim.
review: The two naval spirits sit harmoniously together in this delicately flavoured Gimlet.
history: Created in 2010 by yours truly and named due to the Gimlet's Navy origins and British sailors being nicknamed 'limeys', thanks to mixing their daily rum ration of with lime or lemon juice and sugar to help prevent scurvy. The mixture was known as Grog.

name: Lincoln Club Cooler
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Basically 'a rum and ginger ale Highball' but Lincoln Club Cooler has more of a ring to it.
history: Adapted from a recipe originally published in George J. Kappeler's 1895 Modern American Drinks. Kappeler's original recipe called for St. Croix rum.

name: Mock Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of salt rim and lime wedge.
review: While this is a long way from a fine 100% agave tequila based Margarita, the flavour is surprisingly reminiscent of an alcoholic Margarita.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2010 by Cheri Loughlin AKA The Intoxicologist, USA.

name: New Amsterdam
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A delicately flavoured and lightly balanced genever based drink with hints of kirsch and aromatic Creole bitters.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2006 by Jim Meehan at PDT, New York City, USA. This cocktail features in Jim's 2011 The PDT Cocktail Book.

name: Orange Negroni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.

STIR.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Orange and bitter liqueur combine well, especially when supported by sweet vermouth.
history: Negroni cocktail history and recipe variations

name: Oranjey Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Dry, and yes, very orangey with bittersweet orange complexity.

name: Perpetual Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Delicate berry fruit and chocolate influences herbal vinous vermouth.
history:
Recipe adapted from Hugo R. Ensslin's 1917 book Recipes for Mixed Drinks (pape 26):
"½ Italian Vermouth
½ French Vermouth
2 dashes Crème de Cacao
4 dashes Crème Yvette
Shake well in a mixing glass with cracked ice, strain and serve."

name: Petruchio Cocktail

DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
review: Gin-based and bittersweet, this bright orange drink has a foamy head, which may mislead some into thinking it is sweet and easy.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2010 by Jamie Boudreau at his Vessel bar in Seattle, USA.

name: Rhode Island Red
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring ginger beer.

garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon and lime zest twists.
review: Tequila with a hint of berry fruit, slightly sweetened with agave nectar, spiced and made long with ginger beer.
history: Created in 2009 by Vincenzo Marianella at Copa d'Oro, Santa Monica, USA.

name: A Sinking Cocktail

garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist and strawberry.
review: Copper red and bitter sweet with wine, elderflower and a touch of cherry liqueur.

name: Sketcher's Pet
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: A sweet creamy, fruity dessert style cocktail.
history: Created in January 2010 by Zdenek Kestanek at Quo Vadis, London, England for Petek Sketcher.

name: Stratosphere
ingredients: Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: POUR liqueur into glass and TOP with champagne.
review: A sophisticated Kir Royal.
history: Recipe adapted from The Stork Club Bar Book published 1946 in which Lucius Beebe writes, "Leo Spitzel, captain, asserts that a Stratosphere Cocktail will do wonders for you."

name: Sweetie Pie
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of apple wedge .
review: Bittersweet and most intriguing.
history: Adapted from a drink created by Lydia Reissmueller at Eletaria, New York City, USA.

name: Tanglefoot
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.

review: Light rum, lemon and orange juice served short with a tang of Swedish punch.

name: Too Ache Sidecar
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Tuaca replaces Cointreau in this twist on the Sidecar.
history:
Created in 2009 by yours truly (Simon D) at the Cabinet Room, London, England at the insistence of Charlotte Ashburner, UK Tuaca Brand Manager.

See our Sidecar cocktail page for the full story behind the origins of the Sidecar, its history and variations.

name: Unicar
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A mismade but fabulously balanced Sidecar inspired this drink.
history: Inspired by a drink discovered in May 2010 at Babel Bar, Hotel du Rome, Berlin, Germany. Named after a combination of 'Sidecar' and 'Ugni-blanc', the grape from which cognac is most commonly made.

name: Waterloo Sunset

STIR first 2 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
ADD raspberry liqueur.
garnish: Garnish with skewered raspberries.
review: Fruity, pink fizz.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2008 by Dan Warner, then the Beefeater Global Ambassador, hence originally made using said gin.

name: Absinthe Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of star anise floated on surface.
review: A classic Gin Martini made aniseed fresh with a splash of absinthe. Like me, you may prefer the bite-sized version of this cocktail served straight-up in a Nick & Nora glass with half measures: 75ml (1.25oz) dry gin,  15ml (.5oz) dry vermouth, and 2.5ml (half spoon) absinthe.

name: Applejack Rabbit (served long)
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled jar.
review: Apple brandy sweetened by maple syrup, soured by lemon and lengthened with orange.
history: This on-the-rocks and jarred version of The Savoy Cocktail Book classic Apple Jack Rabbit is adapted from a serve created in 2009 by Dino Koletsas at Bourne & Hollingsworth in London, England.

name: Le Dijonnais Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.

FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of cocktail onion.
review: A most unusual cocktail based on a most unusual product - mustard liqueur.
history: Created by Gonçalo de Sousa Monteiro, Berlin, Germany.

name: Fiendtini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of gherkin.
review: A gin Martini 'dirtied' with pickled gherkin brine and garnished with a pickled gherkin.

name: Mantini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with bacon wrapped olive.
review: A gin Martini-style drink made with beer in place of vermouth.

name: Skid Row
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.

EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail, then use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Complex, spirit-forward, and bittersweet with apricot liqueur and amaro laced with  Dutch genever.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2009 by Eric Alperin at The Varnish in Los Angeles, USA.

name: La Bicyclette
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A light and fragrant Sweet Martini style drink with elderflower liqueur and peach bitters.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2008 by Jamie Boudreau, Seattle, USA.

name: Russian Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Sweet brandy meets dry cherry eau-de-vie in this serious vodka-laced cocktail. Contemporary bartenders may prefer to stir this cocktail but it's classically shaken and all the better for it.
history: Believed to be the first vodka cocktail to appear in print, this first appears in a 1911 book, Beverages de Luxe, published in Louisville, USA. It is thought to have originated at the St. Charles Hotel in New Orleans, USA.

name: Rusty Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Rich herbal, honeyed notes add a Scottish dimension to the classic Cuban cocktail.
history: Created in December 2010 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Rusty Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: The rich honeyed notes from Drambuie combine harmoniously with tequila and add an extra dimension to this tasty Margarita. This cocktail is perhaps at its best once the ice has melted to add a little extra dilution.
history:
Created in December 2010 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London.
More  Margarita variations and the history of the Margarita

name: Blue Moon Cocktail (Dale DeGroff's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE blueberries in base of shaker (just to break skin).
ADD vodka and one heaped spoon of oolong tea and stir in base of shaker for 60 seconds (or better still, leave to steep for ten minutes).
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with sage leaf and skewered blueberry.
review: A hint of blueberry fruit with oolong tea, elderflower, sage and lemon.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2010 by Dale DeGroff, New York, USA. Blue moon is an astronomical term for the second of two full moons to occur in the same calendar month.

name: Caribeño
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A Daiquiri that is light on lime and elongated with coconut water. Delicious and very refreshing.
history: Created in 2009 by Martin Cate at Forbidden Island in Alameda, California.

name: Charlie Chaplin Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with apricot wedge or dried apricot half on rim.
review: Dilution is key to this fruity sweet 'n' sour cocktail, hence,  depending on your ice, the Charlie Chaplin may benefit from the addition of a splash of water prior to shaking.
history: Created and originally served at New York's old Waldorf-Astoria prior to 1920. This recipe is adapted from Albert Stevens Crockett's 1935 The Old Waldorf-Astoria Bar Book. The drink is named after Sir Charles Spencer 'Charlie' Chaplin (1889-1977), the English slapstick comic actor of the silent film era, who was at the height of his career when this eponymous cocktail was created.

name: Chocolate Stinger
instructions: STIR all ingredients with ice and strain into glass filled with crushed ice.
review: Chocolate and mint served frappé.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2010 by Klaus St. Rainer at Schumann's Bar, Munich, Germany.

name: Comet Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: This riff on the Sidecar is lighter, less tart, and altogether more approachable than the original, but some may find it lacks the original's cleansing zesty bite.
history:
Created in 1952 by Eddie "King Cocktail" Clarke at the Albany Club, Albermarle Street, London, England. Made to honour the first-ever jet aircraft to fly a commercial route. The de Havilland DH 106 Comet was developed and manufactured by de Havilland at its Hatfield Aerodrome, Hertfordshire, England. The Comet 1 prototype, registered G-ALYP ("Yoke Peter") first flew on 27th July 1949. This same aircraft, on 2nd May 1952 took off from London bound for Johannesburg on a scheduled BOAC service as the world's first jetliner flight with fare-paying passengers.

Eddie Clarke's original recipe called for Van der Hum, a South African orange liqueur, undoubtedly selected by Eddie due to the destination of that inaugural flight. The use of equal parts triple sec and Drambuie is an accepted substitution but leaves this cocktail on the bitter side, hence I've added a spoon of sugar syrup.

name: Corpse Reviver No.Blue
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Considering it's blue, "I quite like it" was the general consensus when we first tried this infamous cocktail. It tastes great when presented with a Jacob Briars' smile accompanied by his booming voice declaring, "I believe you ordered a blue drink."
history: Created in 2007 by Jacob Briars, Queenstown, New Zealand. Around the time a Facebook group started called "A Jihad on all Blue Drinks." This was Jacob's reaction to it. He "blued' the most popular bartenders' drink" at that time, a Corpse Reviver No.2.

name: Creole Gimlet
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: Falernum adds a touch of Caribbean spice to this Gimlet.
history: Created in 2009 by Gonçalo de Sousa Monteiro, Berlin, Germany.

name: Death in Venice
instructions: Select a FLUTE GLASS.
POUR first 2 ingredients into chilled glass.
TOP with prosecco.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: On the bitter side of bittersweet, this is something of a cross between the Italian Spritz and Ernest Hemingway's Death in the Afternoon.
history: Created in 2010 by Tony Conigliaro at 69 Colebrooke Row, London, England.

name: Devil's Horn
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE (just press) chilli pepper in base of shaker to release its flavour. (If you muddle hard then the finished cocktail will be overly spicy)
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with chillies on rim to form devil's horns.
review: A great balance of sweetness, sourness and spice.
history: Created in 2010 by Roger Vilalta at Banker's Bar, in the Mandarin Oriental Hotel, Barcelona, Spain.

name: Dutch Word
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge on rim.
review: A genever-based variation to the classic Last Word cocktail.
history:
Created in December 2010 by yours truly at The Cabinet Room, London, England.
Last Word history

name: Entwistle's Error
instructions: SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with tonic water.
review: Long and refreshing with rum character, lemon freshness and tonic water's slightly bitter bite.

name: Fandango
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf or mint sprig pegged to rim.
review: A heady mix of gin, vodka and the distinctive herbal flavour of Chartreuse.
history: Adapted from a recipe in a book published in Paris in 1929 titled Cocktails de Paris Presentés Par RIP which credits Georges Robineau from California who won the Honorary Cup at the Professional Bartenders' Championship held in Paris on 2nd February 1929 with this cocktail.

name: Fur Collar
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Apricot brandy adds fruity zingyness to what otherwise would be a Screwdriver served straight-up.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Ted Saucier's 1951 Bottoms Up book.

name: Woxum
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: The flavour of Chartreuse shines through in this slightly sweet, spirituous cocktail. Historically served straight-up but we prefer on-the-rocks in an old-fashioned glass.
history: Recipe is adapted from Albert Stevens Crockett's 1935 The Old Waldorf-Astoria Bar Book.

name: Woodside
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE pineapple in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Delicately fruity, light and fluffy.
history: Created in 2009 by Darren Thrower at The Kenilworth, Warwickshire, England and named after Count Basie's "Jumpin' at the Woodside".

name: Voodoo I Do
instructions: MUDDLE pineapple in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass.
review: Pineapple, tequila, orange and apricot with a touch of sour lime all presented in a harmonious Tiki-style concoction.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2011 by Michael Layer at the Chapel Bar in Berlin, Germany.

name: Very Rusty Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Honey and spice notes from honeyed herbal liqueur add interest to this Aged Daiquiri.
history: Created in December 2010 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Up In The Air
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist (discarded) and float (dehydrated) apple slice or wedge on rim.
review: Sweet and sour with apple, almond and zesty lemon fortified with vodka.
history: Adapted from a drink discovered in 2011 at the Chapel Bar in Berlin, Germany.

name: Tropic Thunder
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of dust with freshly ground black pepper.
review: Gin, peach, lemon juice and pineapple juice may not seem the most obvious combination but it's a combo that works.
history: Created by Sean Henry at Epernay Champagne Bar, Leeds, England and to quote Sean is "named Tropic Thunder as it aptly describes what to expect on the palate."

name: Treacle No.2
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR half the rum with two ice cubes in a chilled glass.
Add PX sherry, sugar syrup, bitters, and two more ice cubes.
STIR some more and add another two ice cubes and the rest of the rum.
STIR some more and add more ice.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Made to appeal to drinkers who want a cocktail that tastes like Treacle. And it does.
history: Adapted from a 2011 rework of Dick Bradsell's original Treacle by Fraser Campbell at The Alchemist Bar in Melbourne, Australia.

name: Stone Place
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: Rum loves fruit, and this rust-pink cocktail has plenty of it.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2010 by Willy Shine at Forty-Four in New York City, USA. This twist on the Ward 8 is named after the street where Shine's parents, who were big Ward 8 imbibers, used to live.

name: Staffordshire Delight
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: A modern day pick-me-up / hair-of-the-dog with a rich, enlivening bittersweet bite.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2010 by Thomas Dalloway, United Kingdom.

name: The Dunaway
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A dry sherry-rich aperitivo cocktail.
history: Created in 2010 by Misty Kalkofen at Drink in Boston, USA.

name: The Matinee
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with  kaffir lime leaf.
review: Created in 2010 by Metinee Kongsrivilai at The Bon Vivant in Edinburgh, Scotland.
history: Kaffir lime leaves add zest to this vanilla-infused Rum Sour with added vermouth complexity.

name: Maya Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pink river salt rim and lime wedge.
review: A tasty Margarita with Grand Marnier, Agave Sec liqueur and orange juice.
history: Created in 2010 by Rob Jameson at Maya Bar, Shanghai, China.

name: Prince of Wales Punch
instructions: MUDDLE pineapple in base of shaker
Add next five ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
Float the port on drink.
review: Fruity yet complex with the oak maturation of both spirits and liqueurs adding depth of flavour.
history: Adapted from Jerry Thomas' 1862 The Bar-Tender's Guide (How to Mix Drinks, The Bon-Vivant's Companion).

name: Grand 'O'
instructions: POUR soda water into ice-filled glass to half fill
ADD lemon juice, orange juice and Grand Marnier
TOP with soda.
review: A fruity, zesty, light summery cocktail. Tip - pouring the soda first makes this refreshingly juicy drink easier to mix due to the specific gravities of the ingredients.

name: Grand Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice wheel.
review: The rich cognac and orange flavours of the liqueur shine in this balanced and flavoursome sour.

name: Hey Hey
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Sidecar-like, with a good balance between sweet (triple sec) and sour (lemon juice), fortified by brandy and mellowed by aromatised wine.  Stanley Jones' 1977 recipe calls for equal parts (¾oz a piece), but I prefer a tad more brandy and a scant spoon of sugar. I also added saline and a splash more dilution.
history: The Hey Hey appears in the 1933 book Hollywood Cocktails and, a year later, in William T. Boothby's World Drinks And How To Mix Them. Exactly the same recipe also appears in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book and R. de Fleury's 1934 book 1700 Cocktails for the Man Behind the Bar. Indeed, this same recipe is repeated three times in both books, but under three different names: The Frank Sullivan, Hoop La! and Odd McIntyre. Hey Hey is this cocktail's fourth alias.

name: Italian Milk Punch
instructions: Select a SNIFTER/BRANDY BALLOON GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: The distinctive flavour of moscato grappa shines through in this milky drink, with amaretto adding rich almond notes and Galliano a touch of peppermint and aniseed.
history: A riff on the classic Milk Punch created in 2011 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Merchant of Venice
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: The richness and flavour of honey along with bittersweet aperitivo liqueur jointly balance lemon citrus notes in this majestic vodka-based cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe discovered in 2010 at Eclipse Bar in Barcelona, Spain.

name: The Mulberry
instructions: STIR jam and gin in base of shaker to dissolve jam
Add mint, Pimm's and lemon juice, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with elderflower pressé.
review: Jammy mulberry fruit, Pimm's and gin freshened with mint and lemon, served on the rocks.
history: Created in 2006 at The Manor House Hotel, Moreton-in-Marsh, Gloucestershire, England. The Manor House Hotel make their mulberry jam using fruit from the Mulberry Tree which grows in the hotel's grounds and is thought to be over 350 years old.

name: Nelson's Blood Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: This carpet scary, appropriately named, blood red cocktail balances the rich fruitiness of port with the fortifying complexity of cognac. A popular drink in classic British pubs.
history:
After the Battle of Trafalgar (21 October 1805) when the British Royal Navy demonstrated its navy supremacy by conquering the combined fleets of the French and Spanish Navies the Franco-Spanish fleet lost twenty-two ships without a single British vessel being lost. However, the English lost Admiral Lord Nelson who was mortally wounded during the battle aboard HMS Victory.

To preserve Nelson's body during the return voyage to England, the ship's surgeon ordered that his body be placed in a barrel of French brandy. Although this was lashed to the deck and guarded, stories abound of sailors drinking the brandy out of respect for their commander, Nelson.

name: Netherland Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Cognac and orange curaçao are natural partners, here smoothed with egg white and sharpened with a dash of orange bitters. According to tastes, you may want to reduce or omit the sugar syrup, particularly if you are using a particularly sweet orange curaçao liqueur.
history: Created in the early 1900s as the house cocktail at the New Netherland Hotel, on Fifth Avenue (at 59th Street) New York City, which was for a period the tallest hotel in the world.

name: Noblesse
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A light, sweet and sour, herbal and fruity aperitif cocktail.
history: Recipe adapted from Gary "gaz" Regan's Ardent Spirits November 2010.

name: Papa Ghirardelli
instructions: SHAKE first five ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda.
review: This cocktail is named after the founder of Ghirardelli Chocolate Company, Domingo Ghirardelli, an Italian-born man who moved to Peru and ran a successful confectionery business and, following the gold rush, ultimately ended up in San Francisco. He went on to found Ghirardely & Girard (later changed to the Ghirardelli Chocolate Company), which has been in continuous operation since 1852. In his own way, Ghirardelli helped build and shape San Francisco, and his company has become synonymous with the city. Ghirardelli Square, which his company-built, has been an official city landmark since 1965.
history: Adapted from a drink which was the signature cocktail of San Francisco Cocktail Week 21-27 September 2010.

name: Pisco Bell Ringer
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
RINSE chilled glass with apricot brandy (swirl liqueur around inside of glass to coat and then shake out excess).
SHAKE other ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into apricot-rinsed glass.
garnish: Serve naked, without garnish.
review: Go easy on the bitters and this will be a delicate, aromatic pisco cocktail.
history: Yet again I must thank drinks historian David Wondrich for unearthing this drink and its history. It was created by a bartender named Jim Maloney and first appeared in his 1903 book How to Mix Drinks.
