Maloney patented the name 'Bell-Ringers' as referring to cocktails served in glasses rinsed with apricot brandy.

name: Procrastination Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A wet martini flavoured with copious amounts of limoncello (which slightly sweetens and adds surprisingly subtle lemon favours) and a dash of Chartreuse which shines through.
history: Recipe created by Paul Clarke, the Seattle-based cocktail enthusiast behind the excellent cocktailchronicles.com.

name: Royal Toast
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Rich cherry liqueur flavours are balanced by dry vermouth and lifted by vodka.
history: Adapted from a recipe "by W. E. Edwards" in the 1937 United Kingdom Bartender' Guild Approved Cocktails.

name: Sargasso
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: This orange-red bitter sweet cocktail has a dry oaky complexity.
history: Created in 2008 by Don Lee at PDT, New York City, USA.

name: South Ireland Sour
instructions: DRY SHAKE all ingredients (without ice)
Add ice, SHAKE again and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Smells of sticking plasters and uses Feijoa and Guinness. Only a Kiwi could create such a drink. Give it a try, it's not as crazy as it sounds.
history:
Created by Jacob Briars at Cocktail World Cup 2007 as a reaction to an Irish bartender who said 42 Below Feijoa Vodka was "undrinkable", to which Jacob responded "in large parts of the world people thought the same about Guinness." So as a joke Jacob made this surprisingly tasty cocktail.

The name is a geographical in-joke. The Irishman was from Cork, in southern Ireland. Cork is part of Munster, and they were in Queenstown, in New Zealand's South Island, which was once known as New Munster. With their flat vowel pronunciation, Kiwis say Island and Ireland in exactly the same way.

name: Blue Lassi
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist (discarded) and mint leaf.
review: Baby blue creamy yoghurt with vanilla infused vodka, orange liqueur and aromatic rose water.
history: Created in 2011 by yours truly (Simon D) at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Bombay Lassi Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE cardamom in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: Creamy yet balanced, even slightly tart rather than sweet, with hints of vanilla, cardamom, ginger, and lemon.
history: Created in 2011 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Pineapple Lassi Cocktail
instructions: MUDDLE cardamom and then pineapple in base of shaker
ADD other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Creamy yoghurty cardamom and pineapple laced with vodka.
history: Created in 2011 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Raita Cocktail
instructions: MUDDLE cardamom, cumin and then cucumber in base of shaker
ADD other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Fresh cucumber, mint and yoghurt with a hint of cardamom and cumin spice, laced with vodka.
history: Created in 2011 by yours truly (Simon D) at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Rosie Lassi Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with rose petal floated on cocktail.
review: Creamy vanilla yoghurt with floral rose liqueur fortified with vodka.
history: Created in 2011 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Raspberry Lassi Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE raspberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered raspberry and dust with freshly ground black pepper.
review: Rich creamy vanilla yoghurt with fresh raspberries and raspberry liqueur fortified with vodka.
history: Created in 2011 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Banana Smoothie
instructions: BLEND ingredients with 6oz scoop of crushed ice
Pour into glass and serve immediately with straws.
review: Packed with fruit, this creamy banana flavoured shake is 'hardened' with a double measure of vodka.
history: Created in 2011 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Raspberry Smoothie
instructions: BLEND ingredients with a scoop of crushed ice
Pour into glass and serve immediately with straws.
review: This soft pink, creamy yoghurt drink is loaded with raspberry fruit and laced with vodka.
history: Created in 2011 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Strawberry Smoothie (Alcoholic)
instructions: BLEND ingredients with 12oz scoop of crushed ice
Pour into glass and serve immediately with straws.
review: This radiantly coloured creamy strawberry yoghurt drink is sweetened with honey and 'hardened' with vodka.
history: Created in 2011 by yours truly (Simon D) at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Greek Piña Colada
instructions: BLEND ingredients crushed ice, pour into glass and serve immediately with straws.
review: A yoghurt and rum based pineapple and coconut drink, and if your blender is sufficiently powerful, with tiny fragments of pineapple shrapnel.
history:
Created in 2011 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.
Piña Colada cocktail history

name: Creamy Vanilla Colada
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with cocoa powder.
review: Creamy yoghurt married with aged rum and richly flavoured with vanilla.
history:
Inspired by the classic Pina Colada, this was created in 2011 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.
Piña Colada history and recipes

name: Venetian
instructions: STIR all ingredients with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass.
review: Burnished copper red and bittersweet with wine and a dash of elderflower, fruit liqueur and bitters.
history: Created in 2009 by Rich Hunt at Quo Vadis in London, England. It is based on the flavours of the spritz, but there are no bubbles of any kind.

name: Tonic Boom
instructions: POUR tonic water into old-fashioned glass
Then POUR gin into shot glass
Instruct drinker to hold gin-filled shot glass directly over surface of tonic water and drop shot into old-fashioned glass and consume
review: Essentially a gin and tonic bomb. To quote a Portobello Star regular, "the thinking man's Jager Bomb".
history: Created in 2010 by Edward 'Teddy' McPartland at The Portobello Star, London, England.

name: Bénédictine Conversion
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with thyme sprig.
review: Tequila and mezcal-based with herbal and thyme liqueurs.
history: Adapted from a recipe created circa 2012 by Ian Cameron in London, England.

name: Pink Chihuahua
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Yes, this appropriately named almond Margarita-like drink is pink and fluffy – due to being sweetened and made red with pomegranate syrup (in place of triple sec) and given a foamy head by the addition of egg white.
history:
Created in 2010 by Dick Bradsell at The Pink Chihuahua at El Camion in London, England.

This fluffy little pink drink is named Chihuahua (Spanish: Chihuahueño) after the smallest breed of dog and also the state of Mexico. At El Camion, the front door is painted with an artist's impression of a pink Chihuahua.

name: Pearlinstrum
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI (SMALL) GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist (and a sprig of elderflower when in season)..
review: A pear-flavoured Vodka Martini with delicate, harmonious floral elderflower.
history:
Created in 2012 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

Trying to think of an appropriate 'pear' name, I remembered the Swedish adventurer Per Lindstrand, who, with entrepreneur Richard Branson, was the first to cross the Atlantic Ocean in a hot-air balloon on 2-3 July 1987. The combination of miss remembering his name and my dyslexic spelling gave me Pearlinstrum.

name: Archibald Periscope
instructions: SHAKE first three ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with soda and then carefully FLOAT Rioja wine.
review: Slightly sweet, long and refreshing. To quote the menu at Albert & Pearl where we discovered this cocktail, "a sight for sore eyes".
history: Adapted (considerably) from a drink discovered in 2009 at Albert & Pearl, Islington, London, England.

name: Salty Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of large caperberry on pick.
review: As the name suggests, fino sherry and caper brine add saltiness but this 'Salty Martini' also has a distinct nuttiness from the sherry. We'd suggest both a long stir for generous dilution, and serving with grilled almonds.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2009 by Salvatore Calabrese at Fifty in London, England.

name: Cantaritos
instructions: Select and pre-chill a CANTARITOS CLAY POT OR COLLINS GLASS.
If using a Cantaritos clay pot (instead of a collins glass) submerge the pot in cold water to clean the inside and wet the outside clay.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled pot or glass.
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review:
Juicy (freshly squeezed orange, grapefruit and lime) and refreshing slightly salty tequila, invigorated with grapefruit soda.

If serving in a traditional Cantaritos pot, wetting the porous clay exterior improves its appearance, and as the water evaporates from the outside, it helps keep the contents cold.
history: Commonly made in bars, cafés and even roadside stalls of Jalisco, Mexico. Rustic, locally produced and sustainable, Cantaritos clay pots are often used as disposable takeaway drinking vessels.

name: Milanese Breakfast Martini
instructions: STIR marmalade with gin, bitter liqueur and lemon juice in base of shaker to dissolve the marmalade
SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with sparkling wine.
review: A fruity but dry aperitif drink served 'up' but with a splash of prosecco sparkle.
history: Adapted from a drink originally named Breakfast in Milan by Dawid Steenkamp at Jamie Oliver's Barbecoa in London, England, and inspired by the Negroni and Salvatore's Breakfast Martini.

name: Coessential
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Float mint leaf.
review: To quote from the Oxford English Dictionary, "This is done with the help of the hands of consciousness that extend from the spiritual heart and are coessential to it". It is and John Coe did. Thanks again John, this delicate but aromatic drink is dedicated to you.
history: Created in 2011 by yours truly (Simon D) at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Teaquila
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
review: A Mexican iced tea sweetened with honey.
history: Created in 2011 by Mark Cooke and the rest of the team at North Bar, Leeds, England.

name: Conan Doyle
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A literary riff on a Corn'n'Oil - elementary but tasty. As the ice melts and dilutes this cocktail so the falernum notes amplify and the cocktail mellows and opens.
history: Discovered in 2008 at Tonic Bar in Edinburgh, Scotland. Originally made with Talisker Island single malt Scotch whisky.

name: Celery Gimlet
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with celery stalk.
review: Occupying the ground between the Gimlet and a shrub with pleasing savoury celery notes adding interest.
history: Created in 2010 by Naren Young at Saxon & Parole, in Manhattan, New York City.

name: Brandy Buck
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring ginger ale.
garnish: Garnish with lemon wedge.
review: Lemon juice adds balance to the sweet ginger ale. Brsandy provides the backbone.

name: Absinthe Gimlet
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime wedge .
review: A herbal riff on the classic gin-based Gimlet.
history: Adapted from a recipe created circa 2015 by Tara Garnell at The Cabinet Room in London, England after making Charles Vexenat's Green Beast.

name: Golden Ivy
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Cognac stirred with rich berry flavours of muscat wine along with the relatively dry berry flavour of cranberry.
history: A Christmas cocktail created in October 2011 by yours truly.

name: Alamagoozlum Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Tastes reminiscent of Cough Candy with a quintet of big-flavoured ingredients combining harmoniously, including a whopping 5ml (1/6oz) of bitters.
history:
A "Plantation Alamagoozlum" is referenced as "an excellent preventive of colds and chills" in a 1935 column in The Indianapolis News and then as an Alamagoozlum in a 1937 edition of the Louisville, Kentucky, Courier-Journal. However, it most notably features in Charles H. Baker's 1939 The Gentleman's Companion: Around the World with Jigger, Beaker and Flask as "J. Pierpont Morgan's Alamagoozlum, the Personal Mix Credited to that Financier, Philanthropist, & Banker of a Bygone Era."

Baker gives the following recipe "To serve about 5 cocktails: take 1 jigger each of Jamaica rum, gomme syrup, and yellow or green Chartreuse; add ½ pony yellow Curaçao and ½ pony of Angostura bitters. Add 2 scant ponies of Holland gin, the same of water; donate ½ the white of an egg and hard shake with lots of cracked ice. Serve in a Manhattan glass."

As with so many classic cocktails, our recipe is based on David A. Embury's 1948 The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks, where Embury says, "This cocktail is supposed to have been a specialty of the elder Morgan of the House of Morgan, which goes to prove as a bartender he was an excellent banker."

Both Charles H. Baker and David Embury credit the creation, or at least this cocktail's notoriety, to John Pierpont Morgan Sr. (1837 - 1913), a Wall Street banker and financier who headed the banking firm that went on to be known as J.P. Morgan and Co., then Morgan, Grenfell & Company.

J. P. Morgan liked to drink in style and frequented New York City's most fashionable clubs, including the Union Club. When his friend, John King, president of Erie Railroad, was blackballed from the Union Club, Morgan resigned and in 1891 established the Metropolitan Club of New York. He commissioned Stanford White to "...build me a club fit for gentlemen, forget the expense..." at One East 60th Street; on the corner of 5th Avenue. As the club's first President, he invited John King to be a charter member.

name: Messianic Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Sprinkle with gold leaf if available.
review: Ginger and orange are delicately spiced with clove-forward falernum and fortified with golden rum.
history: Messianic means of the Messiah, so perhaps an appropriate name for a Christmas cocktail. Created in October 2011 by yours truly and updated December 2021.

name: Miami Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of watermelon slice.
review: Light, refreshing and all too quaffable. Bitter liqueur and orange bitters add depth of flavour. And, with a full  20ml of sugar, surprisingly balanced.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Alexandra Fiot at The Lonsdale in London, England.

name: Dry Martini (2:1.5 ratio) Sopping Wet
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Express lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish &/or garnish with skewered olive.
review: Herbal vermouth flavours pervade this Sopping Wet Martini.
history: There is considerable debate about the origin of the Martini. See the Martini Cocktail And Its Evolution.

name: Brandy Fizz
ingredients: Top up with Soda from siphon
instructions: SHAKE first three ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass (without ice)
TOP with soda.
review: A refreshing and tasty dry drink: cognac and lemon balanced with a little sugar and lengthened with soda.

name: Christmas Pudding and Custard Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
POUR Pedro Ximenez sherry into second smaller glass to be served on the side.
review: As the name suggests, this drink combines flavours reminiscent of alcoholic custard with Pedro Ximenez sherry resembling liquid Christmas pudding.
history: A Christmas cocktail created in October 2011 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Christmas Velvet Alexander
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: Combining advocaat, gin and fino sherry, this is the ultimate stereotypical English Granny's Christmas Cocktail.
history: A Christmas cocktail inspired by the classic gin-based Alexander and the popular Brandy Alexander. Created in October 2011 by yours truly.

name: Christmopolitan
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Garnish with red currants.
review: A suitably Christmassy amaretto-influenced Cosmopolitan.
history: A Christmas cocktail created in October 2011 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Dutch Blood and Sand
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A light, fragrant style of the classic, normally Scotch whisky-based, Blood and Sand.

name: Little Smith
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with dehydrated apple slice floated on cocktail.
review: Lightly spicy and delicately fragrant with a faint grassiness.
history: Discovered in 2011 at Ubar, Copenhagen, Denmark.

name: Creole Contentment
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: If using a sweeter Madeira such as Verdelho/Rainwater (medium dry) or Bual (medium sweet) you'll need to adjust this finely balanced cocktail. Even Baker himself says it "needs a little trimming". However, dry Sercial Madeira works best to produce a finely balanced complex cocktail.
history: Adapted from Charles H. Baker's 1939 The Gentlemen's Companion .

name: The Colonial Cooler
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
STIR first 5 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet and pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Basically a Gin and It lengthened with soda and spiced with dashes of Amer Picon, Angostura bitters and orange curaçao.
history:
This recipe is adapted from Charles H. Baker's 1939 The Gentlemen's Companion in which Baker explains that he encountered this drink in mid-1920s at the Sandakan Club, North Borneo, during an excursion from the SS Resolute, a round-the-world steamer. 

The steamer anchored some 14 miles off shore and Baker and his companions set ashore in two lifeboats, both of which failed to make dry land due to engine trouble. A Borneo prahu "with a sail like a striped butterfly and a gent in a G-string and a headdress" raised the alarm and the adventurers were eventually towed ashore by a British North Borneo Company tug.

"We first went out to the Sandakan Club - there'd be a British Club on Mount Everest if 2 Britishers could stand the cold there! - and had these Coolers, through the courtesy of an American who was sentimental enough to fetch a mint root out with him.

"To 1 jigger of dry gin add the same of Italian vermouth. To this base donate 1 dash of each. Angostura and Amer Picon, and 1 tsp of orange Curaçao. Stir with a goodly lump of ice in a small highball or sour glass, and top off with a squirt of soda garnished with 2 sprigs of mint and a stick of ripe pineapple."

name: Brandy Smash
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass (preferably over a chunk of block ice).
review: Sweetened cognac flavoured with mint. Simple but beautiful.
history: A classic from the 1850s.

name: Brazen Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Not for the faint hearted - a great combination of grassy bison vodka with violet Parfait Amour.

name: Brazilian Berry
instructions: MUDDLE fruit in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into glass filled with crushed ice
Serve with straws.
review: This drink combines wine, cachaça and rich berry fruits.
history: Created in 2002 by Dan Spink at Browns, St Martin's Lane, London, England.

name: Breakfast At Terrell's
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
POUR first part of the sparkling wine into chilled glass.
SHAKE next 4 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into glass charged with sparkling wine.
TOP with sparkling wine (slowly pour to raise the head like a soufflé).
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of coin-sized orange zest twist.
review: This creamy orange 'champagne' cocktail is almost as silky smooth as a Sgroppino.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 1999 by Jamie Terrell for Philip Holzberg at Vinexpo in Bordeaux, France.

name: Broadmoor
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail, then use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Bartending law stipulates that this cocktail be stirred. If law-abiding, stir and serve on the rocks with larger (60ml whisky, 15ml Chartreuse, 10ml sugar, and 2 dashes bitters) measures. However, this digestif is best enjoyed straight-up while still alive and foaming from a good shake.
history: Named after the infamous British mental institution, this recipe is adapted from a cocktail created in 2001 by Swedish bartender Andreas Noren at The Player bar in London, England. It was then popularised at London's Milk & Honey club.

name: Shaky Pete's Ginger Brew
instructions: Select and pre-chill a RUM BARREL MUG OR PINT GLASS.
Blitz BLEND first 3 ingredients with two ice cubes for ten seconds.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with beer.
garnish: Serve naked, without garnish.
review: Imagine a ginger-flavoured shandy with a kick – pretty much describes this gin-laced lemon and ginger-flavoured beer-tail.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2012 by Pete Jeary (aka Shaky Pete) at Hawksmoor, Seven Dials, London, England. Pete's original recipe:
35 ml Dry gin
50 ml Lemon juice
50 ml Homemade ginger syrup
120 ml London Pride Beer
The ginger syrup is made by blending 500g peeled and chopped root ginger, 500g caster sugar and 250ml water, fine-strained into bottles.

name: Foxy Lady
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of strawberry.
review: Best described as a strawberry gin sour, the herbal botanicals in the gin combine well with the sweet strawberry.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2005 by Nicolas Skovgaard at Fox Hotel bar in Copenhagen, Denmark. Nicolas revised this recipe in 2013 to use chamomile tea-infused gin.

name: Silk Road
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: As the name suggests, the Silk Road harnesses the exotic herbs and spices found in the gin and Bénédictine to deliver a harmonious, flavoursome drink with a silky delivery.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2012 by Tim Sparrow at Barrio North in London, England.

name: Dopo Cena
instructions: DRY SHAKE all ingredients (without ice)
SHAKE again with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Under the white head lies a dessert cocktail (Dopo Cena is Italian for 'after dinner') with aromatic cherry and marzipan flavours.
history: Created by Adrian Gomes, 10 Dollar Shake, Aberdeen, Scotland.

name: Garden, Grain and Grape
instructions: MUDDLE grapes in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, DRY SHAKE (without ice)
SHAKE again with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Pale green lime with frothy white head, this delicate wine and grape flavoured drink is laced with a large splash of vodka.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2012 by Mal Spence at Blythswood Square Hotel, Glasgow, Scotland.

name: Punch Back
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge on rim with cherries on stick.
review: Cachaça is the raw, spirity love interest in this rum based coconut and lime punch.
history: Created in May 2013 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England for the National Aids Trust.

name: Northern Glow
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pink grapefruit zest.
review: A White Lady with grapefruit juice and so an extra dash of sugar to balance. Tasty.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in November 2012 by Tim McCall, then Assistant Bar Manager at the Great Northern Hotel in London. Tim originally christened his new creation Northern Sour but by the time we discovered it at the hotel's Plum + Spilt Milk bar, in July 2013, it had been renamed Northern Glow, and even Tim says "the name change is a little better."

name: Agua De Cuba
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
Cut watermelon into 16 segments, chop the flesh from one segment into cubes and MUDDLE in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with watermelon wedge on rim.
review: A light, summery, rum-based drink with fresh watermelon and subtle clove spice. We prefer strained into a coupe glass rather than a flute.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2010 by Joey Medrington at Tigerlily in Edinburgh, Scotland.

name: Beautiful Lady
instructions: STIR first 2 ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass
FLOAT thin layer of lightly whipped cream.
review: This dessert-style drink with cherry and chocolate combined under a layer of cream may be reminiscent of a black forest gateau, but the use of chocolate spirit rather than liqueur means it's not too sweet.
history: Created in 2010 by Klaus St. Rainer at Schumann's Bar Munich, Germany.

name: Bridgetown Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with a grapefruit wedge.
review: Rum-laced sweet and sourness with maraschino liqueur brilliantly balancing grapefruit juice.
history: Unknown.

name: Bushranger
ingredients: 45 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: STIR all ingredients with ice and stain into ice-filled glass.
review: Slightly sweet, this complex cocktail combines flavours of red wine with subtle herbal notes and is fortified with light rum.
history: Unknown. Originally, in the early years of the British settlement of Australia, Bushrangers were runaway convicts who had the survival skills necessary to use the Australian bush as a refuge from the authorities. The term then evolved as the Australian term for a highwayman.

name: The Cappa Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A balanced marriage of gin, fortified wine and maraschino.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2012 by Franky Marshall at 15 Romolo in San Francisco, California. Ms Franky named her Cappa cocktail for the black cloaks, or Cappas, worn by the Black Friar monks at the monastery, which now houses the Plymouth gin distillery.

Gary "gaz" Regan reported on The Cappa Cocktail in his The Cocktailian column in The San Francisco Chronicle during August 2013, by which time Ms Franky Marshall was bartending at New York's The Dead Rabbit. In gaz's column, the recipe given is:
"1½ ounces Plymouth gin
1½ ounces Pierre Ferrand Pineau des Charentes
½ ounce Luxardo maraschino liqueur
1 lemon twist, as garnish
Instructions: Stir all the ingredients, except the garnish, over ice, and strain into a chilled cocktail glass. Add the lemon twist."

name: Eastern Promises
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: This rum-based drink was apparently inspired by the golden age of the cocktail, with aromatic mandarin, tart lime, bittersweet vermouth resulting in an elegant cocktail with a complex finish.
history: Created in 2011 by Simone Caporale at Artesian at the Langham Hotel in London, England.

name: El Momento Perfecto
ingredients: 20 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A rum and marmalade Negroni-like cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2011 by Daniel Bovey at Sahara Bar in Reading, England.

name: Elderflower Gin Fizz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FIZZ OR HIGHBALL (8OZ TO 10OZ) GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass. (No ice in the glass.)
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice and mint sprigs bouquet.
review: Be sure to use a small capacity (8 to 10oz max) tall glass, or this cocktail will become over-diluted. Due to the lack of ice in the serve, also ensure the soda is well chilled, and preferably use a frozen bottle of gin.

name: The Getaway (AKA Cynar Daiquiri)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: The bitter vegetal taste of Cynar turns this Daiquiri into a digestif.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2012 by Wayne Curtis at The Passenger, Washington D.C., USA.

name: Moonwalk Cocktail
instructions: Select a FLUTE GLASS.
DROP sugar cube into glass and soak with dashes of grapefruit bitters and orange flower water.
POUR liqueur over soaked sugar cube.
TOP with sparkling wine.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with quarter orange slice on rim.
review: Delicately sweetened champagne with cognac and citrus flavours.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 1969 by Joe Gilmore at The American Bar at The Savoy Hotel, London to commemorate the Apollo 11 moon landing on 20th July 1969.

Speaking to D in January 2012 Joe said, "The moon landings were a big deal to everyone at the time and I thought I've got to make a cocktail for this." Joe, who watched the moon landings repeated on TV when his shift was over, went on to say, "I never got to serve Neil Armstrong personally, but I sent one in a flask to NASA and apparently, it was the first drink he had when he landed. He wrote a letter to me at the Savoy to say thank you." That letter is on display on the wall of the 'museum' to the left as you enter The American Bar.

name: Palmetto Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: The orange bitters are key to balancing and adding depth to the equal parts aged rum and sweet vermouth. However, go easy with those dashes or bitter orange will dominate this complex cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Alaska (Savoy recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: In his 1948 The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks, David Embury says of the Alaska, "This is also sometimes called the Oriental. It can be greatly improved by using less Chartreuse and adding 1 or 2 parts dry sherry. This is the Nome."
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: The Buck / Gin Buck
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lemon wedge.
review: The Buck can be improved by adding a dash of liqueur appropriate to the spirit base. E.g. add a dash of Grand Marnier to a Brandy Buck.

name: Raspberry Daiquiri
instructions: BLEND all ingredients with 1/2 scoop (6oz) crushed ice and serve in chilled glass.
review: A crowd-pleasing, slightly sweet blended fruity Daiquiri.

name: Buck's Fizz
instructions: Select a FLUTE GLASS.
POUR half the sparkling wine into chilled glass.
POUR orange juice.
TOP with the rest of the sparkling wine.
EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail then discard.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A very simple cocktail, but when made with freshly squeezed oranges straight from the refrigerator this is a great brunch drink. (Be sure to fine strain your orange juice.)
history:
The Buck's Fizz shares the same two ingredients as a Mimosa, and apart from the proportions of those ingredients and the Mimosa being served over ice, there is little to divide the two cocktails, even their birth year. But, although there is only sketchy evidence to back up the believed origins of either, their chronological appearance in cocktail books suggests the Buck's Fizz outdating the Mimosa.

The Buck's Fizz is thought to have been created in 1921 by Malachi "Pat" MacGarry, the bartender at London's Buck's Club (which opened in June 1919) and is listed as one of 21 "fizzes" in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book with the proportions "¼ glass orange juice, fill with champagne" in a "long tumbler". Tellingly, Harry doesn't list a Mimosa but does include a "London Buck Cocktail", also served in a "long tumbler" with "1 lump ice, 1 glass dry gin, the juice of 1/2 lemon and 1 split [small bottle] of ginger ale."

This mention of the London Buck (also known as a Gin buck or a Ginger Rogers) cocktail serves as a reminder that a Buck is an old name for a family of mixed drinks lengthened with ginger ale. The Buck's Fizz was a Fizz made at the Buck's Club and lives in the Fizz family, not the Buck family of drinks. The Buck's Fizz has confused some into mistakenly thinking that a Buck is a drink that can be lengthened with ginger ale OR citrus juice, but a Buck is made with ginger ale or ginger beer, NOT citrus juice. Buck is the Christian name of the Buck's Fizz, not the family name/ surname.

Frank Meier's 1936 Artistry of Mixing Drinks also lists the Buck's Fizz as one of 32 Fizzes, indeed, it's the first known book to list both the Buck's Fizz and the Mimosa.

name: Teqroni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
STIR.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Lighter and slightly sweeter than a classic gin-based Negroni with Italian bitter liqueur. However, this Italian and Mexican trio make for pleasing cross-continental bedfellows.
history: Unknown, but as the same suggests this is simply a tequila-based Negroni.

name: La Tour Eiffel
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Bittersweet cognac-influenced palate with lingering flavours of liquorice root, honey, pine and eucalyptus from the Suze.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2007 by Gary "gaz" Regan after a Sazerac-fuelled trip to New Orleans. He was inspired by how the Sazerac might have been if it had originally been created in France rather than New Orleans.

name: Young Man Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: This particular Young Man is essentially a cognac-based Sweet Manhattan with a splash of orange curaçao. I've used rather more curaçao than the "2 Dashes" specified in Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Stout Fellow
ingredients: 30 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice. (BEWARE - the stout may be explosive in your shaker so hold both ends more firmly than usual.)
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with chocolate shavings..
review: In a similar style to an Espresso Martini but with very different ingredients including Guinness stout and coffee liqueur in place of espresso coffee.
history:
Created in 2013 by Matthew Coates (M.C.) at Jake's Bar and Still Room, Leeds, England. Matthew was one of five finalists in the D Beer-tail Competition for London Cocktail Week 2013 held on Friday 9th August 2013 at Simon D's Cabinet Room in London.

Matthew told us, "The inspiration behind my cocktail comes from my love of all of its components – rum, beer (Guinness) and coffee. I call my drink 'a stout fellow' because it's strong and hearty but also warming and 'friendly'. It also refers to the old Guinness advertising of 'Guinness for strength' encompassing more than one meaning of the word stout."

name: Pale Charlie
instructions: SHAKE first five ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with ale.
review: Zesty, fruity and refreshing with berry and stone fruit accompanying lime and grapefruit with hoppy pale ale.
history:
Created in 2013 by Michele Mariotti at The American Bar at The Savoy Hotel, London. Michele was one of five finalists in the Dsguide Beer-tail Competition for London Cocktail Week 2013 held on Friday 9th August 2013 at Simon D's Cabinet Room in London.

Michele's drink was inspired by Charlie Chaplin's stay at the Savoy in 1889.

name: Huckle my buff
instructions: Gently BLEND all ingredients on a slow speed, then warm in a saucepan, gently stirring with a whisk (or microwave for 30 seconds and stir), POUR into warmed glass
If you make several servings in one batch, keep warm and serve from a sous-vide or heated soup kettle at to 60°C.
review: To quote Jamie Oliver, "the Huckle-my-buff is not a Dodo, but a Phoenix".
history:
My adaptation of a recipe presented by Jamie Oliver and Jimmy Doherty on Friday 9th August 2013, at the Cabinet Room as their entry to the D Beer-tail Competition for London Cocktail Week 2013.

Also known as Huckle-my-butt and Huckle-and-buff, Huckle-my-buff is an early 18th century hot drink combining gin or cognac and beer. Jamie and Jimmy resurrected the Huckle-my-buff and gave it a modern twist with the use of nitrous oxide and a sous-vide in place of the traditional red-hot poker.

The Jamie and Jimmy recipe was as follows: whisk 1 fresh egg yolk, then slowly whisk in 20 grams muscovado sugar, 35ml cognac, 150ml Harvey's Stout beer and 0.8ml ginger juice. Pour mixture into a soda syphon, close and charge with nitrous oxide (laughing gas/N2O). Gently warm the filled cream whipper to 60°C in a sous-vide (water bath). Discharge warmed syphon into glass. Finish with freshly grated nutmeg.

Originally a punch still drink, Huckle-My-Butt appears in the Ending With "Party Mixes" chapter of  W. C. Whitfield's 1941 Here's How Mixed Drinks.

name: Buena Vida
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge.
review: The fruits combine brilliantly with the tequila and spice comes courtesy of Angostura.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2011 by Lee Clinton.

name: Perfect Guest
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Clean, grainy vodka forms the backbone of this floral and herbal delicate dessert Vodkatini-style cocktail.
history: Created by Giuseppe Santamaria for the Spanish World Class 2012 bartender competition.

name: Honey Vodka Collins
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: STIR honey with vodka in base of shaker until honey dissolves
ADD other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain into ice-filled glass.
review: The honey and lemon combo are reminiscent of a toddy but here served long and iced with cleansing vodka.
history: Also see Collins Cocktails - recipes & history.

name: Trinidad Especial
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: This deep reddy-brown bittersweet drink is based upon sweet almond syrup balancing bitter notes from the Angostura and lime tartness. With a whole shot of Angostura this cocktail is most unusual.
history: Created by Italian Barman, Valentino Bolognese and the winning drink in the Angostura European Cocktail Competition held at Mood Bar, Paris in January 2008.

name: Honey Bee Mine
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Honey and vanilla laced with white rum and charged with champagne. Perfect for Valentine's Day.
history: Created February 2014 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Sanguinello Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Bitter notes are balanced by sweet, lemony limoncello with blood orange juice contributing citrus freshness.
history: This well-established Italian classic is named after one of the three most common varieties of blood oranges; the Sanguinello orange is native to Spain.

name: Red Opal Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: The combination of gin, blood orange liqueur, and blood orange juice results in clementine-like flavours with subtle flavours of orange flower water.
history: Adapted from the 1920s classic Opal recipe.

name: Le Jardin
instructions: SHAKE first 7 ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with champagne.
review: This salmon pink cocktail combines gin, maraschino liqueur, fresh blueberries, lemon juice and apple juice with rose champagne to create a refreshing harmonious blend. The inspiration behind the drink is a quote by Nicolas Gilsoul, "A garden, like champagne, is a filter, a medium through which the person walking in it awakens his senses and reveals the depth of his emotions."
history: Adapted from a recipe created in January 2014 by Sean Ware at the London Cocktail Club in London, England.

name: Rose Memoire
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
SHAKE first 2 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: Garnish with redcurrants or raspberry.
review: This unusual riff on the classic mimosa unusually calls for redcurrant jelly and sweet clementine juice, topped with rosé champagne.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in January 2014 by Sean Ware of the London Cocktail Club, London, England.

name: Franco Russian Punch
instructions: SHAKE first four ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with champagne and lightly stir.
review: This long refreshing berry and champagne cocktail is a riff on Dick Bradsell’s Russian Spring Punch.
history: Created in January 2014 for Laurent-Perrier by Sean Ware of the London Cocktail Club, London, England.

name: Sicilian Orange Spritz
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: POUR prosecco and liqueur into glass
ADD ice
Top with soda
review: Zesty, bittersweet blood orange with Prosecco effervescence.
history:
Created in 2013 by yours truly for Jamie's Italian bar.

See Spritz cocktail history and more recipes

name: Biscotti Spritz
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: POUR prosecco and liqueurs into glass
ADD ice
Top with soda.
review: Hazelnut and butterscotch liqueurs flavour this Prosecco-based spritz. Better when complemented by a biscotti biscuit.
history:
Created in 2013 by yours truly for Jamie's Italian Bar.

See Spritz cocktail history and more recipes

name: Lychee and Elderflower Spritz
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: POUR prosecco and liqueurs into glass
Add ice and TOP with soda.
review: As the apt name suggests, this slightly sweet spritz is flavoured with lychee and elderflower liqueurs. East meets west in Italy.
history:
Created in 2013 by yours truly for Jamie's Italian Bar.

See Spritz cocktail history and more recipes

name: Parma Violet Spritz
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR prosecco and liqueur into glass.
FILL glass with ice.
TOP with soda.
garnish: Garnish with Parma Violet sweets.
review: Inspired by and garnished with mini Parma Violet sweets, this spritz is flavoured with crème de violette liqueur.
history:
Created in 2013 by yours truly for Jamie's Italian Bars.

See: Spritz family of cocktails

name: Peach and Apricot Spritz
instructions: POUR prosecco and liqueurs into glass
ADD ice
Top with soda.
review: This prosecco-based spritz is fruity but not overly sweet. Peach and apricot sit brilliantly with the sparkling wine, think Bellini with a hint of apricot.
history:
Created in 2013 by yours truly for Jamie's Italian Bars.

Spritz history and family of cocktails

name: Raspberry Frangipane Spritz
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR 5 ingredients (without ice).
POUR into chilled glass already charged with prosecco.
TOP with a splash of soda.
garnish: Garnish with skewered raspberries.
review:
Just like the eponymously named dessert, this slightly sweet spritz is flavoured with raspberry, almond, vanilla and coconut. Butterscotch liqueur adds a biscuity note.

If serving several of these, pre-mix the first five ingredients and use 40ml (1⅓oz) in each cocktail.
history:
Created in 2013 by yours truly for Jamie's Italian Bars.

See: Spritz history

name: Rossini Spritz
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: POUR prosecco and Rossini mix into glass
ADD ice
Top with soda.
review: Perhaps best described as a Strawberry Bellini, sweet strawberry flavours are tamed with the gentle acidity of Prosecco.
history:
Created in 2013 by yours truly for Jamie's Italian Bars.

See Spritz cocktail history and more recipes

name: Bull's Blood
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Orange fruitiness fortified with cognac and white rum. Fruity but not a sweetie.

name: Lemonade Spritz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
review: A minty real lemonade. A seriously refreshing non-alcoholic summery drink.
history:
Adapted from what was a popular drink on the menu of Jamie's Italian restaurant and bar chain.

Spritz recipes and history

name: Bellini Peach Spritz
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: Select and pre-chill a ROCKS GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon slice or fresh peach wedge.
review: Basically, a Bellini served on-the-rocks.
history:
Created in 2013 by yours truly for Jamie's Italian Bars.

See Spritz cocktail history and more recipes

name: Sour Cherry Spritz
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: Select and pre-chill a ROCKS GLASS.
POUR prosecco and liqueurs into chilled glass.
FILL glass with ice.
TOP with soda.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: The name aptly describes the drink.
history:
Created in 2013, this was a popular drink at Jamie's Italian Bars.
See: Spritz cocktail history

name: Gimlet Cocktail (Charles H. Baker's 1939 recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Boozy and very subtly flavoured and edging very slightly towards being sweet.
history: Recipe adapted from Charles H. Baker, Jr.'s 1939 The Gentleman's Companion volume II – An Exotic Drinking Book.

name: Dead Parrot Colada
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
BLEND all ingredients with crushed ice.
Serve with straws.
garnish: Prepare garnish of small stuffed dead parrot that's passed on, no more, expired and gone to meet 'is maker, a stiff, bereft of life, rests in peace, pushing up the daisies, off the twig, kicked the bucket, shuffled off 'is mortal coil, run down the curtain and joined the bleedin' choir invisible. An ex-parrot. And preferably one with 'lovely plumage'..
review: Gin and delicate maraschino cherry with orange and lemon, coconut and a hint of Norwegian aquavit character.
history:
Created in July 2014 by yours truly after watching Monty Python's famed Dead Parrot Sketch performed by John Cleese and Michael Palin at London's O2 arena.

The use of Lysholm Linie Aquavit and the cocktail's colour is inspired by the "Norwegian Blue" parrot while gin represents British humour. The coconut and loose colada theme comes from the late 1960s/70s when the sketch was originally performed. 

In the sketch, the disgruntled customer Mr Praline (played by Cleese) returns a "Norwegian Blue" parrot to the shopkeeper (Michael Palin) explaining, "That parrot is definitely deceased, and when I purchased it not 'alf an hour ago, you assured me that its total lack of movement was due to it bein' tired and shagged out following a prolonged squawk."

name: Almond Pina Colada
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: As the name suggests, this rum-laced cocktail has flavours of pineapple and almond.
history: See Piña Colada history.

name: Hot Gin Toddy
instructions: Place bar spoon in empty (ideally warmed glass)
POUR all ingredients into glass and then STIR.
review: Warmed and sweetened gin with a hint of orange. (Others prefer a grapefruit zest garnish but there is no accounting.)
history: The first published reference to a Toddy was in England in 1741.

name: Mezcal Negroni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
STIR.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish (&/or garnish with olive).
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist (&/or skewered green olive).
review: This mezcal-influenced Negroni is an interesting take on the classic, which will be appreciated by mezcal aficionados.
history: Negroni cocktail history and recipe variations

name: Blanche Corpse Reviver
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Blanche absinthe sits surprisingly well in this riff on the classic Corpse Reviver.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2013 by Stephen Quainton at B.Y.O.C. Bar in Brighton, England.

name: Blanche Refresher
instructions: SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into glass filled with crushed ice
Drizzle Green Chartreuse all over the top of the drink.
review: Challenging ingredients, including a whole shot of absinthe, combine to make a very approachable, aptly refreshing cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2013 by Stephen Quainton at B.Y.O.C. Bar, Brighton, England.

name: Bonaparte
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR chilled soda water into an ice-filled glass.
ADD freshly brewed hot espresso coffee so it layers on the sparkling water.
Lastly, POUR the mandarin liqueur (the tasty part of the Bonaparte), which will sink through the coffee layer, pulling the drink together while leaving the coffee's crema on the surface.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Zesty mandarin orange and espresso coffee served as a tall, refreshing cocktail. A tall drink named after a short man!
history: Created 20th February 2014 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Buona Sera Shot
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SHOT GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Serve naked, without garnish.
review: The use of a dry, or at least not so sweet, espresso-style coffee liqueur is essential to the flavour and balance of this rum-laced nutty coffee shot.

name: Gingerbread Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with gingerbread or ginger cookie rested on the rim.
review: A wet Vodka Martini with nutty almond and warming gingerbread.
history: Created in December 2014 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Nutty Pear
instructions: SHAKE first three ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass over champagne
review: As the name suggests pear and almond are the main flavours with calvados providing backbone and hints of apple, while champagne dries and adds effervescence.
history: Created in September 2014 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Sticky Toffee Fizz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine and briefly stir.
garnish: RIM glass with toffee sauce.
review: An indulgent champagne cocktail.
history: Inspired by a cocktail discovered at London's Light Bar in September 2014.

name: Triple A
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: So named due to this drink being flavoured with absinthe, apple and almond.
history: Created in 2010 by Dick Bradsell, London, England

name: Melon Maple Fizz
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: MUDDLE melon in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda water.
review: Light, fruity and refreshing. Not overly sweet.
history: ADpted from a recipe created in 2014 by Manuel Terron, Midori Global Brand ambassador.

name: Rusty Apple Toddy
instructions: HEAT apple juice, squeeze lemon juice, cinnamon stick and clove in a saucepan
POUR Drambuie into a pre-warmed glass and then top with heated apple juice.

name: Smoky Mocha Spritz
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
STIR.
garnish: Prepare garnish of two segments of dark Toblerone chocolate on the rim.
review: Coffee and chocolate flavours with a faint smokiness, invigorated with sparkling wine.
history:
Created on 30th January 2014 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

See Spritz cocktail history and more recipes

name: King of Orange
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with prosecco.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist, shaped to resemble a crown.
review: Bittersweet, with the richness of Grand Marnier balancing the bitter liqueur and lemon juice. (I'm guilty of rebalancing this cocktail and adding the prosecco.)
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2012 by Dutch bartender Fjalar Goud in honour of King's Day, celebrated across the Netherlands annually on 27th April.

name: Doctor No. 3
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Tangy Swedish Punch freshened with orange and lemon juice.
history: In his 1948 The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks, David A. Embury lists a Doctor No. 3 comprising "One part lemon juice, 1 part orange juice, 2 parts Swedish Punch."

name: Doctor No. 4
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: Retitled 'Swedish Daiquiri', this could be a hit. Aged rum (originally Jamaican rum) combines with tangy Swedish Punch balanced by lime juice. This cocktail loves dilution, so consider adding a splash of water or, perhaps better, serve on-the-rocks.
history: Recipe adapted from Lucius Beebe's 1946 The Stock Club Bar Book.

name: Wordsmith
ingredients: 22.5 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: Punchy pot-still rum with equally powerful Green Chartreuse supported by maraschino liqueur with lime zestiness. Powerful in every respect. (Perhaps split serve between two in Nick & Nora glasses.)
history:
A riff on a Last Word created in 2009 by Chuck Taggart, Los Angeles and named by Dave Stolte.
See: Last Word history

name: Next to Last Word
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review:
A floral riff on the classic Last Word with maraschino to the fore.

The original, equal-parts, recipe is a touch on the sweet side. The addition of saline solution subtly improves this cocktail.
history: Last Word history

SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of butterscotch sweet.
review: Slightly sweet and very suckable.
history: This child of the 1990s was originally called a Butchscotch Martini.

name: Mulled Apple Black

review: Fruity rich and warming with apple (calvados) and blackberry.
history: Created in February 2015 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Electro Batida
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled coconut shell or collins glass.
review: Cachaça and coconut water with a touch of hazelnut, lime and subtly smoky mezcal.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2014 by Jorge Balbontin, Vantguard Brand Development Manager.

name: The Bomb
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A sherry-led aperitif cocktail. Don't be put off by its murky appearance.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Stan Jones' 1977 Jones Complete Bar Guide.

name: Watermelon Margarita
instructions: Cut watermelon into 16 segments, chop the flesh from one segment into cubes and MUDDLE in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Just as it says on the tin – this is a very fruity Margarita.

name: Webber Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of red apple fan.
review: This blackcurrant fruity cocktail has a citrus zing courtesy of the citrus vodka base and the addition of fresh lime juice, the sourness of which adds balance.
history: Created in September 2011 by Jack Webber for the British Brown's Bar & Restaurant chain, it’s since become a favourite on the Brown’s menu where it’s listed under ‘Browns Signature Cocktails’.

name: Atta Boy
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: The orange zest twist makes or breaks this classic, which when properly balanced lets the gin shine supported by herbal vermouth and delicate sweet pomegranate notes.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Byzantine
instructions: MUDDLE basil in base of shaker
Add other ingredients apart from tonic water, SHAKE with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with tonic water.
review: This fruity, herbal drink is even better made the way Douglas originally intended, with basil infused gin instead of muddled leaves.
history: Created in 2001 by Douglas Ankrah for Akbar, Soho, London, England.

name: Trident
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review:
Bone dry and boozy, this cocktail is aimed at those who have a penchant for both sherry and aquavit, not to mention bittersweet artichoke liqueurs - hardly mass market then. Nor are Aston Martin cars but I want one.

Be warned - use the wrong sherry and this drink falls apart - don't be tempted to sub manzanilla with a fino.
history: Robert Hess created this riff on a Negroni in 2002. The Trident name apparently was inspired by the fact that each of the three main ingredients comes from a different seafaring country.

name: Kingston Negroni
ingredients: 30 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice wheel.
review: The huge character of pungent rum is matched with a bittersweet liqueur and sweet vermouth combo. Not for everyone but a Negroni riff worth trying.
history:
Created in 2013 by Joaquín Simó at Pouring Ribbons in New York City, USA. 
See: Negroni cocktail history

name: Brits Spritz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
STIR.
garnish: Prepare garnish of squeeze grapefruit wedge into drink and add cucumber slice.
review: An elderflower Spritz with depth of flavour and British character courtesy of Kamm & Sons
history:
Created by Alex Kammerling of Kamm & Sons, this is the brand's signature cocktail.

See Spritz cocktail history and more recipes

name: Beetroot and Vanilla Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Just as the name suggests, only the beetroot pervades this otherwise classic Daiquiri more than the vanilla. Consider using vanilla-infused rum.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2014 by Josh Powell at Bar44, Penarth, Wales.

name: The Dandy
instructions: STIR first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass
TOP with sparkling wine.
review: Bénédictine adds both saffron flavour and vivid yellow colour to this cognac based cocktail with maraschino contributing fruity notes. Champagne dries and provides effervescence and body to this well-balanced cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe discovered in 2014 at Hawksmoor Spitalfields in London, England. In turn, this apparently comes from a punch served at New York's Waldorf-Astoria in the 1930s.

name: C C Kazi
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A Rude Cosmo without the liqueur.

name: Colletti Royale
instructions: Select and pre-chill a WINE GLASS.
SHAKE first 6 ingredients with ice.
TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Sweet and sour and very fruity - like a good Valentine's date.
history: Adapted from a Valentine's drink created in 2013 by Julie Reiner in New York City, USA.

name: Floradora
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring ginger ale.
garnish: Garnish with raspberry and lime wedge.
review: This pink, gin-based drink is light and fruity with lime juice balancing sweet raspberry liqueur and lengthened with ginger ale. Some make this with raspberry syrup rather than liqueur but why forgo the alcohol?
history:
Dating from the turn of the 20th century, this drink is eponymously named after a successful Edwardian musical comedy that originally opened in London in 1899 and then on Broadway the following year. The show was famous for its chorus line of Florodora Girls, a "sextette of tall, gorgeous damsels, clad in pink walking costumes, black picture hats and carrying frilly parasols [who] swished onto the stage and captivated New York for no other reason than they were utterly stunning."

This cocktail may be named after the Florodora Girls (spelt with three 'o's and one 'a'), but it notably features in Jacques Straub's 1913 Manual of Mixed Drinks as Floradora (spelt with two 'o's and two 'a's) so that's how I've spelt it here.

name: Entente Cordiale Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: This gin laced cocktail is flavoured with apricot liqueur which is balanced and made more complex by dry vermouth. A dash of orange bitters and a light lemon zest twist subtly serve to unify all. (Go easy with the bitters and zest.)

name: Londoner (Wood's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail, then use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Rich blackberry fruit liqueur balanced by pink grapefruit and lime juice, with gin fortifying and adding complexity. The flavour is reminiscent of a Bramble served straight-up.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Joe Wood, Cocktail Stars, England and brought to our attention by Gary "gaz" Regan.

name: Chilcano de Pisco
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Lightly stir.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Pisco and ginger ale made more interesting by a splash of lime juice and a few dashes of Amargo Chuncho bitters.
history: The Chilcano de Pisco is a traditional Peruvian way of serving pisco. Its name should not be confused with the Peruvian fish soup, Chilcano de Pescado, although the drink derived its name from the dish due to the similarity in hue thanks to the colouring effect of the aromatic bitters.

name: Koningsdag cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with coriander sprigs bouquet.
review: This very Dutch celebratory cocktail was created using Dutch vodka, Dutch orange curacao, and Dutch-made ginger liqueur. Carrot juice provides the all-important orange hue and subtle flavour. For the truly orange King's Day experience, make it with 50% Ketel One vodka and 50% Ketel One Oranje, or even replace vodka with 100% Ketel One Oranje.
history: Created by yours truly in April 2015 in celebration of Koningsdag, a festival held in the Netherlands on 27th April each year when the Dutch dress in orange and party to commemorate their monarch's birthday. Any excuse to party!

name: Alexander
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
DRY SHAKE all ingredients (without ice) to emulsify.
SHAKE again with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: This gin-laced creamy Alexander has sadly slipped from popularity, partly knocked by its successors, particularly the Brandy Alexander.
history:
The Alexander, comprising gin, crème de cacao and cream, is thought to have originated early in the 20th century, certainly before 1915, evidenced by an equal parts recipe appearing in Hugo Ensslin's 1916 Recipes for Mixed Drinks.

Historian, Barry Popik's website lists several plausible origins for this drink. The first is a cutting from page 11 of the news section of the 3rd October 1915 Philadelphia Inquirer. "The head bartender has even gone so far as to invent an Alexander cocktail, which he is reserving to be served during the World Series." This referred to The Racquet Club and the 1915 World Series, won by Boston beating Philadelphia. The bartender created the drink honouring Philadelphia pitcher Grover Cleveland Alexander (1887-1950).

Alternatively, a 21st March 1929 newspaper column by New York columnist Walter Winchell links the origin of the Alexander cocktail to Troy Alexander, a bartender at a New York pre-Prohibition lobster restaurant called Rector's. It claims that Troy created his eponymously named cocktail for a dinner celebrating a successful advertising campaign.

The advertisement depicted Phoebe Snow, a fictitious railway traveller, wearing a snow-white dress featured in an advertising campaign for the Delaware, Lackawanna and Western Railroad (DL&W) to promote the company's use of clean-burning anthracite to fuel its locomotives. 

Coal-fuelled trains frequently covered travellers with black soot, but DL&W owned vast anthracite mines in Pennsylvania, so they could legitimately claim that their passengers would arrive clean after a long journey. The first advertisement depicted an image of Phoebe Snow, supposedly a young New York socialite who frequently travelled to Buffalo, New York, wearing a white dress and featured a short poem: 

Says Phoebe Snow  
about to go 
upon a trip to Buffalo
"My gown stays white
from morn till night
Upon the Road of Anthracite"

The popular advertisements first appeared at the turn of the 20th century and ran for nearly 70 years. Phoebe became one of America's most recognized advertising mascots. The Alexander became a Prohibition favourite as the cream and nutmeg garnish helped disguise the rough taste of homemade 'bathtub' gin.

name: Grand Élixir
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: The name refers to the inclusion of Yellow Chartreuse which combines well with cachaça in this drink with honey and lemon juice.
history: Adapted from a drink originally served at Franklin Southie (now closed) in Boston, USA.

name: East 8 Hold-up cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple and lime wedges.
review: Vodka-based, fruity and bittersweet with pineapple and passion fruit.
history:
Created in 2010 by Kevin Armstrong at Milk & Honey, London. The name comes from the part of London where Kevin lived, E8, an area where muggings were commonplace.  

One night Kevin had just dropped Sam Jeveons off during their taxi ride home and Sam stopped to take a leak. While pissing against a wall with his trousers dropped around his feet, a guy came up behind him, stepped on his trousers and told him to hand over his wallet. Sam ended up chasing the guy with trousers around his ankles. The episode became known as the East 8 Hold Up.

name: Violet Negroni cocktail
ingredients: 60 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
STIR.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Bittersweet with a strong hint of gentian. A great aperitif.
history: Created in 2013 by Mathieu Sabbagh of Pernod Ricard.

name: Better and Better
ingredients: 15 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Initially strong and punchy, but as the name suggests, as the ice melts dilution makes this drink better and better.
history: Created circa 2007 by Jan Warren at 10 Downing in Manhattan, New York City but not popularised until 2012 when he was behind the stick at Dutch Kills, Long Island City. Jan's original recipe calls for Fidencio blanco mezcal.

name: Dirty(ish) Banana cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
BLEND all ingredients with 12oz scoop crushed ice.
garnish: Prepare garnish of banana slice.
review: Coffee liqueur, banana and cream laced with cachaça and blended to a creamy icy consistency.
history: Created in 2015 by Jorge Balbontin of Vantguard, Barcelona, Spain.

name: Willi Wonka cocktail
instructions: Muddle strawberries in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain into glass filled with crushed ice.
review: Cachaça and strawberry with subtle hints of lime and chocolate.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2015 by Carlos Londoño at Café Pacifico London.

name: Coco Milky Way
instructions: BLEND all ingredients with 6oz scoop of crushed ice and pour into glass.
review: Cachaça laced pineapple, coconut and cream with hints of almond and hazelnut.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Daniel Sampere at One Ocean Club, Barcelona, Spain.

name: Wet cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Superbly refreshing. The pinch of cinnamon adds delicate spice which combines well with cachaça and ginger ale.
history: Created in 2015 by Jorge Balbontin, Barcelona, Spain.

name: Harvard cocktail #2
ingredients: 30 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Byrrh combines well with cognac in this variation on the classic Harvard.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2015 by Tony Conigliaro at 69 Colebrooke Row, London, England.

name: The Med
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: MUDDLE basil in base of shaker
Add next 3 ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda.
review: A long summery drink with basil and the distinctive flavour of Greek mastiha.

name: Caipirissima
instructions: MUDDLE lime wedges in the base of shaker to release juice and oils in its skin
Add rum and sugar
SHAKE with 6oz scoop crushed ice and pour all without straining into glass.
review: A Daiquiri-like cocktail made in the style of a Caipirinha.

name: Fresh Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE cucumber in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Float cucumber slice for garnish.
review: The distinctive fresh flavour of mastiha paired with cucumber, gin and lemon juice.

name: Pink Khaleesi
instructions: MUDDLE peppercorns in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Pretty in pink but with a hint of spice and the distinctive Greek flavour of mastiha.
history: Created in 2015 by yours truly.

name: Night Vision
