instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with tonic water.
garnish: Prepare garnish of baby carrot.
review: Cachaça, carrot and tonic water make for a very interesting combo in this cocktail. Perhaps more health shop/juice bar than speakeasy, but unusually tasty all the same.
history:
Adapted from a drink originally called "Natural" created in 2015 by Jorge Balbontin, Barcelona, Spain.

Carrots are packed full of Vitamin A, in the form of beta carotene, so are good for the health of our eyes, but contrary to popular belief, carrots don't help you see in the dark. This urban myth stems from WWII. During the 1940 Blitzkrieg, the Luftwaffe struck the southeast of England under the cover of darkness, but the Royal Air Force had the upper hand in the dark skies due to new radar technology. First installed in RAF aircraft in 1939, the secret onboard Airborne Interception Radar (AI) could spot enemy bombers before they reached the English Channel. Not wanting the Germans to develop similar technology, to explain the success of the British pilots, the Ministry of Information spread the myth that they could see in the dark due to eating carrots.

name: Réveillon Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of cinnamon stick.
review: Bittersweet and spicy with underlying warming apple spirit. Originally designed to be served straight-up in a Nick & Nora glass, we think it's better suited to being served on-the-rocks, preferably over a chunk of block ice.
history:
Adapted from a drink created in 2005 by Chuck Taggart, the Los Angeles-based New Orleanian cocktail-loving disc jockey behind GumboPages.com. Chuck reports that after trying the drink for the first time at a 2005 Christmas party, Ted "Dr Cocktail" Haigh declared, "Oh, this is delightful!" adding, "It's like sucking on Santa!"

In French and Portuguese, a réveillon is a long dinner, and often a social gathering, held on the evenings preceding Christmas Day and New Year's Day. In the same way that Americans seem to celebrate St Patrick's Day more fervently than the Irish, Réveillon is particularly celebrated in New Orleans, either due to the city's strong French heritage or it being a damn good excuse for a nosh-up and party. 

Réveillon comes from the French word, réveil meaning 'waking' (or 'alarm clock' according to the very literal Google Translate). Why the name? Well, any good party should last through the early hours. Right!

name: Cosmopolis cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of flamed orange zest.
review: A riff on a Cosmopolitan with the distinctive Greek influence of mastiha.
history: For more recipes, origins and history behind the Cosmopolitan cocktail, see our Cosmopolitan page.

name: Caipirovska
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
MUDDLE lime wedges in the base of shaker to release juice and oils from zest.
SHAKE with 7oz scoop crushed ice and pour all without straining into glass.
Serve with straws.
review: Vodka replaces cachaça in this Caipirinha-style drink.

name: Ionian Spritz
instructions: POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass and lightly stir.
review: The Greeks and Italians are separated by the Ionian Sea, in this Spritz both countries come together with Italian Prosecco and Greek Mastiha.
history:
Created in August 2015 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.
See: Spritz cocktail history

name: Mastiha Mojito
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
CHURN (stir) with bar spoon
Fill glass with more crushed ice and CHURN some more
Keep adding ice and churning until drink fills glass
Serve with straws.
review: Mastiha adds a distinctively Greek fresh flavour to this otherwise fairly classic mojito.
history: For more recipes, its origins and history, see the Mojito Cocktail.

name: Mastiha and Tonic
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR ingredients into chilled glass.
FILL glass with ice.
garnish: Garnish with celery stalk.
review: The very distinctive taste of mastiha with freshening tonic water.

name: Mastiha Sour
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE (without ice) and strain into ice-filled glass.
review: Some use a whole shot of lemon juice and correspondingly a tad more sugar. It’s also common to add a dash of Angostura bitters but we prefer the refreshing flavour of mastiha unhindered.

name: Fresh White Lady cocktail
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE without ice and strain into chilled glass.
review: Mastiha and peppermint bitters bring minty freshness to this riff on the classic White Lady.
history:
Created by yours truly in August 2015 at the Cabinet Room in London, England. Inspired after making the same drink with white crème de menthe (Minty White Lady) and wondering how it would be with mastiha.
White Lady cocktail history and recipe variations

name: Caipiruva
instructions: MUDDLE lime wedges and grapes in base of shaker to release juices
Add cachaça and sugar
SHAKE with 6oz scoop crushed ice and pour all without straining into glass.
review: A grape juice laced twist on the Caipirinha.
history: Created by Dale DeGroff, New York, USA.

name: Tommi's Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express orange zest twist over cocktail and discard. Garnish with maraschino cherry.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: A delicately bittersweet aperitif cocktail that heroes mezcal complexity.
history: Adapted from a cocktail discovered in 2014 at Wahaca's Mezcalería Cocktail Bar, London, England. This cocktail is named after the fabulous Thomasina "Tommi" Miers OBE, who co-founded the Wahaca chain of Mexican street food restaurants.

name: Perfectly Paired cocktail
ingredients: Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: Garnish with pear wedge on rim and dust with cinnamon powder.
review: This autumnal champagne cocktail has notes of tart orchard fruit with vanilla and cinnamon laced with grassy cachaça.
history: Created in 2015 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Black Jack
ingredients: 45 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with a Black Jack chew.
review: For us Brits, this cocktail rekindles memories of the tuck shop and a black tongue from chewing on far too many Black Jacks. Happily, booze now takes the place of the sugar rush.

name: Byrrh Cassis
ingredients: 45 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass in the following order and lightly stir.
review: Rich berry and red wine flavours are lengthened and enlivened with sparkling water.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Le Fizz cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with soda.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: A poor man's champagne cocktail. Refreshing and, providing consumed whilst still cold, unknowingly boozy.
history: Created in 2013 by Joe McCanta, London, England.

name: Keyser Söze
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Gin and cognac sit surprisingly well together as base ingredients with gentian and citrus freshness.
history: Keyser Söze is a fictional character and the main protagonist in the 1995 film The Usual Suspects, while Suze, spelt with a 'u', is the bittersweet gentian flavoured liqueur and main protagonist in this cocktail.

name: Le Sang Et Sable
ingredients: 15 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist (discarded) and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: Reminiscent of a brandy-based Blood & Sand.
history: Adapted from a recipe by Toby Cecchini in New York City, USA.

name: Great Northern
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Prepare garnish of half orange slice.
review: Your choice of honey will greatly influence this drink which harnesses the character of aquavit to great effect with white wine and citrusy notes.
history: Created in 2011 by Jessica Gonzalez at Death & Company in New York City, USA.

name: Foghorn Cocktail
instructions: SHAKE (or stir) all ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Triple sec sweetens dry gin with zesty orange while aromatised wine adds vinous notes. However, it's absinthe that sets this Martini-style cocktail apart. According to bartending convention, this cocktail should be stirred but I much prefer it shaken as when first served this gives the Foghorn a cloudiness that befits its name.
history: A riff on the Corpse Reviver No. 2 created in the Noughties by Ryan Fitzgerald in San Francisco, USA. Absinthe is said to "give the drink an opalescence reminiscent of San Francisco's fog", hence this cocktail's name.

name: La Vendangeuse
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE grapes in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist (discarded) and grapes on stick.
review: Depending on the ripeness of your grapes, and the sweetness of your palate, you may want to stretch to half a shot sugar syrup. Strike the right balance and you’ll be rewarded with a medley of white grapes, white wine and botanical gin freshness with faint, cleansing zesty citrus.

name: Smokin' Paloma
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring grapefruit soda.
garnish: Serve naked, without garnish.
review: Mezcal adds its distinctive flavours to a classic Paloma.
history: Adapted from a recipe discovered in 2015 at Building on Bond in Brooklyn, New York City, USA.

name: Smokin' Hot Paloma
instructions: SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with grapefruit soda.
review: Mezcal provides a subtle hint of smokiness while chilli liqueur adds hot spice. The other guests to Paloma's party bring fruity honeyed sweetness.
history: Adapted from a drink discovered in 2015 at Building on Bond in Brooklyn, NYC, USA.

name: Alexander The Great
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: A creamy combination of vodka-laced coffee and chocolate. (The optional egg white adds body but there's no need to dry shake.)
history: Adapted from a recipe in Lucius Beebe's 1946 The Stork Club Bar Book, where this cocktail is described as "An improvement, as some may think, on the conventional Alexander cocktail," and is credited to a bartender called Nelson Eddy.

name: What's Up, Doc?
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with baby carrot and mint sprig.
review: Carrot juice gives this drink an orange hue and combines surprisingly well with gin. Extra flavour and spice comes courtesy of ginger liqueur, lemon juice and cinnamon.
history:
I discovered this drink at The Wayland in Manhattan's East Village. The What's Up, Doc? cocktail became popular across New York City in 2014, with numerous versions using different base spirits such as vodka, rye whiskey and grappa.

"Eh, What's up Doc?" is the catchphrase of Bugs Bunny, the nonchalant cartoon character who stars in the series of animated short films produced by Warner Bros.

name: Pablo à Paris
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: A dry and angular aperitif with robust flavours of aged rhum agricole and gentian liqueur, lengthened and tamed by dry vermouth with dashes of absinthe.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in March 2015 by Julian de Féral of the Gorgeous Group for Culinaire Bazaar, Paris, France.

Inspired by Picasso and the time he spent in Paris, where at the turn of the 20th century he painted one of his most important and influential masterpieces, Les Demoiselles d'Avognon. As well as being fond of absinthe, Picasso painted a cubist interpretation of a Suze bottle. To quote Julian, "the use of rhum agricole draws parallels between the African influence on Picasso and the culture in Caribbean French colonies." Julian's original recipe calls for Barbancourt 8 rhum agricole, Suze and Dolin Blanc.

name: Calvados Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Apple is somewhat dominated by orange but a pleasing dry, zesty, fruity combo nonetheless.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Batida
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
(ALTERNATIVELY, BLEND all ingredients with crushed ice.)
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Thick and creamy with fortifying underlying cachaça spirit.
history: Originating from Brazil, in Portuguese, batida means shaken or milkshake. Along with the Caipirinha, the Batida could be considered the country's national drink. Both drinks are based on Brazil's national spirit, cachaça.

name: Popeye Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE spinach in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with spinach leaf pegged to rim.
review: Spinach gives this cachaça-based cocktail a vibrant green colour while its flavour is influenced by lime, ginger and rich almond.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Juanillo Falcon at Creps al Born in Barcelona, Spain.

name: Mure and Lavender Batida
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
BLEND all ingredients with crushed ice.
Serve with straws.
garnish: Prepare garnish of blackberries.
review: As the name suggests this creamy Batida is flavoured with blackberries and lavender.
history: Recipe by Jorge Balbontin, Barcelona, Spain.

name: The Cornichon
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cocktail onion and cornichon.
review: A cornucopia of flavours with which to get pickled.
history: Created in August 2015 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England. Refined and much improved in November 2023.

name: Great Chase
instructions: POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass and stir
(Rather than measuring what is around a barspoon of lemon juice, you may prefer to gently squeeze the lemon wedge garnish prior dropping into the drink.
review: A lightly elderflower, relatively dry and refreshing spritz-style drink.
history: First promoted by the Chase Distillery late in 2015, the original specification called for Chase Vodka and Chase Elderflower, hence the name.

name: Elder Fashion
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
STIR.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: Spirit-forward and cleansing with gin, floral elderflower with zesty citrus.
history: Created by in 2007 by Phil Ward at Death & Co in New York City, USA.

name: Sidecar No. Blue
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: One orange liqueur, blue curacao, replaces another, triple sec, to give this Sidecar an aquamarine hue.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in February 2016 by Matthias Lataille, London, England.

See: Sidecar, its history and variations.

name: Cherry Mojito
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE mint (just to bruise) in base of glass.
ADD first 4 ingredients to glass and two-thirds fill with crushed ice.
CHURN (stir) with bar spoon.
Fill glass with more crushed ice and briefly CHURN.
TOP with soda.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: A Mojito with a touch of cherry. Beware, this is no cherry bomb and those looking for something fruitier and sweeter should add 2.5 to 5ml (½ to 1 barspoon) sugar syrup.
history: One of many cherry riffs on the classic Mojito. This one by yours truly in December 2021.

name: The Affiliate
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Judicious dashing of orange bitters and hitting the correct dilution will make or break this drink, which is a fine balance between aged rum and rich cherry liqueur versus dry fino sherry and aromatic bitters.
history: Created in 2013 by Charles Joly at The Aviary, Chicago, USA.

name: Santa Marta Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
FLOAT a spoon of kirsch eau-de-vie.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge on rim.
review: A float of Kirsch eau-de-vie adds wonderfully fruity aromatics to an otherwise classic Natural Daiquiri.
history: This version of a classic Daiquiri was discovered in 2011 by Joerg Meyer within the pages of the 1948 edition of the Cuban Cantinero bartender's own journal El Arte del Cantinero Los Vinos Y Los Licores. However, the cocktail was first published in the 1930 edition of the Cuban book, Club de Cantineros de la Republica de Cuba Manual Oficial.

name: Carré Nouveau
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist .
review: Reminiscent of a Vieux Carré but with aromatised wine replacing rosso vermouth and gentian replacing aromatic bitters.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in February 2016 by Matthias Lataille in London, England.

name: Velours de France
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE (without ice) and fine strain into a chilled glass.
review: Almond and vanilla liqueur combine harmoniously with cognac with lime juice and bitters adding freshness and balance.
history: Created in 2015 by Edgar Pacheco of World Class Bar Celebrity Cruises, Mexico.

name: History and Nobility
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Cognac stirred with orange and apricot liqueurs along with a dash of orange bitters. The original recipe calls for two dashes but go too heavy with your bitters and this subtle drink will be wrecked. Achieving perfect dilution is also key, hence our adding half a shot chilled water (ice allowing of course).
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Daniel Bovey at Be At One, Bristol, England.

name: Blue Nightcap
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of float 3 espresso beans.
review: Two different sherries, honey and coffee combine with cognac and walnut bitters to produce an after dinner cocktail.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2015 by Vito Nicotra at King Schulz Bar, Kassel, Germany.

name: Velvet Roof
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf.
review: Rich berry notes and subtle ginger compliment cognac freshened with lemon and dry aperitif.
history: Created by Adam Murphy at Tavern 219, Palatine, IL, USA

name: Papa Noel
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of cinnamon stick (& originally with a sugar rim).
review: Cognac, aromatic dry vermouth, rich honey, lemon juice and crème de banana with a dash of Angostura.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Vlachakis Marios at A for Athens, Athens, Greece. Vlachakis’ original recipe calls for Martell XO and Martini Ambrato vermouth.

name: The Southern Alliance
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish, along with skewered maraschino cherry.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist and Luxardo Maraschino Cherries.
review: Brandy shines with a delicate fruitiness in this dryish, bittersweet stirred cocktail served on the rocks.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Angelos Bafas at Fabrica Cocktail Bar in Drama, Greece.

name: Desire
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Rosemary sprig.
review: An elderflower influenced cognac sour sweetened with agave nectar and served on-the-rocks.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Traverso Alessandro at Mistral lounge bar in Recco, Genova, Italy.

name: Rachel
ingredients: Top up with Soda (club soda) water
instructions: SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda water and drizzle crème de cassis on surface of drink
review: Subtle lychee notes shine through this long refreshing cocktail with cognac, lemon and a drizzle of crème de cassis.
history: Created in 2015 by Schwarz Hiltraut, France using Martell V.S.O.P. cognac, this cocktail is named after Jean Martell's wife.

name: Rock of Sages
instructions: MUDDLE cherries in base of shaker
Add sage leaves and lightly bruise with the muddler
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass.
review: As the name suggests, sage subtly influences this fruity cognac based cocktail served on the rocks. Fruity and easy.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Dominic Krempels at Archer Street, Soho, London, England. Dominic's original recipe calls for Martell XO cognac and Cocchi Storico Vermouth di Torino rosso vermouth.

name: Preserve Nippon
instructions: Gently MUDDLE shisho leaf in base of shaker (just to bruise), add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: A vanilla lime cognac sour with Shisho leaf aromatics.
history: Created in 2015 by John Stavroulakis at Zaxari kai Alati Bistrot, Athens, Greece

name: Seventh Son
instructions: SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass
Carefully pour beer to FLOAT on top of drink.
review: Cognac forms the base of this cocktail which resembles a luxurious glass of beer, and indeed beer tops the drink. Rich cherry and rose lightly sweeten.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Richard Grimsey at Encore Brasserie Londonderry, Ireland.

name: L'Anniversaire (The Birthday)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Rich yet bittersweet. A good after-dinner digestive.
history: Created in 2015 by Pier Schirru at Century Club in London, England

name: Quitting Ordinary
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Earl Grey tea tannins balance rich crème de cassis in this bittersweet, aperitif cocktail.
history: Created in 2015 by Tom Blundell at Cosy Kettle, London, England.

name: Good Afternoon
instructions: STIR all ingredients with ice and strain into chilled tea cup.
review: Created in 2015 by Roberto Conti at Mojito Café, Italy.
history: Who'd have guessed cognac, genever, white tea and cinnamon syrup could taste so good.

name: Late Night
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of dust with freashly grated tonka bean and dried rose petals.
review: This spirit-forward stirred cocktail combines fruity pear, dry oloroso sherry and fortifying cognac.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2015 by Claudia Agliano at 34 Restaurant in Mayfair, London, England. Claudia's original recipe specifies 52.5ml Martell XO cognac, 22.5ml poire william liqueur, 15ml oloroso sherry and 3 drops Abbotts bitters.

name: Fedora Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: As David says of his drink, "Much like its namesake garment, this cocktail is simultaneously sharp and smooth; and whilst it's rather cool, it's also likely to keep your head warm."
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2015 by David Langley at the Boilermaker in Nottingham, England.

name: Sunny Side Up
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of dehydrated grapefruit and rhubarb slice.
review: Bittersweet zesty citrus and cognac with aromatic wine.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2015 by Christopher McQuillan at Am:pm, Belfast, Northern Ireland.

name: A Few of My Favourite Things
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Bittersweet with zesty citrus, rich sherry, cognac and faint herbal Chartreuse.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Dale Bebington at Hawksmoor Air Street, London, England. Dale’s original recipe specifies Martell Cordon Bleu cognac.

name: Continental Exchange
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SLING GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of mint sprig and dehydrated apple slice..
review: Long and refreshing with subtle flavours of apple, lemon, ginger and plum wine combining well with the cognac base.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Kieran Grieves at Bamboo Door in Sheffield, England.

name: Red Head
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: A bittersweet aperitif style cocktail with light strawberry and delicate lavender notes.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Miran Chauhan at The Bon Vivant, Scotland.

name: Berry Flip
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: A most unusual brandy-laced flip with rich blackberry and lingering ginger spice. Slightly on the sweet side.
history: Created in 2015 by William Baker at Fu Manchu in Clapham North, London, England.

name: French Riviera
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange slice and mint sprig.
review: Cognac provides the French influence and golden rum the sunny warmth with flavours of apricot, honey and lemon.
history: Created in 2015 by Tom Byrne at Charlotte's W4, Chiswick, London, England.

name: Cherry Velvet
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Rich Pedro Ximénez sherry and Italian amaro add complexity and depth of flavour to luxurious brandy.
history: Created in 2015 by Mickael Lenu at Roka Aldwych in London, England. Mickael's original recipe calls for Martell Cordon Bleu cognac.

name: Thyme for...
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
DASH bitters over sugar cube in stirring glass.
MUDDLE cube to crush.
ADD other ingredients and stir to dissolve sugar.
Add ice and STIR.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with scorched thyme sprig.
review: It most certainly is! As the name suggests, aromatic thyme subtly influences this spirituous cognac-based cocktail, with Grand Marnier adding rich but delicate zesty orange notes.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Tom Noori at Montpellier Wine Bar in Cheltenham, England

name: The Sun King
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of white chocolate and fig.
review: Jay explained the origins of this drink as follows, "Whilst on holiday in the south of France this summer I discovered a fig tree growing on the riverside near our Chateau. During dinner, my family challenged me to make a drink with the figs. I raided the cupboards and found Martell VSOP, chocolate liqueur, tawny port and drinking chocolate. The rest is History! I hope you enjoy it!"
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Jay Decker at The Ivy in Chelsea Garden, London, England.

name: Cordial Inviter
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist (discard).
review: Peach liqueur adds subtle orchard fruit notes to cognac with dry vermouth balancing and added complex wine and botanical complexity.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Connor O'Doherty at The Maven in Leeds, England.

name: Le Traite des Pyrenees (The Treaty of the Pyrenees)
instructions: SMOKE the inside of glass with a lightly fragrant cigar and cover
STIR all ingredients with ice and strain into cigar smoked glass.
review: Rich and flavoursome Pedro Ximénez sherry stirred with cognac bitters and delicately influenced by cigar smoke
history: Created in 2015 by Johan Tufvesson at Kosmopol Bar, London, England. Johan’s original recipe calls for Martell XO cognac.

name: Velvet Seine
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of spot 2 drops of Angostura bitters on surface of drink.
review: Reminiscent of a French Martini without the pineapple juice but with the balancing lemon sourness and an even thicker creamy foamy head thanks to the addition of egg white.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Leigh Brown at Hernando's Hideaway in Warrington, Cheshire, England.

name: L'apocalypse Ultime (The ultimate Apocalypse)
ingredients: Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: STIR first 5 ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass
TOP with sparkling wine.
review: This cognac and champagne cocktail is dry and boozy with hints of zesty orange.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Alexander Sourmpatis at Spinte at The Whisky Bar, Thessaloniki, Greece. Alexander's original recipe calls for Martell Cordon Bleu cognac.

name: Early Evening Fizz
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE (without ice) and fine strain into chilled glass (no ice in glass)
POUR soda into drink slowly from a height in order to build a (Ramos Gin Fizz style) thick foamy head
Serve with a straw.
review: Cognac, chartreuse, sloe gin and lemon juice served in a tall foaming fizz – its name inspired by the Morning Glory Fizz.
history: Created in 2015 by Nick Caputo at Sovereign Loss in Brixton, England

name: Charentes Cocktail
instructions: MUDDLE grapes in base of mixing glass
Add other ingredients, STIR with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
review: White grape juice fortified with cognac and lightly sweetened by pineau des charentes with faint hints of chocolate.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Steve Lawson at M.A.S.H, Soho, London, England

name: Charentes
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with raspberries.
review: Kummel's caraway flavours heavily influence this unusual cognac-based cocktail with Pineau des Charentes and lemon juice.
history: Created in 2015 by Tiarnan O'Gara at MASH, London, England.

name: Caribbean Breeze
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: A long cocktail with generous tangy fruit.

name: Brandy Club Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange and lemon grated zest mixed with caster sugar rim.
review: Cognac and zesty orange curacao with sherry. The sugary rim is essential to the balance of this drink.
history: Adapted from a 2015 recipe by Ryu Okada at Kampai Cocktails (a mobile cocktail bar company based in Sussex, England.)

name: Chocolat a l'orange (Orange Chocolate)
ingredients: 15 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: STIR all ingredients with ice and strain into a large brandy balloon over a chunk of block ice.
review: Cognac stirred with aromatised wine, rich orange liqueur with subtle chocolate, cinnamon and zesty orange.
history: Adapted from a 2015 recipe by Jan Kosler at The Celtic Manor Resort, Coldra Woods, The Usk Valley, Wales. Jan’s original recipe calls for Martell XO Cognac.

name: French Kiss No.3
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE raspberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered raspberry.
review: Raspberry and rich almond tamed by lime with underlying brandy character.
history: Created in 2015 by Chanelle O Connor at Salomontini Le Resto in London, England.

name: Grape Expectations
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into a chilled glass.
Two-thirds fill glass with crushed ice and SWIZZLE.
Fill glass with more crushed ice.
garnish: Garnish and serve with a straw.
review: A sauvignon blanc wine, rose liqueur, apple and lemon brandy-based swizzle. Fruity, floral and refreshing.
history: Created in 2015 by Ehren Khoo-Steel at The Blind Pig in St Helier, Jersey.

name: Le Château
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: Brandy stirred and served straight-up with a combination of French herbal and Italian bittersweet liqueurs.
history: Created in 2015 by Scott Burgess at Some Place, Liverpool, England.

name: Sainte Trinite (Holy Trinity)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review:
Cognac, elderflower liqueur and Chartreuse (a trinity indeed) with dashes of bitters and an aromatic citrus twist. This is a boozy cocktail so you may prefer our 25% smaller riff to suit a Nick & Nora rather than the original Coupe glass:
45ml / 1½oz Cognac
15ml / ½oz Elderflower liqueur
10ml / 1/3oz Yellow Chartreuse
1 dash Orange bitters
history: Created by Lewis Roberts at Tariff & Dale in Manchester, England.

name: IS talking
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with tonic water and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge on rim.
review: A brandy and green-based G&T with a zesty citrus influence.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Hans Berendijk at Buster's Bar in Amsterdam, Netherlands.

name: Coffee Thyme
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of thyme sprig.
review: A cognac based Espresso Martini with herbal hints of Fernet Branca and thyme.
history: Created in 2015 by Andrew Cotter at Hawksmoor Air Street, London, England.

name: Nouvelle Histoire (New Story)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf.
review: A refreshing citrusy and minty cognac cocktail.
history: Adapted from a created in 2015 by Eric van Oers at Blind Pig London, England.

name: Elixir De Longue Vie (Elixir of Long Life)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Chestnut, pineau de charente and chartreuse combine with cognac to create a most unusual cocktail with zesty lemon freshness.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Kevin Galliano at Big Easy, Covent Garden, London, England.

name: Harry Dean Stanton
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: The choice and generosity of dash of chocolate bitters is key to the success of this boozy after dinner cocktail. Nail this and the chocolate amplifies fig and raison notes.
history: Created in 2015 by Will Howson at Tonkotsu Mare Street, London, England, and named after the American actor, musician, and singer.

name: The Corpse's Whiskers
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Mickey Britton, the creator of this drink describes it as "the missing link between three classic cocktails: Corpse Reviver No.2, a Satan's Whiskers and the infamous Sidecar."
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Mickey Britton at Public House, Islington, London, England.
Corpse Reviver history and recipe variations

name: Rosa Picante Margarita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of rose-Sea salt rim (optional)Rose petal (optional).
review: A refreshing Margarita, with subtle ginger, pepper spice and rose notes. You can substitute half a barspoon of jalapeno oil in place of the jalapeno slice. We found that chilli liqueur also works well.
history: The 2016 Margarita of the Year as selected by Patron tequila.

name: Rose Crescent
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Ginger and cognac with underlying whisky and herbal complexity.
history: Created in 2015 by Sean Baylis at La Raza, Cambridge, England

name: V.S.M. Flip
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain into chocolate sprayed glass.
review: Creamy and velvety chocolate, coffee and cognac.
history: Created in 2015 by Shaun Spurrier at Harvey Nichols Bar, Birmingham, England.

name: Caribbean Punch
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: Red in colour and innocent looking, this flavoursome drink sure packs a punch.

name: The Burly French Man
instructions: RINSE chilled glass with absinthe and set to one side
STIR other ingredients with ice and strain into absinthe rinsed glass
review: Boozy and served Sazerac-style, cognac with powerful Chartreuse herbal notes and faintly jammy fruit.
history: Created in 2015 by Tommy Long at the Groucho Club in London, England. Originally made with Cocchi Storico Vermouth di Torino rosso vermouth.

name: A L'Ancienne du Bois (A woody Old Fashioned)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
MUDDLE cherries with bitters and maple syrup in base of glass.
Add 3 ice cubes and STIR.
Add brandy and 3 more ice cubes and STIR again.
Add walnut liqueur and 2 more ice cubes and STIR some more.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Despite the name, this Old Fashioned style drink is more fruity and nutty than it is woody, but it's tasty all the same.
history: Created in 2015 by Gary Anderson at Gingers Bar at Purnell's Bistro, Birmingham, England, who says, "The drink came from wanting to make a cognac Old Fashioned (hence a l'ancienne). The maple, griottines and Peychaud's bitters were introduced to keep the idea of the cocktail being very French. Bizarrely, the walnut came from attempting a Google search for "maple and cherry" which displayed an antique wood specialist who along with maple and cherry wood also used walnut. Therefore my drink became "A Woody Old Fashioned" or "A L'Ancienne du Bois".

name: XOXO
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A rich, chocolaty after dinner drink with cognac, pedro ximénez sherry, chocolate and orange liqueurs.
history: Created in 2015 by Josh Powell at Somerset House in Bristol, England.

name: French Diamond
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: This citrusy cognac-laced cocktail is fruity and edging towards the sweet side of balanced with maple syrup, lemon and orange juice.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Ciprian Zsraga at The Hoxton Pony in London, England.

name: Chanteloup Champagne Flip
ingredients: Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: SHAKE first 7 ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE (without ice) and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with champagne.
review: Complex and dry with faint rich raisiny sherry and floral notes.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Chris Pawar at The Luggage Room Bar, London, England. Chris’ original recipe calls for Martell Cordon Bleu cognac.

name: Bakewell Tart Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
Express lemon (or orange) zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherries.
review: Exactly as the name suggests. Also works well served straight-up in a coupe.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Sean Cadger at The Outhouse in Edinburgh, Scotland.

name: Suit and Tai
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: A Tiki-styled cocktail based on aged rum and brandy with lime juice, orange liqueur and almond syrup.
history: Created in 2015 by Matthew Neale at Harbour Kitchen in Torquay, England.

name: Heard It Through The Grapevine
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Garnish with frozen grapes.
review: Delicate and wonderfully complex.
history: Created by Alex Morrell in 2015 at Central and Co. in London, England.

name: Soir d'Ete (Summer Evening)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist and fresh lavender sprig/dried lavender blossom.
review: Fruity cognac with delicate floral tea notes.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Paride Fameli at the Lounge cocktail bar, Novotel London West, England.

name: Jersey Rose
instructions: SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE (without ice) and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP up with cider and add bitters onto foam for aroma.
review: Cognac laced bittersweet strawberry, pomegranate and cider.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Joe Macbeth at Rub Smokehouse & Bar, Nottingham, England.

name: Grapefruit Soda
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Garnish with grapefruit wedge.
review: Superbly refreshing and not at all sweet. A great drink for a sunny afternoon.

name: Amsterdamage
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A genever-based Wet Martini with an orange influence courtesy of triple sec.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Lee Clarke after he and a few friends visited the Bols Museum in Amsterdam. His recipe, a simple variation on a Vodka Martini with equal parts vodka, dry vermouth and triple sec, became the trip's signature cocktail. Given the Amsterdam connection, we thought it appropriate to try using genever in place of vodka with an added dash of orange bitters.

If being in this book, we're not fame enough, in 2023, the Amsterdamage was made on BBC television's Nigella's Amsterdam Christmas Special.

name: Casablanca No.2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: Creamy, citrusy, alcoholic custard.

name: Cascade
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of skewered raspberries.
review: Rich raspberry with hints of citrus and vanilla.

name: Cassini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered blackberries.
review: Not a Cassis Martini, but a Cassini.
history:
Created in 1998 by yours truly and reformulated on 11th October 2025. The original recipe:
45ml (1½oz) Vodka
7.5ml (¼oz) London dry gin
7.5ml (¼oz) Crème de cassis
45ml (1½oz) Cranberry juice

name: Hotel Georgia Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange zest twist &/or dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: An almond gin sour with orange flower water adding a delicate floral note. Lighter and more pleasing than the recipe might suggest.
history:
Adapted from a recipe in Ted Saucier's 1951 Bottoms Up. The original calls for 1oz (30ml) orgeat syrup, which we guess was made to a light 1:1 ratio rather than the richer 2:1 used in the recipe above. Interestingly, Saucier specifies to dry shake, saying "Shake well before adding ice. This gives a nice 'top.'" (I prefer to reverse dry shake as specified in my recipe above.) Saucier's 1951 instruction to dry shake this cocktail is the first known reference to the use of this technique.

The historic 12-story Hotel Georgia opened in downtown Vancouver in 1927 but its signature cocktail, thought to have been created in the mid-1940s, doesn't appear in print until 1951 in Saucier's book. The hotel was refurbished and reopened in 2011 as the Rosewood Hotel Georgia with its original 313 rooms reduced to 155. The hotel is number 11158 in the Canadian Register of Historic Places.

name: Celery Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of salt rim and celery stick.
review: Celery is usually only tasty when loaded with blue cheese. Vodka also seems to make it rather palatable.
history: Created by Andreas Tsanos at Momos, London, England in 2001.

name: Alexandra
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: Creamy and chocolaty - reminiscent of a rum-laced, creamy mocha coffee.

name: Champagne Cup
ingredients: Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: STIR first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass
TOP with sparkling wine and briefly stir.
review: Sweet maraschino helps balance this dry drink.

name: Prohibition Daisy
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
CHURN (stir) with bar spoon.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: A lightly sweetened fruit smoothie with orange, pineapple, and raspberry along with lemon and lime which provide balancing citrus acidity.

name: Charles Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Navy rum and triple sec add special interest to this Daiquiri.
history: Origin unknown, and as far as we are aware, the Charles Daiquiri doesn't feature in any well-known cocktail books.

name: Optimist
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled, rimmed glass.
EXPRESS grapefruit zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit zest twist.
review: Fino sherry and Creole bitters brilliantly set this riff on a classic Daiquiri apart. Beautifully balanced and delicately flavoured. If you apply the charcoal rim, perhaps leave a lipstick saving space rather than encircling the rim.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2014 for Bacardi Legacy by Roman Foltan at the Artesian Bar at The Langham Hotel, London, England.

name: A.B.C. Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: Wonderfully delicate. Mint gives subtle freshness to the classic port and brandy combo.
history: Not to be confused with the earlier and quite different cocktail of the same name which appears in the 1929 book, Cocktails de Paris Présentés Par RIP. This version, the one that has found longevity, first appears in the 1935 menu cocktail book Bar la Florida Cocktails.

name: Cheeky Monkey
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Fire yellow in colour, this drink features the distinctive flavour of Chartreuse with a citrus supporting cast.
history: Created in 2001 by Tony Conigliaro at Isola, Knightsbridge, London, England.

name: Cherry Alexander
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: A rich Black Forest Gateau-like fruity twist on the creamy classic gin-based Alexander and the popular Brandy Alexander. Despite the inclusion of egg white, best not to dry shake this cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created byWayne Collins for Maxxium UK.

name: Meh-Groni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: This Negroni-inspired cocktail will please mezcal loving bartenders and hardened drinkers (we also loved it) but may challenge other drinkers.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Jesse R. Raines at Vieux Carre, Washington DC, USA. Recipe courtesy of gazregan.com.

name: Original Sin (Gin Joint recipe)
instructions: SWIZZLE all ingredients in a Julep cup with crushed ice.
review: Lemon and honey with a touch of bittersweet flavoursome gentian liqueur.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 at the Gin Joint, Athens, Greece.

name: Mexican Vineyard
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE grapes in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered grapes.
review: Perhaps best summarised as being an 'Apple and Grape Margarita', the flavours combine harmoniously in this well-balanced cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created circa 2005 by Stephen Watts at a cocktail bar somewhere in deepest Nottingham, England.

name: Anita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with basil leaf.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created by Thalita Alves in Australia for Campari. Thalita says, "There's only one thing I adore almost as much as mixing beautiful drinks: dancing. Imagining original combinations of ingredients for a cocktail isn't so different from developing a sequence of steps in a well-structured choreography.

"Right foot forward, left foot sweeps in a semi-circle, right foot forward, left foot raised. I still remember how the first choreography I worked on started. I repeated it hundreds of times in front of the mirror, striving for perfect coordination with the other girls. Don't go imagining the corps de ballet at La Scala: we were just an amateur dance company, but we were full of passion and determination.
 
"But choreographies also need a good story, research, inspiration that comes from somewhere. That was always my favourite part of the entire dance creation process: to choose the message, the idea to be translated into motion. I found a muse that inspired me in piecing together a short act: Anita Garibaldi, the greatest Brazilian heroine, a wonderful mix of romance and strength. That would have been amazing to dance and to watch.

"But I never finished my final work. While working on Anita, I discovered a new Thalita. As a girl who had never gone very far from home, I discovered I had a big dream and a life-long passion to do and see more. 
I felt an inexplicable need to seek out new places and suddenly I found myself in an airport for the very first time. I caught a plane to Australia: an unknown country where I had no friends or family, didn't speak the language and had no career. But I knew that this place would be my home.

"I put my creativity to one side for almost 5 years, too busy building my new life. Having gone from job to job, one day I ended up in a cocktail bar. While mixing my first drink I felt that spark from my dancing days again, putting different things together to create a whole new something. My passion was back, albeit in a new shape.

"When I realised I could finally tell stories again, this time in liquid form, I started working on my favourite one: that of Italian revolutionary Giuseppe Garibaldi, who fell in love with Anita, a person of strength and resilience but at the same time also extremely tender and charming. Anita was reborn, as a cocktail.
 
"I decided to narrate that intercontinental love story through the most emblematic spirits and flavours of the two worlds: Campari and fresh and juicy blood oranges symbolising Italy, Sagatiba Velha and bright green limes for Brazil. I sealed this union with a macadamia nut orgeat to celebrate the country I now call home: Australia. Anita, this symbolic mix of 2 worlds, is garnished with fragrant and aromatic basil leaves to finish.

"This cocktail, like the heroine whose name it bears, has become an icon of something much bigger to me: Australia itself, and all the people that have mixed together over the centuries and moved to this beautiful country to build brand new lives here and, ultimately, make it what it is."

name: Alpha
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE raspberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with star anise floated on surface.
review: A tequila-laced, bittersweet raspberry sour.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Patrice Plante, Canada, originally Campari Bitter and for Campari Red Diaries series.

name: Commedia All'italiana
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist..
review: Full-flavoured and bittersweet. Some will prefer this aperitivo with the dilution that comes with serving over ice in an old-fashioned glass.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Seba Atienza in Argentina for Campari.

name: Killer in Red
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of drop of rose essential oil.
history: Revealed in January 2017, this is the cocktail after which the Campari Red Diaries’ founding story, Killer in Red is named.

name: Gringo's Blood and Sand
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express orange zest twist over cocktail and discard. Garnish with maraschino cherry.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: A Blood & Sand made with tequila. A classic Scotch whisky-based Blood & Sand is made with equal parts of all four ingredients, but the cherry brandy, vermouth and orange seem to overwhelm tequila when used in place of Scotch, hence the much higher proportion of spirit in this riff.
history: Created in February 2017 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Jalisco Mint Julep
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and fine strain into julep cup half-filled with crushed ice
CHURN (stir) the drink with the crushed ice using a bar spoon
Top up the cup with more crushed ice and CHURN again
Crown the drink with some fresh crushed ice, garnish and serve.
review: Given the use of tequila, I initially opted for agave syrup over sugar syrup but I found the caramel notes this added somewhat overwhelmed the mint. Instead, perhaps consider using demerara sugar in place of white cane syrup or a mix of the two. To be honest, I found myself preferring the original whiskey-based Mint Julep when tasted against each other but I'd encourage you to give a tequila-based Mint Julep a try, and if you have a better version then please email me at simon@Dsguide.com.
history:
Created in February 2017 by yours truly (Simon D) at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

For the contentious history of the Julep along with its variants, see our Julep page.

name: Tequila Club
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: The similarity of ingredients in this tequila-based riff on the classic Pegu Club to the Margarita are obvious, so it's not surprising the finished drink is reminiscent of a Margarita, only lighter with the lime notes a lot subtler. As well as the tequila, orange curaçao shines in this drink.
history: Created in February 2017 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Stone Cold Fruit
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Tart and dry with zesty orange and lemon. Mouth puckeringly refreshing.
history: Adapted from a recipe by Lana Gailani (Seamstress and Holiday Cocktail Lounge), New York City, USA. Cocktail courtesy of gazregan.com.

name: Coffee Negroni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Lightly stir.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with 3 coffee beans floated on surface.
review: Gin-forward, boozy and bittersweet – as it says on the tin, this is a coffee-influenced Negroni – simple as that.
history: Created in January 2017 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Avenue San Martin
instructions: Select and pre-chill a ROCKS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Bittersweet and tangy. A cocktail to enjoy as an apéritif with nibbles.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2016 at Bar Hommage, Stockholm, Sweden. This cocktail takes its name from a street in Lima, Peru where the creators of this cocktail once ended up at a bar called Ayahuasca.

name: The Blind Side
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of gently squeeze lemon wedge over drink and then drop into drink..
review: Fresh and refreshing – a minty gentian apéritif.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2016 by Riccardo Aletta (AKA 'Ricky D Wing') at Holy Birds in London, England.

name: Green Negroni
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Lightly stir.
garnish: Prepare garnish of galia melon slice..
review: Don’t be put off or misled by the lurid green colour or this cocktail, it is both bittersweet and complex. As the name suggests it is based in a classic Negroni but with Green Chartreuse herbal complexity and a dose of rich green melon liqueur.

name: Che's Revolution
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim.
review: Complex and easy drinking with hints of maple syrup and mint amongst the pineapple and rum.
history: Created in 2003 by Ben Reed for the launch party of MJU Bar @ Millennium Hotel in London, England

name: Dutchess
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: One of those Angostura heavy (very heavy) cocktails that intuition says shouldn't work, but thanks to the rich balancing properties of almond syrup and pineapple, it is not only balanced but tasty. The genever base justifies the perhaps misspelt name and adds its distinctive character. (The name may come from Dutchess County in New York State).
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2014 by Theo Lieberman of Lantern's Keep and Milk & Honey in New York City, USA.

name: Eggs-cellent
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with creme egg.
review: Brandy and advocaat (the eggy element) is a classic and indulgent combo that says Easter and/or Christmas – both spiritual holidays so the addition of Chartreuse is obvious. As this Eggs-cellent cocktail was made for Easter it had to have some chocolate so I added chocolate bitters.
history: Created in April 2017 by yours truly as an Easter Cocktail.

name: Grand Duchess Cocktail
ingredients: 10 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: This vodka-based Tiki-style cocktail is made "funky" by a splash of pot still Jamaican rum while lime juice adds freshness and grenadine adds both a rich hue and sweetness.

name: Rum and IT
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered olives.
review: Created in April 2017 by yours truly (Simon D) at the Cabinet Room, London, England.
history: I mixed these two ingredients together just to ascertain where to go from there when creating a drink with Italicus. Then do I add a dash of maraschino, curaçao or bitters? I tried numerous liqueurs and bitters but I like the simplicity of this two ingredient cocktail. Now try it with gin – yup, Gin & It also works well. Later, the same mixing session also gave birth to For Sake's Sake.

name: Juan and Jeanne
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime peel spiral.
review: Tequila-based with sour citrus balanced by the rich red wine-like berry flavours of cassis.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Jeff Berry at Beachbum Berry's Latitude 29, New Orleans, USA which was brought to our attention by gazregan.com where we learnt that "A Mexican base + a French modifier = Juan + Jeanne."

Our recipe says broadly true to Jeff's original proportions, but we've reduced all the ingredients by 12% to suit a medium-sized coupe.

name: Il Viaggio
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: The ingredients might suggest that this cocktail should be stirred – I tried, but it benefits from the dilution and life that shaking imparts. After some elbow grease, you'll have a delicious bittersweet, herbal almond after-dinner drink.
history: Created in October 2016 by Wouter Bosch, Senior Bartender at The Duchess in Amsterdam, Netherlands. The name is Italian for 'The Journey'.

name: Chocolate, Rum and Raisin
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with raisin and nut chocolate balanced across rim.
review: This cocktail delivers pretty much what it says on the tin – chocolate, rum and raisin. Best enjoyed after dinner with deluxe similarly flavoured chocolate.
history: Created by yours truly in April 2017 at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Killer Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Garnish with drops on cranberry bitters or grenadine on lemon twist (for bloody shark attack).
review: Amaretto and passion fruit dominate this bittersweet sour.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Tess Posthumus, Amsterdam, The Netherlands. Tess says, "The cocktail I won the international Disaronno Mixing Star finals with back in 2013. It is inspired by one of my favourite movies, Jaws."

name: Ginger and Fig Caipirinha
instructions: <strong>1.</strong> Cut lime in half (from pole to pole) and then slice one of the halves into eight 3-4mm thick half-moon shaped segments and drop into the base of a robust rocks glass
<strong>2.</strong> Add 3 bar spoons caster sugar (or ½oz / 15ml sugar syrup 2:1) and MUDDLE
<strong>3.</strong> Add fig and MUDDLE some more
<strong>4.</strong> When sure fruit juice is fully extracted and the sugar dissolved, transfer the muddled fruit and sugar into serving glass (preferably a 30cl / 10oz highball glass)
<strong>5.</strong> POUR cachaça (stored in the freezer) and ginger liqueur into the rocks glass (used to muddle) and swirl to rinse glass before pouring cachaça into serving glass
STIR to ensure all ingredients are well mixed
<strong>6.</strong> Crack (rather than crush) four to five large ice cubes and drop into serving glass
STIR before topping with more ice and serve with a stirrer (but no straws).
history: For information on the history and other variations of the Caipirinha, please see our Caipirinha cocktail page.

name: Cucumber Jalapeño Caipirinha
instructions: <strong>1.</strong> Cut lime in half (from pole to pole) and then slice one of the halves into eight 3-4mm thick half-moon shaped segments and drop into the base of a robust rocks glass
<strong>2.</strong> Add 3 bar spoons caster sugar (or ½oz/15ml sugar syrup 2:1) and MUDDLE
<strong>3.</strong> Add cucumber and jalapeño and MUDDLE some more
<strong>4.</strong> When sure fruit juice is fully extracted and the sugar dissolved, transfer the muddled fruit and sugar into serving glass (preferably a 30cl / 10oz highball glass)
<strong>5.</strong> POUR cachaça (stored in the freezer) into the rocks glass (used to muddle) and swirl to rinse glass before pouring cachaça into serving glass
STIR to ensure all ingredients are well mixed
<strong>6.</strong> Crack (rather than crush) four to five large ice cubes and drop into serving glass
STIR before topping with more ice and serve with a stirrer (but no straws)
history: For information on the history and other variations of the Caipirinha, please see our Caipirinha cocktail page.

name: Banana Caipirinha
instructions: <strong>1.</strong> Cut lime in half (from pole to pole) and then slice one of the halves into eight 3-4mm thick half-moon shaped segments and drop into the base of a robust rocks glass
<strong>2.</strong> Add 3 bar spoons caster sugar (or ½oz/15ml sugar syrup 2:1) and MUDDLE
<strong>3.</strong> Add slices of banana and MUDDLE some more
<strong>4.</strong> When sure fruit juice is fully extracted and the sugar dissolved, transfer the muddled fruit and sugar into serving glass (preferably a 30cl / 10oz highball glass)
<strong>5.</strong> POUR cachaça (stored in the freezer) into the rocks glass (used to muddle) and swirl to rinse glass before pouring cachaça into serving glass
STIR to ensure all ingredients are well mixed
<strong>6.</strong> Use an <a href="/bar-equipment-and-barware/250/ice-tapper">Ice Tapper</a> to crack (rather than crush) four to five large ice cubes and drop into serving glass
STIR before topping with more ice and serve with a stirrer (but no straws).
review: With an overripe banana this drink is delicious, thick and creamy. A meal in itself.
history: For information on the history and other variations of the Caipirinha, please see our Caipirinha cocktail page.

name: Tessmanian Devil
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of two fresh red chilli peppers positioned as devil's horns on rim.
review: The "devil" is in this cocktail's hot spice. Obviously the garnish should not be eaten.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Tess Posthumus, Amsterdam, The Netherlands. To quote Tess, "A little devil in a glass. My first cocktail that made it onto the Door 74 menu back in 2010 and made it into Gaz Regan's 101 Best New Cocktails 2011."

name: Naughty German
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Jägermeister spice and rich cassis combine wonderfully in this after-dinner sipper, with subtle black liquorice and aniseed.
history: Created in 2017 by Florian Beuren, Jägermeister's UK brand ambassador.

name: Black Forest Sour
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE (without ice) and pour into ice-filled glass.
review: Beautifully balanced with complex herbal, berry, pineapple and citrus flavours.

name: Chapeau
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered blackberry or liquorice stick.
review: The richness of blackcurrant syrup (25ml crème de cassis works well as a substitute) is balanced by the sourness of the vinegar with the liquorice and salt adding umami notes to the rich rum base.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created by Christoph Henkel at Shepheard Bar in Cologne, Germany. His original recipe won the German Bacardí Legacy 2017 Final and comprised:
60 ml Bacardí Gran Reserva 8 Años rum
20 ml Blackcurrant syrup
10 ml Vinegar aceto balsamico 
1 pinch Grated liquorice
1 pinch Salt

name: China Beach
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered crystallised ginger.
review: Dry and lightly spiced.

name: China Blue
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Looks sweet, but due to a generous splash of grapefruit is actually balanced and refreshing.
history: Emerged in Japan in the late 1990s and still popular along the Pacific Rim.

name: China Blue Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lychee dropped into cocktail.
review: This simple turquoise cocktail tastes more adult and interesting than its colour might suggest.
history: An almost inevitable short adaptation of the original long drink.

name: Blackberry Agave Caipirinha
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.

Cut a ripe lime in half (from pole to pole) and remove the core from each half by cutting a V-shaped groove. (You'll need one-half of the lime per cocktail.) CHOP into wedges and each wedge chopped in half before dropping the resulting 6 or 8 lime chunks into the base of shaker.
Add agave syrup and MUDDLE.
Add blackberries and MUDDLE some more.
When sure the lime and blackberry juice is fully extracted, POUR the muddled fruit into serving glass (preferably a 30cl / 10oz highball glass).
POUR cachaça (stored in the freezer) into the shaker tin used to muddle and swirl to rinse tin before pouring cachaça into serving glass. STIR to combine with other ingredients.
FILL glass with small cubes or cracked ice. (If using large ice cubes, crack (rather than crush) four to five large ice cubes using an ICE TAPPER and drop into serving glass.)
Briefly STIR before topping with more ice.
garnish: Serve with a stirrer (spoon, wooden lolly stick or stick cut from sugar cane) but no straws.
review: Sweetening with agave nectar rather than sugar helps set this fruity Caipirinha apart and creates a drink that is very slightly sweet.
history: For information on the history and other variations of the Caipirinha, please see our Caipirinha cocktail page.

name: The Joanna
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of small lavender sprigs pegged to rim.
review: Reminiscent of a vodkatini with fino sherry and notes of floral elderflower and lavender.
history: Created in 2017 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Dutch Sour
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE (without ice) and strain into ice-filled glass.
review: A floral orangey riff on a vodka sour.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Tess Posthumus, Amsterdam, The Netherlands. Tess' original recipe called for orange syrup rather than orange curaçao and sugar syrup.

name: Embrasse de la Terre
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with celery stalk.
review: This dry spirituous apéritif, Martini-like, cocktail combines malty genever and bitter celery flavours with Yellow Chartreuse richness dried by vermouth.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Zachary Faden of Mirabelle in Washington D.C., USA. Embrasse de la Terre translates from French to mean 'Kissing the Earth'. This cocktail was the winner of the 2017 Tales of the Cocktail Official Cocktail Competition.

Zachary's original recipe calls for 2 Rutte Old Simon Genever, 1 Dolin Dry Vermouth de Chambéry, ¼ Yellow Chartreuse and 3 dash Bitter Truth Celery Bitters.

name: Scandinavian Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of star anise floated on surface.
review: A caraway-influenced riff on a daiquiri with aquavit replacing rum. Refreshing and suited to a summer's afternoon or an early evening aperitif.
history: Adapted from a 2015 recipe by Kyle Webster at Expatriate, Portland, Oregon, USA. Kyles's original recipe of 2 aquavit, 1 lime juice and ¾ simple syrup (1:1) made from palm sugar.

name: Alice From Dallas
instructions: Select a SHOT GLASS.
Refrigerate ingredients.
LAYER in chilled glass by carefully pouring ingredients in the order listed.
review: Coffee and orange spiked with tequila. The trio layer well and combine to make a tasty shot (perhaps stir together and sip to savour the experience rather than downing in one).

name: French Kiwi and Apple Caipirinha
instructions: <strong>1.</strong> Cut lime in half (from pole to pole) and then slice one of the halves into eight 3-4mm thick half-moon shaped segments and drop into the base of a robust rocks glass
<strong>2.</strong> Add 3 bar spoons caster sugar (or ½oz / 15ml sugar syrup 2:1) and MUDDLE
<strong>3.</strong> Add Kiwi fruit and MUDDLE some more
<strong>4.</strong> When sure fruit juice is fully extracted and the sugar dissolved, transfer the muddled fruit and sugar into serving glass (preferably a 30cl / 10oz hi-ball glass)
