<strong>5.</strong> POUR cachaça (stored in the freezer) into the rocks glass (used to muddle) and swirl to rinse glass before pouring cachaça into serving glass
Add apple juice and anis liqueur, then STIR to ensure all ingredients are well mixed
<strong>6.</strong> Crack (rather than crush) four to five large ice cubes and drop into serving glass
STIR before topping with more ice and serve with a stirrer (but no straws).
history:
This kiwi and apple flavoured caipirinha is made "French" by the merest hint of anis (aniseed).

For information on the history and other variations of the Caipirinha, please see our Caipirinha cocktail page.

name: Chinese Cosmopolitan
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail, then use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Oriental in name and style - perhaps a tad sweeter than your standard Cosmo.
history:
Discovered in 2003 at Raoul's Bar, Oxford, England.

For more recipes, origins and history behind the Cosmopolitan cocktail, see our Cosmopolitan page.

name: Old Tom Gin Fruit Cup (D's Cup No.11)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into glass.
Half fill glass with ice.
Add a citrus slice and a couple of mint leaves from garnish
Fill to brim with ice.
garnish: Finish with rest of garnish.
review: It's is possible, indeed probable, that the original Pimm's Cup served in the eponymous London bar was in fact based on old tom gin rather than London dry gin. However, while flavoursome, old tom does inherently produce a sweeter style drink compared to when the same recipe is made with dry gin or other base spirits.
history: Created in June 2017 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England. For more recipes and history see Fruit Cups and Pimm's.

name: Gin and IT (Rosolio recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of 3 Fragata Green Olives on stick.
review: Bitter orange peel is a botanical which features in many gins, and in this cocktail a bitter sweet bergamot liqueur is mixed with gin to good effect.
history: One of the cocktails promoted with the launch of Italicus Rosolio di Bergamotto in the summer of 2017.

name: IPAlicus
instructions: POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass and lightly stir.
review: Take a citrusy hopped beer and add a tad more citrus in the shape of Rosolio di Bergamotto. The result is predictably citrusy, refreshing and suited to the aperitivo moment rather than a night down the Rose & Crown.
history: One of the drinks promoted with the launch of Italicus Rosolio di Bergamotto in the summer of 2017.

name: Negroni Bianco Bergamotto
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
STIR.
garnish: Garnish with skewered olive.
review: Dry with wine like-minerality, zesty bergamot and piney gin notes.
history:
Adapted from a drink promoted with the launch of Italicus Rosolio di Bergamotto in the summer of 2017. Our original recipe comprised four equal parts:
20 ml Dry gin
20 ml Luxardo Bitter Bianco
20 ml Italicus liqueur
20 Bianco vermouth

name: Caribbean Colada
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HURRICANE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple, Luxardo Maraschino Cherry and paper parasol.
review: Rum and pineapple strike up a tropical harmony, helped by coconut, passionfruit and orange.
history: Adapted from a recipe promoted by Giffard in June 2016 with the launch of their Caribbean Pineapple liqueur.

name: Tropical Pineapple Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: Pineapple and rich almond flavours add a tropical touch to this refreshing Daiquiri.
history: Adapted from a recipe promoted by Giffard in June 2016 with the launch of their Caribbean Pineapple liqueur.

name: Chinese Whisper
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE ginger in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: There's more than a whisper of ginger in this spicy cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe discovered in 2003 at Oxo Tower Bar in London, England.

name: Soixante-Quinze (Washington Herald 1915 recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: This 1915 recipe, originally of equal parts gin, applejack and grenadine (1/3 of each to 1/6 lemon juice) makes this Soixante-Quinze (Seventy-Five) a tad on the sweet side (certainly sweet if you are using a proprietary or housemade 2:1 grenadine). However, who knows what brix the original grenadine/pomegranate syrup was, so we've eased back the grenadine to bring modern-day ingredients more into kilter. The resulting drink (which benefits from a splash of water to aid dilution) remains more a fruity, spirituous after-dinner cocktail rather than an aperitif.
history:
Adapted from a 1915 recipe published in The Washington Herald.
See: French 75 history.

name: Choc and Nut Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of chocolate powder rim.
review: Surprise, surprise - it's chocolate and hazelnut.

name: Limoncello Spritz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a WINE GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: Zesty, almost creamy, lemon enlivened with fizz.
history: A modern addition to the venerable Spritz family of cocktails.

name: PLB
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
MUDDLE lemon in base of shaker and then pour into serving glass.
FILL glass with ice.
Add limoncello and bitters.
TOP with tonic water and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
review: Long and refreshing with a tart lemony bite.
history: Created in 2015 by Philip Duff for Pallini Limoncello, the initials which comprise this cocktails name are an acronym of Pallini Lemon Bitters.

name: Spiked Arnold Palmer
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
STIR.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
review: Tea tannins and sour lemon balance the rich limoncello in this refreshing low alcohol cocktail. Perfect for summer.

name: Black Basil
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
MUDDLE basil in base of shaker.
Add next 4 ingredients (all but tonic water) and SHAKE with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring tonic water.
garnish: Garnish with basil leaf.
review: Light in alcohol, with its basil and zesty lemon flavours, this wholesome-looking cocktail makes for a refreshing summer afternoon drink.

name: Nobile Cocktail
instructions: MUDDLE raspberries in base of shaker
Add next four ingredients (all but bitter lemon), SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with bitter lemon and lightly stir.
review: Fruity but not sweet, with drying bitter lemon. This is a refreshing, low alcohol, aperitif style summery cocktail.

name: Chocolate and Cranberry 'Martini'
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: No additional garnish. (The preprepared rim is garnish enough.)
review: The chocolate rim sounds naff, but it makes this cocktail.
history: Created in 2003 by yours truly in London, England.

name: Biblical Quartet
instructions: SHAKE first three ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass
TOP with dry cider.
review: Four different apple products – three of them alcoholic – combined harmoniously in one cocktail, so obviously appley and just on the dry side of balanced.
history: Created in 2017 by yours truly (Simon D) at the Cabinet Room, London, England. According to Christianity, apples have a lot to answer for?

name: Flat White Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with 3 coffee beans floated in a petal formation in the centre of the cocktail's foamy head.
review: Martini drinkers of the gin/vodka and vermouth variety will describe this drink as being all too flat and all too white, but for lovers of flat white coffees and cream liqueurs, this Martini will hit the spot – even though it's not really a Martini.
history: Developed in Australia and New Zealand in the 1980s, a "flat white" is a coffee made by pouring frothy, velvety steamed milk over a double shot of espresso. It is similar to a cappuccino or a latte, but is smaller in volume and with a higher proportion of coffee to milk. As Dick Bradsell's Espresso Martini became a mass-market cocktail in the first half of the Noughties, so the marketers at Baileys Irish Cream started to promote this cocktail.

name: Merry Widow Fizz
instructions: SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE (without ice) and strain into chilled glass (no ice)
TOP with soda.
review: Rich berry balanced by citrus and charged with soda to fizz. The egg white ensures a lasting thick foaming head.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Stan Jones' 1977 Jones' Complete Bar Guide.

name: Sex On The Beach
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients (the 'beach' part) with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
SHAKE the last 3 ingredients (the 'sex' part) with ice.
STRAIN into glass to sit on top of the 'beach' base layer.
Serve with stirrer and instruct drinker to mix layers together before drinking.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review:
Sweet fruitiness masks the alcohol in this easy-drinking, long cocktail.

"Sex" is associated with red (e.g.red light district) and is represented in this drink in the form of vodka, cranberry juice, and black raspberry liqueur. These 'sexy' ingredients sit atop the sandy yellow "beach"– consisting of peach schnapps, orange juice, and pineapple juice.

name: Sloe Gin Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Sloe gin comprises such a large part of this cocktail that the style and quality of your sloe gin will dramatically affect the finished cocktail. Choose well and this is a delicious, well-balanced, wintery cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Stan Jones' 1977 Jones' Complete Bar Guide.

name: Slow Comfortable Screw Against a Cold Hard Wall With a Kiss
instructions: STIR first 4 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
Then FLOAT amaretto so it bleeds into drink
Finish with a FLOAT of Galliano and overproof rum.
review: The float of amaretto adds the "Kiss" to a Slow Comfortable Screw Against a Cold Hard Wall. If this was served in a Tiki mug it would be a Tiki Drink rather than a Disco Drink – it's fruity and boozy, and just edging towards the sweet side with that splash of amaretto adding rich almond notes.
history: Origins and evolution of the "Slow Screw" family of cocktails.

name: Chocolate Vodkatini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of sugar syrup and chocolate powder rim.
review: Vodka and chocolate made more interesting with a hint of vermouth.
history: Martini cocktail  history and its evolution

name: Slow Comfortable Fuzzy Screw Against the Wall
instructions: STIR first 4 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
FLOAT sloe gin on surface so it bleeds into drink
Finish with Galliano FLOAT.
review: Peach schnapps is borrowed from the Fuzzy Navel to influence this member of the Slow Comfortable family, its presence gives this cocktail a distinctive sweet peachy note.

name: Alabama Slammer (long)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered orange slice and maraschino cherry (sail).
review: A Screwdriver that's flavoured and lightly sweetened with a trio of liqueurs. (Don't be tempted to shake as this detrimentally thins the cocktail. Instead, consider rolling or throwing.)
history:
Said to have been made famous by American former football quarterback Brett Favre. Just eight weeks after a near-fatal car accident resulting in 30 inches (76 cm) of his small intestine being removed, on 8th September 1990, Favre led Southern Miss to a comeback victory over Alabama. The Alabama coach, Gene Stallings, is quoted as saying, "You can call it a miracle or a legend or whatever you want to. I just know that on that day, Brett Favre was larger than life."

Truth is, the Alabama Slammer was popular in the U.S. a decade before Favre's miracle win and was being served in bars, including T.G.I. Friday's, in the 1980s.

There are numerous recipes for this cocktail dating from the late 1980s, some with orange juice and others with grenadine replacing the sloe gin found in better recipes.

name: Hairy Navel
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered orange slice and maraschino cherry (sail).
review:
Fruity and a touch on the sweet side, this is a Screwdriver with added peach schnapps liqueur.

(Don't be tempted to shake as this detrimentally thins the cocktail. Instead, consider rolling or throwing.)
history: The Fuzzy Navel, a mix of peach schappes and orange juice, was named by an adverting agency in 1984 as part of a campaign to support the US launch of De Kuyper Original Peachtree. "Fuzzy" in the name refers to the peach schnapps while "navel" refers to the orange variety of the juice. Given the rise of vodka at the time (Absolut launched in 1980), it was only a matter of time before someone added vodka to the mix, upgrading this cocktail from being merely being "Fluzzy" to "Hairy". The next logical development was to add another popular product at the time, cranberry juice, to make a Pierced Navel cocktail.

name: Red Earl (by Salvatore Calabrese)
instructions: MUDDLE ginger and then raspberries in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Fresh ginger adds a hint of spice which combines well with raspberry fruit in this crowd-pleasing vodka-based cocktail which is freshened by a dash of limoncello.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Salvatore Calabrese – I added half a spoon of sugar to his recipe which you may or not need depending on your raspberries and limoncello.

name: Koko Colada
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: A ridiculously simple, three-equal-parts party cocktail, shaken to produce a pineapple and coconut cocktail fortified with rum and crowned with a foamy head. Off-dry and fruity rather than sweet.
history: Adapted from a recipe promoted by Koko Kanu in 2017 consisting of just equal parts liqueur and pineapple juice.

name: Chocolate Mint Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: No additional garnish. (The preprepared rim is garnish enough.)
review: A 'Martini' for the after-dinner chocolate mint moment.

name: Vodka Soda
instructions: POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass and lightly stir (so as not to lose fizz)
Don't serve with straws.
review:
Dry and refreshing with a pleasant minerality.

The proportion of soda water to vodka in this recipe is 2.5 to 1 (a ratio that appears to have the widest appeal) consisting of one 150 ml can of soda water to 60 ml vodka = 210ml plus ice melt total liquid in glass. If using a 12oz glass (350ml to fill level), the ice displacement = approximately 140ml, so ice forms 40% of the contents of the glass. To raise the percentage of ice to the ideal of around 50%, you should use a 14oz Collins glass or a Spanish-style Copa glass.

name: Chocolate Puff
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
Crumble a piece of Cadbury's Flake over the cocktail.
garnish: Prepare garnish of Cadbury's Flake.
review: Smooth as you like. The orange is surprisingly evident.
history: Created in 2002 by Wayne Collins  for Maxxium UK.

name: Rum and Raisin Espresso Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with 3 coffee beans floated on surface and skewered raisins.
review: A hint of Pedro Ximénez sherry adds the raisin notes to this rum-based riff on Dick Bradsell's Espresso Martini.

name: Le Premier Word
instructions: SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass (preferably over a chunk of block ice)
TOP with cider and lightly stir.
review: A cider top eases drinkers into this spirit forward, Chartreuse-heavy riff on the classic Last Word.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Katherine Boushel at Atwater Cocktail Club in Montréal, Canada. The name (meaning First Word) and indeed the ingredients of this riff on the classic Last Word, reflect Montréal's French and English influences.
Last Word history

name: Mexican Squeeze
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Lightly stir.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: The success of this cocktail is as much about the quality of your tequila as it is about your choice of lemonade. Made well and this Squeeze is zesty, balanced and very refreshing.

name: Apicius
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
review: This digestif cocktail is aromatic and bittersweet with herbal and botanical complexity rounded by rich chestnut liqueur.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2017 by Sam Robertson & Alexander Taylor at Pennyroyal, Cardiff, Wales. They told us, "Apicius is an ancient cookbook which dates back to the 5th century, it provides an insight into the origins of Italian cuisine. Chestnuts, known to aid digestion, were hugely prominent, combined with amaro this cocktail is the perfect after-dinner sipper."

name: Pterodactyl Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
THROW all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Adapted from a drink created in 2017 by Martyn Lourencoat at Kol Restaurant, Reykjavik, Iceland, who says, "Since cocktail culture is still pretty new in Iceland and people aren't used to gentiane and absinthe in their drinks, I chose the name Pterodactyl because I didn't want guests to connect the name to anything they've had before! The fact that it is difficult to pronounce is an advantage as it is a good talking point for us. Pterodactyl is the name of a species of Pterosaur and so is very hard to come up with expectations on taste from the name!
history: A dry and aromatic aperitif cocktail with a long bittersweet herbal finish. (We prefer with half the amount of absinthe).

name: Chocolate Sidecar
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: No additional garnish. (The preprepared rim is garnish enough.)
review: A riff on a Sidecar with chocolate, port wine and sugary richness balanced by lime juice.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2005 by Wayne Collins for Maxxium UK.

name: Flor De Don Juan
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
MUDDLE basil in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with thyme and basil sprigs.
review: A basil tequila sour (Margarita if you will), sweetened with elderflower liqueur – basil and elderflower flavours sit well with the tequila and lime.

name: French Spritz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a WINE GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Lightly stir.
garnish: Prepare garnish of thyme (or rosemary) sprig.
review: This apéritif-style cocktail perfectly fits its name. That said, fans of Italian fizz will be interested to learn how well this spritz works when made with prosecco in place of champagne.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Fred Charlet at La Cabane in Strasbourg, France, who says of his drink, "The idea was to create a fresh cocktail made with French ingredients: Suze, St Germain and champagne: what else do you need to represent the typically French appetizer. May be a baguette and a béret, but that is not the subject here. I hope you will enjoy this cocktail and don't forget, a good French appetizer always match with French cheese."

See Spritz history and family of cocktails

name: Hazelnut Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: There are two distinctively different types of hazelnut syrup available, the "roasted" version being more intensely flavoured than plain hazelnut syrup. If you use light, charcoal-filtered carta blanca-style rum, I suggest you use plain hazelnut syrup. However, this cocktail is perhaps better with a more flavoursome 'gold' rum and roasted hazelnut syrup.
history: Recipe by yours truly.

name: Cider Apple Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of apple wedge.
review: As the name suggests, rich cider flavours with a sharp finish.
history: Created in 1998 by Jamie Terrell at Lab, London, England.

name: Washington's Applejack Mule
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime or apple wedge.
review: Take a Moscow Mule and make with apple brandy in place of vodka and you've a Washington's Applejack Mule. Tasty – arguably tastier than a Moscow Mule.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Jamie Boudreau at Canon in Seattle, USA.

name: Don's Coco Punch
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge.
review: Reminiscent of a tequila Piña Colada with the merest hint of Chartreuse.

name: Susto
ingredients: 15 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Prepare garnish of amaranth/pigweed sprigs or basil leaf.
review: Bittersweet with mezcal and amaro combining harmoniously along with aromatic wine and celery bitters.
history:
Created in 2017 by Sam Robertson & Alexander Taylor at Pennyroyal, Cardiff, Wales.

"Susto is from the Digestives section of our menu. Local Zapotec tribes of Oaxaca, Mexico, attribute illness to ‘Susto’ – emotional shock. Tribeswomen known as ‘Partera’ provide herbal remedies to ease the suffering."

name: G+T Royale
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Lightly stir.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice.
review: A summery aperitif gin cocktail with crisp tonic water and enlivening champagne, flavoured with hints of vanilla and orange.
history: Recipe created in 2017 by Tony Conigliaro with Schweppes 1783 Crisp Tonic Water.

name: Ginger Snap (Fino Sherry and Ginger Ale)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Lightly stir.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest spiral cut with a channel knife.
review: A light and fabulously refreshing summery aperitivo, that's also perfect for Christmas.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Tony Conigliaro with Schweppes 1738 Golden Ginger Ale.

name: The Alchemist
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of float fresh basil leaf.
review: Looking over the liqueur and syrup-dominated recipe, this mezcal-based Margarita meets the Last Word may appear to be sweet. However, the spec is deceiving, and the finished drink is surprisingly balanced, harnessing some big flavours and presenting them harmoniously.
history: Created in 2017 by Sam Fraser at The Rockinghorse in Chichester, England.

name: Le Very French 75
instructions: SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with champagne.
review: A dry, bitter apéritif-style riff on the classic French 75. Try with a splash more sparkling wine than suggested in the recipe and/or a spoon of sugar syrup.
history:
Created in 2017 by Regis Laacher at Kremlin in Paris, France. He says of his cocktail, "Basically, a twist of the classic French 75. By adding Lillet Rouge and Suze without simple syrup, you make the drink bitter and dry, the perfect appetizer to start a night out or to kick a party."
More French 75 history and variants

name: Triangle
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A bite-sized bittersweet aperitif-style cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Daniel Seehuusen at Park in Göteborg, Sweden. Daniel's original recipe called for equal parts Suze, Eau de Vie de Poire de Normandie, and Cointreau. He named this cocktail after those three French ingredients: Suze, first presented at the Exposition Universelle in Paris in 1889, Cointreau from Angers, and Eau de Vie de Poir from Normandy, which when plotted on a map form a triangle.

name: Paycheck
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
RINSE chilled glass with absinthe, discard excess and set aside.
STIR other ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into absinthe-rinsed glass.
EXPRESS flamed lemon zest twist over cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist (flamed).
review: Spirituous and aromatic with rich honey notes balancing bitter gentian.
history: Created in 2017 by Jens Küpperbusch, Essen, Germany.

name: And Now for Something Suze
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist (discarded) and fresh thyme sprig on floating dehydrated lemon wheel.
review: Gary's original recipe for this dry and spirituous aperitif cocktail calls for Navy strength gin but we found it punchy enough with a gin at 40 – 43% alc./vol..
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2017 by Gary Elliott at Dorcol Distilling and Brewing Co., San Antonio, USA.

Gary's original recipe calls for Plymouth Navy strength gin, a "premium dry vermouth such as Carpano", Suze Suze gentian liqueur and Bigallet Thym. He says, "I fell in love with Suze at first taste. The full-bodied citrus and herbs and the long spicy, bitter finish had me! I frequently use it as a modifier to add richness and a little herbal bitterness to various cocktails. Almost a secret ingredient.

I created this cocktail to highlight the flavors of Suze. After trying multiple styles of gin, I settled on Navy Strength Plymouth Gin. Various London dries and Botanist were also very good. I further experimented with vermouth. Sweet vermouth alone does not work well. A premium dry or a "perfect" with dry and bianco are best. I've been making variations of this cocktail for a few years.

Only recently have I added Bigallet Thym. While totally optional, the Thym enhances the woody notes of the Suze and prolongs the herbal flavors into the finish. Just remember that the Thym is the modifier here. More than 3 or 4 drops will result in a powerful taste of thyme. The nose is lemon and faint herbs. The first flavors are herbal and a touch woody. Juniper follows with continued herbs and just a hint of thyme. The finish is herbal, spicy and clean."

name: Citrus Caipirovska
instructions: MUDDLE lemon in base of glass
Add other ingredients and fill glass with crushed ice
CHURN drink with bar spoon and serve with short straws.
review: Superbly refreshing balance of sweet and citrus sourness.

name: Citrus Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Orange undertones add citrus depth to the lemon explosion.
history: Created by Dick Bradsell at Fred's, London, England, in the late 80s.

name: Mandarine Daisy
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wheel.
review: "Citrusy and sour" sums up this refreshing gin-laced cocktail with rich mandarin orange liqueur soured by lime.

name: The Windfall
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE (without ice) fine strain into chilled glass.
review: An autumnal-sounding cocktail to be enjoyed in place of a Daiquiri at any time of the year. Yes, it's appley with delicate armagnac undertones and complexity, but it is made interesting by the birch spirit, which gives this drink faint pine, almost smoky bacon notes.
history: Created by Katie Stocks in St Albans, England.

name: Claret Cobbler
instructions: Select and pre-chill a GOBLET GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: Fortified and slightly sweetened red wine, served cobbler-style with crushed ice.
history: A Claret Cobbler appears in Jerry Thomas' 1862 The Bart-enders' Guide.

name: Am I Blue?
instructions: SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with pale ale and briefly stir.
review: Long and refreshing, based on India pale ale and birch spirit with grapefruit freshness.
history: Created in 2017 by Thom Solberg at Black Rock in London, England.

name: Bermuda Triangle
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain into a chilled glass.
review: As Ralf, the drinks creator, says, "The Batavia plays well with the pineapple and the gomme, and the sherry gives it depth and body" to produce a punch-style cocktail.
history: Created by Ralf Ramirez of The Exhibition Room, Long Beach, CA, at one of Gary "gaz" Regan's excellent educational Cocktail the Country sessions.

name: Claridge Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE or STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Gin for strength, vermouth for dryness and liqueur to sweeten - a balanced combination.
history: This cocktail is first published in Harry McElhone's 1922 book Harry's ABC of Mixing Cocktails where the recipe is credited to "Leon, Bartender, Claridge's Hotel, Champs-Elysées, Paris." The recipe is repeated in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Classic Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Reminiscent of a Sidecar with maraschino liqueur.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Golden Eight
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: I find the original four equal parts recipe a tad on the sour side so I like to trim back the citrus and also add a few drops of saline. And as is the case we so many Daiquiri-style cocktails, I like to add a splash of dilution.
history: Adapted from the Singaporean Bacardí Legacy 2018 winning cocktail created by Sim Sze Wei at Atlas Bar, originally based on Bacardi Ocho Anos and Carta Blanca rums.

name: Queen Street Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Using atomiser, spray two spritzes peated whisky over top of glass and one on the side.
review: Daiquiri-like, delicately sour and, helped by the garnish, wonderfully aromatic.
history: The British Bacardí Legacy 2018 winning cocktail, by Leon Back of Panda and Sons in Edinburgh, Scotland.

name: GDPR Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE raspberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered raspberries.
history:
GDPR is an acronym for General Data Protection Regulation, and this cocktail was created on 25th May 2018 to commemorate the new law coming into effect.

G = Grapefruit juice (preferably pink) 30ml / 1oz
D = Demerara sugar syrup (2:1) 15ml / ½oz
P = Pisco 45ml / 2oz
R = Raspberries 8 fresh

name: Clover Leaf
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE mint in base of shaker (just to bruise).
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf floated on the cocktail or pegged/stuck with spot of honey to the rim.
review: A Clover Club freshened and made more interesting by the addition of fresh mint, and not just as a garnish.
history:
The Clover Leaf is ostensibly the same as the Clover Club, with various similar recipes coexisting since 1909. The commonly accepted difference between these two very similarly named cocktails comes from Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book where he states a Clover Leaf Cocktail is "The same as Clover Club, with a sprig of fresh Mint on top" and so for most it is merely the decoration that distinguishes the two cocktails apart. However, there's a little more to it than that.

The first known recipe for Clover Leaf appears in Jacob Abraham Grohusko's 1908 Jack's Manual, while the first reference to a Clover Club (unearthed by David Wondrich) is in a 23 June 1901 piece in the New York Press where Michael Killackey (or "Killacky"), "the head wine man of the Walforf-Astoria" lists "delicious summer beverages" with recipes, the fourth being the "Clover Club Cocktail."

name: Golden Spritz
instructions: STIR first 4 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with cava and briefly stir.
review: Gin-based with rich ginger balanced by dry sherry, lengthened by apple and invigorated with fizz.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created by Jeff Carmichael at Capa Four Seasons Orlando, USA.
Spritz history

name: Club Cocktail (Butt's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered maraschino cherry.
review: An aromatic, spirited, classical cocktail.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2002 by Michael Butt at Milk & Honey in London, England.

name: Cobbled Raspberry
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered raspberries.
review: A simple 1990s Raspberry 'Martini' with a splash of wine.
history: Created in 2004 by yours truly, London, England.

name: Royal Balkan
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
STIR first 4 ingredients with ice.
POUR tonic water over the cocktail in your stirring glass/tin.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Gin-laced and bittersweet with pleasing herbaceousness.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2018 by Jack Sleaford at Wood & Company in Manchester, England.

name: Coconut Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Classic Daiquiri flavour with a pleasing tropical touch.

name: Armenita Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with apricot wedge on rim.
review: A herbal apricot Margarita.
history: I don't know where this cocktail originated but our recipe was 60ml añejo tequila, 15ml Strega, 15ml apricot brandy, and 30ml lime juice. The conscientious of option/comments below was that this way too sour. Hence, in December 2021 I adapted the recipe with said comments in mind.

name: The Frank O'Hara
instructions: STIR with ice and strain into chilled glass.
review: This ever so simple Martini-style cocktail is surprisingly complex and tasty with the botanicals of both the gin and Italian liqueur combining harmoniously.
history: Probably named after Frank O'Hara, whose 1964 poem The Day Lady Died about the death of Billie Holiday, includes the verse, "and for Mike I just stroll into the Park Lane Liquor Store and ask for a bottle of Strega and then I go back where I came from to 6th Avenue and the tobacconist in the Ziegfeld Theatre and casually ask for a carton of Gauloises and a carton of Picayunes, and a New York Post with her face on it."

name: Golden Tang
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: This vodka-laced cocktail is indeed tangy and golden (well, light orange) with a pleasing banana chew flavour.

name: Angela Luce
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Fruity and herbal – a refreshingly zingy nightcap.
history: Adapted from a recipe by Silvia Picinato. Named after the eponymous Italian film actress and singer.

name: Lightness Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: A citrus vodka sour with Strega and crème de banana.
history: Adapted from a drink discovered at the Icebar, London, England.

name: Witches' Smile
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange slice and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry (sail).
review: This tall vodka-laced drink is lightly herbal, fruity and refreshing.

name: Witches' Yellow Flip
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: Strega liqueur and citrus juice fortified with cognac flavour this aptly named flip.

name: Orange Witch
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange slice wheel.
review: A splash of Strega makes this Gin & Juice somewhat reminiscent of a Harvey Wallbanger.

name: Lazy Lover
instructions: MUDDLE cucumber slices and salt in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain into ice-filled glass.
review: Savoury and lightly bittersweet. An interesting apéritif cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Tess Anne Sawyer at The Up & Up, New York City, USA, which came to us courtesy of gazregan.com.

name: Tuxedo Cocktail No.1 (Savoy and Duffy's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
REGAL STIR all ingredients (including lemon zest) with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Bone dry with faint anise and liquorice notes from the absinthe.
history:
Adapted from recipes in The Savoy Cocktail Book (1930) by Harry Craddock and The Official Mixer's Manual (1934 & 1940) by Patrick Gavin Duffy.
Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Tuxedo Cocktail No.3 (Lowe and Straub's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Although only a spoonful, the sherry used dramatically impacts the finished cocktail. We tried amontillado but found this drink works best with either fino sherry or manzanilla sherries.
history: Adapted from recipes in Drinks As They Are Mixed (1904) by Paul E Lowe and Drinks (1914) by Jacques-Straub.

name: Tuxedo No.4 or Tussetto
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Bone dry and aromatic with green grape, citrus and mineral notes. Also consider garnishing this cocktail with an olive – it suits it. This is a tasty riff on a Dry Martini but it has no vermouth (it uses sherry instead) so isn't really a Dry Martini or even a Tuxedo.
history: Adapted from recipes in Straub's Manual of Mixed Drinks (1913), Jack's Manual 4th Edition (1916) and Old Waldorf Astoria Bar Days (1931) by Albert Stevens Crockett.

name: Cathy Olé Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Gin-laced fino sherry with a splash of lime, brought together by rich, lightly spiced vanilla liqueur.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in March 2018 by John Freeman, an amateur mixologist.

name: Mariposa
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE tomato in base of shaker and then muddle basil leaves.
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with half cherry tomato and small basil sprig on rim.
review: This rum-laced cocktail has attractive savoury flavours with a tangy sourness and floral elderflower.
history: Created by Robb Collins, Group Head Bartender at London Cocktail Club, London, England, who told us he made it for the 2015 Bacardi Legacy UK Finals. "Inspired by a fictional love story of an American soldier who falls in love with a French Cuban girl called 'Mariposa' during WWII."

name: Zima Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice) to emulsify.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of smouldering star anise.
review: Aromatic and perfumed, bison grass, apple, sour lemon and rich passion fruit combine in this lightly sweet and fruity cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Robb Collins, Group Head Bartender at London Cocktail Club in London, England.

name: Dark 'n' Stormy Highball
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass and briefly stir.
OR, layer by pouring ginger beer into ice-filled glass first, then floating rum.
garnish: Squeeze lime wedge into drink and drop in as a garnish.
review: Refreshing on a hot summer's day and darkly appropriate for Halloween.
history: The signature drink of Gosling's Black Seal rum and popular in the brand's home island of Bermuda.

name: Carriaggi Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: The holy trinity - three equal parts: kirschwasser, Strega liqueur and fresh orange juice. The result is surprisingly spirit-forward, fruity and refreshing.

name: Colorado Bulldog
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring cola.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: This dog's bite is subdued by cream.

name: Vive l'apéritif
instructions: Select and pre-chill a WINE GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with cucumber slice, and preferably also strawberry, and mint springs bouquet.
review: Thirst quenching, refreshing and low in alcohol. A great drink for a summer's afternoon or even before lunch.
history: Adapted from a signature serve promoted by Lillet Blanc as “Lillet Vive”. In French, the word Vive means live or long life, so Lillet Vive means Lillet Life while Vive l'apéritif translates as long live the apéritif.

name: Black Rum Swizzle
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into glass half filled with crushed ice.
SWIZZLE and then fill the glass with more crushed ice.
garnish: Garnish with skewered orange slice wheel and maraschino cherry (sail).
review: Dark rum adds depth of flavour to this fruity and refreshing cocktail.

name: Black Rumhattan
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Dark rum adds molasses-like flavours to this Manhattan riff.

name: Caipirinha (no added sugar and low-calorie)
instructions: <strong>1.</strong> Cut lime in half (from pole to pole) and slice on half into 3-4mm thick half-moon shaped segments and drop into the base of a robust rocks glass
<strong>2.</strong> Add sweetener and MUDDLE
<strong>3.</strong> If using cubed ice straight from the freezer, add chilled water (or optionally add fruit of your choice) and MUDDLE some more
If using watery ice then omit water
<strong>4.</strong> When sure muddling has fully extracted fruit juice, POUR the muddled fruit and sweetener into serving glass (preferably a 30cl / 10oz hi-ball glass)
<strong>5.</strong> POUR cachaça (stored in the freezer) into the rocks glass (the glass used to muddle) and swirl to rinse glass before pouring cachaça into serving glass
STIR to mix with other ingredients
<strong>6.</strong> If using cubed ice, crack (rather than crush) four to five large ice cubes and drop into serving glass
STIR before topping with more ice and serve with a stirrer (but no straws)
review: Forget those Caipirinhas blighted by crunchy undissolved sugar crystals, this version is sugar-free.
history:
Adapted from the classic Caipirinha by yours truly in January 2019.
Caipirinha cocktail - history, flavours, and how to make

name: Cosmopolitan (no added sugar* and low-calorie)
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
(Be sure to use naturally sweetened cranberry drink rather than "juice" sweetened with sugar.)
review:
* BEWARE: Although 
free of cane sugar and beet sugar, this cocktail contains small amounts of fructose sugars from the agave nectar used in the Agave Sec orange liqueur.

The cranberry juice you select and even some vodkas may also introduce unwanted sugar into this cocktail, so choose judiciously. Even unsweetened cranberry juice and even lime juice will contain natural fruit sugars.
history:
Adapted from the classic serve by yours truly in January 2019.
Cosmopolitan cocktail history and development

name: Cool Orchard
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
MUDDLE ginger in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: An unusual line up of ingredients combine to make a great drink.
history: Created in 2001 by Douglas Ankrah for Akbar in Soho, London, England

name: Passion Fruit Altern'atini (no added sugar and low-cal.)
instructions: Cut passion fruits in half and scoop out flesh into shaker (keep one half for garnish)
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: A couple of dashes orange bitters helps add a touch of complexity and balance to this vodka-laced fruity cocktail, but beware those bitters may add trace amounts of sugar.
history:
Adapted from the classic serve by yours truly (Simon D) in January 2019.

Also see the Martini Cocktail and its evolution.

name: Vodka Collins (no added sugar and low-calorie)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda and briefly stir.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange slice and cherry.
review: Cleansing, easy drinking, sugar-free and just 100 calories.
history:
Adapted from the classic serve by yours truly (Simon D) in January 2019.

Also see Collins Cocktails - recipes & history.

name: Coolman Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Harmonious apple and zesty orange laced with vodka.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2001 by Jack Coleman at The Library Bar, Lanesborough Hotel, London, England. Our recipe amended in November 2020 with thanks to feedback by Kjell Eriksson.

name: White Lady (no added sugar* and low-calorie)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review:
* BEWARE: Although free of cane sugar and beet sugar, this cocktail contains small amounts of fructose sugars from the agave nectar used in the Agave Sec orange liqueur.

Lemon and orange meringue with light gin botanical notes.
history:
Adapted from the classic serve by yours truly (Simon D) in January 2019.

For more recipes, its origins and the history behind the White Lady cocktail, see our White Lady cocktail page.

name: Stazione
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A spirituous and herbal after-dinner digestif.
history: Adapted from a recipe by Alex Frezza. Incidentally, stazione is Italian for railway station.

name: The Bee Bodice
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of dried lavender sprig.
review: Honeyed and floral with a cleansing bitterness.
history: Created by 2017 by Luke Hanson at Dr Inks Curiosities, Exeter, England. Luke says his drink is "a riff on a riff... The Bee's Knees inspired a drink The Bee's Knickers which was on our original and opening menu here at Dr Inks. For our first birthday, we were asked to reinvent them once again and this beauty came to life."

name: Copper Illusion
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Basically, a Negroni with liqueur replacing sweet vermouth. Like the Italian classic, this is both bitter and sweet.
history: Unknown but brought to my attention in 2005 courtesy of Angus Winchester.

name: Like A Lady
instructions: MUDDLE cucumber in base of shaker
ADD other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: A bittersweet savoury twist on the classic White Lady with a refreshing cucumber flavour.
history: Properly named "Suze It Like A Lady", this is a recipe created by Michael Hajiyianni at Ruby, Copenhagen, Denmark. Michael says it is "based on one of the first Gin cocktails I ever had during my early days at The Refectory Bar at The Plymouth Gin Distillery. Swapping out orange liqueur to make a simple savoury twist on the classic White Lady."

name: La Petite Bouche
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Garnish with white grapefruit wedge squeezed and dropped into cocktail.
review: Dry and refreshing with gentian bitterness and complexity forming the backbone of this apéritif.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Danny Wilson at Tabouche, Cambridge, England. Danny told us, "Due to me working at Tabouche I tend to like to name drinks around my venue and since this drink is very simplistic, I decided to call it the little mouth in French."

name: Blushing Monk
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of angostura Bitters dash in a single line across top egg white foam to form a brown line representing the rope that ties a monk's robe..
review: An aromatic bittersweet sour with heavy herbal influences.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created by Sam Finch at The Milk Thistle, Bristol, England. Sam says, "The Blushing Monk came from a moment of back bar chaos, with the main intention being to combine Bénédictine with Aperol following the blueprint of a classic sour. Suze became the machinery of balance in the drink, the heavy vegetal notes of the gentian offsetting the sweetness already in the mix whilst reinforcing the light bitterness of the Aperol. Lillet Blanc lifts and brightens whilst the lime brings bold zestiness to the party.

The name simply references the drink's lustily 'blushing' hue and spins on the heritage of the Benedictine Liqueur; a fresh, flirty and suggestively lively flavour experience."

name: Dans Le Jardin (In The Garden)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Rosemary sprig.
review: This bittersweet aperitif-style drink's light green hue and garnish befits its name and uses the tried and tested combo of gentian, gin and lemon juice.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Fred Charlet at La Cabane, Strasbourg, France. Fred says, "With this cocktail, I wanted to remember playing in grandpa's garden hut as a child while my family was sat in the garden enjoying an aperitif. This cocktail is an anthem to our childhood."

name: Eye-opener
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Resembling watery orange custard, this cocktail is surprisingly silky and suited to after-dinner.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: The Lemon Peel
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of sage sprig.
review: Just as the name promises, this tall superbly refreshing cocktail delivers intense zesty citrus but is balanced and quaffable. A drink for a scorching hot summer's afternoon.

name: Cucumber and Mint Seltz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a WINE GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Prepare garnish of mint sprigs bouquet.
review:
Light and refreshing. One to serve alongside the cucumber sandwiches!

Also, consider serving as a Spritz: Pour 90ml/3oz Prosecco, 45ml/1.5oz Botanical Cucumber & Mint and 60ml/2oz soda water into an ice-filled glass and briefly stir.
history: One of three signature serves launched in the summer of 2018 to accompany the launch of Ketel One Botanicals.

name: Peach and Orange Blossom Seltz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a LARGE WINE GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange wedge.
review:
Aromatic peach is surprisingly strong in this refreshing and light drink.

Also, consider serving as a Spritz: Pour 90ml/3oz Prosecco, 45ml/1.5oz Peach & Orange Blossom and 60ml/2oz soda water into an ice-filled glass and briefly stir.
history: One of three signature serves launched in the summer of 2018 to accompany the launch of Ketel One Botanicals.

name: Corpse Reviver No.1 (Savoy recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Dry and potent. A 'pick-me-up' hangover cure - or possibly put-you-right-back-down-again!
history: Generally credited to Frank Meier, bar manager at the Ritz Bar in Paris during the 1920s and 30s, although he makes no claim to the drink in his 1936 The Artistry of Mixing Drinks. This recipe is adapted from Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Clear As Rain
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of float single coffee bean.
review: Tequila, kummel and cacao – a trio of powerful flavours diluted and made into a delicate cocktail by birch water.
history: Created in March 2019 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room, London, England.

name: Walter's Waltz
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with cider and briefly stir.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with apricot wedge or dried apricot half.
review: Medium-dry, herbal, fruity, refreshing and delightfully different. Best served as an aperitivo.
history: Adapted from a drink discovered in 2017 at Marks Bar at Hix, Soho, London, England.

name: Martiki
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with coconut slice on rim.
review: Dry and Martini-like. Assertive kümmel flavours pervade a punchy mix of rum and coconut water.
history: Touted as Tiki's answer to the Dry Martini, this cocktail is purported to have originated at the Luau Restaurant in Beverly Hills, California. Coconut water is a later inspired addition by Ricard Godwin in his 2016 The Spirits book, which calls for 60ml rum, 25ml coconut water and 10ml Kummel.

name: After Hours
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: Martini-like but with delicate liqueur richness. Lightly spicy and faintly nutty.
history: Adapted from a recipe by Satvik "Rick" Ahuja, Quarter Bar & Restaurant, Leicester, England.

name: The Absinthe of Normal
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of star anise or piece of coconut.
review: Coconut, liquorice and anise are bold flavours to combine and hopefully make this cocktail deliciously abnormal.
history: Created in 2019 by yours truly at the Cabinet Room in London, England.

name: Country Breeze
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of seasonal berries or apple slice wheel.
review: Not too sweet. The gin character shines through the fruit.

name: Few Words
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably a large cube or chunk of block ice).
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: This lightly spiced mezcal-laced and cherry-flavoured cocktail is equally enjoyable as an aperitif or digestif.
history: Created 2018 by Alexey Poltavskiy at Club No. 43, Phuket, Thailand, this cocktail earned him a place in the London final of the Heering Classics Competition.

name: Virgin Passion
instructions: SCOOP the seeds and flesh of passion fruit into base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Inspired by the Porn Star Martini, this alcohol-free cocktail, which bursts with fruitiness, is balanced rather than sweet.
history: Created August 2018 by Søren Bøgh Brier, a keen Danish home bartender, "as a sort of compensation" for his wife who'd missed out on cocktails while pregnant.

name: Pink Gin Fizz
instructions: SHAKE first four ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with soda and briefly stir to integrate.
review: As the name suggests, this refreshing and fruity long drink is both gin-laced and pink in colour. And yes, it's also fizzy. Well if not out and out fizzy, it is at least invigorated with sparkling water. So perhaps that's why merely fizz rather than fizzy?

name: Arancia Spritz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a WINE GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Reminiscent of a Pimm's No.1 Spritz.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in  2017 by Justin Kaderabek at Monteverde in Chicago, USA.

See Spritz cocktail history and more recipes

name: Parasol
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim and dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: Citrusy pineapple, banana and rum. A slightly tart short drink with flavours reminiscent of a San Francisco.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Shannon Mustipher at Glady's in Brooklyn, New York City.

name: Tremblement de Terre (Earthquake)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Absinthe adds a liquorice note to this spirit-forward cognac-based cocktail.
history:
Originally equal parts cognac and absinthe, this cocktail is said to have been served at parties by Henri de Toulouse-Lautrec (1864-1901, the French Post-Impressionist.

Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book puts an English spin on "The Earthquake Cocktail" with a recipe that specifies "1/3 Gin, 1/3 Whisky, 1/3 Absinthe, Shake well and serve in a cocktail glass." This recipe is followed by a notation to the name, "So-called because if there should happen to be an earthquake on when you are drinking it, it won't matter." Craddock also notes, "This is a Cocktail whose potency is not to be taken toom lightly, or, for that matter, too frequently!"

name: Perfect Storm Highball
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: Spiced rum adds vanilla as well as spice to this Mule-style drink.

name: Morado
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist (discarded).
review: Gin-laced rich PX raisiny sherry, blackberry and coffee combine in this bite-sized after-dinner cocktail.
history: Adapted from a 2019 recipe by Andre Tarantino. His original spec: 35ml Gin Hendriks Orbium, 15ml Lustau East India Sherry, 15ml Créme de Mûre Sauvage Chartreuse, 5ml Borghetti Coffee Liqueur and 2 dashes Peychaud bitters.

name: Suffragette
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with purple and white edible flowers with green foliage pegged to rim.
review: A Wet Gin Martini with a touch of elderflower floral richness.

name: Shaft
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Serve naked, without garnish.
review: Irish cream liqueur adds a creamy mocha note to this Shaft version but some make with cream and others milk, either combining all ingredients prior to pouring or adding milk/cream as a float (as with a White Russian).
history:
Named after private detective John Shaft, the lead character in the 1971 cult movie Shaft, hired by a Harlem gangster to rescue his daughter from the Italian mobsters who kidnapped her. The film's theme song,  Theme From Shaft, was an Academy Award-winning hit in its own right.

The creation of the Shaft cocktail, circa 2000, is credited to Mark Smith whilst bartending at The Living Room restaurant in Calgary, Canada, as a built (rather than shaken) caffeinated pick-me-up shot originally consisting simply espresso with a splash of Kahlúa coffee liqueur and skimmed milk. 

This cocktail became ubiquitous in Victoria, the capital of British Columbia, Canada, leading many to speculate that this is its true birthplace. Indeed, it was a Victoria resident and fellow Discerning Drinker, Sean Gordon, who first brought the Shaft to my attention in June 2019, saying, "There is a locally invented cocktail/shot called a Shaft", adding "some will sip it but it's most commonly downed in one. It's basically a grownup Jaeger-bomb substitute."

As Sean told me back in 2019, "There's variation from one bar to another". From what I can tell, it seems likely that the original Shaft was created in Calgary and, at some point during its journey to Victoria, the drink morphed with vodka added and Irish cream liqueur often replacing milk. The original espresso coffee base is also widely substituted for cold brew coffee.

name: Fizzy Peachtree
instructions: Select and pre-chill a WINE GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of two lime wedges.
review: As the name suggests, sparkling peach with delicate lime notes. Light and refreshing.
history: Promoted by De Kuyper in 2019 as the signature cocktail for their Peachtree peach schnapps liqueur.

name: Picon Punch
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
POUR first 2 ingredients into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda and briefly stir.
FLOAT brandy.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Bittersweet and herbal, a distinctly French aperitif.
history: Thought to have originated at the Noriega Hotel in Bakersfield, California, the Picon Punch has become something of a signature drink of Basque immigrant communities in the USA who have also taken it back to their home region in the French Pyrénées.

name: Sawyer
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass (no ice in glass).
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge on rim.
review: A whopping 14 dashes of bitters with two full measures of gin makes for a robust cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2010 by Don Lee in New York City. First made for WD-50 chef, Wylie Dufresne, and is named after his daughter.

name: Absinthe Mojito
instructions: Squeeze and drop lime wedges into glass half-filled with crushed ice
Add mint and absinthe and churn with ice
Fill glass with more crushed ice and TOP with lemonade (or lemon-lime soda).
review: Absinthe adds its distinctive aniseed and liquorice notes to a Mojito.
history: Created in 2018 by George Rowley.

name: Parisienne Kiss
instructions: Half fill glass with ice and place garnish around glass
Add ingredients (squeeze lime wedges and drop into drink) and top with more ice before topping with tonic water.
review: Absinthe, cucumber and lime served long with crisp tonic.
history: Created by George Rowley in 2018, "in honour of Johnny Depp and Hunter S Thompson (my first ever customers) and our 20 year anniversary of starting the absinthe category world revival."

name: Creamsicle
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A creamy orange number that's surprisingly pleasant.

name: Rum N Coke Float
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: A Rum & Coke but not as you know it.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2015 by Ryan Chetiyawardana at Dandelyan in London, England.

name: Cuban Love
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of fresh or dehydrated pineapple spice and mint springs.
review: Green and herbal (coriander and mint) coffee and pineapple.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Boo Jing Heng, Tess Bar & Kitchen, Singapore.

name: Creamy Bee
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of cinnamon and sugar rim with raspberry.
review: Creamy cinnamon with hints of honey, nuts and berries.
history: Created in 2002 at Hush, London, England and originally made with cinnamon syrup in place of Goldschläger.

name: Pantera Rosa
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of float fresh mint.
review: Fresh vibrant and fruity. If you like French Martinis you'll also like this.
history: Adapted from a recipe made for us in September 2019 by Mattia Ria at the Blind Pig in Rome, Italy.

name: Gangham Spritz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
POUR ingredients into ice-filled glass.
review: Tastes reminiscent of traditional real lemonade.

name: Gin Agrumi Highball
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
history: Citrusy gin and accompanying botanicals, zesty limoncello and grapefruit soda.

name: Gin Sonic Highball
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with a lime wedge.
review: A lower alcohol and lower sugar version of a Gin & Tonic with a smaller serve of gin topped with equal parts tonic water and soda water so reducing the alcohol and sugar associated with the tonic in a regular G&T.
history: Originating in Tokyo, Japan in the Noughties, "Sonic" is a combination of soda and tonic.

name: Banana Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of float dried banana chip.
review: A delicately banana flavoured and sweetened very 'wet' vodkatini.
history: Created summer 2019 by yours truly at the Clocktower in Rye, England.

name: Carajillo (Puesto)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR liqueur into ice-filled glass.
Carefully FLOAT coffee over the liqueur.
garnish: Garnish with 3 coffee beans.
review: Carajilo, coffee and liquor or spirit is a popular serve in Mexico. Licor 43 is one of the preferred liqueurs in this cocktail due to its delicately spiced rich vanilla notes combining well with coffee. Your choice of espresso coffee will influence how well the bitter coffee balances the sweet liqueur.
history:
The Carajillo is a Spanish cocktail thought to have been created by Spanish troops in Cuba when the country was a Spanish province, who mixed coffee with rum to give them courage. 'Coraje' means courage in Spanish, and its diminutive form is corajillo. ( In Catalan, the Carajillo is called Cigaló.)

The Carajillo is popular in Spain, where its form varies by region, from simply pouring brandy into an espresso to heating brandy with lemon juice/zest, sugar and cinnamon, then adding coffee. It is also popular in its simplest form in Latin American countries, such as Colombia and Venezuela, and in Cuba, where coffee is mixed with rum rather than brandy.

An American version of the Carajillo combines a double measure of espresso coffee with chocolate liqueur in an ice-filled brandy balloon, topped with whipped cream. Another version is served with a caramelised sugar rim with rum, triple sec, coffee and whipped cream.

However, it is in Mexico where (since circa 2015) the Carajillo has exploded in popularity. Here, it is served shaken ("shakeado") or layered ("puesto").  Its popularity in Mexico has been helped by promotions by Licor 43 Liqueur; by far the most popular liqueur used to make a Carajillo. Indeed, this cocktail was added to D in 2019 after Licor 43 informed us of its popularity and sent us a glass for the layered version.

name: Creamy Creamsicle
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with ¼ orange wheel on rim.
review: Ultra smooth and creamy. Dessert, anyone?

name: Spanish Coffee
instructions: POUR liqueur and hot coffee into a warmed glass then FLOAT warmed whipped cream on the surface of the drink.
review: Coffee and lightly spiced vanilla smoothed by a creamy top.
history: A cocktail promoted by Licor 43 Liqueur and added to D in 2019.

name: Banana Havana Highball
instructions: POUR first 2 ingredients into ice-filled glass and TOP with banana tea soda from siphon.
review: Simple and just as the name suggests, a banana-flavoured rum highball.
history: Created summer 2019 by yours truly at the Clocktower, Rye, Sussex.

name: Giuseppi's Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
review: A grappa-based sour with faint elderflower.

name: Ancient Mariner
instructions: Select and pre-chill a DOUBLE OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Garnish with scored lime wedge and mint sprigs bouquet.
review: Characterful and well-aged rums with a blend of citrus and all-important pimento dram. You may swap rums but there's no substitute for the pimento dram.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 1994 by Jeff "Beachbum" Berry, Los Angeles, USA, which featured in his 1998 book Grog Log and subsequent 2010  Beachbum Berry Remixed. Jeff's original recipe calls for 30ml Demerara rum and 30ml dark Jamaican rum. He also specifies white grapefruit juice.

Jeff says he created The Ancient Mariner as an early "attempt to re-create the flavour of our favourite Trader Vic's drink, Vic's version of the Navy Grog – which, in turn, used Don The Beachcomber's even earlier version as a starting point..." "We called it the Ancient Mariner because by the time we finished with it, that's how we felt."

name: Art of Choke
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
Lightly MUDDLE (just to bruise) mint in base of stirring glass.
Add other ingredients and STIR with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: A bittersweet cocktail that's best enjoyed as an aperitivo.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2008 by Kyle Davidson at The Violet Hour in Chicago, USA. As for the name, say it again while remembering the original recipe calls for Cynar, a liqueur flavoured with artichoke leaves.

name: Barbacoa
instructions: MUDDLE first 3 ingredients in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, briefly SHAKE with ice and POUR into glass without straining.
review: Savoury and lightly spicy with smoky mezcal backbone.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2009 by Julian Cox at Rivera, Los Angeles, USA.

name: Carondelet
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2011 by Maks Pazuniak at Maison Premiere, New York City, USA.

name: Public Enemy No.1
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: A rich and richly flavoured dessert cocktail highlighting the coffee and cacao flavours of the spiced rum.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Kiki Moka at Union Group; Jakarta. Kiki's original recipe comprised: 30ml Black Tears Spiced Rum, 20ml Cherry Heering, 10ml Luxardo Amaretto and 15ml Cynar.

name: Bywater
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Garnish with maraschino cherry.
review: This spirit-forward, herbal and bitter-sweet cocktail was originally served without ice but dilution tames its fire to appeal to a wider audience as an after-dinner digestif.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2008 by Chris Hannah at Arnaud's French 75 Bar, New Orleans, USA.

Original recipe: Stir all ingredients with ice and strain into chilled Nick & Nora glass:
- 52.5ml (1¾oz) Aged rum
- 22.5ml (¾oz) Averna amaro
- 15ml (½oz) Green Chartreuse
- 7.5ml (¼oz) Velvet Falernum
- 2 dashes Regans' orange bitters
- 2 dashes Peychaud's bitters

name: St Clemence
instructions: SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass charged with half of the tonic water
TOP with rest of tonic.
review: A gin-charged alcoholic riff on the classic non-alcoholic Saint Clements cocktail.

name: Callahan's Painkiller
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: This riff on the classic Painkiller features the distinctive flavours of coffee and cacao spiced rum.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Michael D Callahan at The Compound Collective, Singapore.

name: Crème Anglaise Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with cocoa powder.
review: As the name suggests, reminiscent of alcoholic crème anglaise.
history: Created in 2004 by yours truly in London, England.

name: Drop By Drop
instructions: STIR first 4 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with beer.
review: Chocolate, treacle, zesty citrus and spice with faintly smoky beer.
