history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Katalin Bene at Voodoo Reyes - Potions & Botanicals, Geneva, Switzerland.

name: Cuban Sarsaparilla
instructions: SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with rootbeer.
review: Root beer's vanilla, anise or liquorice notes combine with coffee and cacao rum with underlying bitter and citrus.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Andrew Augustine Lugiman, Barong Bar; Fairmont, Jakarta.

name: Black Corn and Oil
instructions: Select and pre-chill a ROCKS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A coffee and cacao spiced rum riff on the classic Corn 'n' Oil.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Uwe Christiansen at Heart of St. Pauli in Hamburg, Germany.

name: Crème de Café
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
review: Coffee predominates over the creaminess with hints of aniseed and rum.

name: Black Cuban
instructions: MUDDLE mint in base of glass
Add next 4 ingredients, STIR with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
TOP with champagne.
review: A cacao and coffee spiced rum riff on an Old Cuban.
history: Recipe created in 2019 by Helder Fortes Silva, Rotterdam, Netherlands. Helder's original recipe uses homemade mint-lime cordial and black pepper tincture.

name: The Witches
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Express lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use to garnish with a dill sprig.
review: Herbal and complex.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Chantal Tseng at Washington D.C.'s Reading Room. Chantal is inspired to create her "literary cocktails" by works of literature, in this case, the eponymous book by Roald Dahl.

name: Mujer Negra
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist (discarded) and float roasted coffee bean.
review: Coffee and cacao flavours shine in this rum-based aperitivo.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Ian Burrell, London, England.

name: Beneventum
instructions: SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with grapefruit soda.
review: Complex, herbal and fruit with subtle peppermint freshness.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Virginie Amélie Doucet, this cocktail was the winner of the Strega Mixology Award 2019. Virginie's original recipe uses homemade white grapefruit soda which transforms this cocktail into one worthy of a 5/5.

name: Calvados Mon Amour
instructions: SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass
POUR berry liqueur into the centre of drink to form a sunrise effect.
review: Apple brandy, pineapple and orange with a hint of cinnamon and a blast of berry fruit. Sounds sweet and fruity but this cocktail is spirituous and complex.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2006 by Salvatore Damiano, Brand Ambassador & President of United Kingdom Bartenders' Guild.

name: Coffee Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lemon wedge.
review: Just as it says on the tin, a sweet 'n' sour coffee.

name: Coffee Collins
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda and briefly stir.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon wheel slice and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry sail.
review: Long sweet 'n' sour coffee.

name: Marshmallow 'Martini'
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of roasted marshmallow and shot of champagne on the side.
review: Passion fruit and vanilla with a double treat as a garnish.

name: Cinnamon 'Martini'
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Serve with shot of sparkling wine on the side.
review: Vanilla and passion fruit with a touch of cinnamon.

name: Peach 'Martini'
instructions: MUDDLE peach in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: A riff on the 'Porn Star Martini' with peach, passion fruit, vanilla and rosemary.

name: Hot Cross Bun 'Martini'
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: A vanilla and apple brandy Espresso Martini with a touch of cinnamon spice.

name: Valentini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE strawberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with strawberry slice on rim.
review: A rich ripe strawberry and raspberry riff on the Porn Star Martini with lemon freshness and faint saffron.

name: Cranberry Collins
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring soda.
garnish: Garnish with lime slice wheel.
review: Tangy cranberry and raspberry juice with freshening zesty lemon and a touch of bittersweet herbal complexity.

name: Coco Bongo Club
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COPA GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist and half strawberry on rim.
review: Refreshing, light and long ripe strawberry and coconut water.

name: Strawberry Dog
instructions: Select and pre-chill a WINE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of salt-rimmed glass with grapefruit juice (optional) and half strawberry on rim..
review: Rich ripe juicy strawberry and cranberry with zesty grapefruit.

name: Dairy Milk Chocolate
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of cadbury Dairy Milk Miniature chocolate.
review: Perhaps serve with a shot glass of champagne on the side so this cocktail literally contains a "glass and a half".  The picture of the glass and a half of milk is still used on Dairy Milk bars, the UK's best-selling chocolate bar, but sadly the slogan was removed after 80 years in 2010 due to contravening European labelling regulations.

name: Derby Cocktail
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE mint (just to bruise) in base of stirring glass
Add other ingredients, STIR with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: This Martini-style classic was originally devoid of sugar syrup but the hint of sweetness mellows the gin and balances the bitters. Alternatively, use Old Tom gin and omit sugar. The splash of chilled water in our recipe helps open and stretch this cocktail.
history: Adapted from Harry MacElhone's 1926 Harry's ABC of Mixing Cocktails where Harry credits this cocktail to "E. G. de Gastreaux of Canal and Vine Streets, Cincinnati, 1903." Harry also says to "Serve with an olive" but this this accoutrement is omitted when the Derby Cocktail later reappears in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Ají Dulce
instructions: MUDDLE cucumber in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with ginger ale.
review: In South American Spanish ají means 'chili pepper' and dulce means 'sweet', so the name of this drink translates as 'sweet chili pepper'.
history: Created in 2019 by Din Hassan, Singapore.

name: Tears and Dreams
instructions: Select and pre-chill a WINE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Prepare garnish of edible flower and fresh kaffir lime leaf.
review: Fruity but well-balanced with distinctive coffee and cacao flavours from the rum.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Katalin Bene at Voodoo Reyes - Potions & Botanicals in Geneva, Switzerland.

name: Mexican Firing Squad Special
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish of orange slice, pineapple wedge and maraschino cherry.
review:
Tequila dominates this lightly citrusy fairly dry classic. The bitters play a key role in both balance and flavour.

It's worth noting that this drink is a sour served in a highball glass but it is not a Highball and should not be topped with soda. A highball glass should be a max capacity of 10oz / 296ml. Use such a glass filled with ice and this recipe will fill to a good washline without need, or room, for a soda top.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Charles H Baker's 1939 The Gentleman's Companion.

name: Sinnerman
instructions: Put 2 spoons of powdered panela sugar in the glass
Add the vermouth and a splash of soda water
Use a barspoon to dissolve the sugar in the water
Add spiced rum, bitters and ice then STIR.
review: Sugar spice and all things nice would sum up this drink well if only the name was Niceman rather than Sinnerman. The sugar, in this case, flavoursome Panela sugar, contributes more favour than sweetness to what is a surprisingly dry, coffee and cacao laced drink with rosso vermouth (originally Oscar 697 Vermouth Rosso) adding a touch of richness.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Marco Riccetti at Inside cocktail bar in Torino, Italy.

name: Spritzer Fresco
instructions: POUR first 3 ingredients into ice-filled glass
TOP with prosecco.
review: Zesty, floral, aromatic and fresh. A complex and refreshing spritz.
history: Created in 2020 by Salvatore Calabrese at the Donovan Bar in London, England.

name: White Water Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Zesty citrus and fresh mint with delicate rose.
history: Created in 2020 by Salvatore "The Maestro" Calabrese at the Donovan Bar, London, England.

name: Silent Tears
instructions: STIR first 4 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with ginger beer

<strong>Special ingredient #1</strong>: Spiced brine (made with 100g green chilli or Jalapeño cooked in 100g of water until boiling point and then add 70ml white vinegar and 15g of salt (adjust depending on chilli)
Blend and fine strain).
review: Delicate chilli and ginger spice with vegetal chocolate flavours.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Colin Chia at Nutmeg & Clove, Singapore.

name: Grappa and Ginger Highball
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime wedge squeezed and dropped into drink.
review: Aromatic green grapes, cream of soda vanilla and delicate ginger.

name: Bald Head
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist (discarded) and stuffed Fragata Green Olive.
review: This could also be termed a "Perfect Martini" and it could be argued that bald is a perfect hairstyle – at least for us baldies.
history: Adapted from a recipe in David A. Embury's 1948 Fine Art of Mixing Drinks.

name: Cuban Master
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with pineapple wedge on rim skewered with maraschino cherry.
review: Well-balanced and very fruity.
history: An old cocktail discovered by yours truly during a trip to Cuba in 1999.

name: Monte Cassino Sunrise
instructions: SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
POUR grenadine around the surface of the drink
(This should sink to form sunrise effect.)
review: Long, fruity and juicy with aromatic grappa providing the backbone.

name: Tearful Chocolate Mojito
instructions: SHAKE first 5 ingredients with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with tonic
<strong>Special ingredient #1</strong>: Mint Syrup - lightly muddle 10 mint stems with 250g brown sugar, 250g white caster sugar and 600ml hot water.
review: Chocolatey mint mojito with a crisp tonic dryness.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by Din Hassan, Brand Ambassador Black Tears; Singapore.

name: Drones Club
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
review: A characterful and well-balanced rum & brandy nightcap.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by J. E. Clapham of Clapham Cocktails. Originally made with Cocchi Storico Vermouth di Torino rosso vermouth.

name: Eider Duck
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: As Jason Clapham (the D reader who brought this citrusy sweet 'n' sour cocktail to my attention in February 2020) says, it's "essentially a cherry Sidecar." Those with sweeter palates may want to add a splash of sugar syrup.
history: First published in William James "Billy" Tarling's 1937 Café Royal Bar Book.

name: Margaret Rose
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Depending on your lemon juice, this Sidecar-like Daisy may benefit from an additional 2.5ml sugar syrup to balance.
history:
Adapted from a recipe in William James "Billy" Tarling's 1937 Café Royal Cocktail Book where it is credited to "J. W. Fish".
(Our thanks to Jason Clapham of Oxford, England for bringing this recipe to our attention.)

name: Marconi
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with apple slice/wedge on the rim, or float dehydrated apple slice.
review: Calvados shines in this spirit-forward nightcap that, despite convention, is perhaps better on the rocks.
history:
Named after Guglielmo Giovanni Maria Marconi (1874 – 1937), an Italian electrical engineer and inventor who's best-known for creating a radio wave-based wireless telegraph system and is credited for being the inventor of radio.

Recipe adapted from Jacques Straub's 1913 Manual of Mixed Drinks.

name: Back To The Origin
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of blood orange zest twist.
review: Yuzu infused sake dominates this coffee and cacao spiced rum-based cocktail.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2020 by Nicolas Medicamento at the American Bar at The Savoy, London, England.

name: Nutty New Year
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of hazelnut chocolate chunk balanced across glass rim.
review: An Alexander-style dessert cocktail to see the New Year in with.
history: Created in December 2020 to see in the New Year by Angela Pearce, a Discerning Drinker (who tells us that her husband prefers a Salty New Year - see variant).

name: Cubanita
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: The Bloody Mary returns - this time with rum.
history: Other Bloody Mary variants, in addition to its history, can be found on our Bloody Mary cocktail page.

name: Grapefruit Mule
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
POUR all ingredients into ice-filled glass.
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Garnish with grapefruit wedge.
review: A grapefruit riff on the classic Moscow Mule.

name: Lucky Golden Oranges
instructions: STIR first 4 ingredients with ice and strain into ice-filled glass
TOP with tonic water.
review: Zippy citrus and floral notes combine in this refreshing riff on a G&T.
history: Adapted from a recipe, originally based on Jinzu Gin with Mancino Sakura vermouth, by Gianluca Capilungo, London, England.

name: Poinciana
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE raspberries in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon twist (discarded) and fresh raspberry.
review: Bittersweet berry fruit scotch rounded by subtle coconut.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2020 by J. E. Clapham of Clapham Cocktails. Named after
Poinciana, the 1936 song composed by Nat Simon and Buddy Bernier.

name: Dram Queen
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: The powerful and pervasive bittersweet notes of Allspice Dram heavily influence this cocktail which we'd recommend is enjoyed as an after-dinner "Dram".
history: Created by Orlando Grimany Calas at The Limbo, Louisville, KY, USA.

name: Absinthe by Jimmy
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: The absinthe cocktail meets the absinthe sour with lime juice added to the holy trinity of absinthe, sugar and water.
history: Adapted from the first recipe in a 1932 pocket cocktail book called The Green Cocktail Book by "Jimmy". I've respected the original proportions with, as Jimmy says, "sugar to taste." Hence, I'd advise 10ml to 12.5ml sugar depending on your limes and palate.

name: Cazador
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist or dehydrated lime wheel slice.
review: Subtle blackcurrant and anise notes flavour this aged rum maple daiquiri.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2019 by George Hunter, this is the cocktail with which he won the South African final of Bacardi's Legacy bartender's competition, taking him and his cocktail through to the global final.

George created his recipe in Johannesburg, South Africa where Bacardi Ocho is 43% alc./vol. so the above recipe is reformatted for the 40% alc./vol. Bacardi Ocho available in Europe. George's original recipe:
Cazador
60ml Bacardi Ocho
30ml Fresh lime
15ml Maple syrup
10ml Crème de cassis
Absinthe rinse

George told us, "Cazador is my surname in Spanish (Hunter). The cocktail has been created to represent triumph and lessons learnt. Cazador is a symbol to remind people to never give up, to push through the hard times. I started my career as a bar back on minimum wage, I worked hard every single day to learn more and grow as a bartender. The first time I entered Bacardi Legacy, I didn't even make it out the first round. Six years later [2019] I entered again & won the national event in South Africa and get to represent my country on the global stage."

name: Absinthe Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf.
review: Absinthe tamed and served straight-up.
history: Dr. Ordinaire perfected his recipe for absinthe in 1792 and from day one it required the addition of water and sugar to make it palatable.

name: Cucumber and Mint Martini
instructions: MUDDLE cucumber and mint in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: A well balanced fortified salad in a glass - almost healthy.
history: Created in 2004 by David Ramos in the Netherlands.

name: Black Hotel
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of dust with nutmeg.
review: Creamy, chocolaty and delicately spicy with faint rich port fruit.
history: A spiced rum riff on the classic Chatham Hotel Special.

name: Anchorman
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail, then use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Bready genever and Irish whisky with zesty orange and orchard fruit.
history: Created in March 2020 by yours truly.

name: Cucumber Sake-Tini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE cucumber in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of cucumber slices.
review: Subtle and dry. Cucumber and sake are made for each other.
history: Created in 2004 by Lisa Ball, London, England.

name: Grapefruit Rickey
instructions: Select and pre-chill a HIGHBALL (MAX 10OZ/300ML) GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
TOP with soda.
garnish: Prepare garnish of grapefruit wedge.
review: Depending on your grapefruit and how successfully you strike the fine balance between sweet and sour, this can be a deliciously refreshing cocktail. Consider serving with breakfast.

name: Cumbersome
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
MUDDLE cucumber in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Float cucumber slice for garnish.
review: Interesting and fresh as you like with a pleasant bitterness.
history: Created in 2002 by Shelim Islam at the GE Club in London, England.

name: Tovarich
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
DRY SHAKE all ingredients (without ice) to emulsify.
SHAKE again with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of float a dehydrated lime slice wheel.
review: Vodka-laced, delightfully clean and cleansing with kummel's distinctive rich caraway and cumin flavour combining well with lime.
history: Tovarishch, tovarisch or tovarish are all Russian for comrade, friend, colleague or ally. The naming of this cocktail, which first appears in Grosby Gaige's 1944 Standard Cocktail Guide, may have been inspired by Tovaritch, a 1933 play by Jacques Deval.

name: Cuppa Joe
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Nutty coffee fortified with vodka - well balanced.
history:
Created in 2003 at Cellar Bar, New York City, USA.

A variant of an Espresso Martini. See the story behind the Espresso Martini.

name: Hugo Spritz
instructions: Select and pre-chill a WINE GLASS.
ADD mint leaves to chilled glass.
FILL glass with ice.
Add other ingredients.
Briefly STIR.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge and mint sprigs bouquet.
review: This recipe is one of many for this floral spritz and some may prefer to follow the standard 3:2:1 Spritz formula with 60ml prosecco, 40ml elderflower and 20ml soda.
history:
Pronounced 'Ugo', this spritz-style cocktail was created in June 2005 by "Roland 'AK' Gruber" at his Sanzeno wine and cocktail bar in his hometown of Naturno in South Tyrol, northern Italy. Prior to returning home to open his own bar, Ak had bartended in Switzerland and, after he was forced to close his bar, returned there. It was during this second bartending stint in Switzerland that his Hugo Spritz became a well-known contemporary classic.

AK originally named his cocktail "Otto" but quickly renamed this riff on a classic Spritz Veneziano. Originally made with lemon balm syrup, at some point, what is now a Hugo changed to being made with more readily available elderflower syrup and then elderflower liqueur.

name: Curdish Cocktail
instructions: STIR lemon curd with gin in base of shaker until curd dissolves
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Beautifully balanced with the tang of lemon curd.
history: Created in 2001 by Tadgh Ryan at West Street, London, England.

name: Nice Poire Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pear slice on rim.
review: A contemporary balanced and complex Martini with well-integrated fruity and floral flavours.

name: Custard Tart
instructions: MUDDLE physalis fruits in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain.
review: Custardy, strangely enough.
history: Created by Alex Kammerling in 2001.

name: Cherryberry Cherub
instructions: MUDDLE raspberries in base of shaker
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass.
review: Warning: use of a vanilla-flavoured vodka rather than vodka infused with a vanilla pod (as the recipe intends) can make this fruity summery cocktail a tad candy-like.
history: Adapted from a recipe created by Kim van Megen, a Discerning Drinker in Maastricht, The Netherlands.

name: Madame Mayahuel
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Whether you love or hate this spirit-forward cocktail will firstly be driven by your preference for mezcal and secondly by whether you appreciate this cocktail's floral violet notes, not to mention the sherry and amaro notes.

name: Back To Black
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange and lime zest twists.
review: Bittersweet with flavours of roasted coffee, cacao and minty freshness.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2020 by Thomas Girard, Head bartender at Operation Dagger, Singapore.

name: Absinthe Drip Cocktail (French method)
instructions: Select and pre-chill an ABSINTHE GLASS OR OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
POUR absinthe into glass.
PLACE cube of sugar on a slotted absinthe spoon resting across the top of the glass.
Using an absinthe fountain (or a bottle of chilled mineral water with a small hole in the cap), DRIP iced water over the sugar so it dissolves and drips into the glass.
Traditionally the same amount of water is added as absinthe, but full-strength absinthe requires more dilution - Keep dripping until absinthe louches (turns milky white).
Add ice, stir and serve.
review: Patience is a virtue. Slow dripping of the water is essential to dissolve the entire sugar cube and give the drink enough sweetness to balance the absinthe.
history: This is the traditional method of serving absinthe. It was common until shortly before the First World War, when the drink was banned in most countries.

name: Almond Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with three almonds dropped into the cocktail.
review: A almond-flavoured Vodkatini that delivers on its name. Best when made with tonka bitters (or vanilla bitters), adding balance and depth of flavour.
history: Created in 2005 by yours truly.

name: Adelaide Swizzle
instructions: POUR ingredients into chilled glass and half fill with crushed ice
SWIZZLE with a swizzle stick (or churn with a barspoon)
Add more crushed ice to fill and SWIZZLE some more
Serve with straws.
review: A slightly pink, dry, spicy long drink with rum and a hint of cloves and lime.
history: This is the signature cocktail at Café Adelaide's Swizzle Stick Bar, New Orleans, USA. There it is made with a liquid poured from a plain bottle marked 'top secret' but, having tried a drop, we're sure it's Falernum.

name: Alfonso (Special)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Dry yet slightly sweet with hints of orange, gin and warm spice.
history: A vintage cocktail of unknown origin that features in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book, William "Cocktail Bill" Boothby's posthumously published 1934 World's Drinks And How To Mix Them; and Victor J. Bergeron's 1972 Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide Revised.

name: Algeria
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Pisco, apricot and orange combine harmoniously in this medium-dry, balanced cocktail with a tangy bite.
history: This cocktail was served in the 1960s at Joseph Baum's La Fonda Del Sol restaurant in New York City and may have been created there.

name: Allegrottini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Cleansing and citrusy with moderate sourness.
history: Adapted from a cocktail discovered in 2005 at the Four Seasons Hotel in Prague, Czech Republic.

name: Amsterdam Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Orange-yellow in flavour with genever adding a pleasant spiritous bready backbone to orange fruit. Perhaps serve with marmalade on toast for breakfast.
history: Adapted from Hyma Gale and Gerald F. Marco's 1937 book The How and When.

name: Angel Face
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
REGAL STIR all ingredients (including lemon zest) with ice.
STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lemon zest twist and discard. Garnish with apple wedge on rim or float (dehydrated) apple slice.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest and apple wedge or (dehydrated) apple slice..
review:
Rich apricot and apple with a backbone of botanical gin. Balanced rather than dry or sweet.

This cocktail looks better when stirred, but Harry Craddock's original 1930 recipe is shaken, and the extra dilution helps mellow this spirit-forward classic. Hence, I've added a splash of water to increase the dilution of the stirred recipe. Although an act of heresy, a splash of processed and packaged clear "apple juice drink" also works in place of added chilled water.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book which stipulates "Shake well and strain into cocktail glass," rather than to stir as modern convention dictates.

name: Asylum Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass (preferably over a large cube or chunk of block ice).
garnish: Garnish with star anise floated on surface.
review: Seabrook said of this drink, "look like rosy dawn, taste like the milk of Paradise, and make you plenty crazy." He must have been a Pernod lover.
history: Created by William Seabrook, famous for his account of eating human flesh, and first published in Sterling North and Carl Kroch's 1935 So Red the Nose or Breath in the Afternoon.

name: Aviation Cocktail (Savoy spec.)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: Tangy gin-laced lemon sourness with aromatic maraschino - too many of these and you really will be flying.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book.

name: Bermuda Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with orange zest twist (expressed over cocktail) &/or peach wedge on rim.
review: Gin with a sweetening touch of peach, orange and pomegranate.
history: Adapted from Victor Bergeron's Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide (1972 revised edition).

name: Bermuda Rose
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of apricot slice on rim.
review: Delicate, floral, and aromatic, it has a hint of sweetness but not to offend. Despite the lack of juice in this recipe, don't be tempted to stir rather than shake; this is classically a shaken cocktail, and it's all the better for it. At its best, drink whilst still cloudy from shaking.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Victor Bergeron's 1972 Trader Vic's Bartender's Guide (Revised Edition).

name: Bistro Sidecar
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Although significantly twisted from the classic, this is still recognisably a Sidecar in style.
history:
Adapted from a recipe by chef Kathy Casey Food Studios, Seattle, USA. Kathy's original recipe called for a sugar rim and tangerine juice.

See our Sidecar cocktail page for the full story behind the origins of the Sidecar, its history and variations.

name: Amber
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
MUDDLE ginger in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients and SHAKE with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Garnish with apple wedge on rim.
review: A combination of adult flavours in a long, thirst-quenching drink. Also good served straight-up.
history: Created in 2001 by Douglas Ankrah for Akbar at the Red Fort in Soho, London, England.

name: Black Feather
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Spirit-forward but with rounded cognac and a hint of orange. For dry palates only. Some may prefer orange curaçao in place of triple sec.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2000 by Robert Hess and published on DrinkBoy.com.

name: Black Jack Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: More burgundy than black, but dark fruits of the forest dominate this medium-dry cocktail.
history:
Adapted from a recipe in the 1937 bookThe How and When by Hyman Gale and Gerald F. Marco.

The name Black Jack traditionally refers to a water bottle made from air-dried leather. When the leather was dried, it tended to turn black.

name: Blackthorn No. 4
ingredients: 30 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: This drink benefits from a long, chilling and diluting stir. The result is Martini in style, fruity yet dry.
history: This, one of the numerous versions of the Blackthorn cocktail, first appears in the 1930 Swallows edition of William "Cocktail Bill" Boothby's World Drinks and How To Prepare Them.

name: Blade Runner
ingredients: 15 ml OP high ester aged pot still rum
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge and Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: Tangy and fruity but not too sweet.
history: Adapted from a cocktail discovered in 2005 at Zoulou Bar, Berlin, Germany.

name: Bloody Caesar
instructions: ROLL rather than shake all ingredients in a shaker with ice and fine strain into ice-filled glass
review: A peculiarly Canadian fishy twist on the classic Bloody Mary.
history:
Virtually every bar in Canada can make you a Bloody Caesar, it's pretty much Canada's national drink. It may seem odd that a drink invented in Canada took the name of Rome's most famous emperor, but though its origins are Canadian its inspiration is very much Italian.

Charged with inventing a signature drink to celebrate the opening of Marco's Italian restaurant at the Calgary Inn in 1969, Walter Chell was inspired by the flavours of Spaghetti Vongole (spaghetti with clams) and named his new cocktail after the Roman emperor.

The typical finish to what Canadians simply call a Caesar is to wet the rim with lemon juice and then dip the glass in celery salt. However, the Caesar actually lends itself to an array of garnishes. Celery and a lime wedge are more traditional, but since the Caesar has become so popular, restaurants and bars have felt the need to customise the garnish to make their version unique, adopting anything from olives and peppers to pickled asparagus and shrimp. Some drinks end up looking more like an appetiser! I've gone for the elegant and deliciously crunchy green bean to garnish ours.

name: Bloomsbury Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: This pinky/rusty drink benefits from a good long stir but the result is an aromatic, medium dry, spicy vanilla Martini.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2003 by Robert Hess and published on DrinkBoy.com

name: Ambrosia
instructions: Select and pre-chill a FLUTE GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: No garnish to prepare.
review: Dry, fortified fizz with a hint of apple.

name: Brazilian Cosmopolitan
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: The distinctive character of cachaça bursts through the fruit in this twist on the contemporary classic.
history: For more recipes, origins and history behind the Cosmopolitan cocktail, see our Cosmopolitan page.

name: Caipirinha (Contemporary Serve)
instructions: MUDDLE lime wedges in the base of shaker to release juice and oils in its skin
Add cachaça and sugar
SHAKE with 6oz scoop crushed ice and pour all without straining into glass.
history:
In its native Brazil it is usual to serve this drink with cubed ice (actually most usually holly tubular machine ice) but the drink travelled to, and became popular the UK at a time when the use of crushed ice was fashionable, so in this and other 'new' cachaça markets the use of crushed ice has become the norm.

For information on the history and other variations of the Caipirinha, please see our Caipirinha cocktail page.

name: Californian Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A medium dry, fragrant orange Martini

name: Canadian Apple
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of apple slice.
review: Refreshing and balanced with just the right amount of citrus acidity.
history: Adapted from a drink discovered in 2005 at the Four Seasons Hotel, Prague, Czech Republic.

name: Ambrosia Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: Easy-drinking but complex with a herbal edge.
history: Created by yours truly and named after the Greek for "elixir of life, the food of the Gods." However, here in the UK, "Ambrosia" is a brand of custard, so advocaat seemed an appropriate ingredient. And if there is a God, he/she/it surely drinks Chartreuse.

name: Caprice
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A long stir delivers the dilution necessary for this aromatic, delicately spiced and herbal Wet Martini.

name: Caribe Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge on rim.
review: A fruity, clove-spiced Daiquiri.

name: Carrol Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered pickled walnut or onion.
review: Basically a Brandy Manhattan without bitters. Spirit-forward and dry yet easy.
history: A pre-Prohibition era cocktail published in the 1923 book "Harry" of Ciro's ABC of Mixing Cocktails.

name: Caruso Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint leaf floated on the cocktail or pegged/stuck with spot of honey to the rim.
review: Emerald green with full-on mint. Good as a digestif after a tenor-sized meal. This was originally a shaken cocktail, and despite modern-day convention, we prefer the Caruso opened and cooled by a vigorous shake. If all those bubbles upset you, then perhaps throw or, as a last resort, stir.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book, this cocktail was created at The Savoy for the tenor Enrico Caruso in the early 20th century.

name: Casablanca
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker.
DRY SHAKE (without ice).
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Go easy on the aromatic bitters (use drops rather than dashes) or your Casablanca will be more orange than "blanca". Original recipes in The New American Bartender's Guide and Jones' Complete Barguide omit egg white, but this variation on the White Lady tastes and looks better with an appropriately white fluffy head.
history: Eponymously named after Michael Curtiz's 1942 romantic drama movie starring Humphrey Bogart, Ingrid Bergman and Paul Henreid. Filmed and set during World War II, the plot centres around an American expatriate (Bogart) whose love for a women (Bergman) distracts him from helping a Czechoslovak resistance leader (Henreid) escape from the Vichy-controlled city of Casablanca. The women, is the resistance leader's wife and if that were not enough, the script famously delivers lines such as "Play it, Sam," "Of all the gin joints in all the towns in all the world, she walks into mine" and "Here's looking at you kid."

name: Charente Collins
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE mint in base of shaker (just to bruise)
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain into glass filled with crushed ice
Serve with straws.
review: Created in 2005 by Kieran Bailey, The Light Bar, London, England.
history:
Refreshing orange and lemon with a hint of elderflower.

Also see Collins Cocktails - recipes & history.

name: Chatham Hotel Special
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with freshly grated nutmeg.
review: We've slightly changed the proportions and replaced the original lemon zest garnish with a little extra spice.
history: This mid-1900s classic from New York's Chatham Hotel was resurrected by Ted Haigh in his 2004 book Vintage Spirits & Forgotten Cocktails.

name: Chocolarita

name: Club Cocktail (Embury's recipe)

STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.

garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.

name: Club Cocktail (Craddock's recipe)

name: Absinthe Frappé

FINE STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
CHURN (stir).
garnish: Serve with a straw.

history:
Dating from the early 1870s and said to have been created in 1873/4 by Cayetano Ferrer at Aleix's Coffee House, New Orleans, which consequently became known as The Absinthe Room. Today the establishment is fittingly known as the Old Absinthe House.

George J. Kappeler's 1895 Modern American Drinks – How to mix and Serve All Kinds of Cups and Drinks includes two Absinthe Frappé recipes.

name: Crossbow

garnish: Prepare garnish of orange juice and chocolate powder rim &/or orange zest twist.

name: Crux

instructions: Select a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.

garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.

history:
Until I revisited the Crux on 23rd February 2023, our recipe followed the original equal parts proportions with:
30ml (1oz) Cognac
30ml (1oz) Triple sec
30ml (1oz) Dubonnet Red
30ml (1oz) Lemon juice

name: CVO Firevault
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.

garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.

name: Cyder Press

garnish: Garnish with dehydrated apple slice floated on cocktail.

review: Damson and amaretto sharpened by lemon juice.

name: Dark 'N' Stormy (D's recipe)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring ginger beer.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge on rim.
review: A Daiquiri made with pungent dark rum, topped and made refreshing with spicy ginger beer. Part of the Mule family - but is distinctive due to the assertive flavour of the dark rum.
history:
The national drink of Bermuda, where ginger beer and Gosling's rum (a brand of dark rum often associated with this cocktail) are produced.

Who first made or named the Dark 'n' Stormy is unknown, but legend has it that it was created, or at least first enjoyed, by British sailors on shore leave in Bermuda after World War I.

name: DC Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
review: Vanilla, chocolate and a hint of nut. Enjoy as an altern'atini or add more sugar syrup to make this into a dessert cocktail.
history: Adapted from a cocktail discovered in 2000 at Teatro Club in London, England. Firmly from the age of "altern'atinis."

name: Death in the Afternoon
instructions: Select a FLUTE GLASS.
POUR first 2 ingredients into chilled glass and briefly stir.
TOP with sparkling wine.
Express lime zest twist over cocktail and discard.
Float rose petal.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist and rose petal.
review: Bravado (absinthe) dominates this drink, alongside hints of biscuity champagne. I've added a touch of sugar to tame this hardman's cocktail.
history:
Created by Ernest Hemingway (not just named after his book), this recipe was the author's contribution to a 1935 cocktail book titled So Red the Nose, or Breath in the Afternoon. I've toned down the maestro's original recipe a little as it included a whopping ounce-and-a-half of absinthe. The book stipulates: "Pour 1 jigger of absinthe into a champagne glass, add iced champagne until it attains the proper opalescent milkiness. Drink 3 to 5 of these slowly."

This is followed by a note about the drink's origin by Hemingway, "This was arrived at by the author and three officers of H.M.S. Danae after having spent seven hours overboard trying to get Capt. Bra Saunders' fishing boat off a bank where she had gone with us in a N.W. gale." The book continues with a paragraph by its compilers, "It takes a man with hair on his chest to drink five Absinths and Champagne Cocktails and still handle the English language in the Hemingway fashion. But Ernest has proved his valour, not alone in his cups. Captain of the swimming team at Oak Park high school - first American to be wounded on the Italian front during the World War (with 227 individual wounds to his credit) - tossed by a bull in the streets of Pamplona while rescuing his friend Donald Ogden Stewart - deep sea fisherman - big game hunter - and one of the first citizens of Key West - Hemingway is the man who can hold his Absinthe like a postwar novelist."

name: Deauville (D's)
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: A calvados-influenced riff on the Sidecar, or a cognac-influenced riff on the Apple Cart. Either way, served in a more bite-sized portion.
history: My version of the classic Deauville.

name: Dempsey
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: Depending on your grenadine, this cocktail ranges from blush pink to barely coloured and the flavour will also vary. The colour is immaterial, but nail the balance, with absinthe tamed by grenadine but still assertive, and you've a delightful, spirituous aperitif or late-night sharpener.
history:
Recipe adapted from Robert Vermeire's 1922 Cocktails How to Mix Them. The Dempsey also appears in Harry MacElhone's's Harry's ABC of Mixing Cocktails published the same year and is repeated in his 1923 Harry of Ciro's ABC Of Mixing Cocktails. Both MacElhone's books attribute this cocktail's creation to "Fred. Martin, Casino, Deaville, 1922" while Vermeire says "this drink was introduced at Deauville, 1921." Built in 1912, Casino Barrière de Deauville is an elegant palace of slot machines, crystal chandeliers and marble in Normandy, France.

Vermeire, and indeed the drink's name, reference a boxing match dubbed the "Fight of the Century", which saw Jack Dempsey (world heavyweight champion) knockout Georges Carpentier (world light-heavyweight champion) in the fourth round. The bout took place on 2nd July 1921 at Boyle's Thirty Acres in Jersey City, USA, and was the first boxing fight to produce $1,000,000 dollars in revenue.

name: Derby Daiquiri
instructions: DRY BLEND (without ice) first 4 ingredients and gradually add Xanthan gum while continuing to blend
Add half a 7oz scoop of crushed ice and blend again
Pour into chilled glass.
review:
For those seeking the shaken version previously above:
Glass: Coupe
Garnish: Orange zest twist
How to make: SHAKE all ingredients with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
60ml (2oz) Light white rum
22.5ml (.75oz) Orange juice
15ml (.5oz) Lime juice
7.5 (.25oz) Sugar syrup
history: The Kentucky Derby has long been associated with the Mint Julep and in 1959, the folk behind the Florida Derby turned to a famous local bar in search of a libation to serve at their own racecourse, Gulfstream Park in Hallandale Beach. So, in 1959, Mariano Licudine at The Mai-Kai Polynesian Restaurant and Tiki bar in Oakland Park, Florida, created the Derby, the official cocktail of the Florida Derby.

name: Detroit Martini
instructions: SHAKE all ingredients (including mint) with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.
review: Vodka doused mint with the merest hint of lime. Clean and flavoursome.
history: Created by Dick Bradsell in the mid 90s and based on his Cowboy Hoof Martini.

name: Detropolitan
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS flamed orange zest twist over cocktail, then use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: Yet another twist on the Cosmopolitan.
history: Created at Detroit, London, England.

name: Diamond Fizz
ingredients: Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: SHAKE first three ingredients with ice and strain into chilled glass (without ice)
TOP with champagne.
review: Why top a Gin Fizz with soda when you can use brut sparkling wine?
history: A long lost classic.

name: Diana's Bitter
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: One for aficionados of gin-laced bittersweet cocktails.

name: Diplomat
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over cocktail and discard.
garnish: Garnish with skewered cherry.
review: Wonderfully aromatic and dry. More of an aperitivo than a digestivo.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Harry Craddock's 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book. The Diplomat also notably appears in Patrick Gavin Duffy's 1934 The Official Mixer's Manual.

name: Divino's
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with white chocolate shaved or grated over cocktail.
review: The chocolate liqueur takes the acidity off the wine without masking its flavour.
history: Discovered in 2005 at DiVino, Hong Kong.

name: Doctor Funk
instructions: Select and pre-chill a DOUBLE OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: With its generous dose of rum and absinthe, too many of these faintly liquorice-like cocktails and you'll need a doctor.
history:
Created by Don Beach in the early 1950s at his Don The Beachcomber restaurant in Palm Springs. Beach was inspired by and adapted a medicinal absinthe-laced limeade, concocted and prescribed decades earlier by Doctor Bernard Funk, a German physician in Samoa. 

Dr. Funk was the personal physician of Robert Louis Stevenson, the famous author of Treasure Island, during his final years in Samoa. Stevenson, who suffered from lifelong bronchial issues, settled in Samoa in 1890 at an estate he named Vailima. The good doctor reportedly created a medicinal tonic for the ailing writer made with absinthe, lemon juice, lime juice, grenadine, and soda. The potion is described by Frederick O'Brien in his 1921 book Mystic Isles of the South Seas.

Dr. Funk arrived in Samoa in 1880 and left to return to Germany for medical treatment in 1911. He died on 8 April 1911, aged 66, in a Berlin hospital. According to his will, after his death, a block of granite was shipped from Germany to Samoa and erected on the shore of Lake Lanotoo. Inscribed, "Dr. Bernhard Funk" followed by the dates of his arrival and departure from Somoa, and his birth and death. [For more information, tikicentral.com]

name: Dolce-Amaro
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.

name: Dolce Havana

SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge.
review: A bittersweet, rum-laced melange of Mediterranean fruit.

name: Dolores
ingredients: 60 ml Red quinquina e.g. Dubonnet, Byrrh
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon zest twist.
review: Aromatic and well balanced, provided you use French-made Dubonnet.
history: A classic. Some recipes include a splash of orange juice.

name: Double Vision
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with skewered berries in rim.
review: Citrus fresh with apple, red berries and faint floral notes.

name: Downhill Racer
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge.
review: Aged rum sweetened, softened and flavoured with pineapple and amaretto. A crowd pleaser.

name: Durango

TOP with soda.

name: Dutch Breakfast Cocktail
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.

name: Dutch Courage
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
review: A refreshing alternative to a traditional English lemonade.
history: Created in 2002 by Alex Kammerling, London, England.

name: Dyevitchka
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.

FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.

name: Earl Grey Fizz

TOP with champagne.

Created in 2004 by yours truly in London, England.
Collins Cocktail history and recipes

SHAKE all ingredients with ice.

name: Eastern Promise
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.

name: Easy Tiger

Add honey and tequila, and STIR until honey is dissolved.

name: Eden

TOP with tonic water.

Alternatively, mix and warm in pan over heat - do not boil.

name: El Burro
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.

name: Absinthe Sour
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.

name: Finitaly
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.

garnish: Garnish with raspberries.

name: Finnberry Martini

name: First of July

Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and fine strain into chilled glass.

name: Fizz (generic name)
ingredients: Top up with Soda from siphon
instructions: SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice and strain back into shaker
DRY SHAKE (without ice) and strain into chilled glass (no ice in glass)
TOP with soda (ideally dispensed from a siphon).
review: We recommend the Derby Fizz with its combination of liqueur and spirits over these more traditional versions.
history:
Like the Collins, this mid-19th century classic is basically a sour lengthened with carbonated water and at first glance there is little difference between a Fizz and a Collins. However, there are several distinguishing features.

A Collins should be served in at least a 12 ounce, and ideally a 14 ounce tall glass, while that used for a Fizz should be no bigger than eight ounces. A Collins should be served in an ice-filled glass, while a Fizz should be served in a chilled glass without ice.

A Fizz should also be made using carbonated water from a siphon in preference to soda from bottles or cans. The burst of pressure from the siphon helps build a bubbly head while the tiny bubbles generated give off carbonic acid, benefiting the flavour and the mouth-feel of the drink.

For proportions we have turned to David A. Embury's seminal 1948 The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks, in which he recommends: 

"1 - or a little less sweet (sugar, fruit syrup, or liqueur),
2 sour (lime or lemon juice),
3 - or a little more - strong (spirituous liquor), 
and 4 weak (charged water and ice)."

We've interpreted this as follows:

2 shots spirit (gin, whiskey, vodka, brandy)
1 shot lemon or lime juice,
½ shot sugar syrup,
topped up with soda.
We've also added half a fresh egg white, which technically makes the drink a 'Silver Fizz'.

name: Flamingo
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of thin line of Creole bitters across surface of cocktail &/or pineapple wedge on rim.
review: If using packaged pineapple juice (rather than fresh), then it pays to add a couple of muddled wedges of fresh pineapple to the recipe above as this improves both the taste and this pink cocktail's frothy head.
history: Adapted from a recipe in Ted Saucier's 1951 book Bottoms Up where it's credited to The Flamingo Hotel, Las Vegas, USA. The Flamingo Hotel & Casino was opened in 1946 by mobster Bugsy Siegel and his partners as the third hotel on the Strip, where it is now the last surviving casino to have opened before 1950.

name: The Flirt
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with a lipstick kiss on the glass.
review: A fruity sweet 'n' sour cocktail to challenge dishwashers across the land.
history: Adapted from a recipe created in 2002 by Dick Bradsell at Lonsdale House in Notting Hill, London, England.

name: Flirtini No. 1
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge.
review: It's a French Martini so hard not to like.
history: Made famous on television's Sex and the City. It is said to have been created in 2003 for Sarah Jessica Parker at Guastavinos in New York City, USA.

name: Flirtini #2
ingredients: Top up with Brut sparkling wine
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with sparkling wine.
garnish: Prepare garnish of Luxardo Maraschino Cherry.
review: A flirtatious little number that slips down easily.
history: Adapted from a recipe by the New York bartender Dale DeGroff.

name: Floral Martini
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with edible flower.
review: This aptly named gin Martini is floral but semi-dry and spirit-forward.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2003 at Zander Bar in London, England.

name: Daiquiri (Emilio Gonzalez's blended No. 1)
instructions: BLEND all ingredients with 6oz scoop of crushed ice
Pour into glass and serve.
review: Great on a hot day, but the coldness masks much of the flavour evident when this drink is served 'up' or natural.
history:
Emilio Gonzalez is said to have adapted the Natural Daiquiri into this frozen version at the Plaza Hotel in Cuba. However, Constantino "Constante" Ribalaïgua Vert of  Havana's famous El Floridita made the drink famous.
See Daiquiri cocktail history and variations

name: Apple Crumble No. 2
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Dust with cinnamon powder.
review: Easy to make and equally easy to drink.

name: Fluffy Duck

SHAKE first 4 ingredients with ice.

name: Flutter

name: Fly Like a Butterfly

instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.

name: Flying Dutchman
instructions: Select and pre-chill a NICK & NORA GLASS.
STIR all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: A Martini-style genever forward cocktail with bittersweet zesty orange.

name: Flying Grasshopper
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprig tip floated in the centre of the cocktail.

history: A 1970s or earlier more alcoholic riff on the Grasshopper cocktail. Some, including Stan Jones' 1977 Jones' Complete Bar Guide omit the cream: 1 vodka, ¾ green crème de menthe and ¾ white creme de cacao to make a clear emerald green cocktail.

name: Flying Tigre Coctel

name: Frankenjack

(New & Revised Edition) where the recipe is credited to "the two proprietors of a very, very well-known Speakeasy in New York City." The same recipe is repeated in Harry Craddock's influential 1930 The Savoy Cocktail Book, and under the name "Frankenstein Cocktail" in the 1938 book The How and When by Hyman Gale & Gerald F. Marco.

Recipe by yours truly in 2004.

Our Martini cocktail page details its evolution and other common variants.

name: Fruit and Nut Chocolate Martini

Crumble a piece of Cadbury's Flake over the cocktail.

FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist and almond flakes.

name: Fruit Salad

garnish: Prepare garnish of fruit Salad chew.

name: Full Circle

SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into ice-filled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge.
review: Fruity and easy-drinking, yet with complexity from the gin. At least that's how this cocktail tastes when made with pomegranate juice freshly squeezed using a pillar press juicer. (1 pomegranate yields approx. 135ml juice.)
history: Adapted from a drink discovered in 2004 at Mandarin Oriental, New York City, USA. The name is a reference to the bar's location - Columbus Circle, where one of the world's first one-way rotary systems (roundabout) was implemented in 1904.

name: Fu Manchu Daiquiri
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of pineapple wedge .
review: A Natural Daiquiri with a refreshing, clean, citrusy, minty edge.
history: Adapted from a recipe in David A. Embury's 1948 The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks.

name: Funky Monkey
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COCONUT SHELL OR COLLINS GLASS.
BLEND all ingredients with 7oz scoop of crushed ice.
Serve with straws.
garnish: Garnish with coconut strips.
review: Be sure to use a ripe or even over-ripe banana in this tropical style drink.
history: Created in 1998 by Tony Abou-Ganim in Las Vegas, USA.

name: Gin and Sin
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
EXPRESS orange zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of orange zest twist.
review: This is one of those drinks that benefits from a little dilution to prevent the citrus and gin becoming too aggressive.

name: Gin Berry
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COUPE GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
Express lime zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lime zest twist.
review: Berry flavours combine harmoniously with gin - what an appropriate name.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2004 by Chris Lacey, UK.

name: Gin Daisy
instructions: Select and pre-chill an OLD-FASHIONED GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
garnish: Garnish with skewered berries.
review:
When made with good crushed ice, this is a serious gin cocktail, and the combination of botanicals, herbal liqueur, and grenadine gives this classic an almost rose-like flavour.

It's common to add a splash of soda when making Daisies, and if you want to do this, then add the soda to your shaker, after shaking but prior to straining. However, the crushed ice mitigates the need for this additional dilution.
history: The gin daisy dates from to the late-1800s and the first recipe appears in Jerry Thomas' 1876 The Bar-Tender's Guide.

name: Gin Fix
instructions: Select and pre-chill a GOBLET GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass filled with crushed ice.
Briefly CHURN (stir).
EXPRESS lemon zest twist over the cocktail and use as garnish.
garnish: Prepare garnish of lemon slice wheel.
review: A Gin Sour served over crushed ice in a goblet.
history: The fix is an old classic that's very similar to the Daisy.

name: Gin Fixed

SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into glass two-thirds filled with crushed ice.
CHURN and add more crushed ice so the cocktail fills the glass.
garnish: Serve with a straw.
review: Sweet and sour with a spirity pineapple twang.

name: Apple Mojito
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.

CHURN (stir) with bar spoon.
Fill glass with more crushed ice.
TOP with apple soda and briefly stir.
garnish: Garnish with mint sprigs bouquet.
review: An enduring classic given a touch of apple. Those with a sweet tooth may want to add more apple liqueur or even a dash of sugar syrup.
history:
Recipe by yours truly in 2005.
Mojito Cocktail history.

name: Gin Garden
instructions: Select and pre-chill a MARTINI GLASS.
MUDDLE cucumber in base of shaker.
Add other ingredients.
SHAKE with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
garnish: Prepare garnish of float 3 cucumber slices.
review: A veritable English shrubbery with flowers, fruit and vegetables flourishing in harmony.
history: Adapted from a drink that Dan Warner at Zander and Tobias Blazquez Garcia at Steam collaborated on in London, England, in 2001.

name: Gin Genie
instructions: Lightly MUDDLE mint in base of shaker (just to bruise)
Add other ingredients, SHAKE with ice and strain into glass filled with crushed ice.
review: A fruit-led long drink for gin-loving Bowie fans.
history: Adapted from a drink created in 2002 by Wayne Collins, UK.

name: Gin Gin Mule
instructions: Select and pre-chill a COLLINS GLASS.
SHAKE all ingredients with ice.
FINE STRAIN into chilled glass.
TOP with ginger beer.
FILL glass with ice.
garnish: Garnish with lime wedge and mint sprigs bouquet.
review: Zingy ginger and gin's botanical notes make this much more than just another take on the Moscow Mule.
history:
Adapted from a recipe created in 2000 by Audrey Saunders at Beacon, New York City, USA. Audreys's original (and our previous) recipe calls for muddled fresh ginger and sugar syrup (rather than ginger liqueur):
- 2 slice Fresh ginger root (thumbnail-sized)
- 60ml (2oz) Gin
- 15ml (½oz) Lime juice
- 7.5ml (¼oz) Sugar syrup
- 12 fresh Mint leaves
- 82.5ml (2¾oz)Top with Ginger beer

name: Gin Sling
instructions: Select and pre-chill a SLING GLASS.
SHAKE first 3 ingredients with ice.
STRAIN into ice-filled glass whilst also pouring soda.
garnish: Garnish with lemon slice.
review: Sugar balances the citrus juice, the spirit fortifies and the carbonate lengthens.
history: 'Sling' comes from the German word 'schlingen', meaning 'to swallow' and is a style of drink which was popular from the late 1800s.

name: Gina
instructions: POUR all ingredients into chilled glass, add ice and briefly stir.
review: The lemon and blackcurrant mask the character of the gin.

name: Gin-Ger and Tonic
